```
On Mon, 03 May 1999 20:12:41 +1000, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Let me run some real world data tests and see what kind of runtimes I
get. 

Using Realia COBOL (Where I can get good stats and ASM output) - the
size of the code segment in your solution is 77F  - Mine is 767 -
Pretty darn close.  

I need to dummy up some data - say 2Million records or so.  And run
some repeated tests.  I think there was something misleading in your
counts - the "Until's" have OR's in them - and each OR is an
additional compare - which should inflate your counts to be only
slightly less than mine.  OTOH - My check for High Values MUST come
first (unlike what I coded in the examp.cob), and that will really
increase my comparisons by the number of records.  I have to think
that for that reason alone your method will be more efficient at
runtime.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Labels .. Pros and Cons

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372c7628.216165688@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7fg2f3$1oe8@enews3.newsguy.com> <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan> <OAJUXPqj#GA.312@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com> <ueeml7uyyx.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <3725BFC4.5205DB2E@zip.com.au> <bLnV2.4020$B55.313802@dfiatx1-snr1> <3728DD58.D5770059@zip.com.au> <3728e366.72458097@news1.ibm.net> <3729AF61.C2CB9CA6@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Uwk5Ls6lqRdb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <372C1EB6.C3F6F683@zip.com.au>`

```
On Sun, 02 May 1999 19:45:26 +1000, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
wrote:


>The first thing to note is the reverse logic in the break routines,
>first output and then initialise.  So there is some
>'understanding' problems similar to the priming read anyway.
>

I don't understand! <G>  Seriously, I do explain this in the book with
the reasoning behind it.  

>The second thing is the extra test for first occurence for every
>iteration.  If the tests can be exclusive then the priming read
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This example corrects that by changing the order of the Evaluate.

>The third thing is that the intialisation stuff must be duplicated
>a couple of times (once outside in the setup and once in the
>controlling loop).  It just does not fall naturally out.
>

I think what you are talking about is the move of the fields to the
save fields.  This occurs in two places - once for the first
iteration, and then individually in the break routines.

>I wish to note that the code provided by Thane is an internal sort
>and ay actually require structure like this because the read
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the sample into priming read to show it is simpler.

I've removed the sort and changed the code a bit to avoid violating my
publishers copyright.  

Note:  The perform of the lower level breaks is repeated in the
Evaluate.  One COULD perform the lower level break within each
routine.   IE:  The first thing in the Date-Break paragraph could be a
perform of the Type-Break and so on.  I don't do this because it makes
it more difficult later to insert a new level of break in the middle.

  
000010 @OPTIONS MAIN,TEST
000020 Identification Division.
000030 Program-Id.  Examp.
000031* Control Break Example
000043 Environment Division.
000050 Configuration Section.
000055 Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
000056 Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
000061 Input-Output  Section.
000062 File-Control.
000063     Select Input-File Assign To "Input.Dat".
000070     Select Report-File Assign To Printer.
000076 Data Division.
000077 File Section.
000110 Fd  Report-File.
000111 01  Report-Record Pic X(80).
000112 Fd  Input-File.
000113 01  Input-Record.
000114     03  Transaction-Date.
000116         05  Trans-Month    Pic 99.
000117         05  Trans-Day      Pic 99.
000118         05  Trans-Year     Pic 9(4).
000119     03  Transaction-Type   Pic  X(4).
000120     03  Transaction-Dept   Pic  X(8).
000121     03  Transaction-Price  Pic S9(7)v99.
000122     03  Transaction-Qty    Pic  9(3).
000123     03  Filler             Pic  X(40).
000157 Working-Storage Section.
000209 01  Heading-Line-1.
000210     03  Filler      Pic X(12) Value "Created by:".
000211     03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "EXAMP".
000212     03  Filler      Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
000213     03  Filler      Pic X(29)
000214         Value "Transaction Detail by Dept.".
000215     03  Filler      Pic X(7) Value Spaces.
000216     03  Filler      Pic X(5)  Value "Page".
000217     03  Page-No     Pic Z(4)9 Value Zeros.
000218 01  Heading-Line-2.
000219     03  Filler      Pic X(12) Value "Created on:".
000220     03  Date-MM     Pic 99.
000221     03  Filler      Pic X     Value "/".
000222     03  Date-DD     Pic 99.
000223     03  Filler      Pic X     Value "/".
000224     03  Date-YY     Pic 99.
000225 01  Heading-Line-3.
000226     03  Filler      Pic X(12) Value "At:".
000227     03  Time-HH     Pic 99.
000228     03  Filler      Pic X     Value ":".
000229     03  Time-MM     Pic 99.
000230     03  Filler      Pic X     Value ":".
000231     03  Time-SS     Pic 99.
000232* Heading Line Must Be Modified.
000235 01  Heading-Line-4.
000236     03  Filler      Pic X(11) Value "Department ".
000238     03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "Date   ".
000239     03  Filler      Pic X(7)  Value "Type ".
000240     03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "Qty".
000241     03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "Amount".
000243 01  Blank-Line      Pic X(80) Value Spaces.
000244* Detail Line Is New.
000253 01  Detail-Line.
000254     03  Detail-Dept         Pic X(4)  Value Spaces.
000255     03  Filler              Pic X(8)  Value Spaces.
000258     03  Detail-Date.
000259         05  Trans-Month     Pic 99.
000260         05  Filler          Pic X Value "/".
000261         05  Trans-Day       Pic 99.
000262         05  Filler          Pic X Value "/".
000263         05  Trans-Year      Pic 9(4).
000264     03  Filler              Pic X     Value Spaces.
000265     03  Detail-Type         Pic X(4)  Value Spaces.
000266     03  Filler              Pic X     Value Spaces.
000267     03  Detail-Qty          Pic Z(4)9.
000268     03  Filler              Pic X     Value Spaces.
000269     03  Detail-Amt          Pic $$$,$$$.99-.
000272 01  Total-Line.
000273     03  Total-Description   Pic X(28)       Value Spaces.
000274     03  Total-Qty           Pic Z(4)9.
000275     03  Filler              Pic X           Value Spaces.
000276     03  Total-Amt           Pic $$$,$$$.99-.
000277     03  Filler              Pic X           Value Spaces.
000278     03  Total-Commission    Pic $$$,$$$.99-.
000279 01  Desc-Type.
000280     03  Filler              Pic X(11) Value "*   Total".
000281     03  Desc-Type-Type      Pic X(4).
000282 01  Desc-Date.
000283     03  Filler              Pic X(11) Value "**  Total".
000284     03  Trans-Month         Pic 99.
000285     03  Filler              Pic X Value "/".
000286     03  Trans-Day           Pic 99.
000287     03  Filler              Pic X Value "/".
000288     03  Trans-Year          Pic 9(4).
000289 01  Desc-Department.
000290     03  Filler              Pic X(11) Value "*** Total".
000291     03  Desc-Dept           Pic X(4).
000292 01  Save-Fields.
000293     03  Save-Dept          Pic X(4)   Value High-Values.
000297     03  Save-Date.
000298         05  Trans-Year     Pic 9(4).
000299         05  Trans-Month    Pic 9(2).
000300         05  Trans-Day      Pic 9(2).
000301     03 Save-Type           Pic X(4)     Value High-Values.
000302 01  Accumulators.
000303     03  Grand-Totals.
000304         05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
000305         05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
000307     03  Dept-Totals.
000308         05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
000309         05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
000311     03  Date-Totals.
000312         05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
000313         05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
000315     03  Type-Totals.
000316         05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
000317         05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
000319 01  Line-Count           Pic 99          Value 99.
000320 01  Page-Count           Pic 9(4)        Value Zeros.
000321 01  Max-Lines            Pic 99          Value 60.
000322 01  Date-And-Time-Area.
000323     03  Work-Date            Pic 9(6).
000324     03  Work-Date-X          Redefines Work-Date.
000325         05  Date-YY          Pic 99.
000326         05  Date-MM          Pic 99.
000327         05  Date-DD          Pic 99.
000328     03  Work-Time            Pic 9(8).
000329     03  Work-Time-X          Redefines Work-Time.
000330         05  Time-HH          Pic 99.
000331         05  Time-MM          Pic 99.
000332         05  Time-SS          Pic 99.
000333         05  Filler           Pic XX.
000335 01  Done-Flag            Pic X Value Spaces.
000336     88  All-Done               Value "Y".
000339 Procedure Division.
000348 Examp-Start.
000399     Open Output Report-File
000400     Move Space To Done-Flag
000401     Perform Fill-Initial-Headings
000402     Perform Process-Records Until All-Done
000403     Close Report-File
000404     Stop Run
000405     .
000406 Process-Records.
000407     Read Input-File
000408            At End
000409               Perform Type-Break
000410               Perform Date-Break
000411               Perform Dept-Break
000412               Perform Print-Grand-Totals
000413               Set All-Done To True
000414            Not At End
000415               Perform Check-For-Break
000416     End-Read
000417     .
000418 Check-For-Break.
000419     Evaluate True
000424        When  Transaction-Dept Not = Save-Dept
000426              Perform Type-Break
000427              Perform Date-Break
000428              Perform Dept-Break
000429        When  Transaction-Date Not = Save-Date
000430              Perform Type-Break
000431              Perform Date-Break
000432        When  Transaction-Type Not = Save-Type
000433              Perform Type-Break
000434        When  Save-Dept = High-Values
000435              Move Transaction-Dept To Save-Dept
000436              Move Transaction-Date To Save-Date
000437              Move Transaction-Type To Save-Type
000439        When  Other
000440              Continue
000441     End-Evaluate
000442     Perform Process-Detail-Record
000443     .
000444 Process-Detail-Record.
000445* New Detail Record Logic
000446     Perform Fill-Write-Detail
000447     Add Transaction-Qty To Total-Qty Of Type-Totals
000449     Compute Total-Amt Of Type-Totals =
000450             Total-Amt Of Type-Totals +
000451             (Transaction-Qty * Transaction-Price)
000452     .
000453 Fill-Write-Detail.
000460     Move Transaction-Dept To Detail-Dept
000483     Move Corresponding Transaction-Date To Detail-Date
000484     Move Transaction-Type To Detail-Type
000485     Move Transaction-Qty  To Detail-Qty
000486     Compute Detail-Amt = Transaction-Qty * Transaction-Price
000488     If Line-Count > Max-Lines
000489        Perform Heading-Routine
000490     End-If
000491     Write Report-Record From Detail-Line After 1
000492     .
000493 Type-Break.
000494     Perform Print-Type-Total
000495     Add Corresponding Type-Totals To Date-Totals
000496     Initialize Type-Totals
000497     Move Transaction-Type To Save-Type
000517     .
000527 Date-Break.
000537     Perform Print-Date-Total
000538     Add Corresponding Date-Totals To Dept-Totals
000539     Initialize Date-Totals
000540     Move Transaction-Date To Save-Date
000542     .
000543 Dept-Break.
000544     Perform Print-Dept-Total
000545     Add Corresponding Dept-Totals To Grand-Totals
000546     Initialize Dept-Totals
000547     Move Transaction-Dept To Save-Dept
000548     .
000549 Print-Type-Total.
000550* Changed This Paragraph To Double Space.
000558     Move Corresponding Type-Totals To Total-Line
000568     Move Save-Type To Desc-Type-Type
000578     Move Desc-Type To Total-Description
000579     If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 2
000580        Perform Heading-Routine
000581     End-If
000582     Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 2
000583     Write Report-Record From Blank-Line After 1
000592     Add 3 To Line-Count
000598     .
000608 Print-Date-Total.
000618     Move Corresponding Date-Totals To Total-Line
000628     Move Corresponding Save-Date   To Desc-Date
000638     Move Desc-Date To Total-Description
000639     If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 1
000640        Perform Heading-Routine
000641     End-If
000648     Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 1
000649     Write Report-Record From Blank-Line After 1
000658     Add 2 To Line-Count
000659     .
000668 Print-Dept-Total.
000678     Move Corresponding Dept-Totals To Total-Line
000715     Move Save-Dept To Desc-Dept
000737     Move Desc-Department To Total-Description
000738     If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 1
000739        Perform Heading-Routine
000740     End-If
000741     Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 1
000742     Write Report-Record From Blank-Line After 1
000743     Add 2 To Line-Count
000748     .
000758 Print-Grand-Totals.
000768     Move Corresponding Grand-Totals To Total-Line
000778     Move "****Grand Totals" To Total-Description
000788     If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 1
000798        Perform Heading-Routine
000808     End-If
000818     Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 2
000828     .
000838 Heading-Routine.
000848     Add 1 To Page-Count
000849     Move Page-Count To Page-No
000858     If Page-Count = 1
000868        Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-1 After Zero
000878     Else
000888        Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-1 After Page
000898     End-If
000908     Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-2 After 1
000918     Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-3 After 1
000919     Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-4 After 2
000920     Write Report-Record From Blank-Line     After 1
000921     Move 6 To Line-Count
000922     .
000932 Fill-Initial-Headings.
000942     Accept Work-Date From Date
000952     Accept Work-Time From Time
000962     Move Corresponding Work-Date-X To
000972                        Heading-Line-2
000982     Move Corresponding Work-Time-X To
000992                        Heading-Line-3
001002     .
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

- **Message-ID:** `<372D72A1.4A88BC10@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7fg2f3$1oe8@enews3.newsguy.com> <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan> <OAJUXPqj#GA.312@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com> <ueeml7uyyx.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <3725BFC4.5205DB2E@zip.com.au> <bLnV2.4020$B55.313802@dfiatx1-snr1> <3728DD58.D5770059@zip.com.au> <3728e366.72458097@news1.ibm.net> <3729AF61.C2CB9CA6@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Uwk5Ls6lqRdb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <372C1EB6.C3F6F683@zip.com.au> <372c7628.216165688@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

Lots of stuff and some code for me to directly answer 'the
challenge',  it compiles but I have not tested it any other way:

       @OPTIONS MAIN,TEST
       Identification Division.
       Program-Id.  Exampk.
      * Control Break Example
       Environment Division.
       Input-Output  Section.
       File-Control.
           Select Input-File Assign To "Input.Dat".
           Select Report-File Assign To Printer.
       Data Division.
       File Section.
       Fd  Report-File.
       01  Report-Record Pic X(80).
       Fd  Input-File.
       01  Input-Record.
           03  Transaction-Date.
               05  Trans-Month    Pic 99.
               05  Trans-Day      Pic 99.
               05  Trans-Year     Pic 9(4).
           03  Transaction-Type   Pic  X(4).
           03  Transaction-Dept   Pic  X(8).
           03  Transaction-Price  Pic S9(7)v99.
           03  Transaction-Qty    Pic  9(3).
           03  Filler             Pic  X(40).
       Working-Storage Section.
       01  Heading-Line-1.
           03  Filler      Pic X(12) Value "Created by:".
           03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "EXAMP".
           03  Filler      Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
           03  Filler      Pic X(29)
               Value "Transaction Detail by Dept.".
           03  Filler      Pic X(7) Value Spaces.
           03  Filler      Pic X(5)  Value "Page".
           03  Page-No     Pic Z(4)9 Value Zeros.
       01  Heading-Line-2.
           03  Filler      Pic X(12) Value "Created on:".
           03  Date-MM     Pic 99.
           03  Filler      Pic X     Value "/".
           03  Date-DD     Pic 99.
           03  Filler      Pic X     Value "/".
           03  Date-YY     Pic 99.
       01  Heading-Line-3.
           03  Filler      Pic X(12) Value "At:".
           03  Time-HH     Pic 99.
           03  Filler      Pic X     Value ":".
           03  Time-MM     Pic 99.
           03  Filler      Pic X     Value ":".
           03  Time-SS     Pic 99.
      * Heading Line Must Be Modified.
       01  Heading-Line-4.
           03  Filler      Pic X(11) Value "Department ".
           03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "Date   ".
           03  Filler      Pic X(7)  Value "Type ".
           03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "Qty".
           03  Filler      Pic X(8)  Value "Amount".
       01  Blank-Line      Pic X(80) Value Spaces.
      * Detail Line Is New.
       01  Detail-Line.
           03  Detail-Dept         Pic X(4)  Value Spaces.
           03  Filler              Pic X(8)  Value Spaces.
           03  Detail-Date.
               05  Trans-Month     Pic 99.
               05  Filler          Pic X Value "/".
               05  Trans-Day       Pic 99.
               05  Filler          Pic X Value "/".
               05  Trans-Year      Pic 9(4).
           03  Filler              Pic X     Value Spaces.
           03  Detail-Type         Pic X(4)  Value Spaces.
           03  Filler              Pic X     Value Spaces.
           03  Detail-Qty          Pic Z(4)9.
           03  Filler              Pic X     Value Spaces.
           03  Detail-Amt          Pic $$$,$$$.99-.
       01  Total-Line.
           03  Total-Description   Pic X(28)       Value Spaces.
           03  Total-Qty           Pic Z(4)9.
           03  Filler              Pic X           Value Spaces.
           03  Total-Amt           Pic $$$,$$$.99-.
           03  Filler              Pic X           Value Spaces.
           03  Total-Commission    Pic $$$,$$$.99-.
       01  Desc-Type.
           03  Filler              Pic X(11) Value "*   Total".
           03  Desc-Type-Type      Pic X(4).
       01  Desc-Date.
           03  Filler              Pic X(11) Value "**  Total".
           03  Trans-Month         Pic 99.
           03  Filler              Pic X Value "/".
           03  Trans-Day           Pic 99.
           03  Filler              Pic X Value "/".
           03  Trans-Year          Pic 9(4).
       01  Desc-Department.
           03  Filler              Pic X(11) Value "*** Total".
           03  Desc-Dept           Pic X(4).
       01  Save-Fields.
           03  Save-Dept          Pic X(4)   Value High-Values.
           03  Save-Date.
               05  Trans-Year     Pic 9(4).
               05  Trans-Month    Pic 9(2).
               05  Trans-Day      Pic 9(2).
           03 Save-Type           Pic X(4)     Value High-Values.
       01  Accumulators.
           03  Grand-Totals.
               05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
               05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
           03  Dept-Totals.
               05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
               05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
           03  Date-Totals.
               05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
               05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
           03  Type-Totals.
               05  Total-Qty        Pic 9(5)         Value Zeros.
               05  Total-Amt        Pic S9(6)v99     Value Zeros.
       01  Line-Count           Pic 99          Value 99.
       01  Page-Count           Pic 9(4)        Value Zeros.
       01  Max-Lines            Pic 99          Value 60.
       01  Date-And-Time-Area.
           03  Work-Date            Pic 9(6).
           03  Work-Date-X          Redefines Work-Date.
               05  Date-YY          Pic 99.
               05  Date-MM          Pic 99.
               05  Date-DD          Pic 99.
           03  Work-Time            Pic 9(8).
           03  Work-Time-X          Redefines Work-Time.
               05  Time-HH          Pic 99.
               05  Time-MM          Pic 99.
               05  Time-SS          Pic 99.
               05  Filler           Pic XX.
       01  Done-Flag            Pic X Value Spaces.
           88  All-Done               Value "Y".
       Procedure Division.
       Examp-Start.
           Open Output Report-File
           Move Space To Done-Flag
           Perform Fill-Initial-Headings
           Read Input-File
                  At End
                     Set All-Done To True
           End-Read

           Perform Process-dept
                Until All-Done

           Perform Print-Grand-Totals
           Close Report-File
           Stop Run
           .
       Process-dept.
           Initialize Dept-Totals
           Move Transaction-Dept To Save-Dept

           perform process-type
               until transaction-dept not = save-dept
                  or all-done

           Perform Print-Dept-Total
           Add Corresponding Dept-Totals To Grand-Totals
           .
       Process-date.
           Initialize Date-Totals
           Move Transaction-Date To Save-Date

           perform process-type
               until transaction-date not = save-date
                  or transaction-dept not = save-dept
                  or all-done

           Perform Print-Date-Total
           Add Corresponding Date-Totals To Dept-Totals
           .
       process-type.
           Initialize Type-Totals
           Move Transaction-Type To Save-Type
           perform process-detail-record
               until transaction-type not = save-type
                  or transaction-date not = save-date
                  or transaction-dept not = save-dept
                  or all-done

           Perform Print-Type-Total
           Add Corresponding Type-Totals To Date-Totals
           .
       Process-Detail-Record.
      * New Detail Record Logic
           Perform Fill-Write-Detail
           Add Transaction-Qty To Total-Qty Of Type-Totals
           Compute Total-Amt Of Type-Totals =
                   Total-Amt Of Type-Totals +
                   (Transaction-Qty * Transaction-Price)
           .
       Fill-Write-Detail.
           Move Transaction-Dept To Detail-Dept
           Move Corresponding Transaction-Date To Detail-Date
           Move Transaction-Type To Detail-Type
           Move Transaction-Qty  To Detail-Qty
           Compute Detail-Amt = Transaction-Qty *
Transaction-Price
           If Line-Count > Max-Lines
              Perform Heading-Routine
           End-If
           Write Report-Record From Detail-Line After 1

           Read Input-File
                  At End
                     Set All-Done To True
           End-Read
           .
       Type-Break.
           .
       Print-Type-Total.
      * Changed This Paragraph To Double Space.
           Move Corresponding Type-Totals To Total-Line
           Move Save-Type To Desc-Type-Type
           Move Desc-Type To Total-Description
           If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 2
              Perform Heading-Routine
           End-If
           Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 2
           Write Report-Record From Blank-Line After 1
           Add 3 To Line-Count
           .
       Print-Date-Total.
           Move Corresponding Date-Totals To Total-Line
           Move Corresponding Save-Date   To Desc-Date
           Move Desc-Date To Total-Description
           If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 1
              Perform Heading-Routine
           End-If
           Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 1
           Write Report-Record From Blank-Line After 1
           Add 2 To Line-Count
           .
       Print-Dept-Total.
           Move Corresponding Dept-Totals To Total-Line
           Move Save-Dept To Desc-Dept
           Move Desc-Department To Total-Description
           If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 1
              Perform Heading-Routine
           End-If
           Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 1
           Write Report-Record From Blank-Line After 1
           Add 2 To Line-Count
           .
       Print-Grand-Totals.
           Move Corresponding Grand-Totals To Total-Line
           Move "****Grand Totals" To Total-Description
           If Line-Count > Max-Lines - 1
              Perform Heading-Routine
           End-If
           Write Report-Record From Total-Line After 2
           .
       Heading-Routine.
           Add 1 To Page-Count
           Move Page-Count To Page-No
           If Page-Count = 1
              Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-1 After Zero
           Else
              Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-1 After Page
           End-If
           Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-2 After 1
           Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-3 After 1
           Write Report-Record From Heading-Line-4 After 2
           Write Report-Record From Blank-Line     After 1
           Move 6 To Line-Count
           .
       Fill-Initial-Headings.
           Accept Work-Date From Date
           Accept Work-Time From Time
           Move Corresponding Work-Date-X To
                              Heading-Line-2
           Move Corresponding Work-Time-X To
                              Heading-Line-3
           .
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Richard Heathfield" <complangc@eton.powernet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<01be953d$05573ea0$0c000001@quenya>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <372BBA4E.7E4B7EE3@mail.mco.bellsouth.net>`

```
[postings rearranged in chrono-order, for easy reading]

Richard Bivans <rbivans@mail.mco.bellsouth.net> wrote in article
<372BBA4E.7E4B7EE3@mail.mco.bellsouth.net>...
> 
> Karl Thomas wrote:
> 
> > You don't consider VB a real programming language?  My favorite
language is
> > of course C, but VB is my favorite environment for quickly getting real
work
> > done (at least until I have time to use C++ Builder).  I live in one of
top
> > 5 cities in the US for IT professionals and experienced VB programmers
with
> > database experience is in high demand.  The major tenets of any project
is
> > getting the work done on-time, within the required budget, and that
meets
> > the requirements. Often VB meets those specs.  It makes no sense to
dismiss
> > any language out-of-hand because of a warped sense of techno-elitism. I
use
> > to think like you as an egotistical college student but a few years of
> > industry experience and graduate school has taught me to be more
pragmatic.
> 
> 
> Well stated Mr. Thomas!!  Too often computer professionals get wraped up
in
> their own world and forget about just getting the job done for the
client.

Actually, computer professionals don't get wraped up in their own world as
often as they should.

Visual Basic is a fine piece of software, and I've praised it many times (I
don't know whether I have done so on Usenet, though, as I don't recall VB
coming to my attention before in the newsgroups I read). It is excellent
for rapid prototyping of front ends. That doesn't mean it's /necessarily/
the right solution for production systems.

Why not? Well, for a start it's not portable (I see this is cross-posted to
comp.lang.basic.visual so let me clarify that I mean it's not portable to,
say, Unix or mainframe systems). Ha, I hear you say. Who cares? Well, maybe
you don't care today. And maybe not tomorrow. But after you've left,
perhaps a couple of years after, your client is going to ask himself "how
can I get this to run on my DeathStation 9000?"

It is the job of computer professionals to point out the alternatives. The
customer should be the one deciding the importance of issues such as
portability, not the programmer. But it is the programmer's job to
highlight the issues of which the client may not be aware, should the
client be sufficiently naif to not be aware all by himself.

If a client says "I only want this to take an hour to develop, performance
is not an issue provided it's not stupidly slow, and I will never need it
to run on my DS9000" then fine, by all means do it in VB.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Karl Thomas" <kathomas@nox.nyx.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7gjr2d$4e$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <372BBA4E.7E4B7EE3@mail.mco.bellsouth.net> <01be953d$05573ea0$0c000001@quenya>`

```

Richard Heathfield wrote in message <01be953d$05573ea0$0c000001@quenya>...

[snip]

>
>Actually, computer professionals don't get wraped up in their own world as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the right solution for production systems.

>
>Why not? Well, for a start it's not portable (I see this is cross-posted to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Most of the programs I write in C aren't portable either and it is
impossible for them to be portable and still meet the required specs (I have
to use a lot of DOS specific OS routines/console i/o).  Especially since the
majority of programs written these days are written for GUI's it's
impossible to write portable code.  Admittedly you can write code that is
more easily ported if necessary.  There are always tradeoffs among getting
the project done on-time, within budget, and meeting the specs.  Sure I
could probably do more of my work using plain textfiles instead of
databases, using only the i/o functions in <stdio.h>, but would my
co-workers who are forced to use it want to use it?

>It is the job of computer professionals to point out the alternatives. The
>customer should be the one deciding the importance of issues such as
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

However as long as you make your clients aware that the more portable that
you make your code, the less powerful it will be, and it will cost more.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-6RPUQb2kfhdr@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <372BBA4E.7E4B7EE3@mail.mco.bellsouth.net> <01be953d$05573ea0$0c000001@quenya> <7gjr2d$4e$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 3 May 1999 09:38:50, "Karl Thomas" <kathomas@nox.nyx.net> 
wrote:

> Most of the programs I write in C aren't portable either and it is
> impossible for them to be portable and still meet the required specs (I have
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If the language you use is at a high enough level, and the interface 
is isolated from the actual code, you CAN write portable systems.  For
example, you can create a program using COBOL with Flexus' Sp2 as the 
front end, and host it on the PC, UNIX, VMS, and with just a tiny bit 
of effort, on a Mainframe.  However, once you mix the user interface 
in with the code itself, and when you start making low level interface
calls, all the portability is out the window.  
 
-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<372df426.10503885@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <372BBA4E.7E4B7EE3@mail.mco.bellsouth.net> <01be953d$05573ea0$0c000001@quenya> <7gjr2d$4e$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-6RPUQb2kfhdr@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>If the language you use is at a high enough level, and the interface 
>is isolated from the actual code, you CAN write portable systems.  For
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>calls, all the portability is out the window.  

Thane:

You're absolutely correct about eliminating portability by calling low
level API's.  But too many programmers are more concerned with
learning the lower level functions than getting the job done.

It's great to learn all the low level stuff, but in a business
programming environment, it's even more important to produce a
stabile, working business application in a reasonable period of time.

I know too many programmers who are in love with the technology
instead of being focused on the task at hand.  I always say to use the
right tool for the job.  Sometimes that means writing your own low
level C language routine...sometimes that means using 3rd party tools
to avoid re-inventing the wheel.




> 
>-------------------------
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

code......

: Richard Bivans

: > >languages and HTML).  I wanted to REALLY program something, not just do
: > >what any CIS business major can handle.......

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<37304589.9279D74F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <372BBA4E.7E4B7EE3@mail.mco.bellsouth.net> <7go1ek$a9e$2@news.asu.edu>`

```
> what about getting the job done quickly without doubling the chance your
> clients computer will crash..... I'd wager, at least with Java, that you
> can "punch out" a program just as fast in Java as you can some bloated VB
> code......

I like Java better, but VB has a couple of advantages:

1.  It's better tuned to do Windows specific tasks.
2.  VB is (almost) the macro language used by MicroSoft products such as
Excel, dropping the learning curve for people whose requirements
overlap.

Java works on more platforms which I use, and is better suited for
client-server.  It's also cheaper.  I still think in COBOL, and find it
harder to learn all the words in library based languages.

There's no way I believe the claims made for OO languages that testing
is reduced.  In an enterprise environment, I want to test everything
which uses a component which gets changed.  That's not efficient at all
with widely reused components.  Although I have to admit, in real life
reuse isn't nearly what OO advocates claim.

The product which I would love to play with, but am too cheap to get is
Visual Age Cobol.  I imagine if it were available cheaply for people to
use non-commercially, programmers who try it out would recommend it to
their companies where appropriate.  But, who spends that type of money
to play with it?
```

<a id="t-a356241853"></a>
## Thread: Problems using EXAMINE, TRANSFORM and TALLY — 2 msgs

### Problems using EXAMINE, TRANSFORM and TALLY

- **From:** "@ngelo" <scarnata@tin.it>
- **Date:** 1999-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ghv6s$bqv$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Dear Friends,
I'm using an old version of a Cobol compiler ( RM/COBOL v. 2.1A
for DOS 2.n ) and I'd like to know why I'm having problems with
these instructions. When I write one of these in my programs
the compiler stops me with the message "Verb required" while
Tally is undefined... Is it a problem of the compiler or a problem
about writing the instruction in the right way ? Help me, please !
Can you answer to me using my e-mail address ? Thanks !

Examples of instructions:
EXAMINE NOME TALLYING  ALL "A".
TRANSFORM NOME CHARACTERS FROM "A" TO "E".
DISPLAY TALLY.

@ngelo - scarnata@tin.it
Sito-web: http://space.tin.it/computer/egobs
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems using EXAMINE, TRANSFORM and TALLY

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fy6X2.8035$Dx6.62691@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7ghv6s$bqv$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
All these can be handled with the ANSI 74/85 INSPECT verb:

MOVE ZERO to TALLY-VAR
INSPECT  THE-DATA
    TALLYING TALLY-VAR FOR ALL 'A'.

INSPECT NOME
    REPLACING ALL 'A' WITH 'F'.   <<< Might be "BY" F I get confused

TALLY is a special register in older/other COBOLs;using ANSI 74/85 you must
specify and initialize the dataname where the tally (count) will be stored.

MCM

@ngelo wrote in message <7ghv6s$bqv$1@nslave1.tin.it>...
>Dear Friends,
>I'm using an old version of a Cobol compiler ( RM/COBOL v. 2.1A
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>DISPLAY TALLY.
```

## Thread: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler — 30 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** "Harold Grems" <haroldg@mctcnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net>`

```
We have several internal business applications systems that use an old
version (1984) of the MicroSoft Cobol compiler ('74 I think).  Does anyone
know what company took over this compiler and how to contact them for an up
to date version (i.e.; Y2K capable)?

Harold Grems
Hubbard Feeds Inc
harold@hubbardfeeds.com
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372E61D1.C8EA6F5@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net>`

```
Harold Grems wrote:
> 
> We have several internal business applications systems that use an old
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> harold@hubbardfeeds.com

Well, technically Micro Focus (that is now called Merant)
may be considered a successor but since the MS compiler 
was discountinued aeons ago, I would guess that nobody
provides support now (I don't know for sure).

Your option is to migrate to a now-living compiler.

Micro Focus has a MS compatibility option, which, 
as I heard, almost works, but their compiler will cost you $4K+
and will have a dongle.

Another option is to migrate to Fujitsu Cobol.
Siber Systems sells mf2fsc converter that 
converts MF and Microsoft Cobol programs to 
Fujitsu Cobol. You can download evaluation 
copy at http://www.siber.com/sct/convs/, look under mf2fsc.

MS options is invokend with -lang=ms.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gm33h$63n@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

   <snip>
>
>Micro Focus has a MS compatibility option, which,
>as I heard, almost works, but their compiler will cost you $4K+

>

   www.merant.com
you can find out if this is a restriction in your area.

Harold,

Fujitsu COBOL has been used for several customers migrating from Microsoft
COBOL and also to MicroFocus. You can read about Fujistu COBOL at:
http://www.adtools.com/  Send questions you may have to cobol@adtools.com
or reply back to me using my e-mail address: gkr@adtools.com  Fujitsu COBOL
has converters available to make the conversion painless as possible using
the Dialect Converter.

The guys who have responded saying Microsoft COBOL no longer exists are
correct. They stopped a long time ago.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372e727f.98217760@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net> <2XtX2.635$i43.53998@news20.ispnews.com>`

```
On Mon, 3 May 1999 21:36:49 -0600, "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
wrote:

>The guys who have responded saying Microsoft COBOL no longer exists are
>correct. They stopped a long time ago.

One thing we ALL failed to consider - for a short time, Microsoft DID
HAVE their own COBOL that was NOT MicroFocus rebadged as MicroSoft.
One must ensure that this is not that before one can assume MicroFocus
compatibility.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gm3al$6a7@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net> <2XtX2.635$i43.53998@news20.ispnews.com> <372e727f.98217760@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Both Microsoft and IBM had their own compilers in the EARLY '80s.  Microsoft
then "re-logoed" the Micro Focus compiler as their own.  Then Microsoft used
Micro Focus as their COBOL compiler vendor - and then they got out of
"COBOL" entirely - but kept strategic relationships with several vendors
(including both Micro Focus and Fujitsu - that I know of).

Assuming your Microsoft compiler is anything "after" Version 1, then it is
probably a Micro Focus "re-packaged" product - and they do provide the best
"compatibility".  Whether or not it is the best strategic product for your
site, depends on what you plan on doing with your existing COBOL
applications - and how much you plan on spending on them.
```

Judson,  I am not certain what you are "banking" on - but I do guarantee
that all Microsoft COBOL compilers after 3.0 *were* Micro Focus COBOL
compilers (with varying degrees of "modifications" - done by Micro Focus -
not Microsoft - for the Microsoft environment).  What came up on the
"banner" had NOTHING to do with whose compiler it was.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be9a33$72e87880$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<vLGX2.4343$Nn6.923730@news3.mia> <7gnhof$7of@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7gnobv$apg$1@news.igs.net> <A46ABCCEAA029191.see_info@bottom.line> <7gpbtb$a89$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Both MS Cobol 4.5 and 5.0 were Micro Focus relabels
4.5 was the last MS Cobol to be able to target 16 bit OS/2
5.0 can target DOS and Win 3.x but NOT OS/2.

Both come with conversion utilities for converting from MS Cobol 2.2,
but I don't have specifics off the top of my head.  I always inferred from
that
that MS 2.2 was the last of MS's native Cobol compilers.  Of course,
making INFERENCES about MS products is always dangerous. :-)

I have both installed and can dig out more details if someone
needs the information.  

While there are many criticisms of MS products (some valid, some not)
and 16 bit programming is a backwater by today's standards, I found
one thing about MS language tools of that vintage VERY appealing,
they went out of the way to make mixed language programming well 
documented and doable.  Under Windows the model seems to have moved
to don't do mixed language programming, but keep each language into it's
own DLLs.  Then let the DLLs call each other.  Documentation on actually
using languages together, when by direct linkage or by DLL/dynamic linkage
is harder to find, and often sketchy.

My experience with 360/370 etc. architecture was that IBM did an excellent
job at documenting mixed language issues for OS/360 in the late '60's and
early '70's.  But I've had no experience with that architecture since then.
Does anyone here know, are they still documenting mixed lang. as well as 
they were?
```

Judson:

My reply *was* a bit confusing.  What I should have said is that
Microsoft's first release of the licensed version from Micro Focus was
Microsoft COBOL 3.0.  It was *not* Micro Focus version 3.x, but an
earlier version of Micro Focus COBOL (2.5, I believe).

Microsoft later released upgrade versions, including Microsoft COBOL
4.x and Microsoft COBOL 5.x before turning to the Dark Side.

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 3 May 1999 21:36:49 -0600, "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> compatibility.

Yes, I heard that when Bill Gates was 6 years old, he
wrote a Cobol compiler and sold it to a really big company 
for $20M -- that's how he got venture greenbacks to buy MS DOS. 
Or was it a BASIC compiler?

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LMDX2.4022$Nn6.815883@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net> <2XtX2.635$i43.53998@news20.ispnews.com> <372e727f.98217760@news1.ibm.net> <372E792E.370ABEF1@siber.com>`

```
Vadim Maslov wrote:
>
>Yes, I heard that when Bill Gates was 6 years old, he
>wrote a Cobol compiler and sold it to a really big company
>for $20M -- that's how he got venture greenbacks to buy MS DOS.
>Or was it a BASIC compiler?

Not sure if you have your tongue in your cheek :-), but Bill Gates made
the Big Time by licensing Microsoft BASIC to virtually every microcomputer
(weren't called PCs then) manufacturer in the U.S. in the late 1970's.
Microsoft was a successful company already in 1980, which is why IBM
approached Microsoft for a PC OS after Gary Killdall (author of CP/M)
wouldn't talk to them.  Gates is *not* a COBOL weenie, and probably hates
COBOL (if he thinks about it at all).  I would be very surprised if Gates
has ever written a single line of COBOL. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn5rh$lso$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net>`

```
Harold Grems wrote in message <7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net>...
>We have several internal business applications systems that use an old
>version (1984) of the MicroSoft Cobol compiler ('74 I think).  Does anyone
>know what company took over this compiler and how to contact them for an up
>to date version (i.e.; Y2K capable)?

If this is the same version as I used in recent days, which was MS Cobol
2.2, it is probably nothing you can do but to purchase from another vendor.
The migration path should however be easy.

I can see you have got a lot of information about Fujitsu and Micro Focus
already, I have learned those people which has given you the information to
know as reliable sources. Both Fujitsu and Micro Focus are with no doubt
good alternatives.

I was in charge of the move, when we moved from MS Cobol 2.2 to Acucobol
back in those days. There were a few minor syntax changes that were
necessary, other than that it was a straight process. We processed the
convertion electronically, using a parsing program we made. It parsed 1Mb
source/min, which wasn't that bad those days. I believe we spent
approximately two man weeks to do the port (40Mb+ source).

However, we were very satisfied with our move. If you feel that Acucobol is
an alternative to consider, feel free to ask questions you may have. I
recommend it.

Important note: Nor Fujitsu, MF, Acucobol or any other cobol fixes Y2K
issues that you may have in your own cobol source.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroSoft Cobol Compiler

- **From:** Robert Kovacic <rjk@onaustralia.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372E65CD.8B03B10C@onaustralia.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7gllgf$g9f$1@blizzard.mctcnet.net>`

```
Harold Grems wrote:
> 
> We have several internal business applications systems that use an old
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> harold@hubbardfeeds.com

The MicroFocus (now Merant) Cobol compiler is your best solution. We
have used the original MicroSoft Cobol compiler since the 1980's to
support various clients and have converted a large number of programs
from this to the Merant Cobol without too much trouble. There are a
number of compatibility options (some undocumented) which must be set
correctly in the Merant Cobol compiler. Combine that with professional
technical staff to perform the conversion and you should get many more
productive years from your internal business application systems.

Regards, Robert Kovacic.
```

---

### Re: Where is there Acucobol Work??

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gkcgm$ln7$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7fh1b6$q5n$1@eve.enteract.com> <19990430222803.24140.00000609@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
GaryBRC wrote in message <19990430222803.24140.00000609@ng-fu1.aol.com>...
>Do you have any experience with COBOL-WOW?  What kind of applications have
you

I don't know COBOL-WOW, I hear that others say it is a good product, and I
see no reason to doubt that.

>been involved with?

I have been developing accounting, payroll, invoice, inventory,
time/resource management using cobol.

>Have you done GUI COBOL?

yes, Acucobol GUI. Their version is done by an extension to the Cobol
language.
Like:
    DISPLAY PUSH-BUTTON AT xxyy HANDLE IN pb-handle.
    MODIFY pb-handle ENABLED pd-status.

The taste is different, I like it this way.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: RM COBOL - Win Programming? — 26 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Tony M. Mina wrote in message <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>...
>The transition from character to GUI was very easy.  If you use Cobol
>SP2 everything is Cobol using  CALL statements.  You should be able to
>convert a program to GUI within  3 hours.  Visit their site at

With all respect, I have been developing software for a number of years,
both GUI and character mode, and I know one thing for sure, that kind of
convertion is providing you with a GUI on the terms of a character user
interface with a gui add-on at best. If you want to do a real good GUI,
which really gives the user what a GUI is proposed to give, no matter
whether using Flexus or a native solution like Acucobol you will have to
spend a lot more time.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>Tony M. Mina wrote in message <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>...
>>The transition from character to GUI was very easy.  If you use Cobol
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Cheesle

Hi Cheesle!

As far as the time it takes, I think it probably depends most upon how
the original application was written.  Because everyone writes their
code differently, some programs are far more easily adapted to GUI
environments than others.

In Tony's case, it may very well have taken 3 hours per program to do
the conversion, but I do agree that depending upon the source program
and the complexity of the GUI tool, it could take considerably more
time to do a conversion.

One more question...isn't the only native Windows GUI solution
calling WinAPI directly from the COBOL code?  Unless you are directly
calling WinAPI functions, then you are indeed passing through one or
more additional layers of code.  In order to "go native" you would
have to be making the direct calls to WinAPI.

Perhaps I'm incorrect, but that was my understanding.





Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote in message <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net>...
>As far as the time it takes, I think it probably depends most upon how
>the original application was written.  Because everyone writes their
>code differently, some programs are far more easily adapted to GUI
>environments than others.

The condition of the original application does indeed have impact of the
time needed. It does however not be the only factor causing impact. Consider
where you previously had a menu selection, it is not obvious what you should
replace it with in a gui presentation. There could be a menu, a listbox or
radio buttons, in the latter case it would raise a question of whether to
group the selection or not. What should happen then? There may be following
a number of options which is dependent on the above, in character mode, one
may choose not to display them, using gui one may enable/disable, one may
use paneling, tabs. Should there be bitmaps? How about toolbars?

Moving from character to gui is not just a question about reproducing, it is
a matter of an entirely different interface with it's pros and cons.

I am not saying it is impossible to do it in three hours. My statement is
that you get what you do, if one do a conversion from character to gui in
three hours. It is either a very simple screen or one has made a number of
compromisses.

>In Tony's case, it may very well have taken 3 hours per program to do
>the conversion,

I don't know his application / source, just as he does not know the one he
responded to. Hence, I find his statement to be very optimistic at best.

>but I do agree that depending upon the source program
>and the complexity of the GUI tool, it could take considerably more
>time to do a conversion.

And the complexity of the application.

>One more question...isn't the only native Windows GUI solution
>calling WinAPI directly from the COBOL code?  Unless you are directly
>calling WinAPI functions, then you are indeed passing through one or
>more additional layers of code.  In order to "go native" you would
>have to be making the direct calls to WinAPI.

Well, parts of the WinAPI is written in assembly, does that disqualify a C
programmer? The WinAPI is a wrapper itself. I do however see your point.
When I use the term native in this context, I am considering native when it
is a part of the host language, that means I am independent of an add on
module.

Borland Delphi has it's wrappers of the WinAPI, how would you consider that?

>Perhaps I'm incorrect, but that was my understanding.

As many other subjects, I believe that is depending on the interpretion.
Just like we have seen recently considering the term "side-effects" :-)

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372ee9e6.530477@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>Bob Wolfe wrote in message <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net>...
>>As far as the time it takes, I think it probably depends most upon how
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>use paneling, tabs. Should there be bitmaps? How about toolbars?

I'm not entirely in agreement with your analysis that a menu option
should be replaced with one of many different controls.  I think that
proper GUI design techniques dictate that a radio button which is
designed for the purpose of selection of one of several mutually
exclusive options is a good choice for "screen to screen" navigation
which is typically the domain of a menu option, pushbutton or toolbar
icon.  

Listboxes also fall into the same issue.

While I agree that any of those controls *could* be used for
navigational purposes, that doesn't necessarily make them appropriate
candidates for consideration.

I think that the vast number of unique control types in a GUI
environment tend to confuse programmers when deciding upon a proper
user interface design.  I believe that if consideration is given to
the primary purpose of the control, the direction on how the user
interface should be designed becomes much more clear.

>Moving from character to gui is not just a question about reproducing, it is
>a matter of an entirely different interface with it's pros and cons.

I agree.

>I am not saying it is impossible to do it in three hours. My statement is
>that you get what you do, if one do a conversion from character to gui in
>three hours. It is either a very simple screen or one has made a number of
>compromisses.

GUI doesn't have to mean dancing icons and fancy controls.  Those
things are great if they contribute to the navigation, usability or
marketability of a program, but too often people think that the screen
must dance if it is to be considered "GUI."  This is a false
assumption  One of the primary objectives of good user interface
design is ease of navigation and ease of data entry/inquiry.  Quite
often the designed will "overdesign" the GUI and actually detract from
the usability and productivity of the end user.  There's *nothing*
wrong with a simple GUI screen if it serves the desired purpose well.

>>In Tony's case, it may very well have taken 3 hours per program to do
>>the conversion,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>And the complexity of the application.

Of course.

>>One more question...isn't the only native Windows GUI solution
>>calling WinAPI directly from the COBOL code?  Unless you are directly
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>module.

I don't think that I mentioned anything about C programmers in my
comment.  All of the professional COBOL compilers allow you to make
direct WinAPI function calls directly from the COBOL language without
having to know C.

...but unless you are directly invoking functions from the operating
system, you ARE going through an add-on module...it's called a
runtime.  The WinApi calls are not being invoked directly by your
source program...they are being interpreted by an intermediate
executable or dynamic link library which, in turn, is invoking the
operating system API.

Native is direct.  Anything else is interpretive.

>Borland Delphi has it's wrappers of the WinAPI, how would you consider that?

Not native.  Native, in my humble opinion, is direct use of the low
level routines as provided by the operating system itself.  Borland's
wrappers are a set of intermediate code which is, in turn, invoking
the API.

>>Perhaps I'm incorrect, but that was my understanding.
>
>As many other subjects, I believe that is depending on the interpretion.
>Just like we have seen recently considering the term "side-effects" :-)

You hit the nail on the head when you used the term, "interpretation."
If the code is being invoked directly by the compiled application, it
is native.  Anything else is interpretive.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn85i$mj5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net> <372ee9e6.530477@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote in message <372ee9e6.530477@news.enter.net>...
>I'm not entirely in agreement with your analysis that a menu option

And you have a point, however, what matters is that Mina later on sent a
mail describing that there actually were performed a lot more than 3 hours
work. That is what I wanted him to tell the originator of this thread in the
first place.

>I think that the vast number of unique control types in a GUI
>environment tend to confuse programmers when deciding upon a proper

Generally speaking, a discussion of which gui controls when is not something
I want to attend, basically because it too often enters a level of religion.
Also because it in my opionion is an impossible task, considering the minor
details that can affect (correct or not) ones decision to choose method a
rather then method b. I do agree with you that it is a difficult task to
choose the right methods, and in particular if one come from character user
interfaces.

>GUI doesn't have to mean dancing icons and fancy controls.  Those

Unfortunately, the world is full of software, where programmers has not
taken the above statement into consideration. I which there was a mandatory
course for any programmer, stating that the gui is there to improve
readability and understanding of a dialog, not to show how many fonts,
bitmaps and colors one is able to produce. I definitely agree with you here.

>I don't think that I mentioned anything about C programmers in my

No, you did not, I mentioned it, as I said that parts of the Win API were
written in assembly, hence I was asking you by refering to C, if assembly
then would be the only "language" acceptable as native :-)

Just to make sure, by saying native, I think of native to the actual
language, that it is a built in set of the language and not by calling an
external module. If I said native to Windows you would be right.

Example: In Acucobol you may:

    DISPLAY MESSAGE BOX "Title" "Message" buttons&kinds.

rather than

    CALL "USER32.DLL"
    CALL "MessageDialog" USING bla, bla, bla.
    CANCEL "USER32"

or using Cobol spII by calling something.

But, as I said; that is my personal preference, others may like the other
ways.

>comment.  All of the professional COBOL compilers allow you to make
>direct WinAPI function calls directly from the COBOL language without
>having to know C.

Well, it is my experience, that it is no doubt a benefit to know C to
communicate with the WinAPI as its datatypes and most samples inherit from C
(the latter has improved recent years).

>Native is direct.  Anything else is interpretive.

This is as wrong as it can be, compilers making native executable are not
interpretive! Although the development environment and the language itself
may use extensively a "wrapper" of any kind, this does no mean the
executable does. If there is produced a native executable, this is native in
any sense and definitely not interpreted.

>Not native.  Native, in my humble opinion, is direct use of the low

Well, as we have cleared out, I am speaking of native in terms of the
particular language while you are considering the host OS API. Pretty
different approach, eh? :-)

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373050e8.6112358@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net> <372ee9e6.530477@news.enter.net> <7gn85i$mj5$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:


>or using Cobol spII by calling something.

It's COBOL sp2.

>>Native is direct.  Anything else is interpretive.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>any sense and definitely not interpreted.

A compiler which compiles source code to a machine code is native.  An
executable which reads an intermediate file and executes the
instructions is interpretive.

>>Not native.  Native, in my humble opinion, is direct use of the low
>
>Well, as we have cleared out, I am speaking of native in terms of the
>particular language while you are considering the host OS API. Pretty
>different approach, eh? :-)

Is there an ANSI standard COBOL GUI syntax that I am not aware of?  If
not, then there is no such thing.  And if there were an ANSI standard,
the source would still need to be compiled directly to machine code
and not interpreted in order to be native.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gpkmq$hrc$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net> <372ee9e6.530477@news.enter.net> <7gn85i$mj5$1@news.cerf.net> <373050e8.6112358@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote in message <373050e8.6112358@news.enter.net>...
>It's COBOL sp2.

Sorry about that, I was most certain it was named spII.

>>This is as wrong as it can be, compilers making native executable are not
>>interpretive! Although the development environment and the language itself
>>may use extensively a "wrapper" of any kind, this does no mean the
>>executable does. If there is produced a native executable, this is native
in
>>any sense and definitely not interpreted.
>
>A compiler which compiles source code to a machine code is native.  An
>executable which reads an intermediate file and executes the
>instructions is interpretive.

Different approach, saying the same.

>Is there an ANSI standard COBOL GUI syntax that I am not aware of?

Absolutely not.

>not, then there is no such thing.  And if there were an ANSI standard,
>the source would still need to be compiled directly to machine code
>and not interpreted in order to be native.

Native... My NATIVE language is Norwegian. I do however speak a dialect
named trondersk, which is NATIVE to my region of the country. When I talk of
a syntax extension that is NATIVE to Acucobol, this is of course because it
is supported by Acucobol, it is a legal extension to the Acucobol syntax in
the sense that the compiler accepts the extensions, hence it is NATIVE to
the Acucobol dialect.

I have never claimed that this has anything to do with any ANSI standard.
And you are really out for a walk if you say that anything to be named
NATIVE has to be a part of any ANSI standard.

Cheesle
```

Ken

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2E2C175A38FF09F4.B098B4189805DEA2.A78E7ED824E08F45@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net> <372ee9e6.530477@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
(snip snip)
> 
> GUI doesn't have to mean dancing icons and fancy controls.  Those
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
(snip)

This is absolutely true.  So often the "GUI above all else" mentality
overshadows the function of the application itself.  In many cases, the
newest GUI version of a product is more difficult to use (even after the
initial learning curve) because of the "prettiness factor" i.e., the
overdesign of the GUI.  Who benefits?  No one actually using the
product.  The irony is that overdesigned GUIs are perpetuated because
they contribute to the *marketing* of the product.  The dancing-icon
variety gets more attention and always has a bigger crowd at the trade
shows.  ("Does General Ledger balance?  Who cares!  As long as it looks
good...")

I wonder how many in-house (not-for-sale) applications get extensively
redesigned just to improve the interface?  I would guess that adding a
simpler GUI screen is more the norm, and certainly could be done in a
relatively small amount of time.  Can I justify an all-out GUI redesign
for my Accounts Payable system?  Not a chance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372e6898.46649146@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gl3n1$1r$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
On Mon, 3 May 1999 14:16:10 -0700, "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

I responded to a post I believe several days ago in regards to
converting character to GUI.  Yes, it did took us about 3 hours to
convert one program to GUI.  Just to clarify things.  Before we did
the conversion, we had several meetings, discussions, issues, etc.
that we had to iron out, such as look of the screens, user interface,
buttons, images, etc.  Our character based programs had so many go to
statements.  This is due to the fact that we do an accept of each
field and then validate the field.

When we were ready, we started with one program just to get a feel on
how easy to convert to GUI.  This is our first GUI program.  This of
course was achieved with the tremendous help of our GUI vendor of
Cobol SP2 (Flexus International).  This took us about a week.  Once we
got it running, we met with our team and discuss what we did and came
up with standards that everybody has to follow.  From there we kept on
going and took us about three months to convert the entire financial
system.  A screen shot of this system using Cobol SP2 as our GUI
interface is on our web site  www.mc-sys.com.

If we have not done our homework, the 3 hours is impossible to
achieve.


Tony M. Mina
```
