##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net>`

```
Behind much of this "character to GUI" thread is a change in paradigm (more
for the user than programmer) shift that I don't think has been discussed
much.  It was a "biggy" in the days that IBM introduced their (now dead and
little lamented) "CUA" (Common User Access) methodology.  However, I think
it does still impact such conversions.

Mostly (not universally) Character Mode user interfaces are "action-object"
systems.

Mostly (not universally) GUI user interfaces are "object-action" oriented.

By this, I mean that the question is whether you decide what you are going
to do first or what you are going to do it to.  In the old IBM mainframe
ISPF (or Xedit) paradigms, you always used a "menu" to decide what you
wanted to do (Edit, Browse, Add, Change, etc) first - and then you decided
what object (PDS, member, program, etc) you wanted to do it to.

In "modern" GUI systems, it is most likely that you will find the "thing"
that you want to act upon.  Then you will be presented a (customized) list
of what actions are appropriate for this "thing." (If it is a a  program,
you can run it - while if it is a document you may want to edit it.).

When converting a Character interface to a GUI one - doing your "homework"
(as mentioned in another part of this thread) often concentrates on this
"paradigm" shift.  How best to implement it - and how best to *RE*-train
your users.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372eef66.1938645@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Behind much of this "character to GUI" thread is a change in paradigm (more
>for the user than programmer) shift that I don't think has been discussed
>much.  It was a "biggy" in the days that IBM introduced their (now dead and
>little lamented) "CUA" (Common User Access) methodology.  However, I think
>it does still impact such conversions.

Ahhhh!  The good old days of CUA!  If anyone has not had the
opportunity to review the IBM CUA design manual, it is well to do so.
The best thing about the CUA guidelines was the emphasis on
consistency of control usage.

If you look at most of the applications produced by Microsoft, they
employ this strategy as part of their user interface design.  In any
given generation of Microsoft products, the user interface is very
consistent across the variety of applications produced by the company.
Radio buttons ARE used for specific purposes (mutually exclusive
option selection) and are generally NOT used as a "menuing" control.

Consistency of design techniques is probably one of the MOST important
considerations when attempting to achieve "ease of use" nirvana.

>Mostly (not universally) Character Mode user interfaces are "action-object"
>systems.
>
>Mostly (not universally) GUI user interfaces are "object-action" oriented.

Bill...while I agree that they are frequently "used" this way, there
is nothing to prevent you from designing a user interface to be
action-object versus object-action regardless of CUI versus GUI.

End user navigation, productivity and clarity of display should be the
primary considerations when designing a good program user
interface....regardless of whether it is done on a 3270 screen of
Windows 98.

>By this, I mean that the question is whether you decide what you are going
>to do first or what you are going to do it to.  In the old IBM mainframe
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>your users.

But if the end users are highly productive in an "action-object"
character mode environment and the GUI is designed to be
"object-action" oriented, the new system may or may not contribute to
the goals and objectives of the corporation as a whole.

Consider the checkbox versus 1 byte entry field.  Checkboxes look very
"cool" in a GUI environment, but in a head-down data entry system, it
may be more production to just allow the end users to continue typing
a single character in the entry field instead of using a checkbox.

Although the checkbox may be set using the spacebar, some users may
have the tendency to remove their hands from the keyboard and grab the
mouse instead of simply using the tab key and the spacebar on the
checkbox field.

If this results in more time required to enter data, the GUI actually
detracts from the corporate objectives instead of contributing to the
corporate objectives.  GUI is great until the programmer designing the
system places personal preference over end user behavior.

Every company should have a set of GUI design guidelines which are
*carefully* and *methodically* prepared...WITH end user input.  Once
this document is completed, the programming task is actually
simplified, because design considerations are already determined prior
to the programming effort.  The programmer can spend time on improving
the business logic and less time on choosing radio buttons versus menu
bars.

Design guidelines are just as important for companies who resell
software as for the largest corporations in the world, because they
promote a high level of user interface design consistency which
ultimately contrin=butes to the productivity of the end user.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Tue, 4 May 1999 00:11:37, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Behind much of this "character to GUI" thread is a change in paradigm (more
> for the user than programmer) shift that I don't think has been discussed
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Lookout - many random thoughts coming.......

This really does hit the nail on the head.  This is the Event Driven 
vs Procedural model.  It's not really a language issue.  GUI 
applications tend to let the user drive the application rather than 
the application guiding the user.  I'm not sure this is a "Must".  

I was the primary analyst working on a text mode POS system.  When 
word came down that it must be GUI there were several conditions.

1.  Use as much existing code as possible.
2.  Output data sets must be compatible - internally I could do 
whatever I wanted.
3.  Must use Dialog System.
4.  Must be able to function easily WITHOUT the mouse.  Mice at the 
POS terminal are a nightmare.

I started with a relativly small application that we have and 
converted it to GUI using Dialog.  This applicaiton was basically an 
Add/Change/Delete.  I rapidly became frustrated with Dialog and the 
methods required for development.  The shortcomings of the tool aside,
it (like PowerCOBOL) employs an event driven model.  Basically you can
think of it as the user interface calling your program rather than 
your program presenting the user interface.  This is a SERIOUS shift 
in programming style - and probably the PRIMARY reason OO is so 
attractive for this kind of development; it means linking a group of 
componants together to create a whole.  

In a nutshell what I learned is that the original field by field 
accept/display code was unusable.  The project rapidly became a 
rewrite.  Procedural - to Event Driven.  This lead to a whole new set 
of problems.  I solved these one by one, then went back over the 
entire project and came up with some "standards" for performing the 
different required tasks.  I also settled on using a Dialog tool 
called DSRUNNER .  This manages your application and the multiple 
windows/programs that can be active.  This allowed a 1 program per 
window approach that simplified things (but by no means made this 
simple).

The above took about 2 months of solid work.

Then it's on to the POS, a project exponentially more complex than the
simple add/change/delete system I had "converted".  The POS was a 
total rewrite.

--------
At the same time as I was doing this, I was doing work on my own.  I 
had several COBOL systems for small businesses and individuals that I 
wanted to take to GUI.  One thing I did know was that I didn't want to
use Dialog system to do it.  I stumbled on COBOL sp2.  Unlike Dialog 
system it does not use a scripting language to drive the GUI and it 
allows a more procedural development style - while still maintaining 
the apparent event driven model for the user.  I've said plenty about 
sp2 in the past, and if you want to learn more search my posts on 
Dejanews for sp2 and/or Dialog.  I tried to convince my management to 
abandon Dialog and use this tool, but corporate Myopia and buying into
the MicroFocus only idea killed it.  I have converted more programs to
GUI part time than the current staff of 6 programmers using Dialog 
have converted in 2 years.  --------

The bottom line, is that there is no real conversion to GUI using the 
event driven tools - you must write new.  

Cheesle has commented on using ACUCOBOL's tools to rapidly convert to 
GUI, and Tony and I have commented on sp2 - Both of these approaches 
lead to rapid GUI development and neither uses the "event driven" 
programming model.

When the user runs the application, they can't tell the difference.  
However, development time is reduced considerably when CONVERTING an 
application from text to GUI when one does NOT use one of the event 
driven programming tools.

-----
New development:  I can also say that New development, at least for 
me, proceeds faster using sp2 than it does using any of the event 
driven, componant oriented tools.  Additionally, the resulting systems
are much easier to maintain.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn8ql$mpa$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>Cheesle has commented on using ACUCOBOL's tools to rapidly convert to
>GUI, and Tony and I have commented on sp2 - Both of these approaches
>lead to rapid GUI development and neither uses the "event driven"
>programming model.

I have to reply on these two items.

I have never said that the "syntax" of Acucobol allows for rapid convertion
from character to gui, if I expressed myself that this could be understood,
I hereby apologize for the misunderstanding. It is my understanding that no
matter what language or tool one use, a convertion is a difficult task that
varies in complexity from program to program, and as Tony Mina stated very
well; the technical part of it, is just a small detail in the whole
perspective. It is impossible to state that a convertion is simple or
complicated in forehand. My statement was that I preferred the approach of
Acucobol, that the gui syntax is a "native" part of the Acucobol dialect.

In terms of events, Acucobol has an event model, which developers may choose
to use, or they may continue procedural programming, this is a choice the
user has to do. I for one has used both. Although the procedural approach
isn't "correct" in the sense of gui, I feel it is a more efficient approach
for many tasks.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B29258F132F7311E.DF0603E736CD254C.F481CC93DCEC581D@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the application guiding the user.  I'm not sure this is a "Must".

Sometimes it is a "must NOT".  Event-driven doesn't always follow the
real world.  In your POS system, for example, you cannot obtain credit
card approval, or give change, before the items are totaled.  No amount
of event-driven logic can change that.

> 
> I was the primary analyst working on a text mode POS system.  When
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> POS terminal are a nightmare.

We are also rebuilding our POS system, with all the same restrictions
except for #3.  However, since one of our goals was to avoid buying all
new hardware for our stores, we bypassed the GUI temptation entirely and
will continue with the text-based interface.  GUI serves no purpose on a
typical POS system other than for marketing it to those who won't
actually be using it.  

> 
> I started with a relativly small application that we have and
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> The above took about 2 months of solid work.

And when all is said and done, you had a basic add/change/delete
program.  :)

> 
> Then it's on to the POS, a project exponentially more complex than the
> simple add/change/delete system I had "converted".  The POS was a
> total rewrite.

Interestingly, we use sp2 for our text screen handling.  It's a great
product, but we front-end sp2 with another product called IDW.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gnnm1$afe$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <B29258F132F7311E.DF0603E736CD254C.F481CC93DCEC581D@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
David W. Furin wrote in message ...

>Sometimes it is a "must NOT".  Event-driven doesn't always follow the
>real world.  In your POS system, for example, you cannot obtain credit
>card approval, or give change, before the items are totaled.  No amount
>of event-driven logic can change that.


In fact, all code is event driven.  Procedural code is the simplest
case, where there is a one to one event/procedure correspondence.  The
"event"
that triggers the next procedure is the completion of the preceding one.

People have been using event tables to drive code for thirty years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<09EB0DB4BDD31684.3C4A54AF7009F264.A5813129068B100B@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <B29258F132F7311E.DF0603E736CD254C.F481CC93DCEC581D@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7gnnm1$afe$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> David W. Furin wrote in message ...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> People have been using event tables to drive code for thirty years.

You are right.  I was referring to the all-options-are-available-at-once
model that is common among many GUI applications.  In my example of a
POS system, it would be akin to having a tab to jump into tendering the
sale before any line items had been entered.  Procedural code is the
right model for that application, it just doesn't make sense otherwise.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37306AF1.7B69247C@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <B29258F132F7311E.DF0603E736CD254C.F481CC93DCEC581D@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7gnnm1$afe$1@news.igs.net> <09EB0DB4BDD31684.3C4A54AF7009F264.A5813129068B100B@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
"David W. Furin" wrote:

> Donald Tees wrote:
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Dunno, but this reminds me ever more of my first programming job almost 20
years ago. NCR Tranpro with the 8270 Imos OS. They had some kind of
semi-smart(?) CRTs, that accepted a screenfull of data according to a mask,
and when the user hit SEND, transmited it all to a COBOL program. The program
had to validate all the data items, and if ok, proceed with whatever. If not,
it could return to the CRT with some kind of message (inserted into the mask),
and even put the cursor in the offending field.
I can't really see much difference in logic between those old programas, and
the newer event-driven (read: mouse o tab-driven) paradigm. You could force
some kind of procedural code using sequences of masks (screens), just as you
would use separate forms in a Windows-based application. Also, isn't this how
the COBOL Screen Section works? (I never used the screen section myself...).

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-HmVJHBDBduj1@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <B29258F132F7311E.DF0603E736CD254C.F481CC93DCEC581D@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7gnnm1$afe$1@news.igs.net> <09EB0DB4BDD31684.3C4A54AF7009F264.A5813129068B100B@library-proxy.airnews.net> <37306AF1.7B69247C@conexion.com.py>`

```
On Wed, 5 May 1999 15:59:45, Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py> 
wrote:

> 
> "David W. Furin" wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the COBOL Screen Section works? (I never used the screen section myself...).
One small but significant difference - 

Screen sections and CICS screens and sp2 "Windows" are displayed, the 
user interacts and the contents of the whole screen are returned.

The even driven stuff I am talking about, such as PowerCOBOL and 
Microfocus Dialog have an additional layer - they have scripts that 
"run" against every OBJECT on the screen (every field).  This is 
outside, instead of or in addition to the return of data to your 
program.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

>The even driven stuff I am talking about, such as PowerCOBOL and
>Microfocus Dialog have an additional layer - they have scripts that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In effect, though, each "object" is just another screen.  The only real
difference is that you can accept several at once.  After the accept,
the system tells you which one was used.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373374FC.F7A2173C@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk> <19990430215636.24140.00000600@ng-fu1.aol.com> <372b459e.2671086@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca> <7gkc6v$lij$1@news.cerf.net> <372dfee7.13257515@news.enter.net> <7gle0l$c5b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d5VWxX0nidg8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <B29258F132F7311E.DF0603E736CD254C.F481CC93DCEC581D@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7gnnm1$afe$1@news.igs.net> <09EB0DB4BDD31684.3C4A54AF7009F264.A5813129068B100B@library-proxy.airnews.net> <37306AF1.7B69247C@conexion.com.py> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-HmVJHBDBduj1@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> The even driven stuff I am talking about, such as PowerCOBOL and
> Microfocus Dialog have an additional layer - they have scripts that
> "run" against every OBJECT on the screen (every field).  This is
> outside, instead of or in addition to the return of data to your
> program.

I'm aware of that. What I meant is that with event-driven user interfases, the only
efficient (clean?) way to validate the user input is to do a global check after the
user hits the ACCEPT button, or whatever you call it. That reminded me of the
old-fashioned transaction processing. With procedural programming, you can check
each field as the user enters is, because you don't allow them to jump around.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

One big PS to my long message.

When one uses the Event Driven design tools for development, one falls
into the VERY LARGE TRAP of the User Interface dictating the design, 
rather than the design dictating the user interface.
```

## Thread: Seeking advice re: COBOL — 5 msgs

### Seeking advice re: COBOL

- **From:** magearwhig@aol.com (MagEarwhig)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990503222043.03941.00002079@ng137.aol.com>`

```
I am thinking about going into the computer field.  I already have a bachelors
degree(psychology).  I have the chance to enroll in a 30 week intensive program
that offers the following.  I want to get opinions on job opportunities for
someone with the following skills.  Thanks in advance.

How to effectively design solutions to business-related problems 
How to write structured COBOL programs 
How to effectively implement and test programs 
How to effectively design, code, and implement a large-scale online system as a
member of a project team 
How to write OS/MVS/JCL 
How to write CICS instructions within COBOL programs 
How to use relational database concepts with DB2 and SQL
```

#### ↳ Re: Seeking advice re: COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gm3k7$6f1@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990503222043.03941.00002079@ng137.aol.com>`

```
It sounds like this course prepares you for an IBM mainframe environment.  I
am a little worried by the reference to "OS/MVS/JCL".  This sounds like an
"out-of-date" course.  To find out, ask the vendor/provider:

1) Do you offer JCL targeted at:
   OS/390 (good)
   MVS/ESA (mediocre)
   S/370  (they are out of date)

2) Will you learn the linkage-editor (bad) or binder (good)
   (This is sort-of a trick question, as there isn't much difference, but
does show you how knowledgeable the vendor/provider is.)

3) What IBM compiler will you use in your course work:
   COBOL for OS/390 & VM (great)
   COBOL for MVS & VM (OK)
   VS COBOL II (out of date)
   OS/VS COBOL (do NOT waste your money)
```

#### ↳ Re: Seeking advice re: COBOL

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn2ag$eb2$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<19990503222043.03941.00002079@ng137.aol.com>`

```

Thirty weeks sounds . . . interesting.

<a id="t-c796b0df69"></a>
## Thread: Problem with update program. — 3 this month / 10 total

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-c796b0df69)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with update program.

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7glbp5$bmb$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<3728dc5c.7839242@news.rmi.net> <3729cbe5.4583896@news.rmi.net>`

```
John,

Create two test files, both with a very small number of records, just
enough to go through each situation once, then try it again., pointing
at these two test files.

You mentioned that you were getting File Status 23s, however you did
not mention which paragraph was getting them (unless they were all
getting the same error, which would send me to look at my source files
and compare the key fields in both to see if any of the numbers ever
match).

I also notice that there is variable error checking.  What do I mean?
Well, this program assumes that every transaction is valid and
correct.  Even though the transaction file may have been edited for
simple data validity before now, this does not mean that keys in the
transaction file are actually valid keys.
For instance:
  If the tran file says ADD, and there is already a record with that
tran key in the master file, there will be an error.
  If the tran file says CHG, and there is no record with that tran key
in the master file, there will be an error.
  If the tran file says DEL, and there is no record with that tran key
in the master file, there will be an error.

In 300-ADD-TRANS-REC, I can't see any code guarding against this.
There is code to detect the error that might occur, but none to stop
it in the first place.

In 301-DEL-TRANS-REC, there is a way to handle the lack of a key in
the master file.

In 0303-CHG-TRAN-REC and the modules it performs, I can't see any code
guarding against this.

Whenever you:
  ADD, you must first READ the master file to determine whether or not
that key already exists in the master file, and if it doesn't, you
would write that trans-rec to a trans-rec-error-file, so that you or
your client can review it and decide what to do with the invalid
record in question.

  Etc. for CHG.


An editing program can determine whether the tran keys are within a
valid range, but not their true validity viz a viz key existence in
the master file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with update program.

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gpg3t$dvj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3728dc5c.7839242@news.rmi.net> <3729cbe5.4583896@news.rmi.net> <7glbp5$bmb$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
In article <7glbp5$bmb$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>,
  "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote:
<snip>

> Whenever you:
>   ADD, you must first READ the master file to determine whether or not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's just extra I/O.  Why not just attempt the WRITE (ADD) and check the
status?  If you get a status code of '22' you know you had a duplicate key
condition and can respond accordingly.

<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with update program.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gpl9r$2lj$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<3728dc5c.7839242@news.rmi.net> <3729cbe5.4583896@news.rmi.net> <7glbp5$bmb$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <7gpg3t$dvj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You're right, that is extra I/O, and I should handle the error
checking after the WRITE.

duplicate key

### Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** bl227@cleveland.Freenet.Edu (Richard Smol)
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

In a previous article, kathomas@nox.nyx.net (Karl Thomas) says:

>You don't consider VB a real programming language?  My favorite language is
>of course C, but VB is my favorite environment for quickly getting real work
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Still it's no real programming language. It's a dirty hack tool that is
being used by non-programmers feinting they actually know what programming
is. I too often have to clean up the mess of those VB-nonos to be too
pleased by VB. It leaves a messy trail of corrupted databases, crashing PCs
and totally horrid (if any) design.

Heck, I am not really blaming the VB 'programmers' in the first place. Even
a good programmer has a hard time to come up with something decent in VB.
It's just no good. Heck, it's BASIC, what do you expect!

I am not saying that Java is the perfect solution, but at least you can
implement proper OO design in it (although I really hate its lack of
multiple inheritance).

Greetz,

RS
```

- **From:** "Karl Thomas" <kathomas@nox.nyx.net>

- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>`

```

Richard Smol wrote in message <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>...
>Still it's no real programming language. It's a dirty hack tool that is
>being used by non-programmers feinting they actually know what programming
>is. I too often have to clean up the mess of those VB-nonos to be too
>pleased by VB. It leaves a messy trail of corrupted databases, crashing PCs
>and totally horrid (if any) design.


Another elitist techno-snob?  I assure you that when I have a project to do
in Visual BASIC that I use the same project management, system analysis, and
concise coding techniques that I would use in any other language.  It's
crazy to blame the development environment for the weakness of the
developers.  I'm sure that everyone has seen C code that has returned
pointers to local variables, overruns memory, doesn't have robust error
checking, etc.  Do you consider C not to be a real programming language
because of sloppy coding.

>
>Heck, I am not really blaming the VB 'programmers' in the first place. Even
>a good programmer has a hard time to come up with something decent in VB.
>It's just no good. Heck, it's BASIC, what do you expect!
>

I don't think its too much to assume that you've never done a real project
VB?

>I am not saying that Java is the perfect solution, but at least you can
>implement proper OO design in it (although I really hate its lack of
>multiple inheritance).


So now a language needs to be object oriented to be a "real" language,  I
guess the C, and Cobol programmers who are reading this aren't using "real"
languages either.

>
>Greetz,
>
>RS
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** bl227@cleveland.Freenet.Edu (Richard Smol)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7gmopn$n3m$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu> <7globr$epb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

In a previous article, kathomas@nox.nyx.net (Karl Thomas) says:

>Another elitist techno-snob?  I assure you that when I have a project to do
>in Visual BASIC that I use the same project management, system analysis, and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have to solve problems with both languages. It's true that improper C can
cause major havoc. Still, you can write decent code in C, even though you
have to watch your step. 

C is more like a loaded gun that you have to handle carefully. VB is more
like a hand grenade with a pin that may drop out any moment ;)  I have seen
some big VB projects which caused major problems, because VB doesn't give
programmers any real insight about what they are doing. You just have forms
that you can extend at will, leading to horrid cases of the Blob
AntiPattern.

>I don't think its too much to assume that you've never done a real project
>VB?

Well, I usually pick up the pieces after a yet another VB project blew up
or left huge amounts of unmaintainable code (the Lava AntiPattern comes to
mind). 

>So now a language needs to be object oriented to be a "real" language,  I
>guess the C, and Cobol programmers who are reading this aren't using "real"
>languages either.

VB floats in a no man's land between OO and procedural. VB would be helped 
very much by a proper OO implementation, if only to support some sort of MVC
(Model-View-Controller). As it is now, GUI logic and application logic are
very much intertwined, leaving to strange design decisions.

Greetz,

RS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Karl Thomas" <kathomas@nox.nyx.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7gnlo8$oq1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu> <7globr$epb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7gmopn$n3m$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>`

```

Richard Smol wrote in message <7gmopn$n3m$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>...

>C is more like a loaded gun that you have to handle carefully. VB is more
>like a hand grenade with a pin that may drop out any moment ;)  I have seen
>some big VB projects which caused major problems, because VB doesn't give
>programmers any real insight about what they are doing.

Why are you blaming VB for that?  If you mean that conceals the details of
GUI programming (the event loop, the Windows API, etc.) then I agree, but
this is a good thing most of the time.  VB does allow people without any
experience in system analysis to put together a program that looks good on
the outside but is horribly designed.  However no environment forces you to
ignore good analysis and coding techniques.  Even with DCL (Vax's command
language), which has no control structures other than GOTO, doesn't force
you to write spaghetti code - if you understand structured programming
techniques.


>You just have forms
>that you can extend at will, leading to horrid cases of the Blob
>AntiPattern.


It's a different method.  Someone who was formally trained in classic system
analysis techniques (like I was) sometimes frowns on the idea of rapid
application development and iterative development (put the screens together,
write just enough functionality for the user to get the idea of how it will
work, use suggestions to improve the interface, and start the process over).
But then you hit the Real World, where specs change and users don't know
exactly what they want.  Again its not the fault of VB that some programmers
don't know about standard debugging and testing techniques.

From what you are complaining about, C++ Builder would lead to the same
problems.

>
>>I don't think its too much to assume that you've never done a real project
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You've never had this problem with C? At least with VB you usually don't
have to worry about a statement in one line causing a side affect that won't
be apparent for 20 or 30 lines down.  For instance I just had an error in a
C program where writing to an invalid array element that had nothing to do
with the file or the file pointer caused fclose() not to work with an errno
of "File name not found".  Yes I'm well aware of the tradeoffs involved in
the lack of bounds checking in C, but because VB won't let you get away with
this or a myriad of other things that C will, it allows for any programmer
with the same skill level to write *more* robust code than with C.  Also VB
handles the intricacies of GUI development which allows you to get the work
done faster.  It's another side of the standard "make vs. buy" decision that
any IT professional has to make.

If the programmers are writing unmaintanable code you need to train/replace
the programmers not the environment.

>>So now a language needs to be object oriented to be a "real" language,  I
>>guess the C, and Cobol programmers who are reading this aren't using
"real"
>>languages either.
>
>VB floats in a no man's land between OO and procedural. VB would be helped
>very much by a proper OO implementation, if only to support some sort of
MVC
>(Model-View-Controller). As it is now, GUI logic and application logic are
>very much intertwined, leaving to strange design decisions.


I agree that VB should have a stronger OO base (at least a proper destructor
mechanism for classes) but how does VB itself lead to strange design
decisions?  Can you give me an example?

>
>Greetz,
>
>RS
```

On Mon, 3 May 1999 23:05:23 -0400, Karl Thomas <kathomas@nox.nyx.net> wrote:
>
>Richard Smol wrote in message <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>because of sloppy coding.

HOwever, C requires a certain level of competence, whereas VB allows impostor
cretins to impersonate programmers. And don't tell me that VB isn't
intrinsically brain-damaged. There is such a thing as brain-damaged tools.
The fact that smart programmers can find painful workarounds to mitigate
the brain-damage does not vindicate the tool.

VB is nothing more than a certain well-known vendor's gimmick to lock users to
their proprietary application platform.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Richard Heathfield" <complangc@eton.powernet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<01be9724$8b87c420$0e01a8c0@eton.powernet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu> <7ffudr$kt@chronicle.concentric.net> <7gam4n$fcp$2@news.asu.edu> <7ge1ep$6oe$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu> <7globr$epb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7gq0tv$nti$4@remarQ.com>`

```
Scratch Monkey <monkey@duke.infowave.net> wrote in article
<7gq0tv$nti$4@remarQ.com>...
> On Mon, 3 May 1999 23:05:23 -0400, Karl Thomas <kathomas@nox.nyx.net>
wrote:
> >
> >Richard Smol wrote in message <7gjuap$p18$1@pale-rider.INS.CWRU.Edu>...
> >>Still it's no real programming language. It's a dirty hack tool that is
> >>being used by non-programmers feinting they actually know what
programming
> >>is. I too often have to clean up the mess of those VB-nonos to be too
> >>pleased by VB. It leaves a messy trail of corrupted databases, crashing
PCs
> >>and totally horrid (if any) design.
> >
> >
> >Another elitist techno-snob?  I assure you that when I have a project to
do
> >in Visual BASIC that I use the same project management, system analysis,
and
> >concise coding techniques that I would use in any other language.  It's
> >crazy to blame the development environment for the weakness of the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> HOwever, C requires a certain level of competence, whereas VB allows
impostor
> cretins to impersonate programmers. And don't tell me that VB isn't
> intrinsically brain-damaged. There is such a thing as brain-damaged
tools.
> The fact that smart programmers can find painful workarounds to mitigate
> the brain-damage does not vindicate the tool.

It is true that C requires a certain level of competence. In fact, I would
argue that it requires a very large measure of that rarest of human
qualities. And the same can be said of VB. It's true that the learning
curve for VB is less aggressive than that of C. But I see that as a good
thing where Windows programming is concerned. VB was a killer tool when it
first appeared, because it suddenly made Windows programming easy. It has
of course been superseded by better tools - a fact which Microsoft is slow
to realise. But in its day, VB rocked.

I don't particularly like VB because it hides too much that I would rather
was not hidden. But I don't see any mileage in slagging it off. It requires
a certain kind of programmer to write good VB programs, and I suspect that
that certain kind of programmer wouldn't necessarily make a good C
programmer. And vice versa. (My own VB programs are dreadful - trust me on
this).

> 
> VB is nothing more than a certain well-known vendor's gimmick to lock
users to
> their proprietary application platform.
> 

I agree with everything in that last sentence, after the words "nothing
more than" have been removed.

<Off-thread>
  Glad to see you're settling down a bit. Still some way to go though, eh?
:-)
</Off-thread>
```

I remember hearing assembly language programmers saying something similar
about all users of high level languages -- in fact I use to be one of them
over 13 years ago.  To paraphrase someone else in this thread, we all think
we're superior to everyone else at some point in our lives, sooner or later
we grow out of it.


> And don't tell me that VB isn't
>intrinsically brain-damaged. There is such a thing as brain-damaged tools.
>The fact that smart programmers can find painful workarounds to mitigate
>the brain-damage does not vindicate the tool.

correct tool for the job?


>
>VB is nothing more than a certain well-known vendor's gimmick to lock users
to
>their proprietary application platform.

And Sun produces Java for purely altruistic motives?

<a id="t-08e712ed13"></a>
## Thread: CBL_FN_INTEGER-OF-DATE error 173 — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-08e712ed13)._

#### ↳ Re: CBL_FN_INTEGER-OF-DATE error 173

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372e2d02@news1.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7gbavj$p70$1@news.iag.net>`

```
I believe that you need to link the program with the intrinsic function with
"COBINTFN.OBJ" - this will include intrinsic function support in the
resultant executable or dynamic link library.

Hope this helps - TL
```

## Thread: Can anyone recomend a Cobol2 / DB2 book?? — 3 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

<a id="t-63eb611714"></a>
## Thread: "length of" operator — 2 msgs

### "length of" operator

- **From:** Alessandro Santini <a.santini@cgi.it>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372DC518.35397627@cgi.it>`

```
Hi everybody,

I got a problem using the 'LENGTH OF' operator, evaluating the size of a
COMP-3 variable. Example - assume the following declaration -

01    NM-VLR            PIC 9(18) COMP-3.

Assume NM-VLR has value zero. The statement MOVE LENGTH OF NM-VLR TO
[variable] returns in [variable] zero. Someone could explain me the
clue?

The compiler used is MicroFocus Cobol Compiler under AIX 4.2.

Thanking in advance, best greetings to everybody.

Alessandro Santini.
```

#### ↳ Re: "length of" operator

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gkhv9$avq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<372DC518.35397627@cgi.it>`

```
How have you defined the receiving variable?  Is it a single digit by any
chance?

 A Pic 9(18) Comp-3 field will take 10 bytes of storage.  (9 for the digits
and 1 extra for the sign - even though it is unsigned - because there are an
even number of digits in the PIC - which is always "wasteful" for COMP-3).
If you move that to a single digit receiving item, it will truncate to zero.
```

<a id="t-870608b8d8"></a>
## Thread: Job — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Job

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gkldh$emk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Beginner Cobol programmer.

Kitchener, Ontario, Canada
This is a trainee position that will involve (basically)
being thrown in over your head on a three month conversion
project.  Lousy pay, good training.  If it works out, then
it will become a full time position with decent pay.
"Working out" means that the applicant not only is good, but
that a major project also comes online.  We are gearing up
on spec, and cannot make promises.

The pay is going to be in the $25,000 per year range, so this
is probably not of interest to most in this echo. I am posting
it for those people that are complaining about no "beginner" jobs
being available can see that there are, and on the off chance
that someone local is reading this NG.  It is an excellent chance
to get some good experience.

Working conditions are excellent, with pleasant office, open
hours, up-to-date equipment and tools, and interesting real-time
PC based projects.

Donald.
```

<a id="t-64b3cfee50"></a>
## Thread: delete — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-64b3cfee50)._

#### ↳ Re: delete

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris.osborne@eds.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gl5mq$7j6$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<19990423102811.09928.00000487@ng144.aol.com>`

```
Dupavoy,

On the DELETE command: Can I see the code?


In addition, you may not MOVE spaces to a numeric display item.
Spaces are non-numeric.  If you want the bytes occupying that field to
have x'40' (or ascii equivalent), then redefine those bytes as PIC
X(4), and move the spaces to the redefined field.  Of course, any math
operation on the original numeric field will fail after this move, but
since you have a reason to move spaces to it, I doubt the code will be
doing math on the numeric field after it has spaces in it.


Sincerely,
      Chris Osborne



Dupavoy <dupavoy@aol.combatSPAM> wrote in message
news:19990423102811.09928.00000487@ng144.aol.com...
> I would like to use the delete command instead
> of doing the following:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I also get an error because bookprice has an PIC 9(4) not an PIC
X(4).
>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

## Thread: Attention: Registered users of Fujitsu COBOL who have current maintenance agreements — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Attention: Registered users of Fujitsu COBOL who have current maintenance agreements

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_fpX2.543$i43.43745@news20.ispnews.com>`

```
Attention: Registered users of Fujitsu COBOL who have current maintenance
agreements

At Fujitsu COBOL, we are committed to providing great customer service. As
with many fast growing companies we sometimes do not live up to our own
standards.

It has come to our attention that a small percentage of Fujitsu COBOL
Version 4.2 update CDs shipped in March and April may have been lost or
misdirected. If you are a registered user of Fujitsu COBOL Version 4.0 and
have a current maintenance agreement, you are entitled to receive a free
copy of the Version 4.2 update.

If you have not yet received your Version 4.2 update CD, please send a note
to cobol@adtools.com and include your name, serial number and a CURRENT
shipping address (no P.O. boxes please).

We sincerely apologize for any confusion or difficulties that may have
arisen from this situation. Please contact us at cobol@adtools.com if you
have any issues you wish to discuss.

Fujitsu Software Corporation
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7gl70p$8a2$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Try using the Copy feature of whatever software you're writing the
program in, and pasting into the text window of whatever software
you're using to post to this newsgroup.

If that doesn't work, you may try to use a scanner to create a file
from a printed version of the program.

If that doesn't work, type it in by hand, just like your request for
help.  It will be annoying, but then people on the newsgroup can start
helping.

<a id="t-03fbad933a"></a>
## Thread: Rant on lack of implementable new Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Rant on lack of implementable new Standard

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372E44C1.6545D7B1@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<372a5f4c@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990430233239.03942.00000710@ng137.aol.com> <7gdu99$iub@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill:

Your statement shows why I fear that COBOL will die.  The functions that
are in the draft standard that are well defined such as the screen
section, true binary, EXIT PERFORM and others have been needed for the
past 10 years.  The fact that the committee can not get increments to
the standard out at least every other year or every third year is a
disgrace to the computing profession.  Most operating environments get
upgraded at least that frequently.  At age 60, I will probably be
retired before these things get into the language.  The reason
non-vendor organizations find it hard to justify belonging to any
standards committee is that there is no payback in terms of improved
function within any reasonable time period.

The first attempt at an addendum took four years so instead of figuring
out how to streamline the process, the committee gave up.  Thus I will
have to wait another 3 to 5 years for all of the functions that I want
NOW and really wanted 10 years ago.  

Unless the venodrs want excuses for not improving COBOL, they should
light a fire under there respective representatives and tell them to get
the uncontroversial parts out NOW or else find another job.  Getting
them out NOW would probably take a year and another 1 - 2 years for the
vendors to implement the functions but that is better than 5 - 6 years
in the future.  

Clark Morris CFM Technical Programming Services
morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca       

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> KeithWalk wrote in message <19990430233239.03942.00000710@ng137.aol.com>...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Klein (wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com)
```

---

<a id="t-de3cf72636"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu & Call Win32API — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-de3cf72636)._

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu & Call Win32API

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EatX2.609$i43.50164@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<7gcjd4$i9r$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Hmm...the question here might be to ask, if he wants the DOS window to
maximize or minimize and not in PowerCOBOL?

The feature to minimize and maximize windows is already capable to coding
in PowerCOBOL for its GUI apps.  It's the DOS window, which tells us
another story.  From what I understand and from what I've read on the
subject, I believe you have to manually make the DOS screen full size
manually and manually minimize or maximize, whatever.

All Fujitsu COBOL products running in DOS are actually running under
Windows on their Windows  system.  Trying to run Fujitsu COBOL in DOS
without windows in memory would not work because Fujitsu COBOL has all its
compiler/runtime files meant to run using Windows. Thus the reasoning, why
not send a call to make this api function possible?  Sorry, but the
function isn't there nor will it ever be.

However I think there is a solution here on this subject. You CAN I believe
create a INF file (I think it's named, I'm probably wrong, but some of you
will know what I mean.).  It's for when running a DOS program and when a
DOS program runs, it looks for the INF?? file and if it's there, it will
look at the settings you want for that DOS window to appear. If you want
full screen, it will do that.  If you want minimized first, it will do
that.  This may be what you're looking for for those of you who want to
control the window size when it first opens.

Hope this helps.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support


Euromercante wrote in message <7gcjd4$i9r$1@duke.telepac.pt>...
>How can call win32api in Fujitsu Cobol?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: "Mastering COBOL" — 1 msgs

### "Mastering COBOL"

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@my-dejanews.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8DBAD40361234ABCD5678EFGH@localhost>`

```
This may be old news - but I have not seen it and a search of 
DejaNews does not lead anywhere.

A version of Fujitsu COBOL v4 is shipped with SYBEX book 
"Mastering COBOL".  The new editor is included - but not all the 
tools that are shipped with v3 shipped with other texts or with 
the CD.  NOTE: the license appears to be for trial use only, 
though it does not time out.  This version is not available for 
production work.

This is based on 24 hour review of the book - anybody have any 
additional information?
```

<a id="t-659867316f"></a>
## Thread: R/M COBOL 85 to COBOL-WOW Conversion — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: R/M COBOL 85 to COBOL-WOW Conversion

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gkbt8$lg3$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<19990501075147.26181.00000893@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
GaryBRC wrote in message <19990501075147.26181.00000893@ng-fs1.aol.com>...
>Does anyone have any experience in converting DOS, character based R/M
COBOL 85
>applications to the COBOL-WOW environment (from England Technical Services
/
>Liant)?  Is there a better solution to get from the character mode to GUI -
we
>do not use screen section or any other screen definition software - it's
all
>DISPLAY and ACCEPT embedded in the source.

This is actually one question in two.

As far as concerns moving your character mode application to run as an
windows application that should not be a problem, at my former employer we
moved from Microsoft Cobol to Acucobol in roughly one week, only a few
syntaxical changes were necessary. The application looked and behaved as
before, as the Acucobol runtime were simulating the character screen for our
application, while the runtime itself were a native windows executable. The
upside of this being that the application could continue to run on any
character mode system be it unix, dos or OS/2 console. The downside was that
there still was no windows features.

The other question then, do you get GUI? No, your interface is still the
same, which makes sense. Although computer software improves every day in
terms of automate tasks, something really is difficult to accomplish. Hence,
making a GUI is still up to you.

I know, however without having tried it myself, that the integrated
development environment which ships with Acucobol, does have an opportunity
to "read" screen layout from old programs, and provide you the lay out as a
template to work on.

I also know that you may have both gui and character screen layouts in your
Acucobol code without much overhead.

In Acucobol GUI is implemented as an extension to the language rather than
depending on external forms, this is a matter of taste, personally I prefer
doing:

    DISPLAY ENTRY-FIELD  MULTILINE AT 1001 HANDLE entry-fld.
    ACCEPT entry-fld UPDATE ON EXCEPTION ...

rather than:

    CALL "SomeForm" USING SHOW-CMD.
    CALL "SomeForm" USING ACCEPT-CMD GIVING return-code.
    EVALUATE return-code
        WHEN ...
   END-EVALUATE.

Cheesle
```

wrote:

- **From:** brian_j_sullivan@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ii5iv$n19$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<L%KX2.3484$S07.26250354@typhoon.stlnet.com> <372faf88.179391292@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In article <372faf88.179391292@news1.ibm.net>,
  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> On Tue, 4 May 1999 18:01:45 -0500, "Robert
Levitt" <rblevitt@mvp.net>
> wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ACUCOBOL - At least in Europe the development
system will run as well
> as the runtime -- but in the US?  You can get a
Linux runtime.
> RM - supposedly soon.
> MicroFocus (some versions) will run under
specific emulations.
>
> Free?  Cheap?  No runtime fees?  None.

Actually, PERCobol has no runtime fees.  It makes
no sense for the intranet, internet and
cross-platform development to have runtime fees.
(See http://www.synkronix.com)  No runtime fees
makes PERCobol a lot cheaper in the long run.

-- Brian Sullivan


--== Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/ ==--
---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

#### ↳ Re: Linux COBOL? - GNU-COBOL!

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@ulm.mch.sni.de>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3732C0DA.A03C036A@ulm.mch.sni.de>`
- **References:** `<L%KX2.3484$S07.26250354@typhoon.stlnet.com>`

```
1999-05-07 12:23:31 MEST

Robert Levitt wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know of a COBOL compiler for Linux?

Believe it or not, there is a GNU-COBOL-Compiler
project about. Here are some URLs:

http://acm.cs.umr.edu/~gnu-cobol/
http://www.gnu.org/software/cobol/

After inspecting the page and the mailing-lists,
I got the impression that the project is in very
early stages of implementation. Furthermore, they
are only doing Cobol85 and not Cobol2000.

I am afraid we still have to wait some time.
Well - maybe it pays to wait.

Herwig Huener
```

#### ↳ Re: unknown error

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DfJX2.1842$JC1.109286@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<19990504162434.10952.00002346@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
Dupavoy wrote in message <19990504162434.10952.00002346@ng-cm1.aol.com>...
>I get this error:
>Run time error number: 013
>eventhough the program compiles and ran fine
>just 2 days ago.  I changed nothing in the data
>or in the program.  What could be causing this?


Good question.  Without knowing what compiler you are using or the operating
system you are running it's hard to tell from here.  Do you know what
statement it was trying to run when it crashed?  Have you run it in debug?
If not have you thrown in a number of temporary DISPLAY statements to let
you know where you are? (display "read at 110", read .........  display
"finished read at 110").  Has someone changed a file you referenced with a
COPY statement?

Without some specifics, it is hard to offer any help.
```

## Thread: Any good books dealing with ISAM??? — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Any good books dealing with ISAM???

- **From:** twozoogs@mailexcite.com (zoogs)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372e43db.32808972@news.prodigy.net>`

```
This current lab requires the creation and maintenence of an indexed
master file and I'm having problems with many of the concepts. The
book assigned to us in class is Welburn's Advanced Structured COBOL,
published pre-85. The presentation of the material is awkward and the
code is all over the place, IMHO, and am looking for a better
reference. Any suggestions greatly appreciated.

Also, is it true that computer programmers have the highest rate of
suicide among all occupations?  Just wondering.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any good books dealing with ISAM???

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372e51f9.89890778@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<372e43db.32808972@news.prodigy.net>`

```
On Tue, 04 May 1999 01:01:20 GMT, twozoogs@mailexcite.com (zoogs)
wrote:

>This current lab requires the creation and maintenence of an indexed
>master file and I'm having problems with many of the concepts. The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Try COBOL Unleashed for a more "Advanced" view of Indexed files.  (I
wrote the chapter on Indexed files, directly following Judsons
chapters on file handling).   Look at it in the book store to see if
it has what you require.  However, I think you really want a more
basic understanding - and for that try my book "Sams Teach Yourself
COBOL in 24 Hours".  I have 3 chapters relating to Indexed files.
Check out http://www.barnesandnoble.com and search for my name or the
title of the book and you can see an outline of what the three
chapters cover.  I think this will give you the leg up that you need
to get a good grasp of the subject.  In COBOL Unleashed I assume an
understanding of Indexed files and touch on using them in a multi-user
environment and I also cover VSAM files and their use at a basic
level.

>Also, is it true that computer programmers have the highest rate of
>suicide among all occupations?  Just wondering.

I dunno about that.  I bet cops have them beat.
```

William Lynch wrote in message <372FB49F.90171D21@att.net>...

>> According to statistics I've found, the suicide rate among police
>> officers is TWICE that of the general public.  Good guess, Thane.
>
>I've read that it's high among psychiatrists, cardiologists & dentists,
>too.


In the light of all the internet talk of the last few weeks, I went to the
library and did some reading on gun death stats for Canada/USA/Britain
last week.  In Canada, the police death rate from gunshot would drop
by 75% by taking guns away from the police.  65% were suicide, 10%
were accidental. The remaining 25% were "criminal" of some sort.

BTW, the deaths from gunshot 1970-1980 in Canada was 115. In the
USA it was 375,000.  There were no figures for Britain.  At the time,
the USA population was 10 times that of Canada.  Those from
governmental stats departments.  The USA figure did not include
Vietnam.  Those did not quite  double it.
```

>William Lynch wrote in message <372FB49F.90171D21@att.net>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Vietnam.  Those did not quite  double it.

You will also note that Jamaica, Mexico, and Washington, D.C. have
SEVERE penalties for owning, selling, or using guns and have
gun-related homicide deaths from ten to sixty times the U.S. average.
OTOH, gun ownership is (almost) universal in Switzerland (where
almost every home has a sub-machine gun) but gun deaths are
close to zero. Point being that guns, or the availability thereof, is not
a determinant or significant factor in homicides, suicides, or
accidents.
```

## Thread: Microsoft Cobol Workbench 3.2.50 & Fujitsu Cobol 4 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microsoft Cobol Workbench 3.2.50 & Fujitsu Cobol 4

- **From:** "Alms" <alms@a1plus.at>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<925822252.69351@proxy.styria.com>`

```
Hi there!

Is there any chance to move from MFC Workbench 3.250 (16Bit) and Dialog
System
to Fujitsu Cobol 4.

The Destination-Environment is Win95/98 and WinNT ... the Application should
run in 32Bit ... not in the lame 16Bit-Cobol who nearly frezzes WinNT & Co.

My special Questions:

 - Can i convert the Dialog System-Screens from MF to Fujitsu ?

 - Does any one know why MFC Runtime takes so much advantage on the
   CPU-Power on Win95/98 and specialy on WinNT??

 - Is there a possibility of changing Dialog System-Screens to Visual Basic
or
   to run it from Visual Basic (Version 5 or 6) ??


Thanx for all your help

:-)
A. Ledineg
```

#### ↳ Re: Microsoft Cobol Workbench 3.2.50 & Fujitsu Cobol 4

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-JodhVcnkeJ67@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<925822252.69351@proxy.styria.com>`

```
On Tue, 4 May 1999 12:44:05, "Alms" <alms@a1plus.at> wrote:

> Hi there!
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Total rewrite.  MF panels and Fujitsu Forms are incompatible.  "Dialog
code" behind objects will have to be converted to COBOL - which is the
scripting language used by Fujitsu.  Also, your "separate" application
program goes away.

This is the DANGER involved with using any vendor specific tools or 
extensions.  It makes it virtually impossible to make an economic move
to another compiler.  If you had used a third party tool, such as 
COBOL sp2 you would be able to change compilers easily - including 
moving from 16 to 32 bit.

>  - Does any one know why MFC Runtime takes so much advantage on the
>    CPU-Power on Win95/98 and specialy on WinNT??
> 

Dunno.

>  - Is there a possibility of changing Dialog System-Screens to Visual Basic
> or
>    to run it from Visual Basic (Version 5 or 6) ??
> 

Same issues as moving to PowerCOBOL


-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```
