<a id="t-f1be416d6c"></a>
## Thread: Tables — 7 msgs

### Tables

- **From:** "Rana S Alexander" <JOSHNYA@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Can someone give me an explanation how to set up a 3 x 2 table without hard
coding it?????  Then how do I reference it for computation.

   Example:
                 High          Low
           20
           30
           40
```

#### ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn01e$blb@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I am not positive what you mean by "not hard coding it".  My guess is that
you are in an introductory COBOL course and need to look at (learn) the
topics related to
   - The OCCURS clause
   - Subscripting and indexing

If these are terms that you really do know already - but that you want to
"avoid" for some reason, then the same functionality (with a lot less
clarity IMHO) can be achieved by "reference modification".  The start
position of reference modification can be an arithmetic expression (as can
the length) - so you could "compute" your way thru the table.  Again, as
anything other than a fairly obscure academic exercise, I wouldn't recommend
this approach - but it CAN be done.
```

#### ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fXDX2.4038$Nn6.821242@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Rana S Alexander wrote:
>Can someone give me an explanation how to set up a 3 x 2 table without hard
>coding it?????  Then how do I reference it for computation.

Rana, below is a post I recently made on the subject.  Perhaps you will find
it helpful.

Think of a table (any item which contains an OCCURS clause) to be several
copies of that item, all with the same name.  Because they all have the
same name, you must have another way to differentiate between them.  This
is done by appending a 'subscript' (or 'index', see below) to references
to the item.  The term 'subscript' is taken from math, where variables of
the same name but different incarnations are indicated by subscripts.

   a    = (a  + (n / a )) / 2
    i+1     i         i

A subscript is always an integer that indicates which one of the items
of the same name are being referenced.  Since computer languages (with
the notable exception of APL) are usually written using plain text,
true subscript fonts are not available, so COBOL uses parenthesis (C
and Pascal use square brackets) to designate the subscript.  You can
think of the table as a row of boxes, each with the same name, and the
subscript designates which of the boxes is being referenced.  In this
example:

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...

   MOVE MY-ITEM(subscript) TO ...

The value of (subscript) determines which of the 20 MY-ITEM elements is
being referenced.  'subscript' can be a numeric literal, a numeric data
item, a numeric data item +/- a numeric literal, or an index, or an
index +/- a numeric literal.  It must always evaluate to an integer
between 1 and the limit in the OCCURS clause (20 in this example).
Indexes are a special type of subscript.  Indexes are associated with,
and must be used only with, a particular table, and are defined by the
INDEXED BY clause on that table:

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...

This creates an index named MY-INDEX associated with the table MY-ITEM.
The specific internal format of indexes is left to the implementor, but
the general idea is that an index can be in a form other than an integer
representing the 'occurrence number' of the entry in the table.  It is
essentially a construct for permitting a form which can be manipulated
more efficiently by the CPU.  The details vary by implementation, and
are not important here.  There are a few differences in the way indexes
are used, as opposed to subscripts.  One is that you must use the SET
verb to change the contents of an index.  Instead of

   MOVE 1 TO MY-INDEX
   ADD 1 TO MY-INDEX
   SUBTRACT 2 FROM MY-INDEX

you would use, respectively

   SET MY-INDEX TO 1
   SET MY-INDEX UP BY 1
   SET MY-INDEX DOWN BY 2

You can also set and change the value of an index by using the VARYING
clause of the PERFORM or SEARCH statements.

   PERFORM ... VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MY-INDEX > 20

If you declare a table with an index you can also use the SEARCH verb
to search the table.

   SET MY-INDEX TO 1.
   SEARCH MY-ITEM
      AT END ...
      WHEN MY-ITEM(MY-INDEX) = ...

If your table is completely populated and the values are in ascending or
descending order, you can use the SEARCH ALL syntax (see ASCENDING and
DESCENDING clauses of OCCURS), which may do a non-linear search (such as
binary search) of the table, resulting in much faster execution.  You
also do not have to set the initial value for the index before executing
SEARCH ALL.

Note that when you reference an individual table element, you must use
a subscript or index to indicate which element you refer to.  But when
you reference the table as a whole, such as in the SEARCH statement,
you do not use a subscript or index.

The power and usefulness of tables and subscripting is best understood
in the context of loops.  It is the use of these two concepts together
which permits writing one set of code which can operate on a number of
different elements.  Consider a program that tested some sort of code
to be valid, and there were 500 different codes.  Without the use of
subscripts, you would probably need to individually code 500 IF tests,
or 500 EVALUATE ... WHEN clauses.  What if there were 5000 different
codes?  What about 50,000?  With a table, you can load all the values
into the table, either through VALUE clauses in Working Storage if the
table is static, or from a data file.  Then just use a short SEARCH
statement or loop to test the codes against the values in the table.

If your table has subordinate entries like this

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...
       05  MY-SUB-ITEM-1 ...
       05  MY-SUB-ITEM-2 ...

Then each of the subordinate items MY-SUB-ITEM-1 and MY-SUB-ITEM-2 are
referenced using subscripts or indexes in exactly the same way as the
item MY-ITEM.  The difference is that you may not reference any of the
subordinate items as the object of the SEARCH verb.  You can reference
subordinate items in the WHEN clauses of a SEARCH.

Two dimensional tables merely carry subscripting one step farther.

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...
       05  MY-ITEM-A
       05  MY-ITEM-B  OCCURS 10 TIMES ...

Each of the 20 copies of MY-ITEM contains one MY-ITEM-A and 10 copies
of MY-ITEM-B.  There are 200 total copies of MY-ITEM-B in the entire
structure.  A reference to MY-ITEM or MY-ITEM-A is resolved by the use
of one subscript.  But if you reference MY-ITEM-B, you must use two
subscripts, the first to designate which of the 20 MY-ITEMS, and a
second to designate which of the MY-ITEM-B entries is intended within
that MY-ITEM.  Both subscripts are enclosed in the same parenthesis,
optionally separated by a comma.

   MY-ITEM-B(14, 6)
or
   MY-ITEM-B(14 6)

This references the 6th MY-ITEM-B, inside the 14th MY-ITEM.  You can
think of this as a grid of boxes, with the first subscript indicating
the row and the second subscript indicating the column.

Additional levels of subscripting merely carry this concept farther.

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...
       05  MY-ITEM-A ...
       05  MY-ITEM-B  OCCURS 10 TIMES ...
           05  MY-ITEM-B1  OCCURS 5 TIMES ...
           05  MY-ITEM-B2 ...

   MY-ITEM-B1(14, 6, 3)

References 3rd MY-ITEM-B1, in 6th MY-ITEM-B, in 14th MY-ITEM.  You can
think of this as a 3D rectangle of boxes, with the first subscript
indicating the row, the second subscript indicating the column, and the
third subscript representing the level.  There are 20 * 10 * 5 = 1000
total copies of MY-ITEM-B1 in the structure, and 20 * 10 = 200 copies
of MY-ITEM-B2.

Two dimensional tables are used occasionally, three dimensional tables
are pretty rare, and you almost never see four or more dimensions.  I
won't even attempt to describe a method for visualizing a table with
more than three dimensions. ;-)  Thane Hubbell describes an interesting
way in chapter 12 of his book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", from
Sams.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gntrp$25g$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <fXDX2.4038$Nn6.821242@news3.mia>`

```

> Two dimensional tables are used occasionally, three dimensional
tables
> are pretty rare, and you almost never see four or more dimensions.
I
> won't even attempt to describe a method for visualizing a table with
> more than three dimensions. ;-)  Thane Hubbell describes an
interesting
> way in chapter 12 of his book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours",
from
> Sams.

I don't know how Thane did it, but I describe multi-dimensional tables
like this.

Imagine a cube or a dice, a small one.  Line up a row of 10 of them,
and you have a 10 element 1 dimension table (in COBOL, each cube can
hold an entire record layout, limited only by memory).  Line up nine
more rows of 10 cubes, and you have a 10,10 two dimensional table.
Stack nine more sets of 10,10 cube sets on top of the first, and you
have a 10,10,10 three dimensional table.  Set nine more such
super-cubes up in a row, and you have a 10,10,10,10 four dimensional
table with 10,000 entries.  This scales up conceptually as far as you
want.  The next step goes to 100,000 entries, then 1,000,000 for the
six dimensional table.  If each cube held just a 100 byte record
layout, that would require 100 megs of RAM, at least.

Why would you want to?  That's a different story . . .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7go9do$ks2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <fXDX2.4038$Nn6.821242@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>Two dimensional tables are used occasionally, three dimensional tables
>are pretty rare, and you almost never see four or more dimensions.  I
>won't even attempt to describe a method for visualizing a table with
>more than three dimensions. ;-)  Thane Hubbell describes an interesting
>way in chapter 12 of his book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", from


A multi-node tree is really a infinitely dimensioned array.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZiXX2.1744$3f2.544463@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <fXDX2.4038$Nn6.821242@news3.mia> <7go9do$ks2$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>A multi-node tree is really a infinitely dimensioned array.

                                                     ^
                                                   sparse  :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn3j3$f6o$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Rana S Alexander <JOSHNYA@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com...
> Can someone give me an explanation how to set up a 3 x 2 table
without hard
> coding it?????  Then how do I reference it for computation.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Rana S Alexander <JOSHNYA@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:7gms7d$50j6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com...
> Can someone give me an explanation how to set up a 3 x 2 table
without hard
> coding it?????  Then how do I reference it for computation.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ditto what William Klein said: What do you mean by "without hard
coding it?????"

Setting up a table involves writing out its definition in working
storage, that could be viewed as a form of hardcoding.

As to how you reference a table, you use either subscripts or indexes,
and there must certainly be code to do that.

Do you mean: Without using literal values for the subscripts?
"Some-Table-Name (1)" versus "Some-Table-Name (Some-Table-Index)"
where Some-Table-Index is a value you control in your program so you
can refer to the portion of the table you need to as the program
progresses through its logic?
```

<a id="t-b9eec9f78b"></a>
## Thread: Cobol en espa���ol — 1 msgs

### Cobol en espa���ol

- **From:** "Andr���s Montes" <cabasc@larural.es>

<a id="t-da4732dda0"></a>
## Thread: Tables(2) — 2 msgs

### Tables(2)

- **From:** "Rana S Alexander" <JOSHNYA@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn1vt$39b0$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Yes I am in an introductory course.  I do know how to access them just I
don't know how to set them up.
This is what I have so far:

       01  EMP-TABLE.
           05 LOCATION-CODE
               OCCURS 3 TIMES                         PIC 9(2).
              10 PERFORMANCE-CODE
               OCCURS 2 TIMES                         PIC 9.
.
   .
     .
       01  PROGRAM-SUBSCRIPTS.
           05 LOCATION-SUB                             PIC 9(4) COMP.
           05 PERFORMANCE-SUB                          PIC 9(4) COMP.

If anyone can give me some help on the synatx I would be very thankful.
                                                                Angie D.S.
```

#### ↳ Re: Tables(2)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4%GX2.4371$Nn6.931765@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7gn1vt$39b0$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Rana S Alexander wrote
>Yes I am in an introductory course.  I do know how to access them
>just I don't know how to set them up.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>               OCCURS 2 TIMES                         PIC 9.

The problem with this has absolutely nothing to do with it being a
table.  The problem is that LOCATION-CODE has a PIC, therefore cannot
also be a group level item.  The proper way to set it up can only be
determined *after* you have defined what the table is to store, and
how it is to be organized.  For example, do you have values which must
be stored under LOCATION-CODE, or all of them to be stored under
PERFORMANCE-CODE?  For example:

This table allows ET-LOCATION-CODE to be stored and referenced by only 1
subscript: ET-LOCATION-CODE(subscript)
but field ET-PERFORMANCE-CODE may only be referenced using 2 subscripts:
ET-PERFORMANCE-CODE(subscript1, subscript2)

       01  EMPLOYEE-TABLE.
           05  ET-LOCATION                OCCURS 3 TIMES.
               07  ET-LOCATION-CODE           PIC 9(02).
               07  ET-PERFORMANCE-CODE        OCCURS 2 TIMES.
                                                  PIC  9(01).

This table does not have ET-LOCATION-CODE, but ET-PERFORMANCE-CODE works
the same way as above.

       01  EMPLOYEE-TABLE.
           05  ET-LOCATION                OCCURS 3 TIMES.
               07  ET-PERFORMANCE-CODE        OCCURS 2 TIMES.
                                                  PIC  9(01).

This table has several fields which may all be referenced using two
subscripts: ET-xxxxx(subscript1, subscript2)

       01  EMPLOYEE-TABLE.
           05  ET-LOCATION                OCCURS 3 TIMES.
               07  ET-PERFORMANCE             OCCURS 2 TIMES.
                   09  ET-xxxxx                   PIC  ...
                   09  ET-xxxxx                   PIC  ...
                   09  ET-xxxxx                   PIC  ...
```

<a id="t-11e54883fc"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus file lock problem — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus file lock problem

- **From:** George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uL58eXkl#GA.346@ntawwabp.compuserve.com>`

```
Hi,

   In my company we've created a mirco focus app. We compiled it 
and put an exe on a central server. All of our workstations are 
running the same exe on the server. 

   What has happened is that, on some workstations, we are 
getting problems with files being locked (error 65) even though 
those files are opened as multi user (i-o). All of the files have 
the "lock is automatic" set.

   This doesn't happen on all workstaions. What we've found is 
that, on the workstations where this DOESN'T happen, the 
microfocus development software has been installed. We believe 
that this is the problem.

   We can't install the microfocus development software on each 
workstation. We've contacted Microfocus and they are not much 
help. Anyone have any suggestions? Are there any dlls we need or 
something?

Thanks,
Neil
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus file lock problem

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <Gael.Wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gn656$p2f@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<uL58eXkl#GA.346@ntawwabp.compuserve.com>`

```
Hi,

As Micro Focus weren't much help, I'll try...No prizes for anybody spotting
the contradiction <g>

Check whether the machines that have the development software installed have
configuration files set up such as EXTFH.CFG, COBOPT.CFG or configuration
files pointed to by the EXTFH or COBCONFIG environment variables. If they
do, they may contain settings which affect the file locking modes in use,
which could explain the locked status you were seeing.

If that doesn't solve the problem I'm sure that if you contact them again,
the Support people in Micro Focus will do their best to help you. As I'm
sure you can appreciate, if it isn't an obvious mistake it may not have an
obvious answer.


George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM> wrote in message ...
>Hi,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Neil
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus file lock problem

- **From:** smayer@t-online.de (Sebastian Mayer)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372F277D.32652CA2@maysoft.de>`
- **References:** `<uL58eXkl#GA.346@ntawwabp.compuserve.com>`

```


George Roldan schrieb:

> Hi,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the "lock is automatic" set.

Hi Neil,
I think your problems is caused by the 2 read of the same record (from a
2 client). If a record is read (in the combination of automatic and i-o)
it is locked until the record is rewritten or the file is explicitley
unlocked.
Hope it helps.
Sebastian Mayer
(http://www.maysoft.de)
```

```
Der Itziar,

We use the product RL&DT product named CobolAccess+.
It works with RMCobol and MFCobol, Informix-SE, Informix-OL, 
Oracle and SQLServer database-tables.
We this tool you can maintain database-tables using your 
Cobol programs as if they were Cobol-files!

for more info; look at www.rldt.fr

grtx,
Anton
```

<a id="t-78f1cedcd7"></a>
## Thread: TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE Conference (TCS'99) Announcement — 1 msgs

### TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE Conference (TCS'99) Announcement

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<uspdi-0405991757020001@1cust145.tnt1.tco2.da.uu.net>`

```

The 16th International Conference on TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS'99)
will be held June 14-18, 1999 in the Washington, D.C. area. The Conference
is on June 16-18, expanded with pre-Conference Tutorials. The theme of the
Conference is "Future Trends in Testing."

The Conference provides an opportunity to participate in the leading
international conference on software testing. The goal of the 1999
Conference will be to explore which testing technologies we will need to
take into the new millennium and which ones we will not. Learn how testing
professionals and experts have used advanced methods and testing
applications to improve the effectiveness of their testing, reduce testing
costs and meet schedule deadlines.

The complete TCS'99 program can be explored on the Web:
           <http://www.uspdi.org/conference/overview.html>

KEYNOTE and FEATURED SPEAKERS:

"What Has Software Testing Research Done For Me Lately, and What Will
    It Do For Me in the Future?" by Elaine J. Weyuker
"Operational Profiles -- Where Testing Is Going" by John D. Musa
"Software Testing in the Next Ten Years: The Ugly Duckling Grows Up"
   by James Bach
"To Automate or Not to Automate -- That Is the Question"
   by Mark Fewster
"The Future of Software Liability" by Cem Kaner
"Conference Reflections -- and a few surprises thrown in!"
   by Boris Beizer

PRE-CONFERENCE TUTORIALS (June 14-15) will present comprehensive technical
sessions for software testing practitioners:

 1. "Test Automation -- Starting Test Automation" by Mark Fewster
 2. "Practical Testing Approaches" by Simon Mills and Louise Tamres
 3. "Fundamentals of Internet/Intranet Application Testing"
     by Marnie Hutcheson
 4. "Java Testing" by Jonathan Beskin
 5. "Millennium Inspections: Advances in Software Engineering Quality
     Control" by Tom Gilb
 6. "Introduction to Software Test Management" by Dorothy Graham
 7. "Test Automation -- Automating Test Execution Successfully"
     by Mark Fewster
 8. "Foundations of Software Testing Techniques" by Boris Beizer
 9. "Survive and Prosper in Your "Risky" Test Environment"
     by Marnie Hutcheson
10. "More Reliable Software Faster with Software Reliability
     Engineering" by John D. Musa
11. "Risk Assessment in Software Projects" by Denis Meredith
12. "Inspection for Software Testers" by Dorothy Graham

Detailed descriptions of the Tutorials can be checked out:
           <http://www.uspdi.org/conference/workshops.html>

CONCURRENT SESSIONS will be divided into four parallel tracks, presenting
a wide variety of topics and issues in major areas of:
Management; Advanced Topics; Automation; Object Oriented; Distributed
Testing; Java; Techniques; Model Based; Requirements; Real-time. The
Conference sessions were designed to meet the needs of software
professionals, ADP managers and their organizations.

For detailed descriptions of the Technical Program, including Keynote and
Featured speakers see:
           <http://www.uspdi.org/conference/program.html>

VENDOR EXHIBIT of products and services will provide valuable support
services and software information to your computer software testing
efforts.

CONFERENCE VOLUME
A comprehensive set of conference proceedings, papers and view graphs will
be provided to each participant of the Conference. These materials are
useful not only during the Conference as learning guides, but also as
valuable references after the Conference.

OBTAINING INFORMATION
You can receive the printed TCS'99 brochure by:
         phone - 301-270-1033
        e-mail - admin@uspdi.org
           web - <http://www.uspdi.org/conference/brochure.html>
  web download - <http://www.uspdi.org/conference/printbrochure.html>

                              * * * * *

To register, or obtain additional information please contact: 

Regular Mail: USPDI, 612 Ethan Allen Avenue, Suite 100; Takoma Park,
              MD 20912-5400, USA.
Phone:        301/270-1033
Fax:          301/270-1040 
E-mail:       admin@uspdi.org (Subject: TCS'99)
Web:          <http://www.uspdi.org/conference>

                              * * * * *
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>people I've met are abnormal....


Usually its the opposite.  Computer Science graduates coming straight out of
school think technology is the be-all and end-all (btw I was CS undergrad),
when it fact the opposite is true.  The business needs are primary, technolo
gy is secondary.  You choose the technology that best meets the needs of the
project.  It's pure idiocy to dismiss the best technology for a given need.
I would never choose to do a project in Visual C++ that could easily meet
the specs and get done in half of the time with half of the maintenance
nightmares using Visual Basic.


>
>
```

<a id="t-a25fc3e875"></a>
## Thread: Visual Object Cobol ( Printing) — 4 msgs

### Visual Object Cobol ( Printing)

- **From:** "Victoria Monta���ez" <vicky@correo.ulima.edu.pe>
- **Date:** 1999-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gnahi$hkr$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
Hi there,

I was wondering how could I make printing reports from Visual Object Cobol,
does it have so like a report designer or do I have to implement the
printing routines? It would be nice if VOCobol could communicate with the
printer device under the windows environment with previews and printer
setup.

Vicky
```

```
	Pentium III chip with the individual serial number that can
track your web surfing and buying habits can now have the ID number
turned on and off by software.  Following some links I found the
www.fightdivx.com website and noticed that they have a Intel Boycott
page with links, quotes and info on why you should boycott the
invasion of privacy Pentium III chips. Just like everyone suspected,
the ID number can be taken without a customers knowledge. Just like
cellular phone fraud, once someone has your unique ID number, they
could pose as you on the internet.  Do not be fooled by reports that
this problem is fixed because Intel disabled this feature by software
on their up coming chips.  Information is power. They want to know
your surfing and buying habits. That is what this is all about. Here
is the link to the page with the boycott info and links. 

http://www.fightdivx.com/intelboycott.htm

Also you will find a Boycott Intel screen saver and banner on their
page above. Spread it around.
```

```
Anybody who really wants to "Pose as you" on the Internet can do it
with or with out this PIII Id Number.  There are a whole host of
Numbers you system transmits out to the web when you are surfing.
Some of these you have control over some you don't.  

FWIW - Any body who can get you PIII Machine to give up the ID to then
and then cause his Machine transmit it (or a simulation of it).  Has
enough knowledge of PCs and the Internet to get all of info he needs
to "pose as you" Even if you don't have a PIII.  

As far as your buying and surfing habits on the web.  That is also
already available to anybody willing to pay for it.  Most ISPs will
sell that data in flash.  Oh, they may say they don't but do you
really believe that.  

These new chips aren't going to present any new issues for your
"On-line privacy" on the web.  This is just easy enough that the media
can some what understand it and cause the public to get all freaked
out with their Misinformation and Misrepresentation of the facts.  

People love to hate companies like, Intel and Microsoft but most of
them don't know why.  Chances are if you are reading this.  You and
using at least one chip (most likely more) made by Intel, and AT LEAST
ONE software package made by Microsoft.  Just take a minute and think
about the ways These two "unthinkable monsters" have affected your
life. ....  (I'll wait)

That's what I thought.  Life is a whole lot nicer with them around.
Isn't it?  Well, I guess we could go back to the Commodore 64s right.

That's my $0.02
S
```

<a id="t-163c7c3b9c"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus Accept Statement — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### MicroFocus Accept Statement

- **From:** "Claire" <CMGovern@nbs.co.za>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gp5v7$l8q$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
Hello

I was wondering if it is possible to right-justify an alphanumeric field so
that when it is accepted using the accept statement, characters fill in from
the right-hand side, as if it was defined as  pic zzzzzzzz9.
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Accept Statement

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <Gael.Wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gpag2$7mf@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7gp5v7$l8q$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
Claire,


Claire wrote in message <7gp5v7$l8q$1@hermes.is.co.za>...
>Hello
>
>I was wondering if it is possible to right-justify an alphanumeric field so
>that when it is accepted using the accept statement, characters fill in
from
>the right-hand side, as if it was defined as  pic zzzzzzzz9.
>

If you are using ANSI standard ACCEPT statements it is not possible to right
justify an alphanumeric. If, however you use the extended ACCEPT/DISPLAY
syntax or the SCREEN SECTION there are various options available to control
how data is input and the JUSTIFIED clause is available. Your language
reference manual will have full details on how to use the extended syntax.
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Accept Statement

- **From:** bhkrause@mmm.com (Bernie Krause)
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3739E094.C8F24F95@mmm.com>`
- **References:** `<7gp5v7$l8q$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
Wouldn't the "justified" clause work?  Accept into a right justified field
should work, or accept into a normal field, then
move to the justified field (following sample may help).

Initializing a Right-Justified Field:

     01  ANJUST                 PIC X(8)  JUSTIFIED RIGHT.
     01  ALPHABETIC-1           PIC A(4)  VALUE "ABCD".
         .
         .
         .
         INITIALIZE ANJUST
             REPLACING ALPHANUMERIC DATA BY ALPHABETIC-1


   +------------------------------------------------------------------------+
   ï¿½ ALPHABETIC-1          ï¿½ ANJUST Before          ï¿½ ANJUST After          ï¿½
   +-----------------------+------------------------+-----------------------ï¿½
   ï¿½ ABCD                          ï¿½ ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½                           ï¿½
ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ABCD              ï¿½
   +------------------------------------------------------------

Claire wrote:

> Hello
>
> I was wondering if it is possible to right-justify an alphanumeric field so
> that when it is accepted using the accept statement, characters fill in from
> the right-hand side, as if it was defined as  pic zzzzzzzz9.



Opinions expressed herein are my own and may not represent those of my employer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Accept Statement

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ei3%2.15014$2Z4.1982@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7gp5v7$l8q$1@hermes.is.co.za> <3739E094.C8F24F95@mmm.com>`

```
If it does work it isn't portable nor ANSI.  ACCEPT is always Left
Justified.
Just Accept it and then MOVE to a JUSTIFIED RIGHT item.

Bernie Krause wrote in message <3739E094.C8F24F95@mmm.com>...
>Wouldn't the "justified" clause work?  Accept into a right justified field
>should work, or accept into a normal field, then
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
>   � ALPHABETIC-1          � ANJUST Before          � ANJUST After
�
>
+-----------------------+------------------------+-----------------------�
>   � ABCD                          � ��������                           �
>����ABCD              �
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I was wondering if it is possible to right-justify an alphanumeric field
so
>> that when it is accepted using the accept statement, characters fill in
from
>> the right-hand side, as if it was defined as  pic zzzzzzzz9.
>
>
>
>Opinions expressed herein are my own and may not represent those of my
employer.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Accept Statement

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373edf58.227032365@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7gp5v7$l8q$1@hermes.is.co.za> <3739E094.C8F24F95@mmm.com> <ei3%2.15014$2Z4.1982@news4.mia>`

```
On Fri, 14 May 1999 20:30:35 -0400, "James King"
<mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>If it does work it isn't portable nor ANSI.  ACCEPT is always Left
>Justified.
>Just Accept it and then MOVE to a JUSTIFIED RIGHT item.
>

Have you tried that?  You will be suprised when it does not work.
Justified Right is VERY misunderstood.  It DOES NOT right justify an
item when the target of a move or an accept.

What it DOES do is facilitate the move or usage of the right most
portion of a source field into a target field.

Ie:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  TESTCX.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DATA-ITEM PIC X(10) VALUE "1234567890".
       01  DATA-TARGET PIC X(5) JUSTIFIED RIGHT.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-OF-PROGRAM.
	   MOVE DATA-ITEM TO DATA-TARGET
	   DISPLAY DATA-TARGET
	   STOP RUN
	   .

The result in Data-Target will be 67890.

If Data-Item contained "12345     " the result would be "     ".

If both fields were 10 characters long, the source and target would
always contain the same values.

Play with the program and see what I mean.
```

---

<a id="t-b605d8c5c6"></a>
## Thread: Cobol programmer Help — 3 msgs

### Cobol programmer Help

- **From:** rfoster@hhs.net (Bob Foster)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<740Y2.466$te5.43384924@dca1-nnrp1.news.digex.net>`

```
I have a business that produces bills is a comman delimited ascii data 
structure.   I have a customer that requires this data submitted 
electronically for thier mainframe.    I do have an Alpha that has Microfocus 
cobol installed  for several purchased applications, but have no inhouse 
programmer do do custom work.

My customer requires a file that would be:

position         length         Type       Description

1-2                  2            numeric        year

3-5                  3           alphanum      right justified event number

6-7                  2          numeric          distribution center

8-12                5         numeric           right justified zero filled to 
                                                        the left if necessary

13-14              2         Alpha               charge type

15-18              4        numeric packed         amount       S9(5)V99 right 
justified 0 filled to the left

19-28              10       Alphanum                 Versions

29-32                 4     Numeric packed        Quantity S9(7) Comp-3 right 
justified 0 filled to the left

33-37                 5      Numeric packed       order number  S9(9) Comp-3 
right justified zero filled to the left

I can produced comma or other delimited ASCII data in a flat file

WOuld like someone to write this code... I will pay for it.

bob Foster,   Keystone Mialing Services Inc.   412-881-5000  
rfoster@keystonemailing.com
```

<a id="t-4bab25da07"></a>
## Thread: Tenders? — 10 msgs

### Tenders?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gqkm7$37u$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Would it be out of place to submit a tender request for Cobol work
in this echo?  I have been thinking that it might be time to try contracting
work out over the net.

If so, what kind of agreements should we make?  Doing business over
the net is somewhat new.  Should we be talking an irrevocable letter of
credit, standard PO?, what currency, etc.

What happens re tax?  <;<)>
```

#### ↳ Re: Tenders?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gsa58$ffq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7gqkm7$37u$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <7gqkm7$37u$1@news.igs.net>...
>Would it be out of place to submit a tender request for Cobol work
>in this echo?  I have been thinking that it might be time to try
contracting
>work out over the net.

It should work, in some sense that is exactly what Linus Thorvalds and his
Linux guys has done...
Interesting idea, I support it.
There will have to be a "central" that's for sure. I can also see a problem
with programming conventions, like sections vs parapgrahps :-)

>If so, what kind of agreements should we make?  Doing business over
>the net is somewhat new.  Should we be talking an irrevocable letter of
>credit, standard PO?, what currency, etc.

Sounds difficult, I for one, would suggest that the "central" I spoke of
administers the pay. Anyone who wants a particular piece of work to be done,
pays all or a part of it as a deposit to the "central", the "central" in
turn distributes tasks, and pay for the work when done. I then consider the
"central" of being the neutral link here. I think it is necessary that there
is no pay until the work is actually done. In order to convict the
programmer that he/she will get paid, it should be a deposit of the whole or
parts of the amount though.

You know, the more I think of this, the more scary it becomes, there raises
so many if's... It will be very dependent of reliability and honesty. I am
afraid that there might be someone out there which won't. Consider another
option, Cobol is targeting businesses at heart. Is it likely that businesses
will let out there source to an anonymous force?

Please do not misunderstand me, I still love the idea, I am just afraid of
all the if's.

>What happens re tax?  <;<)>

I believe most countries has the rule that you are taxable to the country
were the actual work is performed, thus, if I were doing such a job and
living in New Zealand, I would pay tax there. I am however convinced that
this is a minor task, that the individual programmer can handle, they
probably know what to do in their respective country. Keeping the broking
inside US will be beneficial as internet dealing is free of tax there.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tenders?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7guskn$5aq$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7gqkm7$37u$1@news.igs.net> <7gsa58$ffq$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

Cheesle wrote in message <7gsa58$ffq$1@news.cerf.net>...
>It should work, in some sense that is exactly what Linus Thorvalds and his
>Linux guys has done...
>Interesting idea, I support it.
>There will have to be a "central" that's for sure. I can also see a problem
>with programming conventions, like sections vs parapgrahps :-)


I do not see that need.  There are lots of standard banking methods for
handling international credit; the irrevokable letter of credit I mentioned
is
just one.  Specifications would, though, be crucial.  Most users do not
know how to write a decent spec, and even fewer know how to design the
spec with proper sign-off procedures.  That should not be a problem with
pro's subcontracting to other pro's, though no doubt it will be.

The conventions angle is something I also would not worry about. Before
letting a contract, all that should be settled.  Consistancy first, but in
fact
I would accept most of the main styles espoused in here with the exception
of single sections.  Most of us can live with variations in style, and most
of us
can vary our style to fit an existing system.  At least that is true with
the better
coders and with the common styles.

>Sounds difficult, I for one, would suggest that the "central" I spoke of
>administers the pay. Anyone who wants a particular piece of work to be
done,
>pays all or a part of it as a deposit to the "central", the "central" in
>turn distributes tasks, and pay for the work when done. I then consider the
>"central" of being the neutral link here. I think it is necessary that
there
>is no pay until the work is actually done. In order to convict the
>programmer that he/she will get paid, it should be a deposit of the whole
or
>parts of the amount though.


Too much overhead.  If you cannot trust the company that you are dealing
with, then you should not deal.  I would say that the currency should be any
major currency, but probably the one of the company issuing the cheque.
Where it is deposited is up to the programmer.  As a customer, I could
care less if you declare your income.  Credit checks are international, and
a standard bussiness practice.  The irrevocable letter of credit is a
standard
banking document, and could be used for dubious credit.

>You know, the more I think of this, the more scary it becomes, there raises
>so many if's... It will be very dependent of reliability and honesty. I am
>afraid that there might be someone out there which won't. Consider another
>option, Cobol is targeting businesses at heart. Is it likely that
businesses
>will let out there source to an anonymous force?
>
>Please do not misunderstand me, I still love the idea, I am just afraid of
>all the if's.


Security could be a problem, but again, reputation and reputable dealing
are going to be the major factor.  There are lots of people in this echo
that
I would trust with my code.  Any contract programmer is exposed to this
problem;  it is not new.  Funnily enough, the contract I was thinking of is
particularly imune to this ... I was thinking of making it public domain
after
it is done.
>
>>What happens re tax?  <;<)>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>inside US will be beneficial as internet dealing is free of tax there.


That was a bit tongue in cheek.  When you work on-line over the net, and
payment is made from a foreign country, income taxation becomes almost
unenforceable.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tenders?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7guuq7$6m8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7gqkm7$37u$1@news.igs.net> <37311a0f.21848850@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <37311a0f.21848850@news1.ibm.net>...
<snippage>

The project that I am thinking of is rather interesting, and could be made
public domain when done.  It also would be easy to sign-off, so is well
suited
to a "world-wide tender".

About nineteen years back, I wrote a program that allows you to define
screen sections in a data-base.  It is very similar to the MF "panel"
approach,
where each screen is defined as a fixed background and multiple overlays.

The overlays consist of Cobol pictures, each with a from and to dataname,
and 0-4 attributes.  The system allows you to paint the screens, and build
a screen database for each "system".  It then writes all the screen
sections.
Screen I/O is all accept/display using the names from the database. The
database is standard Isam, and the code includes a dump to a nice flat ASCII
file, as well as a reload database from a flat ASCII file.

About seventeen years back, I converted my library to PC's.  I used MF Cobol
running on an Alto's.  My first step was to convert my screen generator, and
use
it to generate all new screen sections.  At the same time, I re-wrote the
system
itself so that it generated all of its own screens.  So the screen system
screen I/O
is written by itself.

I have followed that basic conversion pattern through half a dozen migration
patterns.  The last was to Fujitsu, and I now have hundreds of programs
converted
to Fujitsu code using the Fujitsu screen section (ugly, but they work and
are
completely tested as standard Cobol).

The next step is to re-write "Screen" to produce SP2 code.  Testing and
acceptance
is simple.  It has to re-write itself successfully, and run as a GUI when
done.
Obviously, there will be no new features in the first new version.  All
screens
will be a mimic of an 80*24 character mode screen, and all data-editing will
be according to standard Cobol picture strings.  However that step will give
me
a basic conversion of all existing code, a solid example, and allow me to
proceed by either extending Screen, or starting to convert the existing code
using a more "GUI" oriented tool.

It also has the advantage of no re-training, and I can just change the
illustrations
in the documentation to upgrade the documentation.
```

---

<a id="t-e6191d2a96"></a>
## Thread: "Native" Language support for a feature — 12 msgs

### "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I just thought I would start a new thread - to follow up on a side
discussion in another thread.  (Basically, I disagree with Bob Wolfe - for a
change - but just thought I would raise some issues in this new thread.)

It seems to me that there are a few types of "support" for features that can
be in a programming language.  I will "assume" COBOL here, but much (not
all) applies to other languages as well.

At a minimum, there are (some overlapping with others):

 - Portable (Standard conforming) "native" language support
 - Non-Portable (extensions) "native" language support
 - "Alien" language support (e.g. EXEC SQL or COBOL ENTER statement - not
ENTRY statement)
 - Callable API support (related to "Library" support)
 - Interpreted vs compiled vs object vs machine level support

***

I (personally) do not consider the difference between "interpreted" and
"compiled" code to be relevant to discussions of what a specific language or
compiler supports.  I consider "compiled" and "interpreted" JAVA to be the
same - and I consider "compiled" and "interpreted" Micro Focus COBOL to be
the same.

I (personally) consider that AcuCOBOL's "GUI" extensions to be "native" (but
not portable) COBOL language support.  While CICS' "EXEC CICS SEND/RECEIVE"
to be "alien" language support (the EXEC to END-EXEC statement "escapes"
from the compiler).  While COBOL systems that require "Call WinAPIxxx"
statements are using external APIs.

It is interested where there are also "overlaps" between "native" language
support and some of the others - via "translators" or "pre-compilers".  The
SPC Report-Writer product allows the application programmer to code their
source code as if they were using native COBOL language (ANSI defined)
report-writer syntax - but actually "translates" this into other statements
(but not MOVEs followed by CALL statements - rather standard COBOL I/O).
Similarly, all EXEC SQL and EXEC CICS systems use some form of "translation"
that may or may not look transparent to the programmer (but do end-up being
a MOVE followed by a CALL).

Other fine lines are systems like Micro Focus' C-ISAM support which
"translates" native COBOL file syntax into run-time calls to a 3rd party
product.  (Similar to IBM mainframe applications that use SyncSort or DFSort
to "implement" the native COBOL SORT/MERGE syntax).  On the other hand, IBM
mainframers who use "CALL CBLTDLI" or PC coders who make explicit calls to
BTRIEVE modules are clearly using "API interfaces".

  ***

As the saying goes, all of this is (eventually) translated into on/off (1s
and 0s) - not even HEX much less Assembler.  It seems to me that the
"significant" question is what the application programmer codes.  If we are
talking about COBOL, then the minute that they need to put a "CALL some-api"
or EXEC statement in, then it is not native language support. If they can
access the functionality via a "COBOL verb," then it is native language
support.  If the verb (and all the operands, semantics, and syntax) are as
defined in the ANSI/ISO Standard, then it is "portable native language
support".

   ***

It is hard for me to think of any of this as "controversial" - but maybe it
is.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37317FED.D25C4BB8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I have found it impossible to produce portable code that works on
MVS and PC.  There is a one line difference on the file statement,
minor but a pain none the less.

If I was doing screen IO it would be major problems.  The only
solution is to isolate the interface and make the back end
portable.

What I want is a truly portable screen process that works on big
blue and little chinese (yes I run cheap PC's).  If this was done
by a preprocessor I would not care, if it only handled a fair
subset I would not care.  It just has to be portable.   This is
the restraint on Cobol today, it needs to be addressed.

Ken

Regarding bytecode java as oposed to compiled java, try running a
compiled PC program on a Mac and there is a difference :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00

- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <37317FED.D25C4BB8@zip.com.au>`

```

for a PC application. Who says you have to use the latest GUI?

>blue and little chinese (yes I run cheap PC's).  If this was done
>by a preprocessor I would not care, if it only handled a fair
```

#### ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37319c39.5565579@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I just thought I would start a new thread - to follow up on a side
>discussion in another thread.  (Basically, I disagree with Bob Wolfe - for a
>change - but just thought I would raise some issues in this new thread.)

Bill:

That's ok...it certainly wouldn't be the first time and it certainly
won't be the last time either.

;-)

>It seems to me that there are a few types of "support" for features that can
>be in a programming language.  I will "assume" COBOL here, but much (not
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>statements are using external APIs.

I guess that I was just basing my statements on past discussions with
compiler company people of "native code" compilers versus
"interpretive code" compilers.  My discussions on this topic with
various employees of compiler companies over the years has always
seemed to follow this generalization.

I gladly and humbly defer to your expert judgement on this matter and
stand corrected!

See...you CAN teach an old dog a new trick!  Arf!  Arf!

>It is interested where there are also "overlaps" between "native" language
>support and some of the others - via "translators" or "pre-compilers".  The
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>is.

I didn't think that it was controversial either.  I'm certainly not
religious about the issue.  I think that some of the more important
considerations are:

- Does the program work?
- Is the performance acceptable?
- Is the source code easy to maintain?
- Is it easy to hire experienced programmers who are familiar with the
language/extensions?
- Is the program portable (IF that is important to YOUR organization)?
- Is the source code ANSI standard (IF that is important to YOUR
organization)?
- Please add your own additional citeria which I haven't considered!

Whether the code is a compiled executable or interpreted by a runtime
shouldn't make much difference if all of the above are satisfactory.

NOT "The COBOL Frog"...The COBOL "Dog!"  Arf!  Arf!




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gsua0$hf0@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <37319c39.5565579@news.enter.net>`

```
I wanted to add a few "thoughts" to my original post - which Bob's note
reminded me of (and which may not be clear from my original post).

1) The differences between compiled and interpreted code may WELL impact
"performance."  Obviously, this needs to be considered in producing
production code. On the other hand, the difference between interpreted code
and compiled code  almost certainly will impact WHAT you port - if not where
you can port it to.

2) Even for "interpreted" code, there are differences between systems.  For
example the (I don't know if it is still current) requirement for AcuCOBOL
applications to "rebuild" their run-time system for each application that
calls C subroutines - while this is not true for Micro Focus ".INT" code.

3) There are portable applications and then there are portable systems.  The
use of GoBack or Comp-3 (as packed-decimal) are clearly non-ANSI code.
However, given IBM's dominance in the 60's and 70's in the COBOL-mainframe
worlds, these are *almost* universally supported and portable.  On the other
hand, the use of the PADDING CHARACTER phrase in the FD is ANSI-conforming,
but unlikely to give "portable" results - if you expect it to do anything
useful with it.

4) As discussed in another thread,  it is often (usually?) a good idea to
"isolate" your vendor-specific or operating system specific code in called
sub-routines - if you think your application will be "ported" to other
vendors or environments.  (Alternatively, - for example in the case of the
SELECT/ASSIGN statement - a COPY statement may do the trick.)  ON THE OTHER
HAND, if you are developing a response-time critical application, using
every "low-level" interface and extension - without a CALL statement, may be
the best way to go.

5) In my original note,  I hope it was clear that I was trying to describe
some "classifications" and NOT trying to say one was "good" while another
was "bad".  We have already all (I think) said that learning to use the
right tool for the right task is clearly the way to go.  On the other hand,
when you are still trying to decide which tool to use, it is NICE if you can
use terms that mean the same thing to all involved in the discussion and
decision making process.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gsac8$fk5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
> - Portable (Standard conforming) "native" language support
> - Non-Portable (extensions) "native" language support
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Interpreted vs compiled vs object vs machine level support

>As the saying goes, all of this is (eventually) translated into on/off (1s
>and 0s) - not even HEX much less Assembler.  It seems to me that the
>"significant" question is what the application programmer codes.  If we are
>talking about COBOL, then the minute that they need to put a "CALL
some-api"
>or EXEC statement in, then it is not native language support. If they can
>access the functionality via a "COBOL verb," then it is native language
>support.  If the verb (and all the operands, semantics, and syntax) are as
>defined in the ANSI/ISO Standard, then it is "portable native language
>support".

I support your definition 110%!

Cheesle
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-l8CljpwQ98DP@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Thu, 6 May 1999 00:07:53, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> As the saying goes, all of this is (eventually) translated into on/off (1s
> and 0s) - not even HEX much less Assembler.  It seems to me that the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What would you consider Dialog System to be?  

For example:  Both MicroFocus Dialog and Fujitsu COBOL have you code 
scripts behind each object on a window.  The difference is that 
PowerCOBOL is turned into calls and compiled, where Dialog System is 
interpreted at runtime by the runtime system.  

I am not sure this is not the case also for AcuCOBOL and it's 
extensions to Accept and Display for Windows functions.  
-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gv79i$rg3@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-l8CljpwQ98DP@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
(Talking to myself again - I better watch out <G>)

In the middle of last night, it suddenly dawned on my why Bob had originally
talked about "native" versus "interpreted" support - and that vendors had
used this distinction.

Going back to my Micro Focus days, there is a concept of "NCG" or "Native
Code Generator" which is a "part" (well sort of) of the compiler.  This is
the "thing" that converts ".int" (or interpreted code - or p-code as it is
called in some environments) into "machine" or "object" code - which is
referred to as "native code" - and which is certainly DIFFERENT from
interpreted code - and which differs on each machine platform (operating
system).

When I (we?) talk about "native language" support for a feature, we are
talking about SOURCE code.  When vendors talk about "native code" support at
run-time, they are talking about machine code.

I had never thought of "confusing" these two concepts, but I certainly can
see how it could happen.

Sorry, Bob.  You are right (when talking about THAT type of "native" code)
that it is quite different from "interpreted" code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Native" Language support for a feature

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gvea5$hs1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7gqmhm$ejo@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-l8CljpwQ98DP@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>What would you consider Dialog System to be?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>extensions to Accept and Display for Windows functions.

The extended syntax like push-button is considered an object, but it is
inside the cobol application and any event may not be handled and may be
handled. The user has the option to assign event procedures and/or
exceptions corresponding.

As such one may have multiple screens concurrently and trigger events via a
traditional accept or by a loop. I may also add that Acucobol supports
threads.

The acucobol syntax allows controls to be a part of Screen section entries
as well.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: NetExpress Character Windows — 2 msgs

### NetExpress Character Windows

- **From:** "Euromercante" <EUROMERCANTE@MAIL.TELEPAC.PT>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gp91l$6b3$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
By default, the title of a character-base cobol aplications under Windows is
the root of the executable name. However, i can set the title for our
aplications. How???
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Character Windows

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <Gael.Wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7grho4$pqc@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7gp91l$6b3$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
If you want to set the title at build time, create a resource file
containing a string with id 100, compile it using the resource compiler and
specify the .RES when linking.

Alternatively, if you want to set the title at run-time you will need to
call the SetConsoleTitle API.



Euromercante wrote in message <7gp91l$6b3$1@duke.telepac.pt>...
>By default, the title of a character-base cobol aplications under Windows
is
>the root of the executable name. However, i can set the title for our
>aplications. How???
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
               WRITE O-GOOD-LINE FROM WS-HDR1-2                             
               WRITE O-GOOD-LINE FROM WS-HDR1-3                             
               WRITE O-GOOD-LINE FROM WS-HDR1-4                             
               WRITE O-GOOD-LINE FROM WS-HDR1-5                             
               WRITE O-GOOD-LINE FROM WS-HDR1-6.                            
805-ERROR-REPORT-HEADERS.                                        
               WRITE O-ERROR-LINE FROM WS-HDR2-1 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE       
               WRITE O-ERROR-LINE FROM WS-HDR2-2                            
               WRITE O-ERROR-LINE FROM WS-HDR2-3                            
               WRITE O-ERROR-LINE FROM WS-HDR2-4                            
               WRITE O-ERROR-LINE FROM WS-HDR2-5                            
               WRITE O-ERROR-LINE FROM WS-HDR2-6.                           


Thanks for any help whatsoever.
```

#### ↳ Re: My program does NOT work!! Someone please look and help!

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gpok3$4po$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<19990505005454.06309.00002613@ng-fk1.aol.com>`

```
In 300-TABLE-LOOKUP, it looks like you could use the SEARCH verb
instead of inline PERFORMS to find stuff in the table you are using.

In addition, consistently indenting lines inside of statements like
READ and PERFORM by two to four spaces can improve readability, which
can ease debugging.  No indenting, or indenting only some lines, makes
it harder to read the code.  Also, when END-* explicit scope
terminators aren't lined up with the statement they are terminating,
that also makes the code harder to read.


In the following code block . . .

>            300-TABLE-LOOKUP.
>                MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND-PRODUCT
>                MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE
>                PERFORM VARYING WS-INFO-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
>                    UNTIL WS-INFO-SUB > 10 OR WS-FOUND-PRODUCT =
"YES"
>                IF I-PRODUCT-NO = WS-PRODUCT-NO (WS-CUST-SUB)
>                   MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND-PRODUCT
>                PERFORM VARYING WS-INFO-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
>                      UNTIL WS-INFO-SUB > 10 OR WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE =
"YES"
>                   IF I-UNIT-CODE (WS-INFO-SUB) =
>                        WS-UNIT-CODE (WS-INFO-SUB WS-CUST-SUB)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>                END-IF.


In the deepest IF statement, the MOVE statement is indented two
spaces, but the PERFORM below it is not, making it appear that it is
not a part of the IF statement, however there is an END-IF below the
PERFORM, including it.  Further distracting the eye is and END-PERFORM
directly below the END-IF, making it look like it belongs to the
PERFORM, although it can't because the PERFORM isn't inline.  This
END-PEFROM actually belongs to a PERFORM five lines up and two spaces
to the left.  Two lines down, there is another END-PEFORM, it also
looks like it could belong to PERFORM 400-GOOD-REPORT, etc.  However
it belongs to a PERFORM 13 lines up and two spaces to the left (more
on this later).  So, there are two END-PERFORMS (arguably starting in
column 14), that belong to PERFORMS starting in column 12.

However, looking at it again, I realize that that second END-PERFORM
is a bit odd, because two lines down there is a third END-PERFORM, and
we only have two inline PERFORMS in this paragraph.  Even allowing
that PERFORM 400-GOOD-REPORT can be successfully terminated by
END-PERFORM, I don't see how that termination can happen across the
END-IF statement that comes directly after PEFORM 400-GOOD-REPORT.

I am not sure whether the following is correct, but it is how I would
have indented it:

 300-TABLE-LOOKUP.
     MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND-PRODUCT
     MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE
     PERFORM VARYING WS-INFO-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
         UNTIL WS-INFO-SUB > 10 OR WS-FOUND-PRODUCT = "YES"
         IF I-PRODUCT-NO = WS-PRODUCT-NO (WS-CUST-SUB)
             MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND-PRODUCT
             PERFORM VARYING WS-INFO-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
                 UNTIL WS-INFO-SUB > 10 OR WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE = "YES"
                 IF I-UNIT-CODE (WS-INFO-SUB) =
                    WS-UNIT-CODE (WS-INFO-SUB WS-CUST-SUB)
                     MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE
                     PERFORM 400-GOOD-REPORT
                 END-IF
             END-PERFORM
*END-PERFORM
         END-IF
     END-PERFORM
     IF WS-FOUND-PRODUCT = "NO"
         PERFORM 500-NO-PRODUCT-ERROR-REPORT
     ELSE
         IF WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE = "NO"
             PERFORM 520-NO-UNIT-ERROR-REPORT
         end-if
     END-IF.

Notice how all the terminators now line up with their statements, and
all statements inside IFs and PERFORMs are indented.

At the end of the paragraph, I also put the PERFORMS of 500 and 520 on
the same line as the paragraph name.  Putting two statements on the
same line isn't against any rule I know, but I don't do it personally.

I added the small-case end-if.  I'm not sure how your compiler would
have interpreted a missing terminator, but since it was the last line
of code in the paragraph, it may not have mattered.  If there had been
more code, it most certainly would have mattered.

Note the line of code that is commented out.  This is where I'm not
sure what will happen.  Since there was an extra terminator in this
paragraph, I can't see how the program compiled, but I don't know the
compiler.  It would not have compiled successfully under VS COBOL II
(at the least it would have given a RC=4 and a warning, and I think I
might get an RC=8 with a "END-PERFORM was found without matching
PERFORM, the statement was discarded." etc.


ECS already responded about the problem in 100-LOAD-PRICE-TABLE, so I
won't duplicate his work.

Try putting the suggested fixes in and see if they work, then come
back and tell us what happened.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: My program does NOT work!! Someone please look and help!

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jB%X2.2170$JC1.114658@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<19990505005454.06309.00002613@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7gpok3$4po$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
ChrisOsborne wrote in message <7gpok3$4po$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>...
<snip>
>In the deepest IF statement, the MOVE statement is indented two
>spaces, but the PERFORM below it is not, making it appear that it is
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>                         UNTIL WS-INFO-SUB > 10 OR WS-FOUND-UNIT-CODE =
"YES"
>                 IF I-UNIT-CODE (WS-INFO-SUB) =
>                    WS-UNIT-CODE (WS-INFO-SUB WS-CUST-SUB)
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>same line isn't against any rule I know, but I don't do it personally.


Good example.  One statement per line, indenting conditional statements two
spaces after the IF, WHEN, AT END, etc and lining up related PERFORM with
END-PERFORM and IF:ELSE:END-IF makes sense.  One suggestion though might be
to indent a continued statement six or so spaces to identify it as a
continuation and not a conditional and not a stand alone statement executed
when the previous line is.  As an example note the placement of UNTIL in the
quoted part above.

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: My program does NOT work!! Someone please look and help!

- **From:** bfetml@aol.com (BFETML)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990505021359.05463.00000754@ng-cf1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990505005454.06309.00002613@ng-fk1.aol.com>`

```
hi,
try using some displays to see where you are at and what you expect.  (Assuming
mvs environment) .  I generally place a display inside 000-main to start.  Egs.

 DISPLAY 'INSIDE MAIN RTN'. 

start from there and add more displays to follow the logic of your program. 
That is how I learned cobol.

You have nested performs and 2 dimensional tables weird things can happen 
given the limitations of the human mind.

Recommend eg.
for   WS-PRODUCT-NO (WS-CUST-SUB)   right before or after

DISPLAY ' '.
DISPLAY 'WS-CUST-SUB=' WS-CUST-SUB.
DISPLAY 'WS-PRODUCT-NO='  WS-PRODUCT-NO (WS-CUST-SUB).

You can also display an entire record:
eg :
DISPLAY 'I-ORDER-FILE-REC=' I-ORDER-FILE-REC.

If you have a large input file try the displays with say 5 records and follow
the logic.

Try and use periods in your coding, you may sometimes be surprized at what
defaults you may get from the compiler.  

cheers
eric
```

## Thread: Vermont - IBM VM - Learn UNIX/ORACLE — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Vermont - IBM VM - Learn UNIX/ORACLE

- **From:** Joe Bergamo <jbergamo@bergamo-associates.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37304FBC.11D34B6F@bergamo-associates.com>`

```
Our client, located in Northwest Vermont, is looking to fill a full time
position for a Senior Technical Support Analyst.

The candidate needs experience supporting users in an IBM VM mainframe
environment.

The company is moving to a UNIX/ORACLE environment within a year.  They
will cross train this candidate in UNIX/ORACLE and eventually move them
into the new environment.

Benefits are good and salary is in the 50 - 65K range.

Contact me if you are interested.

Thanks,
Joe Bergamo
Bergamo & Associates, Inc.
PO Box 4093
Middletown, NJ 07748
(732)495-5400 FAX (732)495-5404 EMAIL jbergamo@bergamo-associates.com
```
