<a id="t-dda4b60a3d"></a>
## Thread: Seattle - MQ Serials Specialist need urgently - up to $70/hr — 4 this month / 10 total

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-dda4b60a3d)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Seattle - MQ Serials Specialist need urgently - up to $70/hr

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dwledet@worldnet.att.net (Douglas J. Woods-Ledet)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bc.jobs,can.jobs,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.corba,ab.jobs
- **Message-ID:** `<372fc87d.8573866@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<Va4W2.7431$e4.1558@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <3728e2ea.72334668@news1.ibm.net> <3729B032.F42E55B3@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-FIGR6WqOHN9b@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
>On 30 Apr 1999 14:33:29 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
>this kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

OK, that is the last insult that I can take without responding.

There is no Y2K "problem".

The systems we wrote in the 70s where correct and functional for the
life span we were told to write them for, that being an average of 5
to 10 years, not the 20 to 30 that they have been running. 

Are you still using the Word Processor you used five years ago, much
less ten or twenty years ago?

Those mainframe applications that we wrote are still running corporate
America and with a little patch to the date routines, will be running
corporate America for a few more years... at least until something 
better comes along.  And nothing has yet, or they would have been
replaced years ago.

Just for your knowledge, machines at that time were physically limited
in both RAM and Disk space.  If we have not "shorten" things, dates 
are just one of those things we shorten, the applications would not
have been possible at all.

Applications made computers a needed item and the need reduced
the cost and now we have PCs that have more resources than the
"corporate" computers we wrote the applications on. 

Before you mindlessly repeat the current flashy sales slogans and
trash talk, think about it.

I believe in PCs.  They have enabled individuals to produce more 
work, individually, than any other invention since the adding machine.

But as good as they are in a "personal" environment, we are still in
the initial stages where a PC will help the "group" produce more and
much more work will have to be done before the Mainframe applications
can be retired....

What am I doing?  I must be crazy to think that anyone will listen,
not to mention understand.  The world is PC happy, and reason is a 
forgotten art.

Doug
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Seattle - MQ Serials Specialist need urgently - up to $70/hr

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bc.jobs,can.jobs,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.corba,ab.jobs
- **Message-ID:** `<37305654.7500912@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<Va4W2.7431$e4.1558@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <3728e2ea.72334668@news1.ibm.net> <3729B032.F42E55B3@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-FIGR6WqOHN9b@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <372fc87d.8573866@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
dwledet@worldnet.att.net (Douglas J. Woods-Ledet) wrote:

>>On 30 Apr 1999 14:33:29 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>>Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
>>this kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.
>
>OK, that is the last insult that I can take without responding.

Doug:

I feel compelled to respond.  First of all, Thane is a great guy who
is just poking a little harmless fun at the whole Y2K issue. He is
*not* being insulting, nor is he being malicious in any way, shape or
form.  It's just a bit of humor.

The thread is significantly cross posted, so you may not know Thane.
Very few people in the newsgroups frequented by Thane will dispute
your statements regarding mainframes.  Actually a very significant
number of "regulars" to these newsgroups are indeed mainframe
programmers and are *very well* aware of the reasons why two digit
year dates were used in lieu of 4 digit years.  Your assessment of the
current situation is quite accurate and very succinct.  It is apparent
that you do have a keen understanding of the Y2K issue.

Thane is both a PC and mainframe programmer and has been for years.
Thane is also an accomplished author and is highly respected in the
newsgroups which he regularly frequents.

He was simply poking a little fun at an industry which is subject to
the unfair stereotypes that we are all a bunch of nerdy, humorless,
zero-personality, technogeeks who are as dry as popcorn farts.

I applaud Thane's efforts to make life a little more lighthearted and
suggest that you look into things a bit further before blasting him in
a public forum.

>What am I doing?  I must be crazy to think that anyone will listen,
>not to mention understand.  The world is PC happy, and reason is a 
>forgotten art.

You are correct that nobody is listening.  Most of us do not have Via
Voice or Dragon Naturally Speaking connected to our newsgroup readers.
(This is humor...it's actually a bit of fun!)

I am quite sure that many of us are indeed *reading* your comments and
am also quite sure that most of us agree with your assessment of the
causes of the Y2K issue.  I suspect that "Mainframe" Thane does as
well (sorry, Thane...more humor..please don't flame me).  

Visit some different newsgroups and you may be surprised to find the
vast amount of chatter regarding mainframe programming and systems.

It might be fun!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Seattle - MQ Serials Specialist need urgently - up to $70/hr

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bc.jobs,can.jobs,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.corba
- **Message-ID:** `<7gq0i2$u0b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<Va4W2.7431$e4.1558@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <3728e2ea.72334668@news1.ibm.net> <3729B032.F42E55B3@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-FIGR6WqOHN9b@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <372fc87d.8573866@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <372fc87d.8573866@netnews.worldnet.att.net>,
  dwledet@worldnet.att.net (Douglas J. Woods-Ledet) wrote:
> >On 30 Apr 1999 14:33:29 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> >Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Doug, sorry I offended you - It's a JOKE man.  As with any joke, it's only
funny if there is a grain of truth to it!  As HUMANS we abbreviate everything
we can.  We are a brief people.

> The systems we wrote in the 70s where correct and functional for the
> life span we were told to write them for, that being an average of 5
> to 10 years, not the 20 to 30 that they have been running.
>

I know this - I wrote/maintained a great number of them.  Your years of
experience are greater than mine as I started in 1983 - but I have been in
mainframes ever since.  Currently I work on an OS/390 system.

> Those mainframe applications that we wrote are still running corporate
> America and with a little patch to the date routines, will be running
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I AGREE - and in some cases this code HAS been ported to the PC.  There are
systems I wrote that have code now running - the same code - on PC's, System
36's, AS/400's and Big fire breathing mainframes.

> Just for your knowledge, machines at that time were physically limited
> in both RAM and Disk space.  If we have not "shorten" things, dates
> are just one of those things we shorten, the applications would not
> have been possible at all.

Oh, I know.  I know that we could not upgrade disk drives because it would
cost us a million dollars we didn't have. I know how much money and time has
been saved over the years - and it more than makes up for the present expense
that is encountered in patching the code today.


> Before you mindlessly repeat the current flashy sales slogans and
> trash talk, think about it.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know what in my post led you to believe I had no Mainframe experience,
or that I was a "PC" kind of guy.  Heck, I was correcting the poor lady who
called MQ SERIES MQ SERIALS!  As you and I know, MQ SERIES was originally a
product designed FOR the mainframe!

> What am I doing?  I must be crazy to think that anyone will listen,
> not to mention understand.  The world is PC happy, and reason is a
> forgotten art.

Doug, I don't think you are crazy - I think my little tagline joke caught you
after a rough day trying to convince idiots that after 40 years you actually
DO know what you are doing and WHY.  You have my apologies for offending you.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
ChrisOsborne wrote:
> 
> Computer Associates has JCLCHECK, and I'll find a copy of Enterprise
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Ken

We used to have JCLCHECK until a few years back.  Then they decided to
replace it with JCLPREP, but I can't remember the name of the Vendor. 
JCLPREP has the additional feature of being able to do automated JCL
conversions by applying rules.  

You can probably find an ad for JCLPREP in a recent issue of Enterprise
Systems Journal.
```

## Thread: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature — 3 msgs

### Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature

- **From:** Intelz@nospam.net (Intel No Privacy)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<3765acd0.5881092@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Take the Pentium III Boycott Survey
http://mail.infotrieve.com/isurvey/index.cfm?vendorid=6045&formid=F0006045
	

	Pentium III chip with the individual serial number that can
track your web surfing and buying habits can now have the ID number
turned on and off by software.  Following some links I found the
www.fightdivx.com website and noticed that they have a Intel Boycott
page with links, quotes and info on why you should boycott the
invasion of privacy Pentium III chips. Just like everyone suspected,
the ID number can be taken without a customers knowledge. Just like
cellular phone fraud, once someone has your unique ID number, they
could pose as you on the internet.  Do not be fooled by reports that
this problem is fixed because Intel disabled this feature by software
on their up coming chips.  Information is power. They want to know
your surfing and buying habits. That is what this is all about. Here
is the link to the page with the boycott info and links. 

http://www.fightdivx.com/intelboycott.htm

Also you will find a Boycott Intel screen saver and banner on their
page above. Spread it around.
```

Oh look.... he's reposted it! AGAIN!

If you really want to go paranoid, do you realise that alot of people
save word documents and templates, and send them about on the net,
without realising there is a way of tracking the author back.
Word records the MAC id number (unique to each network card) in every
.doc and .dot you save. (assuming you have network card, that is)
Have a look for "PID_GUID" in a word document (you'll have to load it
with a hex editor, or something other than word).

The author of Melisa didn't know this.... Guess he does now! :-)

Dodgy.

- **Message-ID:** `<7gnr8p$rum$1@wanadoo.fr>`

Hi

I have just discovered this wonderful tool (PowerCOBOL v3 the free one).

Here is my questions.

1) I have created a main sheet.
Here is my BUTTON1 - CLICK PROCEDURE.

MOVE POW-OFF TO POW-ENABLE OF MAIN

So the main sheet is not enabled, now i am in the MainPilote sheet.
In the MainPilote sheet, i have created a Button when i click on it, i want
to do this

MOVE POW-ON TO POW-ENABLE OF MAIN

but there is error compilation.
How to access to the properties or method of main sheet when you are in the
MainPilote
sheet?

2) How to center all my sheets in my screen?

Can CHOIX variable be used in all my project(all sheets) or just in all
procedure of my Main sheet?

HP-UNIX
Now i am using PowerCOBOL, where to put this code(all files must be
accessible in all sheets)?
input-output section.
file-control
select fpilote assign to "pilote" organization indexed
access dynamic record key codepil.
select flhisto assign to "lhisto" organization indexed
access dynamic record key codhisto with duplicates.
select favion assign to "avion" organization indexed
access dynamic record key codav.

data division.
file section.

fd fpilote.
01 pilote.
02 codepil pic x(8).
02 nom pic x(20).
02 prenom pic x(15).
02 naissance pic 99/99/9999.
02 solde pic 9999,99.

02 codhisto pic x(8).
02 codemv pic x(2).
02 dat pic 99/99/9999.
02 Vol.
03 numav pic x(4).
03 duree pic 9,99.
02 Vers.
03 nature pic x.
03 montant pic 9999,99.
03 numchq pic 9(10).
fd favion.
01 avion.
02 codav pic x(4).
02 nomavion pic x(10).

Thanks a lot
Sydney from FRANCE

<a id="t-1c744143bc"></a>
## Thread: File Conversion Program is released — 1 msgs

### File Conversion Program is released

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFB8nCF.C1A@netcom.com>`

```

OK, the May issue of  Hot VS  (a Wang-centric e-zine) has 
been released to the public.  Late, but out.   

   www.hotvs.com

The utility I wrote, the one that helps you unpack fields,
is in there but in the rush to meet the deadline I had do 
leave out support for binary fields and a formal readme.txt.  
I'll get those in v1.1 and uploaded in the next week or two.

This utility helps you get rid of the binary, overpunched,
and packed decimal fields that makes moving data to a PC
difficult.  The format of the outgoing numeric is under
your control... number of digits (left & right of decimal),
leading/trailing signs, etc.

It also lets you insert literals and move fields 
around... handy for Y2k work:  MMDDYY --> "19"YYMMDD. 


Though it's written in Wang VS Cobol-74 and Wang Procedure
Language (scripts) the writing is sufficiently generic that any
programmer worth his/her salt can adapt it to other platforms.

I'll help you if you need explanation for any Wang-isms. 
```

hi,
It sure does sound like the program at depaul. The same program I completed 2
1/2 years ago.  Here is my story.  I also made a switch from psychology
(graduate degree) to computer science and have not looked back.  Given the
income prospects for a bachelors in psychology versus a job in computer
science, unfortunately, there is no comparison. I had been in psychology for a
decade.  

If it is the depaul program, ask them about their placement rate, i think
practically everyone who wanted to got a first job. I am still in mainframes. 
The only down side I can think of is that the mainframe market might be slowing
down somewhat, you should ask about the current market for graduates.
Currently, I work for a big 6 consulting firm and actively enrolled in one of
depaul's masters programs,  paid for by my employer. (Try and get tuition
reimbursement from your employer in psychology if you do not work for the govt 
:-)  )  Sorry I am a walking banner  for depaul.

my biased view
eric
```

<a id="t-02c163d57b"></a>
## Thread: GUI Programming questions — 15 msgs

### GUI Programming questions

- **From:** Pete <pivkovic@amiscu.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>`

```
The company I work for uses Microfocus COBOL to program a large system
of programs that run in DOS. Our clients currently run those programs on
DOS, Win 95/98, and Win NT computers. Nice for us since we only need one
set of programs. However, as with most places I am sure, our customers
are now clamoring for Windows interfaces. I am hoping some of you who
have experienced this already will share with me the solution you came
up with. Did you drop COBOL entirely for those systems running windows?
Use Visual Basic or C to do interfaces for your programs? Use one of the
new cobol compilers like Micrfocus' Netexpress? Anything other ideas?
And how is it working out for you?
```

#### ↳ Re: GUI Programming questions

- **From:** smayer@t-online.de (Sebastian Mayer)
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3731EC41.B92B52FA@t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>`

```


Pete schrieb:
> 
> The company I work for uses Microfocus COBOL to program a large system
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> And how is it working out for you?

Hi Pete,

itï¿½s too much to say that we have experience with the migration to GUI,
but we are 
beyond evaluation. Our program system contains ca. 500 programs which
have user
interfaces. 

The present interface is a DOS interface. We have developped our own
tools to generate a program out of a resource file (for example a dos
dialog box or a dos table). A generated program consists of two parts.
One part is once generated and then adapted by a programer (for example
to handle constaints) the other part is 
always new generated if the resource file gets changed. 
As you said the customers are now clamoring for Windows interfaces.
What we have looked for is a tool which alows us to migrate to windows
with the minimum of changes of the Code. All the partner firms of us
which have already started with window gui have made the same
experience. All have recoded their programs (from pascal to delphi, from
MF Cobol to Visual Basic, from MF Cobol to oracle ...) and all of them
have failed to do this in the estimated time (mostly 1 year). The nearly
have all spent twice of it. But the worst is, that the programs
have become incompatible to theire predecesors, will say features that
were highly estimated by the DOS users are now missing.
To resume our situation: only 1/2 of our programs have user interfaces,
those you have interfaces are well structured, will say have a very
common pattern (due to the code generator we have used).
The choice of visual basic (or other "foreign" languages) was quickly
dropped because we canï¿½t use our old code which is well testet. For
example if we enter a vat code (we have 120 differnt one to handle all
german special cases) it was checked whether the amount enter before has
the right sign, if it fits to the entered account, the percentage is
read from the vat table, the vat acount number and so on. Cause of the
same reason we donï¿½t have choosen dialog system because the interface is
capsuled in it and we have strong interactions between the data fields.
We have some NetExpress version installed at our place and I started to
play with OO-Cobol and especially the gui classes. After the usual
starter problems they are a nice thing to deal with (but itï¿½s hard to
find some one who has experience with them).
When playing with the features i have come across the gui paradigma
which means that you have an event driven programm, that means you enter
a dialog box, make your input, press the ok-button and check the input
for consistence. Our DOS programs make one accept at one field and then
analyse at once the input. What we did is we bend the event handler.
When a data field is entered and would lose itï¿½s focus, we stop the
event handler and the program goes on like in DOS. So what we have
actually done is replace the "accept" statment by a winapi call. Our dos
dialogboxes can be nearly automaticly changed to windows resource files.
All the code to do this is wrapped in an automaticly generated dialog
descriptor and in a special class doing the dialog handling. The result
is, that our code (the part which is adapted by the programmer) must be
changed only by 3 percent. The behaviour of the program stays the same
in the way of handling input but is enhanced in the way that you can use
the mouse, hot keys, context menus and push buttons.
Conclusion:
Most of our migration work is to adapt the tools we need, i.e general
dialogbox class, a table handler, adapt the source code generator and so
on. We think that we do this in 2 man-month. Afterwards we hope to port
a dos programm to window in between 0 (totally done by generator) and 3
days.

regards
Sebastian
(see http://www.maysoft.de/progen.htm)
```

#### ↳ Re: GUI Programming questions

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gsren$lgt$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>`

```
Pete wrote in message <3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>...
>up with. Did you drop COBOL entirely for those systems running windows?
>Use Visual Basic or C to do interfaces for your programs? Use one of the
>new cobol compilers like Micrfocus' Netexpress? Anything other ideas?

I once was in charge of an experiment doing one of your above suggestions.
The project were ran on behalf of the European Union Research Council. What
we tried was to move a part of an Inventory/Invoice system from Acucobol to
Delphi. The lessons learned was that moving an application from 1 language
to another is not easily accomplished. Generally speaking the most
significant factors were:

    - Modern languages like Delphi, VB, VC is a lot more complex and have a
learning curve that
      makes the task complicated. It isn't just to hire new employees which
now the actual language,
      they do not have the application experience of the current staff.

    - User interface, is not just user interface. When moving from character
based to graphical you
      are taking a huge step. I would say it is almost impossible to do a
straight forward transfer.
      Where the character edition had a limited number of ways to accomplish
a dialog, a gui offers
      an enormous number of options. Finding the right one and using it wise
is difficult.

    - While at least Delphi and VC allows you to do "anything" you will in
most instances come into
      a situation where you wish you had Cobol. Cobol is made for business
logic.

    - With Cobol you have a database as a part of the language, with Delphi,
VC, VB you will have to
      make a choice.

I would advice you to stay with Cobol and extend your current applications
into the wonderful world of gui from there. Doing so will allow you to focus
on the important issue; To produce the best possible user interface for your
users. Changing to Delphi, VC, VB would cost you a lot of effort to get
familiar with those environments, it will take years to obtain the same
level of knowledge that you do now have for Cobol.

Most modern Cobol's support gui today, although in different ways. Acucobol,
the choice of mine supports gui by having extended the language to support
gui controls. Like
    DISPLAY LABEL AT 0301 Title "This is a label".
Others use third party utilities like Flexus sp2

I don't know for other Cobol's, but Acucobol let you run gui and character
mode instantly, depending on the host. You may also run charactermode using
a native Windows runtime as you do today and upgrade program by program. I
have done so for a few programs. Although it might look stupid having a
character interface, pressing a key and suddenly have a full featured gui
window, it is really efficient. Implementing gui gradually means low cost
and effective production as continously maintainance also applies to new
development with no extra effort.

I am satisfied with the way Acucobol has implemented gui support, most of
its gui controls also have character counterparts, thus may be used on a
variety of platforms. You will certainly receive opinions from others as
well, there is a number of good products out there, it is a consideration
you will have to go through. I am most certain that you will do a good
choice whatever you decides to go for.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: GUI Programming questions

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-7HW01iv5BdBj@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>`

```
On Thu, 6 May 1999 17:33:43, Pete <pivkovic@amiscu.com> wrote:

> The company I work for uses Microfocus COBOL to program a large system
> of programs that run in DOS. Our clients currently run those programs on
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

No, you certainly do NOT drop COBOL.  What I use is the COBOL  of my 
choice (or yours) and COBOL sp2 from Flexus - see 
http://www.flexus.com

Plug alert - 

If you don't presently receive "The COBOL Report" go to 
http://www.objectz.com and subscribe.  In the next issue will be an 
article, by me, about new COBOL GUI development, and also conversion 
of existing text based systems to GUI using COBOL.



-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: GUI Programming questions

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37323b98.24878231@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>`

```
We went from Micro Focus Cobol DOS to Micro Focus GUI using Cobol SP2
as our GUI interface and then switched our compiler to Fujitsu Cobol
using the same Cobol SP2 for our GUI.  Make sure you do your homework
right before you attempt to do the conversion.  The decision we took
was everything in Cobol.

Visit our site  www.mc-sys.com to see the Fujutsi/Cobol SP2 screen
shots.

Tony M. Mina

On Thu, 06 May 1999 13:33:43 -0400, Pete <pivkovic@amiscu.com> wrote:

>The company I work for uses Microfocus COBOL to program a large system
>of programs that run in DOS. Our clients currently run those programs on
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: GUI Programming questions

- **From:** "Robert Kovacic" <rjk@onaustralia.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uNY2.26816$MB3.38737@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>`

```
In your circumstances you could consider NetExpress and use it to build web
based "GUI interfaces". i.e. use a browser interface and remold you COBOL
programs as CGI programs.

Although such an interface has restrictions compared to a true GUI
interface, if you are coming from a COBOL character based environment you
are unlikely to miss all the interface bells and whistles available with VB,
Delphi, etc. The potential long term benefits in GUI independance and client
flexibility are a plus.

Regards, Robert Kovacic.

Pete wrote in message <3731D276.78C3292E@amiscu.com>...
>The company I work for uses Microfocus COBOL to program a large system
>of programs that run in DOS. Our clients currently run those programs on
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
garybrc@aol.com (GaryBRC) wrotf:

>Did you look at ETC's COBOL-WOW product when deciding what tools to use to go
>from character to GUI?  We are currently looking at both sp2 and COBOL-WOW. 
>sp2 seems to be much simpler GUI. Does this translate into a limited GUI
>interface?  We are converting DOS, character mode source to GUI.  

***  No we didn't.  Mainly because I wasn't familar with it when we
were doing the evaluation.  Because we are an MF shop, we evaluated
Dialog (when it was still a separate product and not part of
NetExpress) and SP2.  We chose SP2 for a number of reasons:

1)  COBOL call convention to interface with the product
2)  Compiler independent
3)  And, at least to us, it was much more STABLE than Dialog,
     especially the earlier versions, in a development environment.

I've been working with the product for about 3 months now.
We have four major software modules and I'm just about done
"gui-izing" one with SP2.  I guess my user base will be the final
judges as to whether it is "limited".  I don't think so.

Mike Madara
```

## Thread: Useing AcuCobol programs in batch (background) processing. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Useing AcuCobol programs in batch (background) processing.

- **From:** blakew@balsa.fuller.com ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn7j37pp.l6r.blakew@fullerbrush.com>`

```
Hello Cobol World!

We have a job written in Acucobol on our Tru64-Unix 4.0 system.  We would like
to have the job run in batch (background) processing.  It would be REALY nice
if we could run this job with a cron / crontab entry.

Every time we try to do this the AcuCobol job crashes and in the log file
it puts...

	Memory access violation

Any suggestions?

Thank you for your time.
```

#### ↳ Re: Useing AcuCobol programs in batch (background) processing.

- **From:** "D.Newman" <Domenyk.Newman@MCL-Group.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<926007795.5961.0.nnrp-11.9e98ccda@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<slrn7j37pp.l6r.blakew@fullerbrush.com>`

```
What happens if you run the program from the command prompt. Do you get the
same error.
```

#### ↳ Re: Useing AcuCobol programs in batch (background) processing.

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fAzWTEAVuxO3IweE@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<slrn7j37pp.l6r.blakew@fullerbrush.com>`

```
Hi,

Have you checked with Acucorp support? I seem to remember a
problem a long time ago where certain operations could cause a
problem in a background program (I think ACCEPT FROM
SYSTEM-INFO was one). This problem was resolved quite some
time ago, so if you have an older release an upgrade should fix it.

I'm guessing you're in the USA so you can just call
1-800-COBOL85, or email support@acucorp.com


Best regards

Nigel

<a id="t-00da3b7254"></a>
## Thread: DYNAM or NODYNAM? — 3 msgs

### Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990506154006.19530.00000212@ng-cc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<23.z2.d12RGX7gukq4UNPt4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`

```
A brief warning to some who read this thread and think chopping CANCELs might
be a viable approach in all such poor performance situations.

Quite often the CANCEL is a reflection of a design decision that is intended to
refresh a number of variables in the called routine to their original values
specified in the VALUE clauses when the subroutine was compiled.

((In modern COBOL this can actually be achieved with the INITIAL keyword on the
PROGRAM-ID, which triggers data refresh without a program reload. But you need
access to the source of the submodule for this solution.))

It is especially true that when dealing with sourceless modules, an
experimenter will have no idea if it is safe to remove the CANCEL. Regrettably,
the removal may look safe in test environments (especially if small data files
are used in test), and produce harmful effects in production (where volume and
data variety is more vast).

The comments about performance improvements are right on, and the well phrased
indication of the human frustration in the cited episode is very reflective of
how costly this mysterious COBOL feature can be  But chopping CANCELs in the
absence of source code is a roulette game with a dangerous implement.

Incidentally, speaking of design considerations, it is not rare that a project
leaders decision to deploy CANCELs in drivers involves poorly designed
programs, and in some cases imperfect understanding of the programming
environment on the part of the original coders. 

So in cases where you do have access to the source, and do have the resources
and time (certainly scarce things these days), it is often better to get into
the submodule(s) and rectify their initialization logic, than to use the
brutish CANCELs.

The central point is that good initialization logic in the procedure division,
or mechanisms like INITIAL will reset all or minimum necessary subcomponents of
the data division, whereas CANCEL-and-call-again sequences from the driver will
reload the submodules executable code image repeatedly.  In most cases the code
reload involves disk access which is orders of magnitude slower than the data
memory refresh accesses.

It is also worth noting that the traffic that CANCEL-reload sequences place on
I/O devices and channels has major implications for other tasks in a large
computing environment. (Especially when the phenomenon is performed from within
loops as in the cited case). When an entire application is dominated by the
CANCEL solution, other concurrent apps can run sluggish indeed as their I/Os
wait for the thrashing to ease up.   Other concurrent apps definitely run
faster after the offending app is revised.

The CANCEL solution can be used for many reasons. It is common for it to be
favored by batch project team leaders and project managers who had their
technical hayday in online environments wherein things are dynamic for
operational reasons, and to whom static components seem irksome and even
malfunctioning.

At any rate, it is not utterly and entirely unreasonable to actually place an
unCANCELed sourceless submodule into production. It is just that you should not
bet the farm on it. And as a previous poster has effectively indicated,
removing the CANCELs (blindly or with open submodule code) will definitely
improve performance.
 
Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gtjhh$aci$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<23.z2.d12RGX7gukq4UNPt4.NR@nospam.nowhere> <19990506154006.19530.00000212@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
Further, to this, I got burnt recently by going from a cancel oriented
overlay system to a statically linked single module with all subroutines
set as initial.  I thought I could avoid the type of thing that Ray talks
about by placing the Initial statement in each programs-id, but there
were still a couple of wrinkles revolving about the initial state of
data records in files(pre and post open).

#### ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujVfNfNm#GA.239@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<23.z2.d12RGX7gukq4UNPt4.NR@nospam.nowhere> <19990506154006.19530.00000212@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
Just goes to show - poor design/coding practice used up front. I remember
one of the things in Elements of Programming Style by K&P - always
initialize constants at compile time if possible, but especially always
initialize variables at run time (except maybe for first-time flags and
even then it's better to not design your program this way in the first
place if at all possible). Ideally, all routines should be serially
reuseable at the very least; reentrant is even better.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!


RKRayhawk wrote in message
<19990506154006.19530.00000212@ng-cc1.aol.com>...

>Quite often the CANCEL is a reflection of a design decision that is
intended to
>refresh a number of variables in the called routine to their original
values
>specified in the VALUE clauses when the subroutine was compiled.
```

Darren Greer <drgreer@qtiworld.com> wrote in article
<373100a8.543318130@news.qgraph.com>...
> -->'Twas 29 Apr 1999 23:04:37 GMT, when monkey@duke.infowave.net (Scratch
> -->Monkey) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 4 digits for a year?

4? Four? ONLY FOUR? That shows a lack of forward planning. Why not just use
an int? Even on 16-bit systems, that gives you a good few tens of millennia
to play with. And as systems get more bits on the bus, int will get ever
bigger (we hope). Most systems nowadays seem to use 32-bit ints, which
gives you a range of around +/- 2000000000 years (rounded to the nearest
megamillennium).

(If you can't tell whether I'm being serious or sarcastic, well, neither
can I.)
```

<a id="t-9c49bbd5b6"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL - compile-time messages and documentation — 3 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-9c49bbd5b6)._

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL - compile-time messages and documentation

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gt7er$864$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<7eg2ra$84s@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
For new programmers, such a book would be an invaluable resource,
saving weeks of wasted time trying to figure out what all the error
messages are.

They are not, in any way, self-documenting (except to those who
already know what they mean), since many errors are often caused by
problems unrelated to the line on which the error occurs.  "Code on
line xxx can never be executed and was therefore discarded," really
means that the data-items on the IF statement surrounding the code
cannot be properly compared.  But to read that message you would think
there is a problem right there, not back up in the Data Division.

I've figured out what the majority of the error messages mean, and
because I'm familiar and comfortable with them, I can figure out new
ones without too much difficulty.  My question is, why should there be
any difficult at all?  I've started a Word document that contains a
list of all ABENDs codes my programs take, and why, and how I solved
it.  This way, I never have to reinvent the wheel of discovery a year
down the road.  I haven't thought to put compiler error messages in
it, but after this I will.  I regularly email this document to a
circle of friends much newer to programming than I, they like it and
say it saves them time.  I doubt it will go farther then that, as I'm
not technical enough with some of it, and a get the feeling a couple
of my conclusion may have been wrong, but I just don't have enough
information.  Why don't I?  Because there is no decent compiler/abend
diagnostic manual.

There was a book written about this subject several years ago, going
by the title Application Program Debugging under MVS: COBOL and COBOL
II.  It's out of print though, and I haven't been able to find it.


William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7eg2ra$84s@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com...
> There have been some comments about compile-time messages in the
IBM-MAIN
> list server recently.  I put out the following post there - but
would also
> be interested in input from comp.lang.cobol people as well.
>
> NOTE WELL:
>     This is intended as an IBM compiler "survey" only.  There are
very
> different (but related?) issues for other compilers and their
messages - and
> documentation.
>
>    ***
>
> There have been comments in several threads recently about the lack
of an
> <IBM> COBOL compile-time "messages and codes" manual.  I now
understand (but
> didn't originally) that what people WANT is a manual that says,
>
>   "If you receive message xyz, then the probable causes are 1, 2,
and 3, -
> and the best way to resolve these are to take steps a, b, can c."
>
> As has also been pointed out IBM's stated (public) position is that
ALL
> compile-time messages are "self documenting" and that there is
nothing more
> that they can/should put into a separate manual.
>
> Now for my question(s) - to better understand the issue(s):
>
> 1) Do you think there is a difference between the current COBOLs
(either VS
> COBOL II or COBOL for this-and-that) versus the older OS/VS COBOL or
DOS/VS
> COBOL compilers?  In other words, do you think there ever was such a
> manual - or do you think that the old messages were clearer than the
new
> ones?  OR do you think that this isn't a new problem - but just one
that is
> still aggravating and seems to be more visible because your
programmers
> aren't used to the new COBOL compilers?
>
> 2) When your programmers have problems with some of the messages, do
these
> same programmers have access to the LRM (Language Reference
Manual) - but
> still can't figure out the message(s)?  If they have access, is it
to the
> hard-copy or soft-copy versions of the manuals?
>
> If it is hard-copy, is this their own "personal" copy of the LRM -
or one
> that is shared with others in their group or from a common library?
>
> 3)  Can you give me (private email is OK - or public if you prefer -
but I
> don't know how much of IBM-MAIN is interested in this) some examples
of the
> messages that cause your programmers problems?  Particularly, if
there are
> specific messages that seem to come up frequently - I would be
interested in
> them.  (I will probably share these with IBM - but if you don't want
me to,
> please let me know.)
>
>   ***
>
> Thanks in advance. I hope that if this "problem" gets clearer, a
better
> solution may be made possible.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL - compile-time messages and documentation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gtrv2$g27@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7eg2ra$84s@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <7gt7er$864$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Chris,
  I thought that this thread had died out.  I am interested in your
comments - but will admit that they seem to be a "bit" in the minority (but
certainly not unique).  Personally, I would prefer to teach a "new"
programmer HOW to resolve the messages they are getting - rather than giving
them a cookbook that probably is misleading (in at least some cases).  For
example, I think that the message that you mentioned (about code being
discarded) can teach a programmer a couple of things:

1) Learn the differences between I- and W-level messages - versus more
severe ones. (This is a W-level message as I recall.)

2) I wouldn't agree with you that it is usually a data division problem, but
then again it DOES take looking at your specific code for ANY message that
you get.

I am CC'ing my IBM contacts on this note (to convey your input) but
personally, I am not yet convinced that any manual would really help in most
cases (and certainly not in the one you mention).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL - compile-time messages and documentation

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gutho$88t$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<7eg2ra$84s@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <7gt7er$864$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <7gtrv2$g27@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William,

Yes, I saw the month old date, but I was having trouble with my news
server and couldn't always review old messages or post responses to
them.  There were several other old threads I didn't post to, but this
one I couldn't resist.

All right, I'll admit it, having to figure out what every error
message meant with no help at all (no one to ask, in my case), did
help me.  But it also took, in some cases, a day or two to figure out
what they meant (when your inexperienced, sometimes more than one
thing can be affecting what you're doing, causing some problems to
become harder to solve).

I'm forced to concede that compiler errors are easier to solve than
logic errors, and for that there is no fixed set of information
available to solve problems, only general techniques.


Sincerely,
    Chris Osborne



William M. Klein <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7gtrv2$g27@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com...

than giving

cases).  For

being

more

problem, but

1)  Start with comments, * in column 7.  What does the program do?

<a id="t-350d13a379"></a>
## Thread: student needs help! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: student needs help!

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MftY2.28$Uc1.2472@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<373233e5@news.one.net>`

```
>This is a simple control break program but the subtotal is printing twice
>and I can't figure out where the second print is coming from.  A point in


If you can't find it in such a small program, that probably means your logic
for calling that subroutine 0070-SUBTOT is and has to be executing twice.
Check your logic again.

Without seeing the data and running it myself, I would guess the problem is
in the logic which executes 0700-SUBTOT. Can't really verbalize it, but try
commenting out the PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT in 0700-SUBTOT, and see what that
does. That's just instinct talking, but I think you are not properly moving
data after a control break. Hopefully your teacher gave you an algorithm as
an example to follow. Look at that and compare it to your. That may indicate
where the error might be.

Looking more into it, it think the problem is here:
-->288            IF (PREV-VEND-ACCT = VEND-ACCT) AND
   289               (VEND-ACCT = PAY-VEND)
   290                PERFORM 0040-CALC
   291                PERFORM 0050-UPDATE
   292                PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT
   293                PERFORM 3000-READ-PAY
   294            ELSE
   295                PERFORM 0070-SUBTOT.
   296            IF VEND-ACCT < PAY-VEND
   297                MOVE NEW-WS-REC TO NEW-REC
   298                WRITE NEW-REC
   299                PERFORM 2000-READ-VEND.

What happens if you get 2 input records where the first IF statement is
false?


.....just a personal comment....
One thing that can save typo and debugging problems is to eliminate the use
of ".". Use the appropriate scope terminators:END-IF, END-PERFORM, ETC.
Periods are only required before the next paragraph or section label.


I am mostly grasping at straws here, so apologies if this throws you way
off.

If I read your logic correctly, the following is occurring in paragraph
020-MAIN-PROCESS, just to look at it in a different way:
           EVALUATE      (PREV-VEND-ACCT = VEND-ACCT)
                    ALSO (VEND-ACCT = PAY-VEND)
                    ALSO (VEND-ACCT < PAY-VEND)
                    ALSO (VEND-ACCT > PAY-VEND)
              WHEN TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO FALSE ALSO FALSE
   290                PERFORM 0040-CALC
   291                PERFORM 0050-UPDATE
   292                PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT
   293                PERFORM 3000-READ-PAY
              WHEN ANY ALSO FALSE ALSO TRUE ALSO FALSE
   295                PERFORM 0070-SUBTOT
   297                MOVE NEW-WS-REC TO NEW-REC
   298                WRITE NEW-REC
   299                PERFORM 2000-READ-VEND
              WHEN ANY ALSO FALSE ALSO FALSE ALSO TRUE
   295                PERFORM 0070-SUBTOT
   301                PERFORM 6000-PRINT-ERR
   302                PERFORM 3000-READ-PAY
           END-EVALUATE
           EXIT.

This logic would seem to indicate that when both control break fields change
from one record to the next you get 2 control breaks with no detail
processing in between, for instance:
record num    VEND-ACCT    PAY-VEND    result
1             a            a           ?????
2             a            a           detail processing (first WHEN)
3             a            b           control break     (second WHEN)
4             a            b           control break     (second WHEN)
5             b            b           detail processing (first WHEN)

Record 3 and 5 should do control break processing then force detail
processing immediately following. Record 4 should be performing just detail
processing.

I think I am grasping at too many straws here, so I will cut myself off.

good luck.
```

---

<a id="t-7568823668"></a>
## Thread: CAREER CHANGE H E L P — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### CAREER CHANGE H E L P

- **From:** Shenovda@cableinet.co.uk
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373224E6.16D6@cableinet.co.uk>`

```
Hi everybody



my name is marcos, i live in the UK, i have no experience in

programming, the problem is that i have a genuine interest to change my
career, from catering to programming,i also passed some aptitude tests
that suggested that i am competent to get involved with programming. A
friend suggested to

learn COBOL, i don't really know where i am standing, i would much
appreciate if someone could send me some information in regards to

where to begin, is it worth the commitment in future (baring in mind

studying fees versus employment prospects) family life and my age (30).

any information or links are mostly appreciated,

if i am sending this message to the wrong group please accept my

appologies

thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: CAREER CHANGE H E L P

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7guur8$cvq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<373224E6.16D6@cableinet.co.uk>`

```
Shenovda@cableinet.co.uk wrote in message <373224E6.16D6@cableinet.co.uk>...
>Hi everybody
>my name is marcos, i live in the UK, i have no experience in
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>where to begin, is it worth the commitment in future (baring in mind

Wow! What an attitude. I am impressed, I don't think I would have made such
a decision. However, programming is a wonderful occupation, I am most sure
you will love it. You should however remember a couple of things;

    1. Be patient. As with any craft, be it a carpenter, plumber or a
programmer. Becoming
        experienced and rewarded demands years of humble work.

    2. Do not be alone. Use either electronic forums like this or real
people sharing your interest to
        get hold of tips and tricks, discuss technics, find new ideas. When
I was at university, we could
        sit discussing programming techniques for hours. It is my opinion
that this kind of influence is
        way more important to your maturing process than any book and/or
lecturer.

    3. You *MUST* have a computer at home, and your wife and family *MUST*
allow you to spend
        HOURS in front of it. It's a hard world, you won't get anything for
free, thus hard work is the only
        way.

    4. Theory is important. The A - B - C introductory course do no good for
you aspiring to become
        a professional. There is a lot of theory in computer programming,
although you may rarely need
        the theory in everyday Cobol programming, it will lift your eyebrows
and give you an
        understanding  of programming that will easen your work. Compare it
to a mechanics at a gas
        station. He knows the engine, so, to service it, he knows what to
look for and what to do. I can
        service my car too, I can change oil to it, but what's next?

    5. Understanding what is behind the scenes. It is not strictly
necessary, just like many people work
        as drivers and knows nothing about an engine, it is perfectly ok to
use a computer and not have
        a single idea of what's going on in there. I would however recommend
you to get an idea. First
        of all it will improve your understanding of what may be the error
in case of runtime errors.
        Second, if you are up to date with the heart and soul of your
computer, any new technology
        is easier to understand.

    6. Find yourself an application to do and do it. Keep improving it,
everyday, find new applications
        to do. This is just like pilots, they need a particular number of
flight hours to qualify, so it is, also
        for you and your programming skills.

    7. Find yourself an adoptive company. Make a resume, do your initial
courses and go THERE.
        Sell yourself! And offer your services for free as an apprentice. I
believe you could do this after 1
        year of school. After the second year, you should argue for a decent
pay. Of course, if you get
        paid from the beginning, its perfect, if not, you are earning
experience.

Welcome to the wonderful world of software programming, I wish you good
luck!

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CAREER CHANGE H E L P

- **From:** turquemada@aol.com (Turquemada)
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990528041654.05561.00002097@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01bea094$730eb420$LocalHost@moore>`

```
 I am from the U.S. so perhaps the job market might be different -- but for a
while there was federal assistance for schooling if you were unemployed. Since
my temp assignment had ended I could claim unemployment and then be eligible
for 60% tuition off. Well sounds great but the curriculum at CHUBB was
primarily (80%) devoted to COBOL , 20% c,Vb4 Access and a million other
dabblings.
For absolute beginners Programming is not the field  you want to be the jack of
all trades and the master of none-- just be Careful about wieghing which one
you start with since your time is "leaned on" by the wonderful  forces of
practical survival.  Incidentally I wanted to learn C since it really opens up
a lot more avenues in programming -- it provides the foundation for everything
thats hot in PC world and beyond. Though Cobol may be somewhat practical,  I
personally find C much more rewarding even as a novice c programmer. The
disadvantage with C or languages based in it ( by this I mean if you master C
all the others fall in place:C++.Java and all other Object oriented languages.
) is that its pretty hard to get hired as an entry level. (incidentally its
just as hard with entry level  Cobol to get employed  if your school doesn't
place you -- because say you still have a balance with them) So if practicality
is wieghing on you, definitely investigate each tech school/or program and
whether there is government assistance for tuition. Then Find out the
conditions of their placement program. 
Well Good Luck from Brooklyn N.Y. - Turquemada@aol.com
```

Although a programmer should have a language like COBOL somewhat mastered,
one is never embarrassed at having too many arrows in one's quiver.
Although I was not hired as a C programmer, C programs and scripts I've
written using a variety of Unix tools have made my job as a COBOL programmer
much easier and productive, for example, identifying lines of code which may
present a Y2K problem.

Ron, 

I don't cover PowerCOBOL at all.  The book is a "beginners" book,
teaching mostly (I say mostly because I conver the Screen Section
also) standard COBOL.  I searched around for compilers that I could
include with the book, and Fujitsu was the best fit.  My examples
(Including the Lotus Screen Cam Movies) all use Fujitsu COBOL 3.0.
The programs are usable with other COBOL compilers as well, with some
minor modifications to the Screen Section and CRT Status.
```

## Thread: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!! — 5 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-d46bad4e25)._

#### ↳ Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!!

- **From:** "Paul Tabour" <ptabour@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7guqjq$a1k$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
Hello all --

I also have a file conversion issue from MVS to WinNT.  My issue involves a
file with multiple record layouts containing binary and COMP-3 data.  I
would like to avoid massaging the data on the mainframe, so this is what I
would like to do: bring the file down without translation, and use some
utility to read it in.

I found one called FilePort, from a company called SyncSort; this product
takes the file and a COBOL copybook and knows how to convert it to ASCII.
The catch?  It only runs on UNIX right now.

My question is this: do any of you know of a product that does a similar
thing for NT?  Even if it doesn't take copybooks, I'd appreciate information
about any utility that can handle binary and COMP-3 fields and multiple
record layouts.

Thanks,
Paul
ptabour@worldnet.att.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-lpb0mf0uTJ3U@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <7guqjq$a1k$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Fri, 7 May 1999 13:39:54, "Paul Tabour" <ptabour@worldnet.att.net> 
wrote:

> Hello all --
>> My question is this: do any of you know of a product that does a 
similar
> thing for NT?  Even if it doesn't take copybooks, I'd appreciate information
> about any utility that can handle binary and COMP-3 fields and multiple
> record layouts.

If you use a MicroFocus product, such as NetExpress there is a tool 
called WFL.  

With this, you transfer your file using BINARY, so that no 
EBCDIC/ASCII conversion is performed, then you run it through the WFL 
(You can even write a program that calls it, so it is not a manual 
process).  With WFL you define the "Profile" and the "Mask".  The 
"Mask" can be generated for you from the FD.  This mask is then used 
for the conversion.  I used this extensively and have seen no better 
product for the task.  It is fast and accurate.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3737299d.23484551@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <7guqjq$a1k$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Laura Sabourin <laura@ragdolls.net> wrote:

>Did anyone reply to this ? I have a similar problem but we don't even have the
>code from the COBOL systems which has produced the files which we would like to
>use to populate an ACCESS database. Anyone know of a PC/DOS based file dump that
>I could use to figure out the file out ?

Laura:

Please forgive me for holding my nose while providing this advice, but
it is very important for me to be completely honest with you.

"Hode on dow whide I grab by node"  ;-)

Ok...the answer you seek might be found at:

http://www.datajunction.com/

Data Junction has tools to extract data from various types of COBOL
files and automatically convert them to other files types (such as
Microsoft Axes Files).

Before you go out and buy a copy of Data Junction, you should also be
aware of the following:

A.  It is highly likely that you will need to know the record layout;
and

B.  I also suspect that you will need to know the specific compiler
which was used to compile the COBOL program which you have been using
to date.

...and now for the reason I hold my nose...

Unless you have somewhat "smallish" file sizes, I suspect that you
will not be happy using Access considering that you came from COBOL.
I know that this may sound completely backwards, but if you do have
large files or do a tremendous amount of file I/O, going from COBOL to
Access is kind of like trading in your Ferrari for a 1965 Plymouth
Valiant.

(my own humble opinion)





>
>Paul Tabour wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-83f0999ae8"></a>
## Thread: student needs help! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: student needs help!

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gv3c1$bqc$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<373233e5@news.one.net>`

```
First, please post your code inline with your request.


Here's my take.  It's based on the way I know to do control break
logic, so if you know another reliable way, please take this in good
grace.

First, the key between the two files is the vendor account.  In the
vendor file, the vendor account is defined as VEND-ACCT, and has a
single element under defined as VEND-ACCT-9, which is PIC 9(5).  This
is where I have some confusion.  Why does VEND-ACCT have a
sub-element?  Two different names to refer to the same storage
defined, effectively, the same way.  Why?

In 010-BOJ: the following important things happen
1:READ the payables file.
2:READ the vendor file.
3:ZEROS are MOVEd to PREV-PAY-VEND.
4:VEND-ACCT-9 is MOVEd the working storage field PREV-VEND-ACCT.

In 020-MAIN-PROCESS:

1-We determine whether (PREV-VEND-ACCT = VEND-ACCT) and (VEND-ACCT =
PAY-ACCT)

Right here, we have a problem.
There is an attempt to match the vendor-file against the payables
file, but there is also an attempt to match the vendor-files against
itself, probably an attempt at controlling the break when the a new
vendor is found.  However, at least as far as I know, without actually
reading the next record from the vendor file, and literally having two
records on hand at once, one an "active" record, and one "the next
record", this isn't easy (I've tried the way presented here, but could
never make it work).

In control break logic, a working storage field is used to "hold" the
value of the "active" record, or whatever elements of that record that
need to be passed on to other parts of the program or output files.
Why?
Because of the need to READ another record.  That way, the buffer
represented by the 01 record layout under the FD statement holds "the
next record".  We must know what is in the second record to proceed.
Once you have this, the program can then check in the main loop
whether the "active" record in working storage is different from "the
next record", and whether to do the break logic.

If the active record is different from the next record, do the break
logic, and print/write info on the active record, then move the next
record into the hold area, where it becomes the "active" record, then
another record is read and it becomes "the next record" against which
the "active" record will be checked.

If both are the same, accumulate info on the "active" record, move
"the next record" into the hold area, where it becomes the "active"
record, then another record is read and it becomes "the next record"
against which the "active" record will be checked.

The way this program is set up, the hold field is pre-set to be equal
with the key of the active record.  This assumes that the actual
physical 1st and 2nd records in the vendor file are the same, even
though what it's really trying to do, though it can't, is attempting
to set up a special first-time-through condition.  This is not
possible . . .

The active record is in the FD buffer, and info on "the next record"
is (no offense) "faked" in working storage for the first pass.  But
the reality is that there is no way for the program to really know
that the actual physical second record in the vendors file is
different or the same; therefore there is no correct way to set the
working storage hold field (AFAIK).

Try:

READ vendor-file
READ payables-file
MOVE vendor-file's ACCT to ws-vendor-rec (whole copy of record in
working storage)
READ vendor-file



IF ws-vendor-acct = payables-vend-acct
    accumulate
    READ payables-file
    any other needed stuff
ELSE
    control break logic
    any other needed stuff
END-IF


IF ws-vendor-acct < payable-acct
    MOVE vendor-file to ws-vendor-rec ("the next record" becomes the
"active" record)
    READ vendor-file                  (a new record from the
vendor-file becomes "the next record")
    any other needed stuff
END-IF

IF ws-vendor-acct > payables-vend-acct
    READ payables-file
    any other needed stuff
END-IF

Where "any other needed stuff" is code for other stuff that may being
going on that happens where it needs to (before or after the other
code I've put in).

Try this and see if things work out.  Come back and tell us what
happened, or what you did to solve it on your own.


Scott Honn <skip@one.net> wrote in message
news:373233e5@news.one.net...
> Here's the problem:
> This is a simple control break program but the subtotal is printing
twice
> and I can't figure out where the second print is coming from.  A
point in
> the right direction would be much appreciated.  I've attached the
listing if
> you're willing.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> * scotthonn@iname.com

     1 000001 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
     2 000002 PROGRAM-ID.                PG3SMH.
     3 000003 AUTHOR.                    SCOTT HONN.
     4 000004 INSTALLATION.              CTC.
     5 000006 DATE-WRITTEN.              10011998.
     6 000007 DATE-COMPILED. 06MAY99.
     7 000008*
     8 000010*REMARKS.  enter program function (MINIMUM = 1
PARAGRAPH).
     9 001000/
    10 001001 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
    11 001010 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
    12 001011 FILE-CONTROL.
    13 001020     SELECT  REPT-FILE      ASSIGN TO "a:\pg3smh.rpt".
    14            SELECT  NEW-MAST       ASSIGN TO "a:\vendora.fil".
    15            SELECT  ERR-FILE       ASSIGN TO "a:\pg3smh.err".
    16            COPY    "a:\vendors.sel".
    17C
    18C
    19C002051*   *  UNIVERSAL SELECT STATEMENT FOR THE -VENDORS.FIL- *
*
    20C002051*   *  UNIVERSAL SELECT STATEMENT FOR THE -VENDORS.FIL- *
*
    21C             select vendors
    22C                    assign to "c:\realcob\DATA1\vendors.fil".
    23C002053*
    24            COPY    "a:\payables.sel".
    25C
    26C
    27C*  *  *  - PAYABLES TRANSACTION FILE - * * *
    28C
    29C            SELECT PAYABLES
    30C                   ASSIGN TO "C:\REALCOB\DATA1\PAYABLES.FIL".
    31C*  *  *
    32 001030*
    33 002000/
    36
    37 002001 DATA DIVISION.
    38 002002 FILE SECTION.
    39            COPY "a:\vendors.fd".
    40C
    41C
    42C003030********* VENDORS FILE FD *********************
    43C
    44C003031 FD  VENDORS  LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.
    45C
    46C003033 01  VEND-REC.
    47C003034    05  VEND-ACCT.
    48C003035        10 VEND-ACCT-9           PIC 9(5).
    49C003036    05  VEND-NAME                PIC X(24).
    50C003037    05  VEND-ADDR                PIC X(25).
    51C          05  VEND-ATTN                PIC X(25).
    52C003038    05  VEND-CITY-ST-ZIP.
    53C003039       10  VEND-CITY             PIC X(10).
    54C003040       10  VEND-STATE            PIC XX.
    55C003041       10  VEND-ZIP              PIC 99999.
    56C003042    05  VEND-NUM-DATA.
    57C003043       10  VEND-LAST-DATE.
    58C003044          15 VEND-LAST-YY        PIC 99.
    59C003045          15 VEND-LAST-MM        PIC 99.
    60C003046          15 VEND-LAST-DD        PIC 99.

REPORT".
   195        01 ERR-HEAD4.
