```
   201        01 DET-LINE.
   202            05 DET-VEND             PIC X(27)     VALUE SPACES.
   203            05 FILLER REDEFINES DET-VEND.
   204                10 DET-NO           PIC ZZZZ9.
   205                10 FILLER           PIC XX.
   206                10 DET-NAME         PIC X(20).
   207            05 FILLER               PIC X         VALUE SPACES.
   208            05 DET-INV-NO           PIC X(6)      VALUE SPACES.
   209            05 FILLER               PIC XX        VALUE SPACES.
   210            05 DET-AMT              PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.
   211            05 FILLER               PIC XX        VALUE SPACES.
   212            05 DET-TAX              PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.
   213            05 FILLER               PIC XX        VALUE SPACES.
   214            05 DET-TOT              PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.
   215            05 FILLER               PIC X(13)     VALUE SPACES.
   216        01 DET2-LINE.
   217            05 FILLER               PIC X(8)      VALUE SPACES.
   218            05 DET2-VEND            PIC X(19)
   219                                    VALUE "*VENDOR TOTAL BILL*".
   220            05 FILLER               PIC X(9)      VALUE SPACES.
   221            05 DET2-AMT             PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.
   222            05 FILLER               PIC XX        VALUE SPACES.
   223            05 DET2-TAX             PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.
   224            05 FILLER               PIC XX        VALUE SPACES.
   225            05 DET2-TOT             PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.
   226            05 FILLER               PIC X(13)     VALUE SPACES.
   227        01 CALC-FIELDS.
   228            05 INV-TOT              PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   229            05 CALC-PCHS-TO-DT      PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   230            05 CALC-TAXS-TO-DT      PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   231            05 CALC-TOTAL-TOT       PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   232            05 VEND-AMT-TOT         PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   233            05 VEND-TAX-TOT         PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   234            05 VEND-TOTAL-TOT       PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   235            05 GR-TOT-AMT           PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   236            05 GR-TOT-TAX           PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   237            05 GR-TOT-TOTAL         PIC S9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
   238 002501* Constants, Totals, Headings, etc.
   239
   240 003000/
   241
   242 003001 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
   243       *A   B       2         3         4         5         6
7
   244 123456*8901
345678901234567890123456789012345678901234567890123456789012
   245 003005*
   246 003010 000-CONTROL-PARAGRAPH.
   247            DISPLAY "000-CONTROL-PARAGRAPH" ACCEPT WS-A.
   248 003020     PERFORM  010-BOJ.
   249 003030     PERFORM  020-MAIN-PROCESS
   250 003035       UNTIL EOF-SW-VEND = "YES" AND PAY-EOF = "YES".
   251 003040     PERFORM  030-EOJ
   252 003050     STOP RUN.
   253 003100*
   254 003200* * *  * * * * * * * *  * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
* * * *
   255 004000*   BOJ (Beginning-Of-Job) does Housekeeping, Init READs,
etc.
   256
   257 004001 010-BOJ.
   258            DISPLAY "010-BOJ" ACCEPT WS-A.
   259            OPEN OUTPUT REPT-FILE.
   260            OPEN OUTPUT ERR-FILE.
   261            OPEN OUTPUT NEW-MAST.
   262            OPEN INPUT VENDORS.
   263            OPEN INPUT PAYABLES.
   264            ACCEPT RPT-HEAD-DATE FROM DATE.
   265            MOVE RPT-HEAD-DTE-YY TO RPT-HEAD3-YY.
   266            MOVE RPT-HEAD-DTE-MM TO RPT-HEAD3-MM.
   267            MOVE RPT-HEAD-DTE-DD TO RPT-HEAD3-DD.
   268            PERFORM 4000-RPT-HEAD.
   269            PERFORM 5000-ERR-HEAD.
   270            PERFORM 3000-READ-PAY.
   271            PERFORM 2000-READ-VEND.
   272            MOVE ZEROS TO PREV-PAY-VEND.
   273            MOVE VEND-ACCT-9 TO PREV-VEND-ACCT.
   274            MOVE ZEROS TO CALC-FIELDS.
   275            MOVE ZEROS TO PAGE-NUM.
   276 004010*
   277 004020*
   278 004900 010-EXIT.
   279 004910     EXIT.
   280
   281 004999* _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _  _ _ _  _ _ _ _
_ _ _ *
   282 005000*   MAIN-PROCESS is the primary function of this program
   283
   284 005001 020-MAIN-PROCESS.
   285            DISPLAY "020-MAIN-PROCESS" ACCEPT WS-A.
   286 005010*
   287 005020*
   288            IF (PREV-VEND-ACCT = VEND-ACCT) AND
   289               (VEND-ACCT = PAY-VEND)
   290                PERFORM 0040-CALC
   291                PERFORM 0050-UPDATE
   292                PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT
   293                PERFORM 3000-READ-PAY
   294            ELSE
   295                PERFORM 0070-SUBTOT.
   296            IF VEND-ACCT < PAY-VEND
   297                MOVE NEW-WS-REC TO NEW-REC
   298                WRITE NEW-REC
   299                PERFORM 2000-READ-VEND.
   300            IF VEND-ACCT > PAY-VEND
   301                PERFORM 6000-PRINT-ERR
   302                PERFORM 3000-READ-PAY.
   303            EXIT.

In the simplest possible scenario, we have one Vendor rercord



   304 005900*^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^ ^
^ ^ ^ *
   305 006000*    EOJ (End-Of-Job) performs final tasks,
   306 006001*        Prints Totals, Closes Files, etc.
   307 006002 030-EOJ.
   308            DISPLAY "030-EOJ" ACCEPT WS-A.
   309 006010*
   310 006020*
   311            PERFORM 0080-GR-TOT.
   312       *    PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT.
   313            CLOSE REPT-FILE.
   314            CLOSE ERR-FILE.
   315            CLOSE NEW-MAST.
   316            CLOSE VENDORS.
   317            CLOSE PAYABLES.
   318 006900 030-EXIT.
   319 006910            EXIT.
   320
   321        0040-CALC.
   322            DISPLAY "0040-CALC" ACCEPT WS-A.
   323            COMPUTE INV-TOT = PAY-AMOUNT + PAY-TAX.
   324            COMPUTE CALC-PCHS-TO-DT = PAY-AMOUNT +
VEND-PCHS-TO-DT.
   325            COMPUTE CALC-TAXS-TO-DT = PAY-TAX + VEND-TAXS-TO-DT.
   326            COMPUTE CALC-TOTAL-TOT = CALC-PCHS-TO-DT +
CALC-TAXS-TO-DT.
   327            ADD CALC-PCHS-TO-DT TO VEND-AMT-TOT.
   328            ADD CALC-TAXS-TO-DT TO VEND-TAX-TOT.
   329            ADD CALC-TOTAL-TOT TO VEND-TOTAL-TOT.
   330            COMPUTE GR-TOT-TAX = VEND-TAX-TOT + GR-TOT-TAX.
   331            COMPUTE GR-TOT-AMT = VEND-AMT-TOT + GR-TOT-AMT.
   332            COMPUTE GR-TOT-TOTAL = VEND-TOTAL-TOT + GR-TOT-AMT.
   333            EXIT.
   334        0050-UPDATE.
   335            DISPLAY "0050-UPDATE" ACCEPT WS-A.
   336            MOVE INV-TOT TO DET-TOT.
   337            MOVE PAY-INVOICE TO DET-INV-NO.
   338            MOVE PAY-AMOUNT TO DET-AMT.
   339            MOVE PAY-TAX TO DET-TAX.
   340            IF PREV-PAY-VEND = PAY-VEND
   341                MOVE SPACES TO DET-VEND
   342                MOVE DET-LINE TO REPORT-LINE
   343            ELSE
   344                MOVE PAY-VEND TO PREV-PAY-VEND
   345                MOVE VEND-ACCT-9 TO DET-NO
   346                MOVE VEND-NAME TO DET-NAME
   347                MOVE DET-LINE TO REPORT-LINE.
   348            EXIT.
   349        0070-SUBTOT.
   350            DISPLAY "0070-SUBTOT" ACCEPT WS-A.
   351            MOVE VEND-TAX-TOT TO DET2-TAX.
   352            MOVE VEND-AMT-TOT TO DET2-AMT.
   353            MOVE VEND-TOTAL-TOT TO DET2-TOT.
   354            MOVE VEND-ACCT-9 TO PREV-VEND-ACCT.
   355       *    MOVE DET2-LINE TO REPORT-LINE.
   356            PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT.
   357            MOVE ZEROS TO DET2-TAX.
   358            MOVE ZEROS TO DET2-AMT.
   359            MOVE ZEROS TO DET2-TOT.
   360            MOVE ZEROS TO VEND-TAX-TOT.
   361            MOVE ZEROS TO VEND-AMT-TOT.
   362            MOVE ZEROS TO VEND-TOTAL-TOT.
   363            MOVE ZEROS TO INV-TOT.
   364            EXIT.
   365        0080-GR-TOT.
   366            DISPLAY "0080-GR-TOT" ACCEPT WS-A.
   367            MOVE " *GRAND TOTAL BILL*" TO DET2-VEND.
   368            MOVE GR-TOT-TAX TO DET2-TAX.
   369            MOVE GR-TOT-AMT TO DET2-AMT.
   370            MOVE GR-TOT-TOTAL TO DET2-TOT.
   371            MOVE DET2-LINE TO REPORT-LINE.
   372            PERFORM 1000-PRINT-RPT.
   373            EXIT.
   374        1000-PRINT-RPT.
   375            DISPLAY "1000-PRINT-RPT" ACCEPT WS-A.
   376            IF RPT-LINE-CT > 54
   377                PERFORM 4000-RPT-HEAD.
   378            WRITE REPORT-LINE.
   379            MOVE SPACE TO REPORT-LINE.
   380            EXIT.
   381        2000-READ-VEND.
   382            DISPLAY "2000-READ-VEND" ACCEPT WS-A.
   383            READ VENDORS
   384                AT END MOVE "YES" TO EOF-SW-VEND
   385                       MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO VEND-REC.
   386            MOVE VEND-REC TO NEW-WS-REC.
   387            EXIT.
   388        3000-READ-PAY.
   389            DISPLAY "3000-READ-PAY" ACCEPT WS-A.
   390            READ PAYABLES
   391                AT END MOVE "YES" TO PAY-EOF
   392                       MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO PAY-REC.
   393            EXIT.
   394        4000-RPT-HEAD.
   395            DISPLAY "4000-RPT-HEAD" ACCEPT WS-A.
   396            ADD 1 TO PAGE-NUM.
   397            MOVE 8 TO RPT-LINE-CT.
   398            MOVE PAGE-NUM TO RPT-HEAD-PGE-NUM.
   399            WRITE REPORT-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD1.
   400            WRITE REPORT-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD2 AFTER ADVANCING 2
LINES.
   401            WRITE REPORT-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD3.
   402            WRITE REPORT-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD4 AFTER ADVANCING 2
LINES.
   403            WRITE REPORT-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD5.
   404            WRITE REPORT-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD6.
   405            EXIT.
   406        5000-ERR-HEAD.
   407            DISPLAY "5000-ERR-HEAD" ACCEPT WS-A.
   408            MOVE SPACES TO RPT-HEAD-PGE.
   409            MOVE RPT-HEAD-PGE-NUM TO PGE-NUM-HOLD.
   410            MOVE ZEROS TO RPT-HEAD-PGE-NUM.
   411            WRITE ERR-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD1.
   412            MOVE PGE-NUM-HOLD TO RPT-HEAD-PGE-NUM.
   413            MOVE ZEROS TO PGE-NUM-HOLD.
   414            MOVE "PAGE " TO RPT-HEAD-PGE.
   415            WRITE ERR-LINE FROM ERR-HEAD2 AFTER ADVANCING 2
LINES.
   416            WRITE ERR-LINE FROM RPT-HEAD3.
   417            WRITE ERR-LINE FROM ERR-HEAD4 AFTER ADVANCING 2
LINES.
   418            WRITE ERR-LINE FROM ERR-HEAD5.
   419            MOVE 8 TO ERR-LINE-CT.
   420            EXIT.
   421        6000-PRINT-ERR.
   422            DISPLAY "6000-PRINT-ERR" ACCEPT WS-A.
   423            IF ERR-LINE-CT > 54
   424                PERFORM 5000-ERR-HEAD.
   425            WRITE ERR-LINE FROM PAY-REC.
   426            EXIT.
```

### MF COBOL in Chicago

- **From:** "Mark Lewis" <mlewis@worldnetcorp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gvk86$kb6$1@remarQ.com>`

```
Dear Consultant,
                            My client is seeking a MicroFocus COBOL expert
for a 6+ month assignment in Chicago.

Candidate MUST have worked with versions 1,2,3 and 4 and have performed
upgrades in at least one of these versions.

Please send all qualified resumes, including rate to:

mlewis@worldnetcorp.com

Thanks and have a great day.

Mark Lewis
Worldnet Corporation
www.worldnetcorp.com
847-839-1900 x 18
```

<a id="t-620deaef04"></a>
## Thread: Experience w/ IBM Global Svcs? — 3 msgs

### Experience w/ IBM Global Svcs?

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gurnr$7ft$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Subject: Experience w/ IBM Global Svcs?

We have received notice that our company's entire IT operation is being
outsourced to IBM Global Services.  This includes operations in 4 North
American locations, and all functional areas -- data center operations,
networking, tech support, help desk, applications maintenance.  Has anyone had
a similar experience, particularly with IGS?

I'm 47 years old,have been with my current employer 14 years and wasn't ever
expecting to be looking for another job.  Relocation is not acceptable at this
point.

Slightly off topic: The last 3 years my job title has been Oracle DBA, and I
have been to several of the Oracle classes, but actual task assignments have
not allowed me to develop much skill in that area and I'm not comfortable
about presenting myself to a prospective employer as an Oracle DBA, though I
would like to persue that line.

Given the situation I find myself in, does anyone have any adivce?
```

#### ↳ Re: Experience w/ IBM Global Svcs?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7gvkob$lcm$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<7gurnr$7ft$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Well, I work for a different outsourcer, and if your location had been
bought by us, and you had the Oracle education you did have, you would
be "highly desirable".  Maybe they wouldn't see you as being ready to
design a terabyte datawarehouse, but they'd want you.

There is a demand for people with Oracle skills, so that looks good.

IBM just had a blowout quarter, so maybe they won't be doing any
layoffs.

Still, the specifics depend on the nature of the contract made between
your corporation and IGS.  Without knowing what's in that contract,
nothing can be said for sure.


Sincerely,
     Chris Osborne


Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com> wrote in message
news:7gurnr$7ft$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Subject: Experience w/ IBM Global Svcs?
>
> We have received notice that our company's entire IT operation is
being
> outsourced to IBM Global Services.  This includes operations in 4
North
> American locations, and all functional areas -- data center
operations,
> networking, tech support, help desk, applications maintenance.  Has
anyone had
> a similar experience, particularly with IGS?
>
> I'm 47 years old,have been with my current employer 14 years and
wasn't ever
> expecting to be looking for another job.  Relocation is not
acceptable at this
> point.
>
> Slightly off topic: The last 3 years my job title has been Oracle
DBA, and I
> have been to several of the Oracle classes, but actual task
assignments have
> not allowed me to develop much skill in that area and I'm not
comfortable
> about presenting myself to a prospective employer as an Oracle DBA,
though I
> would like to persue that line.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)
```

#### ↳ Re: Experience w/ IBM Global Svcs?

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37346416.1810@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<7gurnr$7ft$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote:
> We have received notice that our company's entire IT operation is being
> outsourced to IBM Global Services.  

Usually, IGS would offer employment to all employees of your company who
are part of the outsourced areas.  There might be salary adjustments to
compensate for any difference in benefits, and there might be a
guarantee of continued employment with IGS for some period of time if
that was negotiated by your company.  But IGS will need your systems
knowledge, at least for the 'intermediate-term', or they will not be
able to cost-effectively support your systems.  My advice - don't worry,
go with the flow...you will have ample opportunity to make an impression
on IGS mgmt, and to make decisions about your future.

Bob
```

```
1999-05-07 12:33:21 MEST

GNU-Cobol-Compiler project. Most of my
fellow-workers did not know about it,
so I rather spread the information a
little (Fujitsu-Cobol and AcuCobol
are more often quoted).

Here are some URLs:

http://acm.cs.umr.edu/~gnu-cobol/
http://www.gnu.org/software/cobol/

Apologies to those who already knew
about it.

On inspecting the pages I got
the impression that the project is in its
infancy - nothing what you can download next
week. However, I stay tuned.

Does anyone place a bet on when it will
be ready?

Herwig
```

<a id="t-43814b814d"></a>
## Thread: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me

- **From:** charlie.smith@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<926140889.1@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com>`

```
 -=> Quoting ptabour@worldnet.att.net to All <=-

 pt> From: "Paul Tabour" <ptabour@worldnet.att.net>

 pt> Hello all --

 pt> I also have a file conversion issue from MVS to WinNT.  My issue
 pt> involves a file with multiple record layouts containing binary
 and
 pt> COMP-3 data.  I would like to avoid massaging the data on the
 pt> mainframe, so this is what I would like to do: bring the file
 down
 pt> without translation, and use some utility to read it in.

 pt> My question is this: do any of you know of a product that does a
 pt> similar thing for NT?  Even if it doesn't take copybooks, I'd
 pt> appreciate information about any utility that can handle binary
 and
 pt> COMP-3 fields and multiple record layouts.

You don't need to worry about binary and COMP-3 data from the
 mainframe.

I'm in a similar situation - I'm doing mainframe (EBCDIC) to UNIX
(ASCII) and back to mainframe.

We've written our own codeset translator as a called / linked-to
module that can handle several different record definitions.  All we
do is pass and a unique identifier for each record, the input &
 output
codesets, which translation to perform and a couple of areas large
enough to hold the inbound and outbound buffers.

The translator then looks at the "NULL" record buffer (the values
loaded into a record type with the INITIALIZE) verb and if a
particular byte is either a space (ASCII X'20') or zero (ASCII
 X'30'),
we replace the character in the inbound buffer with it's equivalent
character in the outbound buffer.  If the it's not one of those 2
characters, just move the byte from inbound to outbound.

WARNING - I don't know what compiler you're using, but under
MicroFocus v3.? (and probably others) you must use caution with
 signed
numeric usage display fields - PIC 9(05).  MF does NOT initialize any
bytes with a sign unless you use a SET directive.

This is what we've done and it seems to work.  Hope this helps.

Charlie Smith


-----------------------------------------------------------------
Charlie Smith                      Smith Data Processing Services
csmit002@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com      The CyberSpace BBS - 1:231/992
Decatur Township Info Network      BBS/PPP/FAX --- 1 317 856 9020
-----------------------------------------------------------------

... Backup not found: (A)bort (R)etry (S)lap nearest innocent
 bystander.
```

<a id="t-2568d413b1"></a>
## Thread: Out-of-line?? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Out-of-line??

- **From:** "arez" <pingu@penguin33.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h13pq$ie4$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
hi,  can someone plz tell me wot

out-of-line and in-line programming is?????



most grateful



arez
```

#### ↳ Re: Out-of-line??

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37342338.54ADAD71@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7h13pq$ie4$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
It refers to the PERFORM verb. Basically, if  PERFORM is executing a
paragraph or a section, it is 'out of line'. If the code is contained
within a PERFORM...END-PERFORM construct, it is 'inline'.

*out of line *
       PERFORM some-para-or-section ...

*inline*
      PERFORM ...
           [optional statements]
      END-PERFORM



arez wrote:

> hi,  can someone plz tell me wot
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> arez
```

<a id="t-6f13be603f"></a>
## Thread: * New "Draft" of the Draft Standard being processed by J4 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### * New "Draft" of the Draft Standard being processed by J4

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h2pq2$8r8@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The J4 technical committee mailing list has just been notified that a new
draft version of the draft (notice a pattern here, i.e. DRAFT) Standard is
available.  I have seen the PDF version of this which has "hot-spot" or
"hot-link" capabilities so you can actually jump to cross-references and
pages from the index or table of contents. (Nifty and/or useful!)

I do not know how widely available this document (hard or soft-copy) is
being distributed, however, if you are interested (and want to find out for
yourself how/if you can get it), *and* you are in the US, then contact the
J4 chair (Don Schricker) at

   doncobol <at> mediaone.net

If you are outside the US, then check out the WG4 web page for information
on how to contact your own "national body" group.  I am getting errors when
I look for it today, but at least in the past, the WG4 web page was at:

   www.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc22/wg4

(If this doesn't work for you and you want to follow up on this outside the
US, I suggest that you contact your own national standards group, e.g. BSI,
DNI, ITSJ.)

NOTE WELL:
   When "requesting" this document, make certain that you ask for draft "CD
1.5" dated April of 1999.  Anything other than this is NOT the most current
J4 document.
```

## Thread: Need advise: Cobol or C++ — 4 msgs

### Need advise: Cobol or C++

- **From:** atrakic@polaris.umuc.edu
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h361t$nke$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Currently work with C++ in a small company.

Have an offer to go to bigger and better company but was offered to work with
COBOL.

What would you suggest me? I don't have any COBOL experience ...

Is COBOL going to dissapear? Anywhere i can read about the future of this
language?

Thanks,
Ado

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Need advise: Cobol or C++

- **From:** carloss940@aol.com (CarlosS940)
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990509014252.07039.00000525@ng-co1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7h361t$nke$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
No, it is impossible for COBOL  to disappear.

Carlos A. Santos            
E-mail to: CarlosS940@aol.com
Life is wonderful only if you know what it means! : )
```

#### ↳ Re: Need advise: Cobol or C++

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3736dcc0.2355154@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<7h361t$nke$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sun, 09 May 1999 05:22:06 GMT, atrakic@polaris.umuc.edu wrote:

>Currently work with C++ in a small company.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>What would you suggest me? I don't have any COBOL experience ...

I'd try and stay current on both.  But, if you have to choose, a lot
depends on what kind of programming you wish to do.

Both languages are very good and radically different.  Which means,
their particular strengths should be used for completely different
systems.

If you like business applications, go with COBOL.  If you prefer
utilities, architure and support systems featuring, go with C++.


>
>Is COBOL going to dissapear? Anywhere i can read about the future of this
>language?
>

COBOL has 60% of all source code worldwide, not including replications
(not counting copies).  That might give you an idea how much business
application software demand there is.  What do you think?



Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need advise: Cobol or C++

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1999-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990517153442.27481.00001576@ng-fs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3736dcc0.2355154@news.teo-computer.com>`

```

Obviously, COBOL will not disappear within the next couple of decades, 
possibly never.

OTOH, working in C seems like it would lead to more interesting fields of
work as opposed to creating A/R spreads in COBOL.

For example, a lot of the software written to control the experiment packages
on board the Mars Surveyor spacecraft (including the lander and orbiter
currently
enroute to Mars) is written in C, within an operation system called VxWorks.

Now that sounds like pretty interesting work to me, even if JPL is not
currently
running ads for applications programmers.  If I had it all to do over again,
I'd 
probably go back to school and become proficient in C while I had the chance
to enjoy it.

JC Jones
... who is counting the days until retirement.
```

---

<a id="t-3705a98836"></a>
## Thread: UK Cobol Programmer/Anaylst — 2 msgs

### UK Cobol Programmer/Anaylst

- **From:** "Tris Wines" <tris.wines@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

<a id="t-4cf569f890"></a>
## Thread: Numeric Group — 3 msgs

### Numeric Group

- **From:** "Steve Beattie" <steve@sbeattie.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h4rvl$jdf$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi,
I'm trying to define a numeric group in cobol that will have the same effect
as the following....

03  ws-current-date pic 9(6).
03 filler redefines ws-current-date.
    05 ws-current-yy  pic 9(2).
    05 ws-current-mm pic 9(2).
    05 ws-current-dd pic 9(2).

I'm not sure if it can be done, but something in the back of my mind says
I've seen it done something like this...

03 ws-current-date usage is display.
    05 ws-current-yy  pic 9(2).
    05 ws-current-mm pic 9(2).
    05 ws-current-dd pic 9(2).

The first example works fine, I just want to know if it can be done
something similar to the second example.

TIA, Steve.

---
Steve@sbeattie.removeme.freeserve.co.uk
http://www.sbeattie.freeserve.co.uk/index.html

To reply remove 'removeme' from email address.

Play Java Asteroids at the above site.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Group

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h4uj6$fmh@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7h4rvl$jdf$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
If you want both a group-item and all of its subordinate elementary items to
be available as "numeric" fields, then your first method is THE way to go.
It is easy to get a group item as alphanumeric and all the subordinate items
as numeric (which is what you actually always get  - by default) and is what
happens with the 2nd set of code that you have shown.  You can also -
sort-of - get a pseudo-group item as numeric and subordinate items as
alphanumeric by using reference modification of a USAGE DISPLAY numeric
elementary item, but in that case the subordinate items don't get a name -
which is OK for an occasional use, but not great for maintenance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Group

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gs3%2.15034$2Z4.1942@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7h4rvl$jdf$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
The first is fine, the second isn't: you haven't told it what to redefine.

You can however do:
01  MY-ITEM  PIC X(100).
01  REDEFINES MY-ITEM.
      05  MY-ITEM-2     PIC X(..)
...
..

Steve Beattie wrote in message <7h4rvl$jdf$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>...
>Hi,
>I'm trying to define a numeric group in cobol that will have the same
effect
>as the following....
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-d0c52a7e18"></a>
## Thread: TABLE -- The "reserved" word — 2 msgs

### TABLE -- The "reserved" word

- **From:** GRZNAR <grznar@omega.uta.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.3.96.990509172004.7368J-100000@omega.uta.edu>`

```

Does anybody know why TABLE is a reserved word in COBOL?  I mean, I know
what a table is, but I know of no syntactical use of the word "TABLE."
Am I missing something here?

John Grznar
```

#### ↳ Re: TABLE -- The "reserved" word

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h5maf$7v2@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.96.990509172004.7368J-100000@omega.uta.edu>`

```
Check out the "COMMUNICATION SECTION" support in the ANSI/ISO Standard.
Although few vendors currently support it, that is where "TABLE" is
reserved.
```

## Thread: RMcobol and NWclient32 — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RMcobol and NWclient32

- **From:** juanmaria@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h6gav$tda$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi group!

I've been running RMcobol 5.36 programs on Netware servers from
Win95 workstations using Microsoft's client for Netware with no
problems, but when I try the same programs with Novell's client32 I get
a 30.01 (DOS invalid function code) error every time the program tries
to perform an OPEN OUTPUT.

Since the latest versions of Novell Netware don't work properly without
Novell's client32 I find it a serious problem but I haven't found an
explanation/solution anywhere.

I'd be grateful if someone could give me some clues!.

Thanks in advance.
Juan Maria.


--== Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/ ==--
---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37392885.2137077@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<7h6gav$tda$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Hi.

What is the version of the Client32 that you are using.
What is the configuration of the ms-dosprompt you are using.
What is the version of the Netware server.
What is the service pack installed on the Win95 machine.

Have a look at the Microsoft site and search for the following file

"vrdrupd.exe "

it may help.


FF
```

- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00

```
client fro Netware.

> What is the version of the Netware server.
I've found this problem with NW 3.12 and NW 5, I've tried IPX with
ethernet 802.2, IPX with 802.3 and TCP/IP (NW5 only).

Actually I'm performing the tests from Win98 Workstations.

>
> "vrdrupd.exe "
I'll look it.

I could test RMcobol V6.07 yesterday evening and it worked fine, at
least when an OPEN OUTPUT was performed, so I'm thinking that RMcobol
v5.36 might call a DOS function which is not supported by client32. The
problem is that RMcobol 5.36 was an expensive package and i'ts hard to
tell a customer that he has to buy a new,and expensive too, version.

Thanks for your answer and I'm going to get the "vrdrupd.exe " file and
see if it aids me.

Saludos. Juan Maria.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RMcobol and NWclient32

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U5q_2.1551$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7h6gav$tda$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7h9f2a$fn$1@news.cerf.net> <7hbdr9$kng$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Here's dumb things I've done to generate run-time errors in the file system:

OPEN OUTPUT (an existing file)
OPEN OUTPUT (a keyed indexed file)  (needed to be existing, opened I-O)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RMcobol and NWclient32

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7henih$pcf$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7h6gav$tda$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7h9f2a$fn$1@news.cerf.net> <7hbdr9$kng$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <U5q_2.1551$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>Here's dumb things I've done to generate run-time errors in the file
system:
>
>OPEN OUTPUT (an existing file)
>OPEN OUTPUT (a keyed indexed file)  (needed to be existing, opened I-O)

So, the OUTPUT mode is what is actually causing it. Definitely sounds like
an access rights problem, could you do:

    WHOAMI
    RIGHTS
    FLAG <filename>

On the actual directory? And post me the results. Feel free to scramble
computerid and/or userids, that's not what I am looking for.

Are both RMCobol versions 32 bits windows?

Cheesle

>
>--
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RMcobol and NWclient32

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hfi2t$1fe$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7h6gav$tda$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7h9f2a$fn$1@news.cerf.net> <7hbdr9$kng$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <U5q_2.1551$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7henih$pcf$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

Cheesle wrote in message <7henih$pcf$1@news.cerf.net>...
>    WHOAMI
>    RIGHTS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
He might want to check that the directory exists as well. Open
output will only fail on a PC if the disk is full, the directory or disk
does not exist, or the file does exist and is protected.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<csg%2.2910$mj.76536@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7h6gav$tda$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7h9f2a$fn$1@news.cerf.net> <7hbdr9$kng$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <U5q_2.1551$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7henih$pcf$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Er, I was speaking of checking your COBOL program for logic errors in the
OPEN, not of doing network diagnostics.

Even veteran COBOLers screw up. (I did , just this week Wednesday. Tried to
access the '01' of a file not currently opened and was rewarded with S0C4
errors. Screwed around for an hour before I found it. Then I got a headache
after whupping myself upside the head for being so dumb.).
```

<a id="t-1e4a8308d2"></a>
## Thread: Direct quote from the butler contra report — 3 msgs

### Direct quote from the butler contra report

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3736C9EE.B3A60374@zip.com.au>`

```
Topics covered this month:
COBOL - A Natural Candidate for E-Business!
The Games People Play
On the Internet, Brand is Everything
Some Findings in the UK


COBOL - A Natural Candidate for E-Business!
===========================================

Sounds like heresy doesn't it. 
After all, in a culture that professes 'new 
is best', COBOL should have no right to make claims in 
e-business. The facts are rather different, however. 
Many large companies have a huge pool of COBOL-based 
applications that constitute their core business systems, 
and e-business means that these systems need to be 
integrated and connected to the outside world. As a 
matter of fact, this is the most pressing issue facing many
large companies. Forget about Java and XML, many IT managers 
would be happy if their applications could talk to each other 
and provide an interface to the outside world.

It just so happens that some of the highest profile e-business 
applications are based on COBOL server applications. Federal 
Express and Schwab have both utilised COBOL extensively for 
the application servers that do the actual work of processing 
data. There are in fact many other examples, and it is my 
belief that at the server end of these applications, COBOL, 
and particularly Object-Oriented COBOL, as supplied by 
vendors such as Micro Focus (now confusingly called Merant), 
will play a major role in allowing companies to migrate 
their applications into an e-business environment.

The alternative is too awful to contemplate. Rewriting 
applications in Java may give purists a feeling of 
euphoria, but in reality no one is doing this. Or if you 
feel really masochistic, you could try and rewrite them 
in C++.

Every large company has to start from where it is. This 
means taking old, arthritic applications and 'wrapping' 
them so they become a stable starting point for future 
developments. These applications also need to be integrated, 
and CORBA has proved to be particularly useful in this 
respect. The integration between COBOL and CORBA is now very 
mature, and equally importantly, there is a rich set of 
experience to draw upon.

It is a testament to the power of fashion 
in the IT industry that a language eminently suited to the 
development of business applications is ridiculed at the 
expense of one that is more suited to creating drivers 
for analogue to digital converters.

As far as I am concerned, there is no good reason not to use 
one of the contemporary versions of COBOL for developing the 
server end of e-business applications, and in reality many 
rganisations will. After all, Merant is growing at around 
40 per cent a year, so quite a few companies must be using 
its products.

As is usually the case in this industry, someone will spend 
years cobbling together a Java-based e-business, applying 
copious amounts of tape and string, and by the skin of their 
teeth get something that performs a useful business function; 
and we will all marvel at it. The fact that it could have 
been written in COBOL in a tenth of the time will be totally 
overlooked: after all, we must celebrate how rapidly we are 
advancing in this industry. We might not buy so much otherwise.

The problems associated with a wholesale adoption of COBOL are 
cultural, and largely based on prejudice. But it is simple. If 
you are a large company and you want to move forward from where 
you are without taking huge risks, COBOL is worth at least 
a look.
```

```
web enablement project designed to build electronic commerce functionality
into legacy systems.  Project teams will be based in New York and London.
```

<a id="t-78fd4bef37"></a>
## Thread: File formatting. — 3 msgs

### File formatting.

- **From:** "John" <nospam@nospam-please.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DoEZ2.3184$%x.2654@wards>`

```
I am writing a program in Cobol to convert an ASCII file into a formatted
file so that I can import the files into a database.

The original file has different length fields with fields in different
places - eg. NAME[Neil Capel]AGE[21]ADD1[------- etc.

I want to read a line at a time and then look at each character for [ and ].
Do I have to move the read line into, say -
 1 conv-line.
     3 1char  x.
     3 2char  x.
     3 3char  x.

etc.

and then look at each character?  Is there a better way?  PLEASE HELP.

Also I will need to search and replace - is it possible to replace end of
line chars with spaces?  How do I look for end of line chars.

Please e-mail me:

neilc@1sitedesigns.net
```

#### ↳ Re: File formatting.

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udFZ2.102$r14.11510@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<DoEZ2.3184$%x.2654@wards>`

```

John wrote in message ...
>I am writing a program in Cobol to convert an ASCII file into a formatted
>file so that I can import the files into a database.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I want to read a line at a time and then look at each character for
[ and ].
>  Is there a better way?  PLEASE HELP.

Yes.  Try the UNSTRING statement.  First define a numeric field to help with
this statment:   01  WS-POINT    PIC 999.    I also assume you would define
FIELD-NAME and FIELD-DATA there as well. Then in the procedure division:

MOVE 1 TO WS-POINT.

And within a loop or PERFORM statement:

IF IN-REC(WS-POINT:1) NOT = SPACE
  UNSTRING IN-REC DELIMITED BY "[" INTO FIELD-NAME POINTER WS-POINT
  UNSTRING IN-REC DELIMITED BY "]" INTO FIELD-DATA POINTER WS-POINT
   ... Code to process each field would go here.

>
>Also I will need to search and replace - is it possible to replace end of
>line chars with spaces?  How do I look for end of line chars.

Define your input file ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL and you won't have to.
Short lines will be padded out with spaces.  The IF statement checks to see
if you have reached the end.


Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: File formatting.

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-dejanews.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h7gcl$ir5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<DoEZ2.3184$%x.2654@wards>`

```
In article <DoEZ2.3184$%x.2654@wards>,
  "John" <nospam@nospam-please.com> wrote:
> The original file has different length fields with fields in different
> places - eg. NAME[Neil Capel]AGE[21]ADD1[------- etc.
>
> I want to read a line at a time and then look at each character for [
and ].

Here's another way to use the UNSTRING statement to analys=ze the
contents of the line.

 77  sub1                        VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(4).
 01  work-line                                       PIC X(100).
 01  my-table.
     05 my-item    OCCURS 3.
       10 my-word                                    PIC X(10).
       10 my-data                                    PIC X(50).
 01  myrec-rec.
     05 myrec-name                                   PIC X(15).
     05 myrec-age                                    PIC X(02).
     05 myrec-add1                                   PIC X(25).
*
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     MOVE 'NAME[Neil Capel]AGE[21]ADD1[---]'
       TO work-line
     UNSTRING work-line
       DELIMITED BY '[' OR ']'
       INTO my-word(1)
            my-data(1)
            my-word(2)
            my-data(2)
            my-word(3)
            my-data(3)
     PERFORM
       VARYING sub1 FROM 1 BY 1
       UNTIL sub1 > 3 OR
             my-word(sub1) = SPACE
       EVALUATE my-word(sub1)
       WHEN 'NAME'
         MOVE my-data(sub1)          TO myrec-name
       WHEN 'AGE'
         MOVE my-data(sub1)          TO myrec-age
       WHEN 'ADD1'
         MOVE my-data(sub1)          TO myrec-add1
       END-EVALUATE
     END-PERFORM
```

```
Debugging and Maintenance in OS/390.

Course covers debugging using SYSUDUMPs and LE CEEDUMPs and supports COBOL II
and the LE COBOL compilers. (No Abend-Aid or Dump Master: we do it the old
fashioned way by choice.)

Also covers subroutines, Linkage Editor, and the Program Binder. See our
website for details (http://www.trainersfriend.com).
```

## Thread: RMcobol and NWclient32 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RMcobol and NWclient32

- **From:** juanmaria@hotmail.com (juanmaria@hotmail.com)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc.1dcede5ad96100001dcede5a4d4b843c.4013e5d@city.magnet.at>`

```
Hi group!

I've been running RMcobol 5.36 programs on Netware servers from
Win95 workstations using Microsoft's client for Netware with no
problems, but when I try the same programs with Novell's client32 I get
a 30.01 (DOS invalid function code) error every time the program tries
to perform an OPEN OUTPUT.

Since the latest versions of Novell Netware don't work properly without
Novell's client32 I find it a serious problem but I haven't found an
explanation/solution anywhere.

I'd be grateful if someone could give me some clues!.

Thanks in advance.
Juan Maria.
```

```
respect. The integration between COBOL and CORBA is now very 
mature, and equally importantly, there is a rich set of 
experience to draw upon.

It is a testament to the power of fashion 
in the IT industry that a language eminently suited to the 
development of business applications is ridiculed at the 
expense of one that is more suited to creating drivers 
for analogue to digital converters.

As far as I am concerned, there is no good reason not to use 
one of the contemporary versions of COBOL for developing the 
server end of e-business applications, and in reality many 
rganisations will. After all, Merant is growing at around 
40 per cent a year, so quite a few companies must be using 
its products.

As is usually the case in this industry, someone will spend 
years cobbling together a Java-based e-business, applying 
copious amounts of tape and string, and by the skin of their 
teeth get something that performs a useful business function; 
and we will all marvel at it. The fact that it could have 
been written in COBOL in a tenth of the time will be totally 
overlooked: after all, we must celebrate how rapidly we are 
advancing in this industry. We might not buy so much otherwise.

The problems associated with a wholesale adoption of COBOL are 
cultural, and largely based on prejudice. But it is simple. If 
you are a large company and you want to move forward from where 
you are without taking huge risks, COBOL is worth at least 
a look.

Message-ID: <3736C9EE.B3A60374@zip.com.au>
Path:
magnet.at!newsfeed03.univie.ac.at!newscore.univie.ac.at!news-ge.switch.ch!newsfeed.mathworks.com!newsfeed.enteract.com!newsfeed.berkeley.edu!intgwpad.nntp.telstra.net!news1.optus.net.au!optus!newsfeed.zip.com.au!the-fly.zip.com.au!not-for-mail
```

<a id="t-d7a36e42d7"></a>
## Thread: Need advise: Cobol or C++ — 1 msgs

### Re: Need advise: Cobol or C++

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc.1dcede5a667700001dcede5a1ee3a249.4016204@city.magnet.at>`

```
On Sun, 09 May 1999 05:22:06 GMT, atrakic@polaris.umuc.edu wrote:

>Currently work with C++ in a small company.
>
>Have an offer to go to bigger and better company but was offered to work
with
>COBOL.
>
>What would you suggest me? I don't have any COBOL experience ...

I'd try and stay current on both.  But, if you have to choose, a lot
depends on what kind of programming you wish to do.

Both languages are very good and radically different.  Which means,
their particular strengths should be used for completely different
systems.

If you like business applications, go with COBOL.  If you prefer
utilities, architure and support systems featuring, go with C++.


>
>Is COBOL going to dissapear? Anywhere i can read about the future of this
>language?
>

COBOL has 60% of all source code worldwide, not including replications
(not counting copies).  That might give you an idea how much business
application software demand there is.  What do you think?



Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

```
Debugging and Maintenance in OS/390.

Course covers debugging using SYSUDUMPs and LE CEEDUMPs and supports COBOL
II
and the LE COBOL compilers. (No Abend-Aid or Dump Master: we do it the old
fashioned way by choice.)

Also covers subroutines, Linkage Editor, and the Program Binder. See our
website for details (http://www.trainersfriend.com).
```

- **Message-ID:** `<fc.1dcede5a1c8a00001dcede5afd953dd0.4017dfb@city.magnet.at>`

```
I am writing a program in Cobol to convert an ASCII file into a formatted
file so that I can import the files into a database.

The original file has different length fields with fields in different
places - eg. NAME[Neil Capel]AGE[21]ADD1[------- etc.

I want to read a line at a time and then look at each character for [ and
].
Do I have to move the read line into, say -
 1 conv-line.
     3 1char  x.
     3 2char  x.
     3 3char  x.

etc.

and then look at each character?  Is there a better way?  PLEASE HELP.

Also I will need to search and replace - is it possible to replace end of
line chars with spaces?  How do I look for end of line chars.

Please e-mail me:

neilc@1sitedesigns.net
```

<a id="t-c26d0cdcce"></a>
## Thread: File formatting. — 1 msgs

### Re: File formatting.

- **From:** warrenp123@netdoor123.com (warrenp123@netdoor123.com)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc.1dcede5a849400001dcede5afd953dd0.4018ba6@city.magnet.at>`

```

John wrote in message ...
>I am writing a program in Cobol to convert an ASCII file into a formatted
>file so that I can import the files into a database.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I want to read a line at a time and then look at each character for
[ and ].
>  Is there a better way?  PLEASE HELP.

Yes.  Try the UNSTRING statement.  First define a numeric field to help
with
this statment:   01  WS-POINT    PIC 999.    I also assume you would define
FIELD-NAME and FIELD-DATA there as well. Then in the procedure division:

MOVE 1 TO WS-POINT.

And within a loop or PERFORM statement:

IF IN-REC(WS-POINT:1) NOT = SPACE
  UNSTRING IN-REC DELIMITED BY "[" INTO FIELD-NAME POINTER WS-POINT
  UNSTRING IN-REC DELIMITED BY "]" INTO FIELD-DATA POINTER WS-POINT
   ... Code to process each field would go here.

>
>Also I will need to search and replace - is it possible to replace end of
>line chars with spaces?  How do I look for end of line chars.

Define your input file ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL and you won't have to.
Short lines will be padded out with spaces.  The IF statement checks to see
if you have reached the end.


Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

<a id="t-8fe73418ed"></a>
## Thread: File formatting. — 1 msgs

### Re: File formatting.

- **From:** emilne@my-dejanews.com (emilne@my-dejanews.com)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc.1dcede5aeab000001dcede5afd953dd0.401b59a@city.magnet.at>`

```
In article <DoEZ2.3184$%x.2654@wards>,
  "John" <nospam@nospam-please.com> wrote:
> The original file has different length fields with fields in different
> places - eg. NAME[Neil Capel]AGE[21]ADD1[------- etc.
>
> I want to read a line at a time and then look at each character for [
and ].

Here's another way to use the UNSTRING statement to analys=ze the
contents of the line.

 77  sub1                        VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(4).
 01  work-line                                       PIC X(100).
 01  my-table.
     05 my-item    OCCURS 3.
       10 my-word                                    PIC X(10).
       10 my-data                                    PIC X(50).
 01  myrec-rec.
     05 myrec-name                                   PIC X(15).
     05 myrec-age                                    PIC X(02).
     05 myrec-add1                                   PIC X(25).
*
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     MOVE 'NAME[Neil Capel]AGE[21]ADD1[---]'
       TO work-line
     UNSTRING work-line
       DELIMITED BY '[' OR ']'
       INTO my-word(1)
            my-data(1)
            my-word(2)
            my-data(2)
            my-word(3)
            my-data(3)
     PERFORM
       VARYING sub1 FROM 1 BY 1
       UNTIL sub1 > 3 OR
             my-word(sub1) = SPACE
       EVALUATE my-word(sub1)
       WHEN 'NAME'
         MOVE my-data(sub1)          TO myrec-name
       WHEN 'AGE'
         MOVE my-data(sub1)          TO myrec-age
       WHEN 'ADD1'
         MOVE my-data(sub1)          TO myrec-add1
       END-EVALUATE
     END-PERFORM
```

## Thread: Re(2): File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re(2): File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (rtwolfe@flexus.com)
- **Date:** 1999-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc.1deb005acea500001deb005ad91bb402.401a5db@city.magnet.at>`

```
Laura Sabourin <laura@ragdolls.net> wrote:

>Did anyone reply to this ? I have a similar problem but we don't even
have the
>code from the COBOL systems which has produced the files which we would
like to
>use to populate an ACCESS database. Anyone know of a PC/DOS based file
dump that
>I could use to figure out the file out ?

Laura:

Please forgive me for holding my nose while providing this advice, but
it is very important for me to be completely honest with you.

"Hode on dow whide I grab by node"  ;-)

Ok...the answer you seek might be found at:

http://www.datajunction.com/

Data Junction has tools to extract data from various types of COBOL
files and automatically convert them to other files types (such as
Microsoft Axes Files).

Before you go out and buy a copy of Data Junction, you should also be
aware of the following:

A.  It is highly likely that you will need to know the record layout;
and

B.  I also suspect that you will need to know the specific compiler
which was used to compile the COBOL program which you have been using
to date.

...and now for the reason I hold my nose...

Unless you have somewhat "smallish" file sizes, I suspect that you
will not be happy using Access considering that you came from COBOL.
I know that this may sound completely backwards, but if you do have
large files or do a tremendous amount of file I/O, going from COBOL to
Access is kind of like trading in your Ferrari for a 1965 Plymouth
Valiant.

(my own humble opinion)





>
>Paul Tabour wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

involves a

information

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Bob Wolfe, flexus
```

<a id="t-09ce89eadb"></a>
## Thread: What is TSO/JCL? — 33 this month / 47 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** jerrygas@aol.com (JERRYGAS)
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
         I have heard that it works in conjunction with COBOL and there is
on-line training for it. Anybody know how to find it? I couldn't find any books
on it at the bookstore.        Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37398177.C6567562@email.ais.unc.edu>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
TSO stands for Time Share Option.
JCL stands for Job Control Language.

As an application programmer (on a mainframe), you don't really need to
know very much about TSO.  ISPF is used with TSO and is akin to DOS for
a PC.  It would be helpful to know ISPF commands.  It is nothing you
really need to "learn."  It is pretty simple stuff and you should be
able to pick it up within a day or two of using it.  There are other
things like CLIST, REXX, DFSORT, and FILE-AID you could learn to
strengthen your MF skills.  CLIST and REXX are programming languages
which are used in a variety of different ways.  FILE-AID is a powerful
utility for manipulating files.  DFSORT is another utility.

JCL is a very unfriendly language - until you get the hang of it.  It is
used to run most anything on the mainframe.  It would be quite helpful
if you had a good command of JCL.  



JERRYGAS wrote:
> 
>          I have heard that it works in conjunction with COBOL and there is
> on-line training for it. Anybody know how to find it? I couldn't find any books
> on it at the bookstore.        Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990513100338.17404.00000058@ng-fe1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37398177.C6567562@email.ais.unc.edu>`

```
 Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu> writes ...

>TSO stands for Time Share Option.
>JCL stands for Job Control Language.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>able to pick it up within a day or two of using it.  

This mind set really frosts me. Yes, lots of ISPF is pretty simple stuff, but
there is a lot of sophisticated stuff in there, too. All too often management
has the attitude that programmers new to OS/390 don't need ISPF training. But
when I teach classes I find most programmers woefully inadequate in their ISPF
skills.

For example, few programmers know all the features of even such basic skills as
'find' and 'change' commands much less the techniques of more than 2 split
screens, appending entries to a dslist, and so on. These simply are not the
kinds of skills you find by spending a few hours playing with ISPF: you simply
wouldn't even think to ask questions that would lead you to these techniques.

This is why I created my "Advanced ISPF" course: to do a fast and thorough run
through of all the useful features as well as some of the advanced features of
ISPF, for experienced ISPF users. Sometimes management will pay for an
'Advanced' class where they will not pay for an 'Introduction' class, even
though the 'Introduction' class is what a person may really need.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374018F6.960E09E6@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<37398177.C6567562@email.ais.unc.edu> <19990513100338.17404.00000058@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
> This mind set really frosts me. Yes, lots of ISPF is pretty simple stuff, but
> there is a lot of sophisticated stuff in there, too. All too often management
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> though the 'Introduction' class is what a person may really need.

Perhaps I don't understand enough of ISPF to gauge its complexity, but
I've used overlays, column shifts, and searches between labels within a
week of first using ISPF.  I read the directions and there doesn't seem
to be much to it.  Most of the team I worked with was pretty competent
as well.  I'm curious what topics would be covered in "Advanced ISPF". 
I don't understand how ISPF could be considered sophisticated in
comparison to Assembler, CICS, IDMS, IMS, DB2, COBOL, and JCL.  It took
me 3 months to get comfortable with JCL.  I used IMS for 6 months and I
still don't feel comfortable with it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990517210448.04826.00001750@ng138.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<374018F6.960E09E6@hotmail.com>`

```
MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com> writes ...

>
>Perhaps I don't understand enough of ISPF to gauge its complexity, but
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>as well.  I'm curious what topics would be covered in "Advanced ISPF". 

First, congratulations. My experience in the class room is that most
programmers who have worked with ISPF for five years or more still don't have a
clue about the kinds of techniques you mention. Either they just don't bother
to keep up with changes, they don't have (make) time to learn, or they are
somewhat lazy. There are exceptions, of course.

So first, I focus on catching experienced people up with facilities introduced
since about ISPF 3.1. If you didn't know you could "do that", even though
you've been using IPSF every workday for five years, then it's "advanced" to
you. 

That was my earlier point: there are a lot of productivity techniques that even
experienced people aren't aware of (even though they should be); many such
people would even want to use these techniques, if they new they existed; using
the rubric of "Advanced ISPF" you might get both their attention and management
funding to have a 2 day intensive class to make their folks more productive.

I've always been wary of the term "advanced" because it means such widely
different things to different people. If everybody could, or would, take the
time to read the relevant IBM documentation, and if they had the time to "play
with" the products, to test and deepen their understanding, I'd be out of work.
But what we trainers do is the synthesis, organizing the information for
learning (instead of just for reference) and provide relevant hands-on
experience through the course labs. In this way, a person can gain as much
understanding in, say, two days in a class as he or she could in maybe five to
ten days reading books and trying to make up labs on their own.

In terms, then, of topics that are not necessarily truly "advanced" (at least
in some people's eyes) but that might help programmers use ISPF more
effectively, I would include at least these:

* referral lists
* saving lists of members or data set names
* appending data set names to 3.4 lists
* named screens and related commands
* various command retrieval techniques
* basic edit macros

>I don't understand how ISPF could be considered sophisticated in
>comparison to Assembler, CICS, IDMS, IMS, DB2, COBOL, and JCL.  It took
>me 3 months to get comfortable with JCL.  I used IMS for 6 months and I
>still don't feel comfortable with it.

I agree. ISPF isn't "sophisticated" relative to those much richer products and
tools. But again I am looking at things in a relative manner. The vast majority
of applications programmers simply don't know how to use ISPF effectively,
especially the newer capabilities.

Over the last two years, my observations on the usenet groups has been that the
folks who communicate on them regularly are the brightest and the best: they
are always searching for (and sharing) deeper understanding, new techniques,
and better ways to do things. In some areas, I can help, although there always
seems to be someone who knows more than I do. In some areas, especially writing
applications programs using LE services, I have tried to maintain a leading
edge level of understanding. In areas such as ISPF skills, I can help, but it's
usually a different audience than you find subscribing to newgroups.

That's all.

Regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37416850.C6786120@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<374018F6.960E09E6@hotmail.com> <19990517210448.04826.00001750@ng138.aol.com>`

```
> That was my earlier point: there are a lot of productivity techniques that even
> experienced people aren't aware of (even though they should be); many such
> people would even want to use these techniques, if they new they existed; using
> the rubric of "Advanced ISPF" you might get both their attention and management
> funding to have a 2 day intensive class to make their folks more productive.

It's important to have a how-to list, because people forget what they
don't use.  Let's say the class shows the very useful feature on how to
use exclude to make editing easier.   You start using it, and remember
the FLIP command, but not what it's called.  Try drilling down the help
command to find FLIP.  It's not easy.

Or edit a file with a string in columns 1-10, by moving columns 8-10 to
9-11.  (use bounds command then ")" as a line command).   Forget the
ZOOM or SHOWPROC command?  How do we find them?   And TSO doesn't come
with a single standard sysout viewer.

I hate the fact that I have to type in KEYS a dozen places, but I do
always change the first 12 Fkeys to as follows:
F1 . . .  HELP       
F2 . . .  start      
F3 . . .  EXIT       
F4 . . .  return     
F5 . . .  RFIND      
F6 . . .  RCHANGE    
F7 . . .  UP         
F8 . . .  DOWN       
F9 . . .  SWAP next  
F10  . .  LEFT       
F11  . .  RIGHT      
F12  . .  retrieve   

The F12 is essential.  I didn't know about start & swap next until
recently.  I had to give up SWAP, which I like, but having more sessions
is more valuable.

Most shops default semicolon to a delimiter, and I always do.  But how
do we turn it off for a command to use it in a search?  Do people
understand the colon?  =2;:file

Certainly they need to know pictures:   c p'<' p'>' all nx
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373CDF12.8A9679A0@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <37398177.C6567562@email.ais.unc.edu>`

```


Matthew Son wrote:
> 
> TSO stands for Time Share Option.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> really need to "learn."  It is pretty simple stuff and you should be
...
  Time Sharing Option used to be an  "Option" -- hasn't been for years,
but the name persists.  TSO is one of the main interactive interfaces to
MVS for programming developement (there are a number of other
interactive interfaces, but they are used more often for interactive
applications).  It used to be possible to run TSO from character-mode
terminals and maybe still is, but I have not seen it used that way in
years.  It is normally used from a 3270-capable device, which is a
capable of full-screen, fully buffered I/O, with hardware support for
subdividing the screen into fields (to restrict what part of the display
can be changed by the user for input fields), mostly used for text but
with occasional use of limited raster or vector graphics. 
  TSO by itself (not ISPF) bears some superficial resemblance to DOS,
more like a DOS window under Windows, in that it has a set of standard
"commands" you can key in and execute one at a time, but the
"environment" that you can pass from one command to the next is vast by
comparison with PC DOS, the scripting languages (CLIST and REXX), unlike
DOS BAT files, are full-fledged programming languages, and the
protections to prevent the [ordinary or non sysprog] user from
deliberately or accidentally causing damage to the underlying operating
system are absolute.  
  However, hardly anyone these days (except a sysprog in an emergency
situation) runs in stand-alone TSO anymore.  Almost everyone executes an
IBM application called ISPF/PDF under TSO, which provides a full-screen
user interface which it would be more accurate to compare with windows
than with DOS (sort of like Windows without the glitzy, and expensive,
graphics).  The user is guided via "panels" containing menus, entry
fields, pull-down menu lists, help panels and help pop-up windows
through all the various functions required for program development in
the MVS world. There are also some ISPF commands, although the one most
commonly used is a "fast-path" command to quickly jump to another dialog
panel.  
  The full-screen editor in ISPF puts MicroSoft PC text editors to shame
(in fact, there are vendors who have ported the ISPF editor user
interface to Windows platforms); and if you find some "feature" lacking,
there is an editor API you can use to extend the editor's function by
designing your own commands, which can be implemented in the language of
your choice (CLIST, REXX, Assembly, COBOL, PL/I, Fortran, C++).  ISPF is
itself extendable as well.  You can write your own panel dialogs and
ISPF "commands", and again the logic can be implemented in essentially
any language.
  Although in principle you can execute almost any program from a TSO
session, things which are expected to take a long time (like a compile
of a huge program) would normally be submitted to run as a batch job in
the background. 
    Job Control Language is used to control the sequenced execution of
programs in a non-interactive (batch job) environment in MVS, the files
or data sets which are to be used during the execution of those
programs, and the routing of any "printed" reports generated by the
batch "job".  There is some limited ability to make execution of a
program dependent on success or failure of earlier programs in the same
job, or to terminate the entire job on certain errors; but the logic
capabilities of JCL are very limited.  Based on empirical evidence,
experienced JCL coders are a rarity.  Most commonly, functional JCL is
cloned and adapted; and redundant, questionable, or unnecessary
specifications which don't cause obvious problems tend to be retained
without full understanding.  In one sense JCL is simple -- there are
only about 10 statement types, and only 3 or 4 of these are heavily
used.  Unfortunately the lists of possible operands and their
interactions run 100's of pages, and keyword, positional, and positional
keyword parameters are indiscriminantly mixed in a way designed to
confuse and confound.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373D2CB8.C7E8DCD4@att.net>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <37398177.C6567562@email.ais.unc.edu> <373CDF12.8A9679A0@acm.org>`

```
(not a response to Joel's useful post, but a general comment on this
thread)

It's probably a good idea to keep (or place) in mind that what we call
"MVS", or even "OS/390", began life as "OS/360" and "OS/360" was
designed & optimized for heavy batch work. The initial versions did not
include TSO (or any other interactive service I can recall), which hit
the marketplace circa '67-'68 (I'm sure there are folks on the NG who
can be specific on this point). It took IBM a looong time to develop an
interactive/online orientation. I first encountered ISPF in 1976, or so,
and I believe this was an early version.

IIRC, the early versions of TSO were enormous pigs and didn't catch on
quickly in the user community. It took IBM several iterations and lots
of money to get an acceptable version (kind of like Windows, now that I
think about it <g>).

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hQ3_2.1716$Yy4.43533@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
I missed the question, but TSO is a datacomm product for IBM mainframes,
kind of like "the C prompt" of MS-DOS. A lot of development software is
written for "the TSO environment." (Most common: ISPF)

JCL is Job Control Language, also for IBM mainframes. It's a lot like MS-DOS
batch files or Unix scripts, except JCL has all the user-friendliness
removed.


You can try some of the resources at http://www.mvshelp.com if you are
interested in pursuing this further.
```

```
some time with it.  Any environment where JCL is used will have a JCL
checker.  Play with it, work with it and learn from it.  C or C++ or
Java or Assembler aren't terribly 'user friendly' but people manage to
muddle through.

If you really are a programmer you should be able to learn.  Just pull
up your socks and get on with it.

they?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00

```
In article <373ae367.70742592@news.uswest.net>,
Dave Richards <dprichards@uswest.net> wrote:
>So take some time to learn it.

What *are* you, some kind of manager?  So allocate some time so it can be
learned!

>Nothing is 'user hostile' if you spend
>some time with it.

Patently absurd.  The amount of time one spends influences one's
familiarity with a situation, not the situation's friendliness or
hostility... or are you saying that hostility is a matter of
interpretation only?

> Any environment where JCL is used will have a JCL
>checker.

This is not my experience... then again, I've worked in shops that don't
have FileAid or ABENDAid, either, and this has been within the past three
years.

>Play with it, work with it and learn from it.

If the company wants you to learn something then the company allocates
time/money for it... one gets what one pays for, remember?

>C or C++ or
>Java or Assembler aren't terribly 'user friendly' but people manage to
>muddle through.

Oh boy, just what I want... a bunch of 'muddled through' JCL controlling
the nightly batch runs!  Dear me, Mr Richards, this is not for an 8088
with a NEC V20 speedup chip... these are the payroll runs that cut *your*
checks!

>
>If you really are a programmer you should be able to learn.  Just pull
>up your socks and get on with it.

... and if you *can* do such things then I suggest going the Independent
Route as quickly as posible... so one might earn what one is worth.

>
>If anyone could do this stuff no one would really need us, now would
>they?

Who am 'us'... and who be 'they'?

DD

>
>>Next to JCL, MS-DOS batch language and Unix scripts *are* user-friendly.
```
