###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** dprichards@uswest.net (Dave Richards)
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373b3a23.1233049@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7hbr7u$r1k$1@news.usf.edu> <T5q_2.1550$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <373ae367.70742592@news.uswest.net> <NKB_2.112$Ku4.6206@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
You make all this sound like JCL is the company's way of penalizing
employees who have better things to do like writing COBOL or taking
coffee breaks.  

Wrong.  There is more to being a programmer than just writing COBOL
or, God forbid, a tough language.

>>So take some time to learn it.
>
>What *are* you, some kind of manager?  So allocate some time so it can be
>learned!

I have been a Junior Programmer, Programmer/Analyst (batch and real
time), Senior Programmer/Analyst, Systems Analyst, Senior Network
Analyst, and, yes, a manager.  I didn't start out knowing everything I
needed any more than you or anyone else did.

I started writing COBOL code in 1969, right out of college and I write
it today, with only minor time taken out for learning other, unrelated
skills.  It's like learning anything else: it takes time and patience,
mentoring is nice if there's a mentor around, but it can be learned by
anyone with the aptitude to write tight, efficient software.

And above all, it can't be learned without wanting to learn it.  I
taught myself DOS JCL (that's IBM mainframe DOS on a System/360-30)
and, when it became necessary, OS JCL on a 360-65 running MFTII.  I
also taught myself all the system utilities I needed as I needed them
and I taught myself IBM mainframe Assembler because it looked like
fun.  None of these were 'user friendly.'  In fact, the term hadn't
been invented yet.  I taught them to myself because there wasn't
anyone else with the time to teach them to me.  I worked weekends and
an occasional evening.  

Why?  Because it was my job to learn them, not because it was my
employer's job to teach them.  Because they were survival skills, like
knowing how to drive a car or balance a checkbook or talk to a
dangerous man with a gun.   Because it's my career and there are too
many good programmers with superior skills and knowledge willing to
work longer hours for less money.  Because I always knew that I wasn't
irreplacable and if I didn't take responsibility for my knowledge base
no one else would.

BTW, for the first several years I worked as a COBOL programmer there
was no such thing as a cross-reference dictionary.  We managed to do
the job despite the lack of 'friendly' tools.

>>Nothing is 'user hostile' if you spend
>>some time with it.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, patently true.  One perceives of something as hostile because they
don't understand it or afraid of it.  JCL isn't intuitive but it is
efficient and concise and despite what you may think, it really isn't
out to get you.

>> Any environment where JCL is used will have a JCL
>>checker.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>years.

JCL should be built while the application code is being tested.  It
doesn't just materialize the night before the application goes into
production.  Plenty of time to build and test it.  No JCL checker?
Well, just submit the job and let the OS tell you where the problem
is.  Don't be afraid to test.  It won't hurt you.

Something else you may want to try.  Find the best JCL mechanic in the
shop and ask for help.  Trade your knowledge for theirs.  You probably
have skills they don't.  Don't try to work in a vacuum.

>
>>Play with it, work with it and learn from it.
>
>If the company wants you to learn something then the company allocates
>time/money for it... one gets what one pays for, remember?

It's your career.  You sound like it's the company's responsibility to
make you marketable.

Grow up.  Companies will give you the minimum resources you need to do
their job.  No company will give you more than they think you need
because (1) it costs them money and any money they spend on you is
money they can't spend on themselves, and (2) the more you know the
easier it is for you to leave, taking all that company-funded
knowledge with you.

>
>>C or C++ or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>checks!

JCL isn't like C or Assembler.  There is very little logic involved.
Except for condition code testing it's all straight-line 'code'.  I'd
be a lot more worried about application software written by someone
who thinks JCL is too tough.

By the way, the V20 was NEC's equivalent to the Intel 8088.
Originally it ran at 4.77 Mhz, like the 8088, but later versions would
run at 4.77 and 8.0 Mhz, generally through the activation of a toggle
switch or some such.  I don't believe there was a 'speedup chip'
involved.  I believe it was all inherent in the chip, although I could
be wrong.  I have one in the basement if you would like it.  You pay
shipping.

>>
>>If you really are a programmer you should be able to learn.  Just pull
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Route as quickly as posible... so one might earn what one is worth.

I prefer the relative stability of working for someone.  Being an
independant contractor takes too much additional time and resources to
please me.  I work to live and I give up my free time very grudgingly.
The people I know who do it well are lucratively rewarded but they
spend a great deal of uncompensated time traveling, searching out
contracts or managing themselves as a business.  I sat down once with
a former coworker who went the indie route to discuss the financial
aspects of his choice.  On a per-hour basis he didn't make much more
than I did, but I had a hell of a lot more free time and a lot less
stress.

>>
>>If anyone could do this stuff no one would really need us, now would
>>they?
>
>Who am 'us'... and who be 'they'?

'Us'  are you and me and all the other programmers out there.  (Duh)
'They' are the people who pay your salary.  Only a fool would pay
someone to do something they have the time, talent and resources to do
themself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cW_2.253$Ku4.13706@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <373ae367.70742592@news.uswest.net> <NKB_2.112$Ku4.6206@iad-read.news.verio.net> <373b3a23.1233049@news.uswest.net>`

```
In article <373b3a23.1233049@news.uswest.net>,
Dave Richards <dprichards@uswest.net> wrote:
>You make all this sound like JCL is the company's way of penalizing
>employees who have better things to do like writing COBOL or taking
>coffee breaks.  

This is what you may be hearing, granted, but this is *not* how I attempt
to 'make it sound'... but let's see.

>
>Wrong.  There is more to being a programmer than just writing COBOL
>or, God forbid, a tough language.

Then there should be more to the payscale to match this... or are you
asking for freebies?

>
>>>So take some time to learn it.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Analyst, and, yes, a manager.

Bingo... I tell ya, I can smell 'em.

>I didn't start out knowing everything I
>needed any more than you or anyone else did.

I do not believe this claim was made, either... but onwards.

[snippage of history]

>I
>also taught myself all the system utilities I needed as I needed them
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>employer's job to teach them.

Ahhhhh... but there is a difference between your learning them and your
using them for your employer.  If your employer is not paying you for the
skill then I would say that the employer is unethical in asking you to
have the skill *unless* the employer is willing to pay for your acquiring
it.

A person who can 'do something' has a certain value.  A person who can 'do
something' *and* learn to do 'something else' has, in my opinion, a
greater value.  An employer who expects greater value in return for no
increase in compensation is, to me, as unethical as an employee who
expects a paycheck for doing No Work.

> Because they were survival skills, like
>knowing how to drive a car or balance a checkbook or talk to a
>dangerous man with a gun.

And, of course, your employer frequently asked you to do these things as a
part of your job, right?  Utter bosh... excuse me, make that 'Utter bosh,
Mr Manager, Sir'.

>Because it's my career and there are too
>many good programmers with superior skills and knowledge willing to
>work longer hours for less money.  Because I always knew that I wasn't
>irreplacable and if I didn't take responsibility for my knowledge base
>no one else would.

Once again, there's a difference between learning something and then using
that learning for someone who does not pay.  Here's an example:

A secretary has a job which pays $x.  He falls in love with a woman of
Hispanic descent and *in his spare time* learns Spanish so that they might
speak to each other.

One day, while on his lunch-break, his manager walks past while he is
speaking to his wife on the pay-phone; later that week, invoking the
'other duties as assigned' part of the job-description the manager
joyously announces that this fellow will now function as a bilingual
secretary... for the same salary.

Calll this A and compare it to B: the manager walks past in the lunch-room
and sees the fellow, nose buried in his 'Learn Spanish in 21 Days' book.
The manager then offers a tuition rebate if the secretary passes the
course with a grade above a certain average and offers a job-change to
Official Multilingual Secretary (with a $y% increase in salary) upon
completion.

I see you as looking at A and saying 'This Is A Good Thing'... and I say
that it is not, that B is a Good Thing.. *especially* since the skill we
speak of here, JCL, has *no* application outside of a mainframe computer
environment which is being used to Make A Company More Money.

>
>BTW, for the first several years I worked as a COBOL programmer there
>was no such thing as a cross-reference dictionary.  We managed to do
>the job despite the lack of 'friendly' tools.

Oh, for the Oldene Dayse, when a Coder could Code Code such as *ten*
Coders cannot Code today!

>
>>>Nothing is 'user hostile' if you spend
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't understand it or afraid of it.

Quite right, of course... once one understands or is no longer afraid then
there's *nothing* hostile about that methane atmosphere of Jupiter,
right?... or are you saying that this atmosphere is not a 'something'?

You may have been a coder, Mr Richards, but you seem to have replaced the
rigorous logic of a Programmer with the banalities of ManagementSpeak.

>JCL isn't intuitive but it is
>efficient and concise and despite what you may think, it really isn't
>out to get you.

To be 'hostile' is not the same as being 'antagonistic', I believe.

>
>>> Any environment where JCL is used will have a JCL
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>production.  Plenty of time to build and test it.  No JCL checker?

That's right.. no JCL checker, contrary to your assertion above.

>Well, just submit the job and let the OS tell you where the problem
>is.  Don't be afraid to test.  It won't hurt you.

That's right.. don't be afraid of a typo of, say, a 'B' for an 'N' so that
your job is not updating all the files for Duluth instead of Des Moines...
after all, the Manager won't be called in to fix it!

>
>Something else you may want to try.  Find the best JCL mechanic in the
>shop and ask for help.  Trade your knowledge for theirs.  You probably
>have skills they don't.  Don't try to work in a vacuum.

You mean the guy with the big, long line outside of his cubicle who also
has a full-time Job to do?  What happens when one finally gets to the head
of the line and a Manager comes storming in, bellowing about a job that's
updating Duluth instead of Des Moines?

>
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>make you marketable.

No, I make it sound like it is the company's responsibility to pay for
what it demands be done... a radical concept, granted.

>
>Grow up.

I thought that expecting mutuality between Employer and Employee *was*
being 'grown up'... guess I was wrong.

>Companies will give you the minimum resources you need to do
>their job.

.. and they will get *exactly* what they pay for, no more and no less...
what is wrong with that?  If they want more, let them pay more.

>No company will give you more than they think you need
>because (1) it costs them money and any money they spend on you is
>money they can't spend on themselves, and (2) the more you know the
>easier it is for you to leave, taking all that company-funded
>knowledge with you.

That is why a pay increase is associated with the successful completion of
a course... or is that too complex a solution?

>
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>JCL isn't like C or Assembler.  There is very little logic involved.

EXEC PGM=IEBGENER,COND=((0,NE,QLBK0010),EVEN)... oh, excuse me, I was in
the other window... so, tell me, why is it that you mention the
user-friendliness of Java or Assembler and then state that JCL isn't like
C or Assembler... what is the relevance here, please?

>Except for condition code testing it's all straight-line 'code'. I'd 
>be a lot more worried about application software written by someone
>who thinks JCL is too tough.

Given that you have expressed concerns about someone who cna learn and
apply new skills jumping ship for a better-paying job (instead, of course,
of paying more) this is understandable... me, I've met folks who can make
CICS jump up and *sing* and yet cannot code a GENER to save their lives.

>
>By the way, the V20 was NEC's equivalent to the Intel 8088.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>shipping.

I have my own, thanks; it is attached to a Kaypro 8088 and is invoked only
when the switch is pressed.  Un-pressed the speed is slower, pressed and
it is sped-up.  Thanks for your information, it is greatly appreciated.

>
>>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I prefer the relative stability of working for someone.

'Employment at Will' is, I believe, the law here in the United States of
America... there was a recent posting here from a coder who unexpectedly
found himself on the street after 14 years or so on the job.  Ask the
programming crew at Drexel, Burham, Lambert about 'stability'.

>Being an
>independant contractor takes too much additional time and resources to
>please me.  I work to live and I give up my free time very grudgingly.

... and yet you advise others to do just that and learn on their own
hours; why do I read that as rank hypocrisy?

>The people I know who do it well are lucratively rewarded but they
>spend a great deal of uncompensated time traveling, searching out
>contracts or managing themselves as a business.

This speaks volumes of the folks you know, not of how the business can be
practised.

>I sat down once with
>a former coworker who went the indie route to discuss the financial
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>'Us'  are you and me and all the other programmers out there.  (Duh)

Let's make a deal here... I won't speak for you, you don't speak for me.

>'They' are the people who pay your salary.

I receive no salary, I receive an hourly rate from my clients for the
services I render them.

>Only a fool would pay
>someone to do something they have the time, talent and resources to do
>themself.

That depends, Mr Richards, on the relative values said 'fool' places on
money, time, talent and resources... it is usually best speak for
yourself, Mr Richards, unless you've been elected to be a mouthpiece for
others.


(alright... what are the odds that I get accused of a 'bad attitude'
next?)

DD
```

```
Only insofar as it verifies syntax; it will not tell me that the SYSIN
card, 28 steps into the job, does not exist or that I mis-spelled and
typed PGM=AKZQM521 instead of PGM=AZKQM521 (anyone for an 806?)

>The reality of employment is -- in almost ALL, if not all, states in the U.S.,
>the EMPLOYER-EMPLOYEE relationship is defined BY LAW to be that of
>MASTER-SERVANT.  Ergo .... employers have a pretty free hand in what they
>demand you do to earn your generally paltry salary.

I do not make any claims on 'reality'... but I do know that I have
concluded that it is just as unethical for me to demand money for work I
have not done as it is for a client to demand from me skills for which
they do not pay.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373D66A0.7B5AF5FF@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <373ae367.70742592@news.uswest.net> <NKB_2.112$Ku4.6206@iad-read.news.verio.net> <373b3a23.1233049@news.uswest.net> <7cW_2.253$Ku4.13706@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Regarding learning something for free.  I have always worked on
this premise, since I reasonably regularly vote with my feet I
find that yes it does always pay to know these things.  I would
not be paid half as much as I am if I waited for someone to spoon
feed me.

The argument is valid in that request some support from
managment.  Two reasons:  

One: It pays to advertise.  When reviews come up enthusiastic
people get more money (mostly appart from scrooge companies).

Two: If they are not very supportative and it is something that
you want to do, it is time to get out "Tuesday's Australian"
(Subsistute the paper with all the Computer job adverts in it).

Put the hard yards in and the money will follow.


Regarding the TYPRUN=SCAN.  This is the difference between keyword
highlighting in the editor and not having it.  Yes you can compile
and get a result but it is better to have instant feedback.  When
you wait for 3 hours for the job to start to see it cumple due to
a miss typed DSN then you really could cry.  A JCL checker is a
must have it saves a lot of waiting and frustration, you do not
have to figure out how to actually restart the job in the middle
and when you do it quickly notes the GDG's that you have to change
from +1 to 0.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** ENOJON <enojon@delphi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3744248A.17DA6E24@delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <hQ3_2.1716$Yy4.43533@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7hbr7u$r1k$1@news.usf.edu> <T5q_2.1550$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <373ae367.70742592@news.uswest.net>`

```
With all due respect, JCL has three basic commands that should get
any programmer up and running, but the varied permutations on the
basics could send a 747 pilot screaming in horror.

For example, let's get down and dirty with one simple COBOL "Display"
verb.

It is simple //SYSOUT DD SYSOUT=*   [and, already we have
at least 40 variations on SYSOUT class].   disposition, if you're
not going to settle for installation default?  copies?  destination?
free'd on close of dataset? checkpointing? output limit?   forms
control? printer character set (font)? and, then, maybe there's a
JES2 or JES3 entry system to handle with another 60 variants.

But maybe you want to do a hardcopy, microfiche and CDROM
imaging, send a copy across the LAN to user workstation and
to the remote printer in Fargo, ND?

ej



Dave Richards wrote:

> So take some time to learn it.  Nothing is 'user hostile' if you spend
> some time with it.  Any environment where JCL is used will have a JCL
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

#### ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** fletcher.adams@eudoramail.com (Fletcher Adams)
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373b507b.1899230@news-server>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
On 11 May 1999 20:48:04 GMT, jerrygas@aol.com (JERRYGAS) wrote:

>         I have heard that it works in conjunction with COBOL and there is
>on-line training for it. Anybody know how to find it? I couldn't find any books
>on it at the bookstore.        Thanks


Well, there's no such thing as TSO/JCL as such.  TSO is an environment
that runs on MVS, or OS/390 as it is now called, on IBM and Amdahl
mainframes.

JCL is Job Control Language , which uses TSO commands to do things
like allocate resources, execute programs (or "load modules," as we
say), and deal with the output.

That, of course, is a dramatically oversimplified version of what TSO
is.  A better (but not perfect) discussion of TSO can be found in a
pair of books published by Mike Murach and Associates.  The last
number I had for them was 800/221-5528 - but please bear in mind that
number is a decade old (though they're still going strong), and for
all I know they may have a web site.

If you have any specific questions, I'll be glad to answer them to the
best of my ability.

Luck -
F
```

#### ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373B8DB8.47597AF9@ragdolls.net>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```


JERRYGAS wrote:

>          I have heard that it works in conjunction with COBOL and there is
> on-line training for it. Anybody know how to find it? I couldn't find any books
> on it at the bookstore.        Thanks
> go to the Museum :-)

TSO - time sharing option used on dump terminals (3270 emulation?)
running on IBM mainframes
JCL = job control language also IBM 360 -----> stuff
```

#### ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** rkowalc@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
As a new Cobol programmer, I have found the follow books to be
invaluable:

System 390 JCL by Gary Deward Brown    and
MVS TSO, Part 1 by Doug Lowe.

Both are available at Amazon and Buy.com.  Buy.com was a couple of bucks
cheaper.


--== Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/ ==--
---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have the same JCL book and it is an invaluable resource.

JCL is what runs the COBOL programs.  It is strange at first but after
seeing enough of it, it will become very easy to you.  Kind of like driving
a stick shift car.  JCL is a very, very powerful language.   I have cut the
run times of small MVS jobs down by 90% just by tweaking the JCL.

TSO stands for Time Sharing Option.  It allows users to take a small portion
of the mainframe's resources for development, testing, and maintenance of
jobs.  It kind of sits between MVS and whatever editor or workbench you use
(ISPF, Roscoe, etc.)

Later,
Jeff

----------
In article <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, rkowalc@my-dejanews.com wrote:


> As a new Cobol programmer, I have found the follow books to be
> invaluable:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

```
<union27@macconnect.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> run times of small MVS jobs down by 90% just by tweaking the JCL.

as long by writing poor JCL.
```

<a id="t-d7d84f4fc1"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus "PC_WIN..." routines, any experience of these? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus "PC_WIN..." routines, any experience of these?

- **From:** grahamr@cix.compulink.co.uk ("Graham Ranson")
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FBL1zK.Gqt@cix.compulink.co.uk>`

```
Does anyone have experience of using the call-by-name "PC_WIN..." 
routines. We're using COBOL 3.2 (and no support contract at the moment!); 
we've had no problems with using the "CBL_..." routines but when we've 
tried to use any of the "PC_WIN..." routines the system comes up with run 
time error 198, "load failure - usually because of insufficient memory". I 
do not believe that there is any problem with memory!

We are using a built WINDOWS .EXE with called INTs running in WINDOWS 95 
and while we have had no problems with the other "CBL_..." routines all of 
the "PC_WIN..." routines fail in the same way. I cannot really believe 
that it's anything to do with memory but I cam worried about either some 
undocumented environmental consideration or simply a bug in the RTS or 
compiler. It seems more than a coincidence that none of these routines 
seem to work while the others ("CBL_..." ) are fine.

We want to use these routines for proper WINDOWs  printer access, so the 
other approach is some alternative method of accessing WINDOWS printers; 
has anyone tried such an approach?

Graham Ranson
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus "PC_WIN..." routines, any experience of these?

- **References:** `<FBL1zK.Gqt@cix.compulink.co.uk>`

```

> other approach is some alternative method of accessing WINDOWS printers;

Print at http://www.flexus.com.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
Anyone know where I can pick up a listing of what the File Status
values mean.  The typical ones are:

10 - end of file
22 - duplicate record
23 - not found

and I just was asked what a '35' was - it seems to be if you attempt
to open an empty file as input using COBOL II or later you will get
the '35' status.

Thanks.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM File Status Codes

- **From:** Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu>

```
If you are on a mainframe there is a "magic" way to get information on
just about anything mainframe related...

qw (stands for quick weference)

You could type "qw filestat" on the command line and it would give you
the entire list of file status codes for VSAM.  You can use qw for just
about everything.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM File Status Codes

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373A2B44.254C345B@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7ha69n$l7v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3738B6E2.60B@saif.com>`

```
In my rarely humble opinion, STATUS CODE 35 is an IBM misinterpretation
when given with an empty VSAM file.  STATUS CODE 35 should be given only
for a missing DD statement.  The file exists otherwise the job step
would have failed in allocation processing.  At one time they issued a
STATUS CODE of 95 for the condition, a practice I wish they had
continued since one of my shop's application software vendors keyed off
that code and went through the IBM required unnatural contortions to
create a zero record file. I will leave the full rant about IBM forcing
you to go through unnatural contortions to create a zero record VSAM
file for another day.  

Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> cobolcicsdude@my-dejanews.com wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM File Status Codes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hde0i$22l@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7ha69n$l7v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3738B6E2.60B@saif.com> <373A2B44.254C345B@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
I understand your "rant" and was involved with "discussions" on this topic
with IBM that occurred at GUIDE in the '80s.  I will say (on IBM's side)
that the Standard really doesn't say what an "present" file is.  On the
other hand, what IBM did with SEVERAL of their decisions (going back to VS
COBOL II - not just the newer COBOLs) when it came to "implementing"
ANSI/ISO Standard COBOL I/O was "strange" at best.

My personal "pet peeve" has to do with the way that IBM creates a "new" (not
available) file when you OPEN it for OUTPUT on MVS or OS/390.  This works
"beautifully" on CMS, on OS/2, on Windows, on every other *non* MVS type
environment.  HOWEVER, rather than creating a "useful" (and cataloged)
output file, IBM "decided" to create garbage (and headaches) that mean NO
(sane) MVS or OS/390 site uses the ANSI/ISO Standard "CBLQDA" option.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM File Status Codes

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-dejanews.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hhi6i$brr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ha69n$l7v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3738B6E2.60B@saif.com> <373A2B44.254C345B@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
In article <373A2B44.254C345B@nbnet.nb.ca>,
  Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote:
> In my rarely humble opinion, STATUS CODE 35 is an IBM
misinterpretation
> when given with an empty VSAM file.

One point to emphasize is that the status codes are subject to
interpretation.  I normally create a copybook that contains the status
code values as 88 names.  I only test the 88 level names rather than the
raw values.  This makes it a lot easier to move code to a different
environment.
```

<a id="t-7bb05b88c9"></a>
## Thread: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character... — 10 msgs

### Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

- **From:** JediBoricua <jediboricua@my-dejanews.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 I want to add a line control feed (line feed, special character code
or something like that)at the end of each record in my output file. Can
it be done?, any help will be appreciated. Thanks.

JediBoricua
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<isYZ2.49$P95.5854@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
JediBoricua wrote in message <7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
> I want to add a line control feed (line feed, special character code
>or something like that)at the end of each record in my output file. Can
>it be done?, any help will be appreciated. Thanks.


In the SELECT statement specify ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL and a
line-feed, possibly a carriage-return as well (on some systems) will be
added to the end of each line automatically.  Check the Programmer's Guide
to see what options you need to specify to either include or not include any
trailing spaces before the end of short lines.
HTH
Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ha25a$ksu$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A sequential file defaults to that behavior on most systems. If your
system does not, then try a WRITE RECORD BEFORE 1 statement.
You should note that on the PC, you will get both a CR *and* a LF.
On a Unix system, you will generally get just the LF.  The general rule
is that you will get the same format as source code for that particular
platform.  It is easily checked with your editor.

JediBoricua wrote in message <7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
> I want to add a line control feed (line feed, special character code
>or something like that)at the end of each record in my output file. Can
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

- **From:** "Gavin Honeybun" <honeybun@webace.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hbcvo$8oc$1@mailint03.im.hou.compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
  01 WB-ASCII-CODES.
     02 FILLER                                   PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 13.
     02 FILLER                                   PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 10.
  01 WB-ASCII-CHARS REDEFINES WB-ASCII-CODES.
     02 FILLER                                   PIC X.
     02 WB-CARRIAGE-RETURN                       PIC X.
     02 FILLER                                   PIC X.
     02 WB-LINE-FEED                             PIC X.


Then move WB-CARRIAGE-RETURN & WB-LINE-FEED to the last two bytes of your
o/p file.




JediBoricua wrote in message <7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
> I want to add a line control feed (line feed, special character code
>or something like that)at the end of each record in my output file. Can
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3739C140.6787@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7hbcvo$8oc$1@mailint03.im.hou.compaq.com>`

```
I was going to refrain from this one, but since all of the suggestions 
use techniques unique to various implementations, I thought I might 
give one that is standard.

You can use the SYMBOLIC CHARACTERS clause in SPECIAL-NAMES for this 
(one of the reasons it was invented).

Like

    SYMBOLIC CHARACTERS carriage-return IS 14
       ...

    MOVE carriage-return TO the-print-line (last-char + 1: 1)

If your program collating sequence is always ASCII, this will always 
work.  If not, you can make one that will work anywhere by specifying 
an alphabet-name equated to STANDARD-1 and change "IS 14" to "IS 14 IN 
alphabet-name".  Note that I used 14 even though the actual value is 
13.  That is because the number is an ordinal in the collating 
sequence associated with the alphabet.  Ordinal numbers start at 1.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oYk_2.1$Ku4.276@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7h9j1j$5p6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7hbcvo$8oc$1@mailint03.im.hou.compaq.com> <3739C140.6787@compaq.com>`

```
In article <3739C140.6787@compaq.com>,
Don Nelson  <don.nelson@compaq.com> wrote:
>I was going to refrain from this one, but since all of the suggestions 
>use techniques unique to various implementations, I thought I might 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>sequence associated with the alphabet.  Ordinal numbers start at 1.

Mr Nelson, *really*... your leave behind you the delightful task for a
maintenance programmer (the stereotypical 2-yr wonder) of understanding
why it was you reached over the top of your head to scratch your left ear
with your right hand.  *Any* compiler I know of which supports the sort of
referencing your example requires also has supported:

01  CRLF.
    05  CARRIAGE-RETURN PIC X VALUE X'0D'.
    05  LINE-FEED       PIC X VALUE X'0A'

... or ..

MOVE X'0D' TO CARRIAGE-RETURN.
MOVE X'0A' TO LINE-FEED.

(someone posted earlier the REDEFining of an S9(4) COMP field with a VALUE
of +14 into two PIC X bytes... I remember something like this from my CICS
course, long ago, and the last bit of 'Look, Mom, I'm a Programmer!' code
I wrote which involved the incrementing of an alpha indicator by binary
addition... cust # 999999A --> 000001B and the like... rather tight code,
if I do say so myself)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Adding a Line Control Feed or special character...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3739e300.10278262@news.indigo.ie>`

```
On Wed, 12 May 1999 19:50:44 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

>In article <3739C140.6787@compaq.com>,
>Don Nelson  <don.nelson@compaq.com> wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD


Also not all compilers support the intrinsic functions.
(so this is not portable)
But all of them support the following (including COBOL 74)
05 var-num   pic s9(4)  binary value 14.
05 var-r redefines var-num.
   10 filler  pic x
   10 char    pic x.

FF
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373A2490.705D@compaq.com>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:

> Mr Nelson, *really*... your leave behind you the delightful task for a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Actually all of them do not.  Hex literals are not standard and many 
do not have them and some implemented them in different ways.  Maybe 
all you know of have hex literals as you indicated, but there are 
hundreds out there.  Have you used every one?  Also, this is more head 
scratching than using something clearly defined in my opinion (which, 
of course is mine and probably differs from yours and lots of others).

nothing to do with intrinsic functions in spite of what the next 
commentor said.

As you indicated, redefining a comp item as bytes is not really a good 
idea.
```

## Thread: COBOL & SQLCODE — 4 msgs

### COBOL & SQLCODE

- **From:** "Victoria Monta���ez" <vicky@correo.ulima.edu.pe>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h8hbg$247$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
hi there,

is it possible to capture the sqlcode when running an .exe (cobol) ?

TIA,

Vicky
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL & SQLCODE

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Iw_Z2.1134$Yy4.23069@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7h8hbg$247$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
The sqlcode (OK, not found, database not open, etc, I presume you mean) can
be detected in the COBOL program; in fact, it SHOULD be, and the programmer
should handle any "not-OK" situation.

Once the program is compiled, though, the user has no access to the calls or
the results of the SQL calls to the database services. If there are
"not-OK" SQL codes not being handled in the program, contact the prgrammer
and demand repairs or a refund.

MCM


Victoria Monta�ez wrote in message <7h8hbg$247$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>...
>hi there,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
yes.

in your program should be a test of the sqlcode.

If you have a problem with a 'foreign' program and your are not able to
change the code, then you must use a database utility. i'm working with
DB2 on MVS/OS390 and the DB2-system protocols each error occured on
database. with a special tool you can look at the protocol and find out
what was wrong. I have no idea about this tool on other systems or
databases. Please ask your database-administrator for this tool.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL & SQLCODE

- **From:** Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373B0A44.AE0DFB07@email.ais.unc.edu>`
- **References:** `<7h8hbg$247$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
Victoria,

I assume you are not running on a mainframe, so I can't help you
directly.  I work on a mainframe and you can "capture" sqlcode by doing
a display or print of it to an output medium (e.g. file) immediately
after the exec sql section.  A new sqlcode is generated each time you
use an exec sql statement, so depending on your input file, you could
get a lot of data.

exec sql
 ...
end-exec.

display "sqlcode for reading/writing is " sqlcode.
display "soc-sec-num                    " soc-sec-num.
```

---

<a id="t-49f8d704a8"></a>
## Thread: Migration from CA-Realia to Fujitsu — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Migration from CA-Realia to Fujitsu

- **From:** John <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7h9c3q$hf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We currently use CA-Realia Workbench 3.0 and Flexus SP2. CA does not
appear to have any plans to incorporate support for ActiveX/COM.
According to CA support, CA-Realia is not capable of calling ActiveX
DLLs. We would like to move our product in this direction. We like SP2
and would like to continue to use it. We make heavy use of Windows
APIs. We plan on using VB in certain instances (True DB Grid Pro 6.0).

Fujitsu advertises that it can access ActiveX DLLs, produce ActiveX
components and controls from COBOL, and that it easily interfaces with
VB. It sounds almost too good to be true.

Has anybody out there migrated from CA-Realia WB 3.0 to Fujitsu? Would
you recommend Fujitsu or Micro Focus? Can I still continue to use SP2
with Fujitsu? How do you rate Fujitsu?

Any advice and recommendations would be most welcome.

Thanks,

John Chafin


--== Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/ ==--
---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

#### ↳ Re: Migration from CA-Realia to Fujitsu

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qKLNlbRz0ebK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7h9c3q$hf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 May 1999 13:42:19, John <jchafin@cgwinc.com> wrote:

> We currently use CA-Realia Workbench 3.0 and Flexus SP2. CA does not
> appear to have any plans to incorporate support for ActiveX/COM.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> APIs. We plan on using VB in certain instances (True DB Grid Pro 6.0).

Since you already use Realia WB 3.0 you are generating 32 bit code, 
correct?

I suggest you contact Flexus and get their add on for ActiveX/COM.  
They presently support OCX files as part of sp2 in their 32 bit 
version.  No need to change compilers or tools.

PS - Fujitsu has a fine compiler.  Conversion from Realia to Fujitsu 
is the hardest of any of the COBOL variants - Realia did things very 
differently.  However, I have done it so I know it can be done.  I did
it to get to 32 bit without buying a $4000.00 compiler from CA.  (I 
was using 16 bit Realia before).  Anyway, the file I/O with Realia is 
the FASTEST there is, so if your application is I/O intensive you 
won't want to do it.

Second, Fujitsu DOES support OCX controls, but ONLY from PowerCOBOL - 
you won't be able to mix this with Sp2.

Lastly, Flexus (http://www.flexus.com) DOES have a version for Fujitsu
COBOL, and it does (with an add on) support OCX controls (as does the 
Realia version).

Personally, I see no need for you to switch - just get the Flexus add 
on.

 -------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

Bob Wolfe, flexus
```

#### ↳ Re: Migration from CA-Realia to Fujitsu

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3738487b.12990480@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7h9c3q$hf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
John <jchafin@cgwinc.com> wrote:

>We currently use CA-Realia Workbench 3.0 and Flexus SP2. CA does not
>appear to have any plans to incorporate support for ActiveX/COM.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>APIs. We plan on using VB in certain instances (True DB Grid Pro 6.0).

Hi John:

You can embed Active X controls into an sp2 panel defintion with our
32 bit Windows version and control both properties as well as methods
for the OCX.

I have sent you the Active X support DLL as an attachment to a
separate e-mail message.

>Fujitsu advertises that it can access ActiveX DLLs, produce ActiveX
>components and controls from COBOL, and that it easily interfaces with
>VB. It sounds almost too good to be true.

We support Active X controls in sp2 regardless of which compiler you
are using, therefore you can continue to use your 32 bit CA-Realia
compiler and still be able to display and control Active X controls
from your sp2 GUI screens.

In addition, our Active X support also works with our Thin Client
add-on which means that you can also run your GUI screens across the
Internet, complete with Active X controls.

>Has anybody out there migrated from CA-Realia WB 3.0 to Fujitsu? Would
>you recommend Fujitsu or Micro Focus? Can I still continue to use SP2
>with Fujitsu? How do you rate Fujitsu?

sp2 does indeed support Fujitsu, but if the only reason that you want
to switch compilers is to gain Active X control support, you can get
it immediately with sp2.  Besides, I suspect the Active X support
capabilities with Fujitsu COBOL, only work with Fujitsu COBOL.  The
Active X control support in COBOL sp2 is completely compiler
independent.  The same screen handling source and GUI screen
definitions work the same regardless of which compiler you prefer to
use.

>Any advice and recommendations would be most welcome.

The best advice I can give you is to check your e-mail in about 5
minutes.

;-)


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
That is correct.

>With your recommended solution the only thing I can't do is create an

Bob Wolfe, flexus
```

```
We migrated from Micro Focus Workbench to Fujitsu Cobol Enterprise and
found no problems at all.  You should be able to use your SP2 with no
code changes to your programs at all.  I believe SP2 now supports
ActiveX.

We are satisfied with Fujitsu Cobol.  In fact, we are using it with
Tuxedo, SP2 thin client, and Visual C/C++ on a Unisys 2200/Clearpath
mainframe.  It's doing a good job in communicating with these other
products.

BTW, you have a switch your SP2 to the SP2 for Fujitsu.

Tony M. Mina
```

<a id="t-e06c0ae6c1"></a>
## Thread: MAINFRAME/DB2 question — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### MAINFRAME/DB2 question

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3738B936.8D@saif.com>`

```
MAINFRAME SITUATION:
We have a single DB2 sub-system that supports multiple test environments
by using unique table ownerid's.  I would prefer to have a separate DB2
sub-system for each test environment, in order to simplify connectivity
to other test system components.

MY QUESTION:
Does anyone else out there share my opinion?
Or better yet, are you working for a shop that utilizes multiple test
DB2 sub-systems?
(I need to prove to my management that my idea is not crackers!)

The only responses I get from the DB2 news group is that it is more
convienent to keep all the test systems under a single sub-system... but
I would expect that from DBAs.

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME/DB2 question

- **From:** Mike <nospam@anywhere.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37398122.9E97E31F@anywhere.com>`
- **References:** `<3738B936.8D@saif.com>`

```
At Caterpiller, Production and Quality have their own sub-system.
But, Unit Test and System Test share a sub-system, the same
way you do.

Mike
DBA contractor

Pete Wirfs wrote:

> MAINFRAME SITUATION:
> We have a single DB2 sub-system that supports multiple test environments
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME/DB2 question

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37398557.BEA87CC9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3738B936.8D@saif.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> MAINFRAME SITUATION:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete

Peter,

DB2 subsystems tend to be very expensive to run for an MVS box. 
Therefore it is more efficient to create one subsystem but
segregate the tables by an owner id.  This means that if one DB2
test area is not used the MVS does not have the added overhead of
a started task.

I have worked extensively with multiple test regions and I have
found that the only reason to set up a third one (one for
production, one for test) is for new release testing and Y2k
testing.

If you are using the owner id inside your program somehow then
this might be where you are coming from.  If you are doing this
remove it and put it into your bind cards.  If you are using
dynamic SQL this is a new area, one solution is to create special
user id that you submit the job under (for each test area) and
then make a view to that userid.

If you can give us specifics of what problems you are trying to
hurdle in your application then we can possibly help you.

I am an application programmer not a DBA!

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME/DB2 question

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3739A339.DF6@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<3738B936.8D@saif.com> <37398557.BEA87CC9@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Pete Wirfs wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken


Whoops, I failed to state the problem that is killing us right now.  We
want to use DB2 stored procedures which run in the SPAS component of the
DB2 sub system, but with a single test DB2 sub-system, we found we can
only run a single test version of any given stored procedure, when we
may have a requirement for more than one test version.  

(We currently specify table ownerid during BIND, only)

Thank you for any ideas you can offer...
Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME/DB2 question

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hc5av$2ck$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<3738B936.8D@saif.com>`

```
At our shop we have a DB2 region for production, test, model office,
and tech support.  The tech support region is usually shut down, and
is activated only when they have installation or upgrade work to do.


Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com> wrote in message
news:3738B936.8D@saif.com...
> MAINFRAME SITUATION:
> We have a single DB2 sub-system that supports multiple test
environments
> by using unique table ownerid's.  I would prefer to have a separate
DB2
> sub-system for each test environment, in order to simplify
connectivity
> to other test system components.
>
> MY QUESTION:
> Does anyone else out there share my opinion?
> Or better yet, are you working for a shop that utilizes multiple
test
> DB2 sub-systems?
> (I need to prove to my management that my idea is not crackers!)
>
> The only responses I get from the DB2 news group is that it is more
> convienent to keep all the test systems under a single sub-system...
but
> I would expect that from DBAs.
>
> Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME/DB2 question

- **From:** Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37398327.28EE20F6@email.ais.unc.edu>`
- **References:** `<3738B936.8D@saif.com>`

```
Pete,

I agree with you.  
I used to work for GTE-DS and we used separate subsystems for our
different environments.  I'm not a database guru, so I don't understand
their side of it, but basically it is probably easier for them to
maintain (with a single sub-system).  I think it would make it a lot
more difficult for the programmers.  Also, besides the connectivity you
are concerned about, test systems have a tendency to need to be reset
quite often.  If you had test group A and test group B working on the
same subsystem, test group B could foul up the database for group A.  I
don't know what functionality you need, but I think in any case it would
be better for the application programmers to have separate subsystems.

Good luck - DBA's usually are stubborn and have clout.
```

---

```
 To: VP, Corporate Administration

 I hope I haven't misunderstood your instructions, because this Y to K
 problem makes no sense to me.

 calendar for the year 2000, per my understanding of the instructions.
 The months now read as follows:
 Januark
 Februark
 March
 April
 Mak
 June
 Julk
(The rest of the months appear to be okay)

Please let me know if there is anything else that needs to be done in
preparation for the year 2000.
```

<a id="t-6854e804bc"></a>
## Thread: [OT] In My Spare Time... — 1 msgs

### [OT] In My Spare Time...

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ra%Z2.974$qh4.62806@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

... I participate in a mailing-list subscribed to by the alumni/ae of my
Alma Mater.  Topics of discussion range from the mundane and gossip-y ('my
widdle kitty-kat did da kyuuuuutest fing!') to the tuesdane and obscure
('Who was that Indian geometer who came up with that alternate proof for
Euclid I.47 in which...').

Anyhow, recently it was the school's Annual Sporting Event, a
croquet-tourney played against the denizens of The Other School In
Annapolis; this is a time when various alums show back up on campus and
indulge in a bit of maudlin bemoaning.  ('Lo, in My Time we swilled down
swich liquors such as *ten* undergrads could not swill down today... why,
I recall we had slatterns who would...' you get the picture)  Anyhow,
there was a bit of trouble at what the undergrads quaintly refer to as
'Watch The Fogeys Try To Recapture Their Youth' parties; the stereo-system
was not quite up to snuff (or maybe completely dead, I do not know).

This, of course, has caused some Heated Exchanges on the mailing-list...
why, when *I* was an undergrad we had a stereo etc. ... and one fellow, a
more recent graduate who now works at ol' Alma in some capacity or
another, posted the following explanation of De Rerum Natura (often
mistranslated as 'The Nature of Things' but actually closer to 'The Way
Things Are', a book by Lucretius which is required reading by all
students).  My response to his explanation follows... perusing it might
give a bit of a hint as to how much I hold myself back in other fora.

--begin quoted text:

Mr xxxxxx observes and opines:

> There is no need to take up any Alumni collection
> though, and the administration of such moneys would be problematic.  Of
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> potato of tagged Alumni donations.  

So, let me get this straight... a 'specifically 'tagged'' donation for
the purchase of stereo equipment to the Waltz Committee, even though it
would wind up in with those you label as 'best suited to utilise it',
would have 'repercussions that would smack of unwanted intervention'?

How very... *intriguing*.  Hypothetically speaking, of course, if it were
possible that there might, perhaps, exist a person so petty, so 'small' as
to take a bit of utterly unsubstantiated-yet-smilingly-cruel delight in
the squabblings of ridiculously self-important College-Kid Committee
Members over the unexpected gift of negligible funds - a sum so small that
even in US Dollars it does not contain a comma! - earmarked for the
purchase of audio equipment which would bring some unexpected delights to
a portion of the Student Body larger than that which composes said
Committees...

... where would this person, so petty, so 'small', send a check and to
whom should it be made out?

--end quoted text


DD
```

## Thread: Porting problem — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Porting problem

- **From:** mark@nevyn.demon.co.uk (Mark Stevens)
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I am having problems converting some Microfocus code to run under the
Fujitsu compiler.  I must admit that my COBOL is a little out of date.
The following code generates a compiler error:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. CONSOLE-INPUT.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 DATA DIVISION.
 working-storage section.
 01  ENTRY-FIELD            PIC -ZZZ,ZZZ.ZZ.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-BEGIN.
     DISPLAY "Please enter a value".
     ACCEPT ENTRY-FIELD FROM CONSOLE.
 PROGRAM-END.
       STOP RUN.

Generates an error about ENTRY-FIELD needing to be fixed length group,
decimal, alphabetic or alphanumeric.

Any pointers would be appreciated.

Thanks in advance,
Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: Porting problem

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3739C38E.2FCB@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Mark Stevens wrote:
> ...
> The following code generates a compiler error:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
The error is quite reasonable.  You have described it as 
numeric-edited, which would probably give strange answers according to 
standard COBOL.

The standard is a bit hazy in this area.  It says the data is just 
plunked into the receiver (sort of).  It also says any conversion 
necessary between the outside and the data item is defined by the 
implementor.  I assume MF edits the data assuming this is 
"conversion".  Many other implementations just plunk it in, so you 
would not get editing.  To have something portable you should accept 
it into an alphanumeric item, and then move that to the receiver using 
the NUMVAL function.  Like

     ACCEPT entry-field-an FROM CONSOLE
     MOVE FUNCTION NUMVAL (entry-field-an) TO entry-field  
```

#### ↳ Re: Porting problem

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<um3fKaQn#GA.59@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
    I think that what you need is to use the AT modifier, ie ACCEPT
ENTRY-FIELD AT 0201.

    Using displays and accepts without the AT drops you into a standard,
totally portable, totally lame (or crippled) screen IO.  They call this
module ADIS, and it handles almost all screen IO outside of the standard
display/accept you were using.

    If you have VERY little screen IO to do, it is possible to clear screen
and move cursor if you REALLY need to save memory.
```

#### ↳ Re: Porting problem

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mk3%2.15017$2Z4.1857@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Glad to see you admitted your COBOL is out of date.
ACCEPT is ALWAYS left justified.
a 'Z' or '*' is a FLOATING EDIT CHARACTER.
You need an extra step.

Mark Stevens wrote in message <3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk>...
>I am having problems converting some Microfocus code to run under the
>Fujitsu compiler.  I must admit that my COBOL is a little out of date.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>Mark Stevens (mark@nevyn.demon.co.uk)
```

- **From:** mark@nevyn.demon.co.uk (Mark Stevens)

- **Message-ID:** `<37401366.1188522@news.demon.co.uk>`

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Porting problem

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O71ya42n#GA.308@ntawwabp.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk> <mk3%2.15017$2Z4.1857@news4.mia>`

```
        I do not believe that -ZZ IS legal.  If you need the minus sign,
use ---9  (3 minus, 1 9).  With either Z's or minus's, leaving off the
trailing "9" will create an implict zero is blank condition.  Also note that
ZZZ will handle numbers from 000 to 999, while -ZZ will handle -99 to 99.
The leftmost minus will be either blank or minus, never a number.
```

```

> ZZZ will handle numbers from 000 to 999, while -ZZ will handle -99 to 99.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting problem

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eYGCDPPo#GA.339@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk> <mk3%2.15017$2Z4.1857@news4.mia> <O71ya42n#GA.308@ntawwabp.compuserve.com> <3740c7c0.13027441@netnews>`

```
I actually meant to say that although ZZZ will handle 000 to 999, --- (3
minus's) will only handle -99 to 99.  The reference to -ZZ was a typo on my
part.

I suppose that some compilers will handle -ZZ.  I have personally never had
a compiler accept it, although I really haven't tried it that often.  Pity
though - I don't see why it should not work as an alternate form of --- (3
minus).  It would seem to do the exact same thing.

One of my first posts and I make a major typo.  Oh well.  That is why I like
AOL 4.0 for email - it makes me look literate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hr31u$lv7@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk> <mk3%2.15017$2Z4.1857@news4.mia> <O71ya42n#GA.308@ntawwabp.compuserve.com> <3740c7c0.13027441@netnews> <eYGCDPPo#GA.339@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
I think that you will find that ANY (and all) ANSI conforming compilers will
accept PIC -ZZ. However, it is NOT the same thing as PIC ---.  If you
move -8 to a PIC -ZZ field, then you will get a hyphen followed by a space
followed by an 8.  While if you move it to a Pic --- field, you will get a
space followed by a hyphen followed by an 8.  The latter has a "floating"
hyphen (negative sign - called floating insertion) while the former has a
non-floating hyphen (negative sign - called fixed insertion).

If you find a compiler that CLAIMS to be ANSI (or ISO) conforming and it
does not accept a "-ZZ" picture clause, then report it to the vendor as
non-conforming.  (On the other hand, if you have an application where you
really do NOT want the negative sign to "float" - you probably have a fairly
unusual application in the first place.)

P.S.  I have verified this in the '85 Standard.  I haven't looked it up in
the '74 or '68 Standards, but I don't think that this is anything "new".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37434abf.2075231@netnews>`
- **References:** `<3739b8f0.1498289@news.demon.co.uk> <mk3%2.15017$2Z4.1857@news4.mia> <O71ya42n#GA.308@ntawwabp.compuserve.com> <3740c7c0.13027441@netnews> <eYGCDPPo#GA.339@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 18 May 1999 02:00:45 -0400, when "Russell Styles"
<RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> I suppose that some compilers will handle -ZZ.  I have personally never had
> a compiler accept it, although I really haven't tried it that often.  

Can you tell me what compiler rejects it?  This has been standard COBOL
since 1974 (probably 1968, though I'm not sure).

> Pity
> though - I don't see why it should not work as an alternate form of --- (3
> minus).  It would seem to do the exact same thing.

No, PIC --- is a floating sign while PIC -ZZ is a fixed sign.  If you move
-1 to PIC -ZZ you get "- 1" while moving -1 to PIC --- gives " -1".
```

```

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

Alex,

Was the indexed file a version 0 file before you changed it to a version 3?
Changing the file version number from 0 to another version, or changing from
another version to 0, will mark the file as needing recovery and require
that recover1 be run on the file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Syncsort and MF striped index files.... Help!!!

- **From:** Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373C16C3.28A04FC1@email.ais.unc.edu>`
- **References:** `<19990512151504.20935.00003812@ng-ch1.aol.com>`

```
What's your problem?
I'm going to take a guess and assume that you are having a problem
sorting or copying because you haven't taken into consideration the key
index length.  If my extra sensory perception is working, I'll bet you
see 

cols 123456789
     joe
     bill

and you are sorting starting at 1 for a length of 9.
you actually have to start at 5 for a length of 9 because you need to
account for the index.

If that isn't your problem, let me know what is.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Source - file extension ?

- **From:** "George Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airfoce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3295E1A1A6FF811D.27F02E840C1BE61E.008A5E22C331A872@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7hbk2l$d05$1@newsread.f.de.uu.net> <7hc3si$s10$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Anything is permissible depending on standards in use at your location.
Some of those that I have run into include the following:

.COB (Program Source)
.CBL (Program Source)
.PCO (Recompilation Required Source Code)
.FD   (File Descriptions)
.SEL (Select Statements)
.WS  (Working Storage Copy books)
.CPY (Copybooks)
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Source - file extension ?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hc46o$s22$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7hbk2l$d05$1@newsread.f.de.uu.net>`

```
Peter Hoffmann wrote in message <7hbk2l$d05$1@newsread.f.de.uu.net>...
>What file extensions can COBOL source files have ?

*.COP (copy files)

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-91d64e2523"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Program returning Param1 ? — 3 msgs

### Cobol Program returning Param1 ?

- **From:** "Victoria Monta���ez" <vicky@correo.ulima.edu.pe>
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hb6fr$qvk$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
Hi there,

mf cobol /  nt4.0
does anybody know how to  make the cobol program return one parameter, lets
say the sqlcode?
is there any way to insert an instruction in the identification section so
the programs knows its returning something?

thanks for any idea,

Vicky
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Program returning Param1 ?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V5q_2.1552$m83.41078@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7hb6fr$qvk$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
A COBOL program can return a parameter using the linkage section.
See the CALL,  PROCEDURE DIVISION USING, and LINKAGE SECTION portions of
your manual for details on your system, but here's a C&D example:

PROGRAM-ID. FIRST.
..
...
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    MOVE 9 TO A B C.
    DISPLAY A B C.
    CALL 'theprog' using a b c.
    DISPLAY A B C.
    STOP RUN.

...
END PROGRAM FIRST.
PROGRAM-ID. THEPROG.
...
LINKAGE SECTION.
 01   X     PIC 9.
 01   Y     PIC 9.
 01   Z     PIC 9.
..
  PROCEDURE DIVISION USING  x y z.
    MOVE 1 to X
    MOVE 2 to y
    move 3   to Z
    GOBACK.

END PROGRAM THEPROG.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Program returning Param1 ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hdd4e$1ho@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7hb6fr$qvk$1@ucayali.unired.net.pe>`

```
As long as you specified MF (which I think you intend to mean "Micro
Focus" - not Mainframe - because you also mention NT), then just using the

   RETURNING

phrase on the GoBack or Exit Program statement.

(In the next Standard, the RETURNING option will be on the PROCEDURE
DIVISION header - now called a "marker" - for such cases - I think.)
```

<a id="t-abf63788bc"></a>
## Thread: Word list — 1 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [1999-04](1999-04.md#t-abf63788bc)._

#### ↳ Re: Word list

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1999-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3738d59a.1650770@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<ZTXO2.10640$cq3.236720@news.uswest.net>`

```
On Wed, 7 Apr 1999 23:18:26 -0700, Garry Dell wrote:

>I in need of a swear-word list that I could place into a table.

>I have a project that will check names against this table to find faulty
>names and swear words used as names.

>Your help will be greatly appriciated.


Investigate message <6df2qi$4ob@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>, from about
a year ago, in alt.usenet.offline-reader.forte-agent .  That would be
a place to start.  The message was for spam filtering, but a
significant part of it may be what you are looking for.

Generally, I don't like the idea of such things, but sometimes you
have to do things you don't like.  Just be sure that your checks are
not so overly-restrictive that you reject valid names that happen to
be somewhat close to those you don't want.

Also, for ease of maintenance, you may want to store the word list in
a secured file and read it into your program's table.  That way, you
don't have to make changes to your program every time the word list
changes.  Any person with appropriate access to the secured file could
make such changes, which could then be read into the program's table.
```

```
On Wed, 14 Apr 1999 08:19:31 -0700, Cheesle wrote:

[snip]

>It is however a generic problem in the programming business that program
>over the years tend to become unstructured and full of old garbage.

Many programs do suffer with age.  It really makes you appreciate a
program that was well-written originally, when minor, or even some
moderate to major, changes can be incorporated without significant
changes to the overall program or to the surrounding code.

Do resist the temptation, though, to change parts of the program not
related to the task at hand.  That just invites disaster.

>                                                                    I think
>we as occupational group should take this matter more serious and attempt to
>improve our production, no matter whether we program in C, pascal, basic,
>cobol, ada, simula what ever.
```

- **From:** Bogy@Texas.NEt (Peter Gilchrist)
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373af715.9414984@news.texas.net>`

```
Using MF cobol, I have an input file containg embeded signs.  What is
the easiest way to xlate the character to a leading and separate sign
? (as in the example below).

000000000788u 000000001637q

-000000007886 +000000016372

Of course I would need the translation for eack "u", "q", etc in order
to program in the conversion.  I hope there is a REDEFINES that would
help be out better.

Please help !
```

#### ↳ Re: Embeded Signs >> HELP

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dy4my9THciVf@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<373af715.9414984@news.texas.net>`

```
On Thu, 13 May 1999 16:03:35, Bogy@Texas.NEt (Peter Gilchrist) wrote:

> Using MF cobol, I have an input file containg embeded signs.  What is
> the easiest way to xlate the character to a leading and separate sign
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You can define a field in working-storage as "Separate Leading" and 
then just MOVE the data to that field.  

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Embeded Signs >> HELP

- **From:** philhickling@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hk8u6$95n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<373af715.9414984@news.texas.net>`

```
In article <373af715.9414984@news.texas.net>,
  Bogy@Texas.NEt (Peter Gilchrist) wrote:
> Using MF cobol, I have an input file containg embeded signs.  What is
> the easiest way to xlate the character to a leading and separate sign
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can't you just move the field to another, edited field?  Thus you
have 2 definitions:
77  FIELD-1     PIC S9(13).
77  FIELD-2     PIC +9(13).
and one comand:
    MOVE FIELD-1 TO FIELD-2.
If you define FIELD-2 as -9(13) only minus signs would show;  with a +
then both + & 1 signs show.

Phil


--== Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/ ==--
---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

<a id="t-7eff791d69"></a>
## Thread: IBM completion codes — 9 msgs

### IBM completion codes

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373AD9C6.36B5F5B0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
When I run an IDMS program under IBM MVS, sometimes I get a completion
code which I'm unfamiliar with.

Is there any place where I can plug my completion code into a Web page
to get clarification of my problem?
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM completion codes

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hgm69$o6g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<373AD9C6.36B5F5B0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <373AD9C6.36B5F5B0@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> When I run an IDMS program under IBM MVS, sometimes I get a completion
> code which I'm unfamiliar with.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think you want to have information on ABEND codes since this is
something like a synonym to completition in the MVS environment.
Unfortunately, there is not a place you can visit, but rather several
places. Firstly, you have to decide whether it is a 'system' or a 'user'
code.

System codes are some kind of hardware interrupts like 'zerodivide' or
'operation exception'. They are hex, have three digits, and mostly, they
have a 'S' as a prefix: S0C7, S013 and so on. System Codes are
accompanied by some diagnostic messages in the joblog.
You look first at the 'MVS System Codes' manual (chapter '2.0 System
Completion Codes') and the you have a look a one of the five 'System
Messages' manuals for the meaning of the accompaning messages. All these
manuals ar online at:
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/Shelves/EZ2MO700

User abend codes are interrupts set by a user program or a subsystem
like the Language Environment or the DBMS. They are numeric, have four
digits, and mostly, they have a 'U' as prefix: U4093. For the
diagnostics, you have first to search for the program which caused the
user abend code and then look for the manuals for this program or
subsystem.
Language Environment codes are in the range of 4001 to 4099, but some
IMS codes are in the same range. User programs may use the same codes
too! The 'OS/390 V2R7.0 Lang Env for OS/390 & VM Debug & Msgs' manual is
at the same place where you find the system codes (see above). If you
still run the 'old' VS COBOL II runtime, look at:
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/Shelves/IGYSH007
Refer to the 'VS COBOL II R4 Appl. Programming Debugging' manual.

You might also need the IDMS manuals for their codes! Consult CA, their
homepage is http://www.cai.com. AFAIK, there is no manual online, but
they can send you a printed manual or a tape about IDMS. They also have
a problems/solutions database which can be accessed by registred
customers only (ask your system programmer for your customer-number and
subscribe at: http://webtrack.cai.com)

If you have a specific problem, you may ask the newsgoup comp.lang.cobol
or, maybe better, bit.listserv.ibm-main.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM completion codes

- **From:** Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373C1283.1B437D11@email.ais.unc.edu>`
- **References:** `<373AD9C6.36B5F5B0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7hgm69$o6g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm relatively new to the industry, but all the shops I've been at have
qw (quick reference).  You can enter just about any abend code on the
mainframe and it will give you a document on it.

e.g. qw s0c7

e.g. qw idcams

you can qw just about anything.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM completion codes

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hhdbs$6qq$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<373AD9C6.36B5F5B0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7hgm69$o6g$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <373C1283.1B437D11@email.ais.unc.edu>`

```
Matthew Son <esmks@email.ais.unc.edu> wrote in message
news:373C1283.1B437D11@email.ais.unc.edu...
> I'm relatively new to the industry, but all the shops I've been at
have
> qw (quick reference).  You can enter just about any abend code on
the
> mainframe and it will give you a document on it.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can qw just about anything.

Not all error messages for all products are either available or
present for MVS/QuickRef.  Of course, how modern your QuickRef is
helps matters.  Mine, for instance, is v3.5, no upgrades since 1991.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM completion codes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<373CD08A.3E8FE408@ix.netcom.com>`
