<a id="t-379bb13387"></a>
## Thread: In-line and out-of-line???? — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** "cleo" <cas.par@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi,

can someone please tell me when it is better to use either in-line or
out-of-line programming?



cheers!!!


cleo
```

#### ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pj%03.343$LI5.49234@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
cleo wrote in message <7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>...

>can someone please tell me when it is better to use either in-line or
>out-of-line programming?


If you are performing code in only one place in your program, an in-line
perform will show what you are performing and (of course) where to find it
in the program.  Code which would otherwise be replicated in various places
in the program should be placed in (a) paragraph(s) or a section to be
performed by name (out of line).

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Cleo,

You should used structured coding.  For the most part, this means that
you will not use in-line performs - unless it is something very simple. 
The processing time saved from in-line performs is typically negligible
compared to the potential added maintenance costs due to a lack of
readability.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37449eed.4400261@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 20 May 1999 18:22:27 -0400, when MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Cleo,
> 
> You should used structured coding.  For the most part, this means that
> you will not use in-line performs - unless it is something very simple.

Cleo, please ignore this person, whose definition of "structured" is
unrelated to its use by the rest of the computer industry.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b_213.231$6y2.8077@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews>`

```
>> You should used structured coding.  For the most part, this means that
>> you will not use in-line performs - unless it is something very simple.


When using any type of inline logic, whether it be PERFORMS, IF blocks, or
READ blocks make sure you indent any inline code inside of the block and
most of all USE SCOPE TERMINATORS. Just in case you are not familiar with
them they are END-PERFORM, END-IF, and END-READ.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews>`

```
Randall,

Thank you for constructively adding to the discussion of in-line
programming.  Your post has clearly delineated what "structured" coding
is and I stand corrected.  Your remarkable inductive and deductive
reasoning (along with some E.S.P.) have been able determine what I mean
by "structured" coding without my explicitly stating it.  Please do not
respond further to my posts because I fear that your mastery of ad
hominem will greatly diminish my self-respect.

For those of you without E.S.P., "structured" programming is based on a
paper by Bohm and Jacopini - "Flow Diagrams, Turing Machines and
Languages with Only Two Formation Rules," written in Communications of
the ACM from May of 1966.  To summarize: a structured program consists
of only three types of logic structures - sequence, selection, and
iteration.  A structure chart goes hand-in-hand with structured
programming.  It is top-down left-right with single-entry, single-exit
point paragraphs.

Typically, you should gather your specifications, clarify your
specifications, design a structure chart, code, then test.  Of
pertinence to the original question is the structure chart.  The
structure chart consists of boxes, lines, and decisions.  Ultimately,
each box should relate to exactly 1 paragraph.  A paragraph can perform
either actual statements or other paragraphs - I don't think you're
supposed to mix the two.  There are alot of other rules of thumb to
follow regarding structured programming, they can be ascertained by
reading a book or through (E.S.P) - your choice.

Now comes the question of in-line performs...  It is ok to use an
in-line perform for something simple.  Any complex in-line perform
should usually be broken down into further paragraphs.  There is a
performance hit with calling more and more paragraphs, but if you've
ever worked in a maintenance shop you'd be of the opinion that
maintainability is of far more importance than a negligible performance
hit.  (The performance cost will be equivalent to performing 1-2 lines
more of assembler - I'm not exactly sure, but i think we are talking
about less than 10 ^ -6 seconds).

Perhaps my understanding of "structured" coding is a bit academic, but
it does seem to work well in industry as well.  Salary is not indicative
of skill, but I have more than doubled mine in my less than two years in
the industry and have standing offers from all of my previous
employers.  

MKS

P.S. Are you an alterego of DocDwarf?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i494h$ej1$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com>`

```
Structure charts are associated with modular programming, not
structured programming. The content of the module could consist
of unstructured code.

Likewise, it is possible to create a program of more than 10 000
lines using structured programming. It is unlikely that such a program
would be modular.

Generally speaking, structured design consists of developing
programs from modules of structured code.

The concepts for modular programming and structured programming
were developed independently by the early 70's. By the mid-70's,
the concepts had been combined as the recommended method for
creating systems. Since then the distinction has been important only
for newsgoup discussions. ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith

MKS wrote in message <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com>...
[...]
>
>[...]  A structure chart goes hand-in-hand with structured
>programming.  It is top-down left-right with single-entry, single-exit
>point paragraphs.
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37498C5F.A8EFAD39@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com> <7i494h$ej1$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick,

Thank you for clarifying.  I did not know that there used to be a
distinction between structured programming and modular programming.  I
was taught (somewhat inaccurately I suppose) that they (structured and
modular) were basically the same thing.  I guess I had a misconception
of what "structured" was versus "modular."  

Anyhow, do you have any suggested reading for a fellow that is
interested in the history and development of COBOL?  I have a few Grauer
books but that's about it.

Matt
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7idghn$pr6$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com> <7i494h$ej1$1@server.cntfl.com> <37498C5F.A8EFAD39@hotmail.com>`

```

MKS wrote in message <37498C5F.A8EFAD39@hotmail.com>...
[...]
>Thank you for clarifying.  I did not know that there used to be a
>distinction between structured programming and modular programming.  I
>was taught (somewhat inaccurately I suppose) that they (structured and
>modular) were basically the same thing.  I guess I had a misconception
>of what "structured" was versus "modular."

There is still a difference between SP and MP and I did not mean to
imply otherwise. What I meant was that after MP and SP were
combined as SD (or more properly SD/SP), the individual using SD
had no need to distinguish between SP and MP, except for newsgroup
discussions.

MP is decomposing the system into small, meaningful, pieces and
writing the code such that the pieces cooperate, but do not interfere
with each other. SP is writing the code to conform to certain flow
control constructs and is an acceptable means for writing code that
conforms to MP.

One other important difference is that MP provides guidelines (rules?)
for encapsulating data and defining interfaces. SP does not.

The definitional problem is that MP defines the structure of the system,
but not the (flow control) code. SP defines the structure of the (flow
control) code, but not the system. Hence, they both deal with structuring,
but do so at different levels.

Further complicating this is that "structured" won the buzzword war.
Hence, we see SA, SD, and SP rather than MA, MP + SP. (SP
being the method for writing the code for MP.)

>Anyhow, do you have any suggested reading for a fellow that is
>interested in the history and development of COBOL?  I have a few Grauer
>books but that's about it.

I imagine the definitive reference is the CODASYL COBOL Commitee,
Journal of Development. These publications might be available in the
libraries of some older universities or in the reference libraries at some
computer manufacturers, who produced COBOL compilers.

I have an article from Datapro Research, August 1980, which is titled
"Proposed CODASYL COBOL Language Standards -- 1978".
This article summarizes the history and approved proposals for changes
in the COBOL language from 1960 through 1978. There is, of course,
a more detailed description of the (not yet approved) proposed changes
for 1978.

Notably, the first item mentioned for the next discussion, in 1978, was
"A facility to define intrinsic and user-defined functions." Intrinsic
functions made it into the 1989 amendment to the 1985 standard and
user-defined functions are proposed for the next standard.

More relevent to modular and structured progamming was the approval
of nested programs, in 1978, and the addition of scope terminators,
CONTINUE, and in-line PERFORM, also in 1978. These were included
in the 1985 standard.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374DC605.4A64D212@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com> <7i494h$ej1$1@server.cntfl.com> <37498C5F.A8EFAD39@hotmail.com> <7idghn$pr6$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick,

Thank you for the thorough, well-written response.  I really appreciate
it.


All,

I find it interesting how the seemingly 'simplest' questions result in
the most elaborate and numerous posts.  It's almost as if questions like
"In-line and out-of-line????", "Contracting from home", "Help needed for
learning COBOL", and "Need help! for cobol user compant" are
metaphysical questions.  I am really glad I have started using
newsgroups (I heard they were for posting porno pictures).  My hats off
to the technical wizards who take the time to discuss and explain things
with those who are less adept.

I would also like others to take note of how Rick's last post was
written.  I think there a lot of people who know a lot about COBOL, but
it is rarer to find someone who is not only technically skilled, but
also socially graceful, patient, well-spoken (written), and
professional.  Instead of writing a useless response such as - 'no, this
is wrong' - he clearly stated what was correct, and further elaborated
with an educated response.  Instead of arrogantly and demeaningly
responding, he answered with class and professionalism.  It is apparent
that Rick is knowledgeable and doesn't need to 'prove' that he is
superior to others.  

I don't recall which thread it was, but there was also a conversation
regarding fair pay and learning new skills - I think it was the TSO/JCL
thread.  Anyhow, I believe it was Mr. Foskey who wrote exactly what I
was thinking in regards to a good professional work ethic - basically
that we should learn more and work hard and let the rest fall in place. 
I found it interesting that the other poster, who is apparently also
very knowledgeable, seemed so petty and immature.  I think the former
attitude will lead to good business relationships, respect from peers,
and general success in life, while the latter attitude will lead to
limited social and professional development, contempt from bosses and
co-workers alike, and ending up like the comic-book store owner from the
Simpsons.

Anyhow, I ramble...  I just wanted to put in my two cents before I move
on to my next job (where I don't think I'll have much time left for
anything else).

Regards,

Matt
```

which the business finds to be valuable and wishes to use then, rightly or
wrongly, I expect to be paid for it.'

To ask otherwise is, I would say, as detestable as my asking for pay for
hours I did not work; I want money, I work for it, an employer/client
wants skills exercised, they pay for it.  The street runs both ways, last

bend over and pay for their own Vaseline.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3746234f.17666131@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 21 May 1999 08:55:01 -0400, when MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Randall,
> 
> Thank you for constructively adding to the discussion of in-line
> programming.  

You are most welcome.

> Your post has clearly delineated what "structured" coding
> is and I stand corrected.

No it didn't, nor did I try to nor claim to.

> Your remarkable inductive and deductive
> reasoning (along with some E.S.P.) have been able determine what I mean
> by "structured" coding without my explicitly stating it. 

You implicitly stated that in-line PERFORMs are unstructured, and this
does not follow from any definition of "structured" I have ever seen.  

> Please do not
> respond further to my posts because I fear that your mastery of ad
> hominem will greatly diminish my self-respect.

Here is where your ESP comes into play.  I am a master of ad hominem, but
I didn't use any in that post.  Ad hominem would be something like "don't
listen to this idiot".  I was doing the opposite, I was pointing out that
while you were calling for structured coding, you had no idea what
structured coding is.

> For those of you without E.S.P., "structured" programming is based on a
<snip>

Here you go into a long diatribe.  Some of it is about structured
programming, some is about modular design, and you've included various
other well regarded techniques.  So I was right, when you say "structured"
you mean any suggested technique.  Your statement "You should used
structured coding" means "you should use suggested techniques", which is a
tautology and should be ignored. 

> P.S. Are you an alterego of DocDwarf?

No, but I take the comparison as a compliment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e#K0dhCp#GA.369@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com>`

```
    I hate to get back on the original subject, but I always assumed that
in-line meant no performs at all.  No goto loops either.  I think that the
MF docs stated that "local" or "in-line" (there's that term again) performs
were slower than "remote" performs, or at least hadrer to optimize.

    I suppose that the original question could refer to either useage of the
phrase "in-line".

    This alternate meaning of the phrase "in-line" can be used only in a few
restricted circumstances.  You need to know how many loops will be executed
at compile time, and the total number of loops must be small.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37468EDC.B5049A3@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37448B23.649DBBBC@hotmail.com> <37449eed.4400261@netnews> <374557A5.DC9B948@hotmail.com> <e#K0dhCp#GA.369@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Out of the ashes of stupidity arises a phoenix, thanks Russell.

What is pertinent from MKS's post is that yes it is better to be
modular and pull exceptionally long sequential code into separate
sections. (Is this what MKS really means?)

I would have assumed that the original reference was the
difference between perform PARA and perform .. end-perform however
Russell, you are quite right it could have been modularisation
concept.

When you break down long sequences into paragraphs of instructions
it tends to be self documenting (well more than a long list of
statements). It does so the structure of the sequential process
clearly (ala a design chart).  As MKS pointed out the effect on
run times is negligible and optimizing compilers probably just put
them in sequence making a documentation only issue.

Personally I don't use design charts.  I code out the paragraphs
and then flesh them out.  Amounts pretty much to the same thing
but no fancy overheads.  You should be able to structure simple
programs in your minds eye.  Note that simple should be relative
to experience.

Ken

PS:  MKS, lighten up, we are all here to converse in a civil
manner and I think that you failed on this basic premise.
```

#### ↳ Re: In-line and out-of-line????

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2XZ03.149$lI5.42337@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7i1na4$ebr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
You use whichever makes the logic clearer and cleaner, except in
those unusual cases where performance is a premium, when you go for
inline code.
```

For a computer assignment I need to investigate a mainframe computer
system
& how it's used in an organisation/company.  (No one seems willing to
give any of their company information away and rightly so).  Please
could anyone  provide the following
information.  A  name of organisation using a mainframe  computer (could
even be a false company name)  and a brief description of the type of
business carried out by it.  The make/model of computer, main memory
capacity, type of storage and  capacity, input  and output devices used,
speed of the computer, processing power of the computer, how is the mini
computer  used by the organisation,
briefly describe the types of applications run on it. Thanks, Wendy.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEED MAINFRAME HELP

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374983E3.D70332CB@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<374409B0.A8B427F7@pop.tig.com.au>`

```
Deri,

I think the Gartner Group does something similar to what you are looking
for.  Typically, they charge hundreds of thousands of dollars for it. 
However, you can probably do the same thing they do.  You can try cold
calling several organizations and see if any will help you out.  There
are thousands of organizations which use mainframes - most banks,
utility companies, etc.  Try contacting people in their IS department
(probably too busy to help you) or you can try people in the Public
Relations department whose job it is to be nice to the public.  If you
tried 2 dozen companies, you should get at least a few responses.

MKS
```

<a id="t-511dbffe55"></a>
## Thread: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe — 18 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net>`

```
When writing to a file (Mainframe batch) I understand that there is no
guarantee of the data actually being written unless the file is closed. Am I
correct?

In C, there is a flush() function that acts a little like a COMMIT. On
COBOL, I don't think there is anything similar. Is this true?

My file is simply a sequential output file. So, my program can keep closing
the file and opening it again in EXTEND. The file may be going to DASD or to
TAPE/CART. Should I be using the "NO REWIND" option?

Is there anything I can specify in the JCL that will force a write-through
of each record? If so, will this me a bigger performance hit than closing
and reopening the file?

I'm sure this is a familiar situation to many folks here. I'd appreciate any
advice as to the best way to handle this.

Thanks,

- Darius Cooper
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<7i0tb5$p4t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net>`

```
In article <g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net>,
  "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com> wrote:
> When writing to a file (Mainframe batch) I understand that there is no
> guarantee of the data actually being written unless the file is
closed. Am I
> correct?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> My file is simply a sequential output file. So, my program can keep
closing
> the file and opening it again in EXTEND. The file may be going to DASD
or to
> TAPE/CART. Should I be using the "NO REWIND" option?
>
> Is there anything I can specify in the JCL that will force a
write-through
> of each record? If so, will this me a bigger performance hit than
closing
> and reopening the file?
>
> I'm sure this is a familiar situation to many folks here. I'd
appreciate any
> advice as to the best way to handle this.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Darius,

First of all, fflush() doesn't work on the mainframe as it would work in
a UNIX system. AFIK it worked with C/370, but not with the current
OS/390 compilers.

But now, the good news: You have not to care about physical writing on
the mainframe. The operationg system does it for you. Even in case of an
ABEND, the Language Environment closes all files for you. Only if you
set TRAP(OFF) or NOSTAE,NOSPIE, the last record might be lost.
If this parameter is set, the only solution I know is to set the BLKSIZE
of the file equal to the LRECL, but this is a HEAVY performance problem,
before all if you write to a tape.

And forget about rewind and open extend. Just open - write - close and
leave the rest to the OS. That's what everybody does.

Daniel
------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com


--== Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/ ==--
---Share what you know. Learn what you don't.---
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37440863.AD82BD0F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net>`

```
Darius Cooper wrote:
> 
> In C, there is a flush() function that acts a little like a COMMIT. On
> COBOL, I don't think there is anything similar. Is this true?
> 

There is no need for flush in MVS because you are not using pipes
nor terminals.  In cobol the only reason that you might have
problems is that if you do not close the file an additional junk
record is written.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net>`

```
Darius Cooper wrote:
> 
> When writing to a file (Mainframe batch) I understand that there is no
> guarantee of the data actually being written unless the file is closed. Am I
> correct?

I don't know what you are talking about.  I think you might be
confused.  A COBOL program writes to the output file when it executes
the write.  There is no "commit".  You might be thinking of the JCL that
might be used to run the job.  You can specify the disposition of the
dataset.  If you choose new,keep,keep - it will allocate a dataset and
keep it if the job ends normally or if it abends.  If you choose
old,delete,delete - it will grab the dataset (no one else can use it)
and delete the file when the job ends (normally or abends).  There are
other dispositions as well, mod and pass.  This is the closest thing I
can think of that you might be referring to.  

> In C, there is a flush() function that acts a little like a COMMIT. On
> COBOL, I don't think there is anything similar. Is this true?

There is a commit when you write to a database - DB2 for example.  I do
not think that there is a commit for a file in COBOL.

> My file is simply a sequential output file. So, my program can keep closing
> the file and opening it again in EXTEND. The file may be going to DASD or to
> TAPE/CART. Should I be using the "NO REWIND" option?

Why would you open and close the file multiple times in the same
program?
 
> Is there anything I can specify in the JCL that will force a write-through
> of each record? If so, will this me a bigger performance hit than closing
> and reopening the file?

It might be easier if you explain what you are trying to do first (big
picture).  I think you might want to specify mod,keep,keep.  This will
use an old dataset if the DSN exists.  It will create a new one if it
doesn't exist.  It will append data to the end of the file.  It will
keep the result whether or not the job abends or not.
 
> I'm sure this is a familiar situation to many folks here. I'd appreciate any
> advice as to the best way to handle this.

Matt
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37441905.40E8EAF8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com>`

```
MKS wrote:
> 
> Darius Cooper wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> can think of that you might be referring to.

But on mainframes, records are saved up until a block is ready to be
written.  Sometimes, when I want to see the progress of a running
program, I will write a page full of displays - to make sure there were
enough data to do an actual write to the output queue.  I don't know
what happens when the system goes down while writing a flat file - I
always start over in this case.  With databases, the appropriate tools
to commit our updates are available.  When we write to a file using
random access, I imagine the write is immediate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37442197.70C9A499@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com> <37441905.40E8EAF8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard,

If you change the disp of your output file to (,keep,keep) you will be
able to see the last record written regardless of whether or not it
abends.  However, if you are in the process of debugging your program,
it might be just as easy to write to the sysout with displays.  

I'm not certain about when the physical write actually takes place (re:
waiting until a block is sufficiently full), but I think it would write
out what is there in the case the program prematurely ends.  If you have
your disposition set appropriately you will be able to see your output. 
If you have a disposition of delete on an abend - the file will not be
out there.

Again, explain what you are trying to do.  

MKS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<3744521B.70ADC79B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com> <37441905.40E8EAF8@NOSPAMhome.com> <37442197.70C9A499@hotmail.com>`

```
MKS wrote:
> 
> Howard,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Again, explain what you are trying to do.

I was trying to add to a discussion.

I have noticed that when I have a program which does an initial display
to SYSOUT=*, I don't see that displayed line.  If I display a bunch of
lines, I do see them.  This indicates to me that, at least of SYSOUT=*,
that nothing is actually written until a block has been filled up, or
until some other event happens to tell the hardware to do the write. 
The COBOL program can force this write by doing a close.  It has been my
impression that if you examine a file saved by (old,keep,keep) after an
abort - you may, or may not find the last record which the COBOL program
thinks it wrote. 

I am guessing that the OS might write the buffer when it closes down the
file after the abort, but obviously a power failure would eliminate this
possibility.  At any rate, I prefer to not trust this to be true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** MKS <m_k_s@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<374474F6.2CAD98CB@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com> <37441905.40E8EAF8@NOSPAMhome.com> <37442197.70C9A499@hotmail.com> <3744521B.70ADC79B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard,

> > Again, explain what you are trying to do.
> 
> I was trying to add to a discussion.

My apologies, I thought you were the original poster.  I did not mean to
be abrupt or rude.  I was trying to get clarification from the original
poster.  
 
> I have noticed that when I have a program which does an initial display
> to SYSOUT=*, I don't see that displayed line.  If I display a bunch of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> thinks it wrote.

SYSOUT=* does not mean anything in itself.  The asterisk tells the job
to use whatever has been specified by the job or system beforehand. 
Usually it seems to mean to write to the output queue.  When you say
that you are not "seeing" the output, where are you looking.  Your job
settings might be displaying the output to the screen or to a queue.  I
haven't been in the business very long, but I have yet to see a physical
processing error, i.e. if the computer is told to write somewhere - it
definitely does write to where it has been told.  There might be a
failure rate, but I think that it would be less than 0.00001%.  As to
the last record being missing from a file after an abend, this depends
on whether or not the last record was successful.  If the program abends
and the last processed record was #101 and the last record you see on
the output file is #100, it is probably because #101 failed (for
whatever reason).  If you check the logic carefully and pinpoint exactly
where the abend happened, you will probably find that the operating
system did what it was supposed to.
 
> I am guessing that the OS might write the buffer when it closes down the
> file after the abort, but obviously a power failure would eliminate this
> possibility.  At any rate, I prefer to not trust this to be true.

Again, in my experience, the computer rarely does not do what it is told
to do.  I have encountered a few problems, but it is very very rare.

Matt
```

(snipped)

> SYSOUT=* does not mean anything in itself.  The asterisk tells the job

It means to use the class from the MSGCLASS parameter in the JOB card.

> Usually it seems to mean to write to the output queue. When you say
> that you are not "seeing" the output, where are you looking.

He's looking in SDSF. This is a common occurance and I believe Howard's
explanation is correct, meaning SDSF doesn't display partial blocks
while the file is open (the partial block in this case is the block
whose buffer is being filled as I'm displaying the SYSOUT for the job).

I'm curious as to what you mean by "output queue"?

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374554B5.6E869149@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com> <37441905.40E8EAF8@NOSPAMhome.com> <37442197.70C9A499@hotmail.com> <3744521B.70ADC79B@NOSPAMhome.com> <374474F6.2CAD98CB@hotmail.com>`

```
Basically SYSOUT is still DASD somewhere on the system.  Some of
the sysout readers (SDSF and others) are not especially good at
getting the data from active jobs out of these areas.  I think
that some MVS systems are not set flush the output as often as
they can, for performance reasons.

You might want to check out the buffering options in SYSOUT if you
really need instant access.  The sequential files are
automatically set to buffers=2 so sysout may have something very
similar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37441C1D.82403AFF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <37440351.3AC7FE0F@hotmail.com>`

```
> Why would you open and close the file multiple times in the same
> program?

Good question.  Let's come up with some reasons:

Open input, process data, close, open extend, add record, close.

Open access is sequential.  Close, open access is random.

I have seen OPEN output write a header, close then open extend - but I'm
drawing a blank at the moment as to why this was done.

Any other ideas?
```

Darius,

being written.

don't need to worry.  If it is DAYS, then you might need to get into 
some checkpoint processing.  There's no need to worrry about forcing 
the write before the close.

On Thu, 20 May 1999 10:02:55, "Darius Cooper" 
<Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com> wrote:

> guarantee of the data actually being written unless the file is closed. Am I
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

-------------------------

this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

#### ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<7i1bil$ptp@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net>`

```
I have read several replies to this original question - some of which are
absolutely correct and others less so; some of which seem to understand
COBOL on MVS (OS/390) more than others.

  NOTE: This thread is talking ONLY about "sequential" files, i.e. QSAM.  I
think the issues for VSAM are similar - but not identical.

In truth and fact, IF (and only if) an OS/390 COBOL program is writing
output to a "blocked" file (I don't think that buffering matters - but I
could be wrong on this), then there is a POTENTIAL problem between any
"logical" WRITE with a "successful" I/O status and the "physical" write of
that block (whether it is at a CLOSE or simply a filled block).  Therefore,
if performance is *NOT* a consideration, then you can use UNBLOCKED files
(DCB=F/V rather than DCB=FB/VB) to "guarantee" that your COBOL program will
obtain the "correct" I/O status code after every WRITE.  (You could also use
blocked records - with the LRECL = BLKSIZE as suggested in other notes, but
I don't see what that would get you - other than more wasted space for the
block descriptor word.)  ON THE OTHER HAND, even this would only let you
know that you have a "problem" when the I/O error occurred - it would not
guarantee that all previous records were "safe" (which is what I think that
the desire for "commit" capability was asking for).  For example, if you did
3 WRITEs that "worked" (to an unblocked file) and then a 4th WRITE got an
error, the chances are that the entire file would be "messed up".  One part
of the thread talked about closing the file after each write and then
opening it up either via the COBOL "OPEN EXTEND" or the JCL "DISP=MOD" - but
here again, if the file has a problem on the next OPEN or WRITE, my guess
(assumption? certainty?) would be that the file would all be messed up
anyway - so all previous WRITEs wouldn't be "saved" by this technique
either.

As someone who "trusts" the OS/390 (MVS) file systems, it is hard for me to
imagine designing a system where I was "guaranteed" that all previous WRITE
statements were "saved" - no matter what happened with my next WRITE.  I
guess you would have to close the file after every write, then copy a
back-up file to a 2nd back-up file, then copy the new file to the 1st backup
file and THEN re-open your active file (or some "obscure" variation of
this).  It would seem to me that looking at the OS/390 and/or COBOL "rerun"
facility would be much more appropriate than this (but I suppose there MIGHT
be some application design requirement that might necessitate such a
"secure" WRITE facility).

The bottom-line (IMHO) is:
   A) The chance of a WRITE in COBOL (for a blocked file) being
uccessful  - but the physical write of the block failing are slim - but not
0%. (If the physical write is what fails, then the COBOL I/O status code
would only show a bad value on that last I/O statement.)
   B) Using unblocked files would let you know with each and every WRITE
statement whether it has succeeded (in a physical WRITE) - but the
performance implications are pretty horrendous.
   C) Nothing other than a rather "tortured" application design is going to
give you GUARANTEED "safe" records after a COBOL WRITE - until you are
finished using that file (and even then - the next OPEN of it as INPUT could
"destroy" it somehow).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<3744878C.D6899A97@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <7i1bil$ptp@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I have read several replies to this original question - some of which are
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> obtain the "correct" I/O status code after every WRITE.  (You could also use
...
> The bottom-line (IMHO) is:
>    A) The chance of a WRITE in COBOL (for a blocked file) being
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Klein
...
> > When writing to a file (Mainframe batch) I understand that there is no
> > guarantee of the data actually being written unless the file is closed. Am
> > I correct?
...

For QSAM output, a write request is made to the operating system
whenever (1) a full-block is contained in a buffer, or (2) the file is
closed, and there is a partial block remaining to be written.  Except at
CLOSE, the write requests proceed asynchronously and the program is only
forced to wait for a successful completion if all buffers are
exhausted.  So, in a situation where the program is able to generate
records much faster than the I/O subsystem can write, the total number
of buffers allocated (QSAM default is 5) could in a worst-case scenario
represent the number of blocks that might be in-flight and subject to
loss in the event of catastrophic failure.  Having said that, such
catastophic failures in the MVS world are very rare - unless you have
power instabilities and are running without a UPS. 

There are a few scenarios short of system failure in which the automatic
CLOSE (either because of program ABEND or because the program simply
fails to CLOSE a dataset) can fail, but all the ones of which I am aware
are the result of application "bugs".  The automatic CLOSE requires that
file control blocks and buffers be intact, so obviously if the program
runs amok in some way that trashes or releases storage containing the
required information, you couldn't expect an auto-CLOSE to preserve
in-flight records.  This is also the only way I can conceive that you
might end up with garbage records in the file, as opposed to just losing
records.

There are environments in MVS where the problem of in-flight records is
explicitly addressed:  VSAM updates under CICS using journaling support
and SYNC points, and DB2 with COMMIT processing.  Both of these use
non-QSAM I/O techniques to insure that there is a point when log records
are "known" to have been successfully written or committed to the
hardware I/O subsystem.  This is a serious issue in online environments,
where re-creation of the user interactions supplying data to the
application can be very difficult.  It really shouldn't be an issue in a
batch application.  There are many reasons why a step in a batch job may
fail: unanticipated input (perhaps a user error), inadequate file sizes
(the user may have doubled the amount of data from the time of
application design), tape drive failure, operator finger-check, etc. 
One of the cardinal rules of batch job design is that there should be
restart capabilty designed into the job stream to handle a failure in
any job step. Files essential to job restart that are changed or
destroyed by the job stream should either have a backup or be
recoverable by some other means.  Then there is no need to treat restart
from a rare system failure any differently than restart from the much
less rare application job-step failure, where the contents of all
datasets with update activity is suspect.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<7i2c8p$oeg$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <7i1bil$ptp@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7i1bil$ptp@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>...

<with regard to file commit snippage>

Bill, I know nothing about IBM mainframe, but if I am following your
explanation, how can you accomplish something like transaction
processing or record level lockout?  Of course why one would want
to on a line sequential file is quite another question.

Some sort of "commit" statement is going to be almost required
to share files well, unless everthing is queued to a server.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i2nfj$dup@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <7i1bil$ptp@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <7i2c8p$oeg$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald (not CC'ing the IBM-MAIN list where this is "understood")

First, the original question explicitly asked about "batch" processing.  By
and large, "QSAM" (which is "record sequential" in PC-ish terms - there is
no "line sequential" as such) is NEVER used in "transaction processing"
environments.  (That is a gross over-simplification - but does somewhat
answer your question.)

"Long ago and far away" - when ANSI/ISO were FIRST starting to think about
what to put in the next Standard (then hoped for about 1996 or 1997!), it
was explicitly decided NOT to add "commit" and "rollback" syntax/semantics
to standard COBOL.  Interestingly enough, they are now even backing away
from requiring record-locking/file-sharing in "new" compliant compilers.
(The IBM DISP=SHR/OLD facility EXPLICITLY provides file-level sharing and
*not* record-level lockouts as mentioned in Donald's question.)

In a "traditional" MVS (OS/390) environment, there are other software
products (IMS, CICS, DB2, - etc) which do the "transaction" processing and
COBOL "sequential" files are either explicitly prohibited (e.g. CICS) or
cause strong application design restrictions (e.g. IMS/BMPs).  (IBMers - I
don't think that we need to get into VSAM ESDS files - which are neither
"fish nor fowl nor good red-meat" when it comes to COBOL sequential file
processing.)

ON THE OTHER HAND (as indicated elsewhere in this thread), the actual risk
of "losing data" because of block (or buffer) WRITE failures are minimal.
IBM also provides (from "ye goode olde tape days") significant "re-run"
features which is how the old non-transaction applications usually handled
such risks.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WRITING TO A FILE on Mainframe

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<rXZ03.8$UA.221@news.megsinet.net>`
- **References:** `<g2R03.38$_c.164@news.megsinet.net> <7i1bil$ptp@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Thank you to all for the replies; very useful.

In a nutshell, it seems I can truct the mainframe. My application runs on
AIX and on the mainframe, and has some stuff that isn't relevant for one
platform or the other. This CLOSE/OPEN loop seems to be one such thing.

Thanks again,

- Darius

PS: Now all I need is for an AIX guru to tell me that I don't need this on
AIX either :)
```

## Thread: Fujitsu V4.2 - Dot matrix. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu V4.2 - Dot matrix.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress third Party Tools

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i1r9p$rre@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20BCE4157A75D2118C0700104B6348C301ADB621@mail1> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-CFcdVlF9xunJ@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <20BCE4157A75D2118C0700104B6348C301B04C0E@mail1>`

```
I am confused.

What makes you think that you need to do ANY conversion when moving a Micro
Focus "DOS" application to NetExpress?

Now, if you actually want to "upgrade" the user-interface, then I can see
that some work will be required, but to get the program to execute the same
way it always has in the past, I think you should simply need to recompile.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress third Party Tools

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37455862.1298937@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<20BCE4157A75D2118C0700104B6348C301ADB621@mail1> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-CFcdVlF9xunJ@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <20BCE4157A75D2118C0700104B6348C301B04C0E@mail1> <7i1r9p$rre@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I am confused.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Peacez

Bill:

I do agree with you 100%.  If Peacez simply wants to upgrade his
development system without modification to his program source or
target environment, then there really should be no need to buy or even
evaluate additional tools.

I believe that Thane assumed that Mr. Peacez also wanted to modify his
program to run in a Graphical User Interface.  We usually suggest to
customers that they try the tools which are included with the compiler
and then try various evaluation versions of 3rd party tools in
conjunction with the preferred compiler before committing to a
project.

The analysis should reveal which is the best combination of tools for
their specific operation.  Sometimes they prefer the tools which are
bundled with the compiler...sometimes they prefer others.  The best
part about this from the customer's perspective is that they can
choose which combination affords them the highest productivity,
essential features and easiest ongoing maintenance.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress third Party Tools

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz at home dot com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i35dk$9jg@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
i'll assume you want to change the UI.

Dialog System in Net Express is a very good & mature GUI builder that was
built specifically for COBOL programs. Net Express also has a GUI builder
("Form Designer") that can help you take your existing application and build
an internet browser based GUI.

more info at:

http://www.merant.com/ads/dc/products/netex30.asp

Gazaloo.

peacez <peacez@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:20BCE4157A75D2118C0700104B6348C301B04C0E@mail1...
> thaks
> We have a lot of Microfocus cobol sources for dos. We want to convert to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3744EF93.E6F580DB@siber.com>`

```
peacez wrote:
> 
> hi all,

We were thinking about one here, at Siber Systems, and
decided to go out and get some collective wisdom on this.

Fujitsu is expected to have a DLL that lets Crystal Reports
read Fujitsu Cobol data files. We heard that there is no such
tool for MF yet.

I have some questions:

- Has anybody heard of such tool?

- Does anybody need such tool?
  If yes, what would be a tolerable price for it.

- What are licensing conditions for MF's EXTFH library,
  if it's used separately from MF compiler, for a project like this.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-a3a8640e35"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol Help / Direction — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol Help / Direction

- **From:** "Chet" <skirk@magicnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i0vkn$2v9$1@comet3.magicnet.net>`

```
Subject: Cobol Direction / Help

We have an accounting package originally written in RM Cobol that we run
through MFocus 32bit.  We had some printing & misc problems when running
their 16bit version so we decided to get them fixed with the 32 bit version
release.  Since installing that version in about 50 customer sites (there's
no turning back), we've had nothing but problems.  The biggest being speed
on a multi-user network and keyboard problems.

The keyboard problems we've had are as follows

1.  If your mouse is moving when you  run exe with mouse and all programs
wait for you to hit a key on the keyboard.  The same program runs fine
through dos or if you do not move your mouse while executing.

2.  Caps Lock - if caps lock is on number keys work like the shift is down .
This seems to be only a Windows 98 problem.

Bottom line is that we've about had it with Microfocus.  We've installed the
latest update and that fixed nothing.  They are also telling us that the
4.38 release is the last.

Can anyone recommend a direction we should go in?  Should we shift gears
completely and go with Fujitsu, IBM, or Accucobol?  Are any of those system
compatible with RM?

We're in a big bind and any help would be greatly appreciated.

Scott
Ucs, Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol Help / Direction

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i17dg$d5f$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7i0vkn$2v9$1@comet3.magicnet.net>`

```
Chet wrote in message <7i0vkn$2v9$1@comet3.magicnet.net>...
>Bottom line is that we've about had it with Microfocus.  We've installed
the
>latest update and that fixed nothing.  They are also telling us that the
>4.38 release is the last.

That's too bad. I understand your frustration and is somewhat surprised that
a company doesn't do anything more to help out there customers. You know
that Microsoft Windows x in general is a pain in the ass when it comes to
networking, and are sure that there is not something with the network/os
configuration causing your problems?

Imagine for instance, that Microsoft Windows NT Server is being installed
from Microsoft with fileserver capabilites off! What is that for a server?
What they do is to optimize the software as an application server/
workstation to gain optimum performance for C/S server apps. Traditional
filesystems suffer significantly of this, particularly when it comes to
record lock.

If you are mixing Microsoft and Novell its even worse, due to configuration
issues and bugs.

If this is the cause, Microfocus is not the one to blame, other than that
they should have helped you by providing how-to's. I don't know Microfocus
products i particular though.

>Can anyone recommend a direction we should go in?  Should we shift gears
>completely and go with Fujitsu, IBM, or Accucobol?  Are any of those system
>compatible with RM?

Generally speaking, I would guess what your target environment is, is
something which will rule the recommendation.

I have been using Acucobol for several years and are well satisfied with
them. I will particularly honor their support which is quick and efficient.
I came from Microsoft, so I have no experience with it, but Acucobol is said
to be RM compatible, you may look up their web (www.acucobol.com) to get
details about that. Acucorp are starting their annual conferences very soon
now, you may want to do as we did back then when we decided, we participated
a conference to see what it was, meet people who used it and gain
impressions. We went for it, and were never sorry about it.

Someone else here told that Fujitsu had a problem being ported between
different versions of Windows, regarding IBM Visual Cobol, I have no idea.

I guess it will not be an easy choice, there is a number of good products
out there, I whish you good luck with your decision. I am certain it will be
wise, whichever you choose.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol Help / Direction

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-diUCtghCwd4R@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7i0vkn$2v9$1@comet3.magicnet.net>`

```
On Thu, 20 May 1999 12:36:39, "Chet" <skirk@magicnet.net> wrote:

> Can anyone recommend a direction we should go in?  Should we shift gears
> completely and go with Fujitsu, IBM, or Accucobol?  Are any of those system
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You probably made the switch to MF because of their RM emulation back 
in the "bad old days" when RM was going down the tubes.  You will be 
pleased to know that RM (now Liant) has been rescued from the Abyss 
and is FLOURISHING.  They have great products, and good support.  Why 
not go back to RM?  http://www.liant.com

 

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol Help / Direction

- **From:** mark.warren@merant.com (Mark Warren)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37485c87.248415061@news.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7i0vkn$2v9$1@comet3.magicnet.net>`

```
On 20 May 1999 12:36:39 GMT, "Chet" <skirk@magicnet.net> wrote:

>Subject: Cobol Direction / Help
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I've followed this up with our Support teams and I believe they are
working with you on the issues right now.

I just wanted to comment that V4.0.38 ( I assume that's what you meant
by 3.38) is not necessarily the last but it is the latest. The
"Classic" family of products (COBOL Workbench, Object COBOL etc.) are
still being supported. We may see fewer complete Update Disks as there
isn't such a need for them, but we will create them if needed. Even
without Update Disks, we do  issue fix packs. I believe a mailing on
this topic was sent to all customers very recently detailing our
support well into the next millenium..

If you have any more questions about the above, please do email me
directly. I will be out next week, so please don't take no reply as a
snub!


Regards,
Mark Warren
MERANT International, Newbury, UK
Email: Mark.Warren@MERANT.COM Phone: 01635 565308
```

## Thread: File Status 9/060 with MF-COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### File Status 9/060 with MF-COBOL

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6YQ03.37$_c.34@news.megsinet.net>`

```
Has anyone ever got a 9/060 code with MF-COBOL? If you did, and you figured
out what was causing it, I'd like to know.

According to the MF-COBOL documentation, there is no such extended code as
"060".

- Darius
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status 9/060 with MF-COBOL

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-pvIIUdoumvXG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6YQ03.37$_c.34@news.megsinet.net>`

```
On Thu, 20 May 1999 09:56:21, "Darius Cooper" 
<Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com> wrote:

> Has anyone ever got a 9/060 code with MF-COBOL? If you did, and you figured
> out what was causing it, I'd like to know.
> 
> According to the MF-COBOL documentation, there is no such extended code as
> "060".

This isn't going to be much help, but I *HAVE* seen this before - 
reported at a client site.  I think it had to do with running Window 
95 as a client to Netware 3.12 or 4.1.  I think we had to have them 
install the Novell Client/32 drivers instead of using the default 
Window 95 Novell drivers.  It only happened on networks where SOME 
clients were DOS and some were Windows 95.

Does this sound like your situation?

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Status 9/060 with MF-COBOL

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<6YQ03.37$_c.34@news.megsinet.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-pvIIUdoumvXG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thank you for the reply.
My application is on AIX.
MF-COBOL says that there isn't such a code, and that our DECODING of the
extended error-code is incorrect.
Since this error came up at two client sites, but does not happen when we
run the app. in-house, we are unable to animate it and see the error-code.

Thanks again,

- Darius

### NEED HELP ON MAINFRAME

- **From:** Deri Best <delboy@pop.tig.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3743FE80.E045765A@pop.tig.com.au>`

```
For a TAFE computer assignment I need to investigate a mainframe
computer system
& how it's used in an organisation/company.  (No companies seem willing
to part with any details of their setup which is understandable)  Please
can anyone provide the  following  information, name of organisation
using mini computer (could use a false name) and a brief description of
the type of business carried out by  the company.  The make/model of
mainframe computer, main memory capacity, type of storage and  capacity,
input  and output devices used, speed of the computer, processing power
of the computer, how is the computer  used by the organisation, briefly
describe the types of applications run on it. Thanks, Wendy.
```

### NetExpress .. A VB alternative ??

- **From:** Bogy@Texas.NEt (Peter Gilchrist)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3745630b.2694269@news.texas.net>`

```
We are thinking of upgrading our MF Object Cobol 32 to NetExpress.
Currently, our MF cobol does a half-assed job at making application
front ends.   Will NetExpress provide a full Windows 95 GUI
application, much like we're using Visual Basic for now ??
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress .. A VB alternative ??

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3745630b.2694269@news.texas.net>`

```
On Fri, 21 May 1999 13:47:09, Bogy@Texas.NEt (Peter Gilchrist) wrote:

> We are thinking of upgrading our MF Object Cobol 32 to NetExpress.
> Currently, our MF cobol does a half-assed job at making application
> front ends.   Will NetExpress provide a full Windows 95 GUI
> application, much like we're using Visual Basic for now ??

The Windows GUI in NetExpress is Dialog System.  I don't know of 
anyone who is using it that really likes it.  

Fujitsu PowerCOBOL is more like Visual Basic than NetExpress is.

I prefer a third party tool - that you can use with your EXISTING 
COBOL - cheaper than buying NetExpress to boot - called COBOL sp2 from
Flexus - http://www.flexus.com

-------------------------

this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress .. A VB alternative ??

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-j5gg2dqwxfJX@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3745630b.2694269@news.texas.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-6WpSydtn8RfY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7i3un0$h8m@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
On Fri, 21 May 1999 15:28:59, "Gazaloo" <gaz at home dot com> wrote:

> Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in message
> news:Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-6WpSydtn8RfY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

There might be 10,000 active seats of NetExpress - which so far *IS* 
the premier package for delivering an HTML (Browser) interface for 
COBOL applications, but I seriously doubt that all 10,000 are using 
Dialog System.  It would inundate George with bug reports and support 
issues.

> if Dialog System doesn't ring your bell, try using the web-based Form
> Designer (included in Net Express) for building your GUI's.
>

As you can tell, I'm no fan of DS - I have developed full scale 
systems using it, and where I used to work, they are still struggling 
to deploy.  It is unstable in the extreme and very difficult to 
program with.  It's interpreted at runtime making Panels v2 calls that
in turn make calls that interface with the Windows API.  Features in 
older versions disappear in newer versions, (Containers anyone?), and 
it's just a pain ..

The word Dialog is used too much - It describes a type of Windows 
Object, the Product, and the scripting language.  The scripting 
language could have easily been made more COBOL like.

Simple examples appear to work fine - but start using multiple 
windows, events such as "gained" and "lost" focus, and event detection
at the object, window and gloal level and things soon become 
unmanageable.

DSRUNNER was the saving grace of the system in that it handles push 
and pops of screensets from the DS stack, but support for it dwindled 
in later versions of NE.  

I can go on and on.... I'll quit now.

Let's hear from those using it here:  

Who uses Dialog System and likes it?  (Not NetExpress - but rather 
specifically the DS componant).
-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

I used it with 2.0, but it corrupted projects so often we ended up 
deploying the 16 bit version.

Presently they are attempting to use 3.0 but my last report from them 
(2 months ago) was that while 3.0 was MUCH better from the Development
Tool side, the runtime still suffers instability and machine and OS 
sensitivity.   This is totally separate from the issues of development
style required by the product.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress .. A VB alternative ??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i43fh$knt@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3745630b.2694269@news.texas.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-6WpSydtn8RfY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7i3un0$h8m@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-j5gg2dqwxfJX@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>As you can tell, I'm no fan of DS - I have developed full scale 
>systems using it, and where I used to work, they are still struggling 
>to deploy.  It is unstable in the extreme and very difficult to 
>program with.  It's interpreted at runtime making Panels v2 calls that
>in turn make calls that interface with the Windows API.  

Thane, 
All Windows GUI products eventually make calls to the Windows API.
In fact, if the feature you want isn't available directly in Dialog 
System, you can often go directly to the API.

>Features in 
>older versions disappear in newer versions, (Containers anyone?), and 
>it's just a pain ..

Containers never disappeared exactly; the problem was that containers were 
an OS/2 control for which a Windows emulation was only available under 
16bit. Containers these days are implemented as an emulation using the 
Windows ListView control.

Finally, newer features such as ActiveX support mean that you don't have 
to use those old controls - you can use a table control bought from a 
third party, perhaps, and put it straight into your screenset.

And *in addition* to all of this, the NetExpress versions of Dialog System 
allow you to access the power of our GUI class libraries, providing yet 
another way for you to add functionality to your DS application (including 
using ListView controls more natively, if you want).

Steve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress .. A VB alternative ??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00

## Thread: Create a BitMap for NetExpress/Sp2 PushButton — 4 msgs

### Create a BitMap for NetExpress/Sp2 PushButton

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990520213902.14538.00003261@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
Hello,

I have a rather difficult problem that hopefully someone can make a comment on.
 I am using NetExpress with Sp2 from Flexus.  I have a screen that has numerous
pushbuttons on it.  These push buttons are defined as Icon/BitMap push buttons.
 So far no problem, here is the difficult part.  Part of the BitMap is text
that the user will set up in a control file.  So I can not build these BitMaps
in advance.  The text needs to be on top of the push button so it must be part
of the bitmap.  I have create several blank template bitmaps using PaintShop
Pro.  But now the problem is merging the text in the control file with the
blank template bitmap.  The user will be changing this control file often so my
program needs to be able to read the control file and then merge the text with
the template bitmap to form the updated pushbutton bitmap.  Is anyone aware of
a third party product that i can "CALL" or "WinExec" to accomplish this merge ?
This application is for a touch screen point of sale system.  Any ideas would
be most welcomed ?
Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Create a BitMap for NetExpress/Sp2 PushButton

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-xdtQ6PF1X4xG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<19990520213902.14538.00003261@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
On Fri, 21 May 1999 01:39:02, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

> Hello,
> 
> I have a rather difficult problem that hopefully someone can make a comment on.
>  I am using NetExpress with Sp2 from Flexus.  I have a screen that has numerous
> pushbuttons on it.  These push buttons are defined as Icon/BitMap push buttons.

Bob - 

There's a simple way to do this, Flexus showed me a month or two ago. 
I don't have my stuff here at work, but I've e-mailed you and Bob 
Wolfe - he's got a demo handy for it - I think.  Not sure about the 
"Dynamic" changing, but I think that's what we went over as I had a 
similar need.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Create a BitMap for NetExpress/Sp2 PushButton

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37455f3c.3052970@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<19990520213902.14538.00003261@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey


Hi Bob!

I think that I have a pretty good solution for you.

COBOL sp2 supports the ability for you to embed OCX (Active X
controls) in your user interface screen.  In addition, you can control
the properties and methods for these OCX's in the same way that you
control all of the standard Windows user interface controls.

FarPoint Technologies develops and sells a set of OCX controls called
Button Objx which allows you to display and control buttons with
embedded bitmaps and text.  The text can be dynamically changed by
modifying a property prior to display of your sp2 screen.

The other cool thing about Button Objx is that you can also display
buttons which are not limited to square or rectangualr shapes...that
means you could display round, oval, triangular, etc. shapes.

You can download a trial version of the Button Objx OCX control at:

http://www.fpoint.com/products/button/button.html

You can also find a wide assortment of OCX components at:

http://www.vbxtras.com

I'll send you the COBOL sp2 Active X control add on in a private
e-mail message.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Create a BitMap for NetExpress/Sp2 PushButton

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-05-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990521172502.16600.00000042@ng-fq1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37455f3c.3052970@news.enter.net>`

```
Hello Bob and Thane,

Thanks as allways for your help.  I believe that your suggestions will work
great.

Thanks again,
Bob Hennessey
```

- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37463cac.445222359@news.chaven.com>`

```

Hello,

We had a developer leave and she took whatever she 
used to write & compile those cobol programs with 
her.  I downloaded the Fujitsu Cobol 85 Editor & 
compiler to try to fix and compile the programs, 
but every line on the program generates an error. 

What gives?  I mean everything looks correct.  
Stuff starts as it should on columns 8 and 12 and 
comments in column 7......

Can anyone offer any suggestions?  IF it is was 
Micro Focus Cobol.....I cannot get it to convert.

Please send e-mail......Thanks a lot,

Thanks,

Arthur
amerar@unsu.com
```

John

>previous rate as a contractor...she's evidently a very intelligent lady...

Unethical and stupid is more like it.  Who wants to do business with

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Problem

- **From:** "JC" <john@jeclark.globalnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i6g1l$msm$1@mendelevium.btinternet.com>`

```
I was being a bit sarcastic there, Judson...

I'm working as a Freelance Contractor, & in all my jobs, I have to sign over
the rights to everything I write to my client & everything has to be handed
over to the client at the end of the contract...

I would suggest though, that her client was rather remiss in not finding out
that she was developing code using her own compiler!!!

John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Problem

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFC56uF.35I@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<359@news.chaven.com> <7i5utd$2hk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
"JC" <john@jeclark.globalnet.co.uk> writes:

>I suggest you get in touch with a lawyer because her Ts & Cs should have
>mentioned not taking anything away from the firm when she left...

So, a carpenter has an obligation to leave his hammer & saws
behind when he completes the job?

Perhaps the client didn't have a Cobol compiler and the programmer
provided her own.  She has an obligation to the client to violate
the compiler's license agreement?  She left behind the source, what
more do you want?

I think there's a few parts missing from the story.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Problem

- **From:** "JC" <john@jeclark.globalnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i6qvf$a8t$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<359@news.chaven.com> <7i5utd$2hk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <dbryantFC56uF.35I@netcom.com>`

```
Your argument about the carpenter is silly & you know it....

I agree that there is more to this story than has been posted, but the
handing over of source code in itself is not the only requirement.

I could write code in S3, which is a low level ICL language, however, the S3
compiler is not supported anymore, & most sites don't have a copy of it,
ergo, if I produced S3 code, it could never be changed & recompiled if I
walked offsite taking my compiler with me.

John
