###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JC" <john@jeclark.globalnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i7bf5$o74$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<359@news.chaven.com> <7i5utd$2hk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <dbryantFC56uF.35I@netcom.com> <7i6qvf$a8t$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <iOD13.1564$%14.24085@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Lol...

I remember on one contract I was working on we had this HND student..(HND -
Higher National Diploma - almost a degree, but not quite)...

Kevin was very bright & firmly believed that not only should the code
perform well, but it should also be as compact as possible...the source as
well as the object.

This resulted in some very unusual code...variables called A, B, C etc
reusing variables when possible & no comments...

We left him to his own devices & when it fell over called him out to fix
it...

Luckily, it wasn't in a critical part of the run, so when he hadn't fixed it
6 hours later we rewrote ourselves...

Kevin never did that again....

John

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>I've inherited some (alleged) COBOL code which may have well as been "S3".
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Mayer Goldberg" <gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FC8JKo.7t0@news2.new-york.net>`
- **References:** `<359@news.chaven.com> <7i5utd$2hk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <dbryantFC56uF.35I@netcom.com> <7i6qvf$a8t$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <iOD13.1564$%14.24085@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7i7bf5$o74$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```

On 22-May-1999, "JC" <john@jeclark.globalnet.co.uk> wrote:

> Kevin was very bright & firmly believed that not only should the code
> perform well, but it should also be as compact as possible...the source as
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> reusing variables when possible & no comments...

I remember this frame of mind. When you wrote programs in basic or assembly
lang with 4K to work with, you did some really nasty stuff like removing any
whitespace that could lexically be removed, you used short variable names,
you condensed everything as much as possible. It took me one assignment to
get out of this frame of mind, having lost points for this "style" on a
perfectly working program. :)

Mayer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Problem

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@commerce.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3749CF47.6897B461@commerce.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<359@news.chaven.com> <7i5utd$2hk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <dbryantFC56uF.35I@netcom.com> <7i6qvf$a8t$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
JC wrote:

> Your argument about the carpenter is silly & you know it....
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Given the mention of Ts & Cs, how can you be sure that handing over the source
code *isn't* the sole requirement?

I tend to agree with both. I'd say the programmer has an ethical obligation to
be sure that the client can maintain the code, but not to the extent of either
violating licensing agreements or giving up her tools. At the least, she should
have left the client instructions regarding what dialect the source code was in.
```

or indexed tables, with no external file, just by using internal
algorythm (comparisons etc...). I've got two levels in my table. Could

Patrick.

#### ↳ Re: NEED HELP FOR SORTING TABLES

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i70fb$c71@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37467711.E2F51364@gecko.fr>`

```
Depending on what COBOL compiler you have, it may already be part of the
language.  It *is* a part of the next COBOL Standard - and I know that at
least Micro Focus has already implemented it as an extension, i.e. "table
sorts" - and there is an example in the LRM (I know - because I put it there
before I left in '96).
```

#### ↳ Re: NEED HELP FOR SORTING TABLES

- **From:** "Dan" <dwm7@fsinter.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i7sua$ftn$1@ffx2nh5.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<37467711.E2F51364@gecko.fr>`

```
Like Bill Klein mentioned, it may be a feature of your cobol compiler.  If
you happen to be using Acucobol, you can use the ASCENDING and DESCENDING
arguments when defining your tables.  More info and code examples are in the
reference manual.

Regards,
Dan Maltes

```

<a id="t-881f933bf0"></a>
## Thread: Current Path Pointer in DMSII on Unisys V Series — 3 msgs

### Current Path Pointer in DMSII on Unisys V Series

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.sys.unisys,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3745ff99.8545011@netnews>`

```
I am having a DMSII problem on a V Series system.  $SAVECP is set in the
compile, which is supposed to cause all record and path pointer operations
to behave exactly the same as on A Series.

I'm looping through a data set adding one to the year in all date fields
(Y2K testing).  There is a set with a date in the key and NO DUPLICATES.
I'm trying to do this as follows:

    SET KEY-SET TO ENDING.
LOOP-HERE.
    DELETE PRIOR KEY-SET.
    IF DMSTATUS(NOTFOUND) GO TO LOOP-EXIT.
    RECREATE DATA-SET.
    fiddle with key
    STORE DATA-SET.
    GO TO LOOP-HERE.

(I also tried this with the DELETE split into MODIFY PRIOR KEY-SET and
DELETE DATA-SET.  According to the documentation, these should function
identically, and they do.)

This loops forever on the last record.

In V Series COBOL ANSI-74 Programming Reference Manual Volume 2, on page
1-10 there is a chart labeled Current Path Pointer States.  The footnote
says the following:  "The state of the current path pointer is not
affected by a FREE operation, a CREATE operation, a RECREATE operation, a
STORE operation, or any unsuccessful DMSII operation".

DELETE sets the current path to deleted state pointing where the deleted
record was.  A subsequent DELETE PRIOR (or MODIFY PRIOR) should get the
record before that.  The intervening RECREATE and STORE shouldn't change
this.  Yet I keep getting the last record over and over.

I find the following on page 1-62:  "Current path pointers are not by the
STORE operation unless the current path is pointing at the record and the
key is changed."  This contradicts page 1-10.  Is the current path
pointing at the record?  I wouldn't expect this to apply when I've done a
DELETE and RECREATE; it should still be pointing where the deleted record
was.  Does RECREATE resurrect this association?  Even so, following the
STORE, if the current path now points to the record just STOREd, shouldn't
DELETE (or MODIFY) PRIOR find the record before that?

How do I get the record before the one I just stored?  Does anyone know if
A Series and V Series (with $SAVECP set) behave differently on this point?
```

#### ↳ Re: Current Path Pointer in DMSII on Unisys V Series

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.sys.unisys,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Iex13.7141$H82.1110338@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<3745ff99.8545011@netnews>`

```
Are the last two keys duplicates?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Current Path Pointer in DMSII on Unisys V Series

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.sys.unisys,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37470e4e.12101295@netnews>`
- **References:** `<3745ff99.8545011@netnews> <Iex13.7141$H82.1110338@news3.mia>`

```
'Twas Sat, 22 May 1999 12:16:40 GMT, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Are the last two keys duplicates?

The DASDL says NO DUPLICATES, and while I haven't verfified that there
aren't duplicate keys which slipped past DMSII I doubt it.  Furthermore I
had this same problem with some embedded data sets earlier, and in that
case I looked carefully at the records and there was no duplicate.  I
worked arround the embedded sets by storing all the embedded records under
a single master record in working storage (there were at most 35 embedded
records under one master record), but that won't work for a data set with
over 500000 records.  I could write all the records to a flat file and
reload them, but I was looking for the way to do this right. 

> --
> Judson McClendon      judmc123@bellsouth.net  (remove numbers)
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> >--

## Thread: tables arrays subscripting — 60 this month / 96 total

### tables arrays subscripting

- **From:** "Pipers" <pipers@tinet.ie>
- **Date:** 1999-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie>`

```
Hi all,
I am a student and I am sitting my end of year exams tomorrow in Cobol.Could
any one please give me a short example of defining a table using the Occurs
statement .Reading and counting items(2 Dimensional) within this table using
W-sub.
In plane English Please!
Thanks in advance!
```

#### ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie>`

```
Pipers wrote:
>I am a student and I am sitting my end of year exams tomorrow in Cobol.Could
>any one please give me a short example of defining a table using the Occurs
>statement .Reading and counting items(2 Dimensional) within this table using
>W-sub.

I hate to rain on your party, but if you have been studying COBOL for
a year and still don't know how to dimension a table, you are in deep
trouble.  You don't define "Reading and counting", but:

       77  W-SUB1                 PIC  9(04)  COMP.
       77  W-SUB2                 PIC  9(04)  COMP.
       77  W-COUNT                PIC  9(06)  COMP.

       01  MY-TABLE.
           03  MT-LEVEL-1         OCCURS 5 TIMES.
               05  MT-LEVEL-2         OCCURS 10 TIMES.
                   07  MT-ITEM            PIC  9(04).

           MOVE 0 TO W-COUNT.
           PERFORM VARYING W-SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL W-SUB1 > 5
                   AFTER W-SUB2 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL W-SUB2 > 10
               ADD MT-ITEM(W-SUB1, W-SUB2) TO W-COUNT.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<374D9C29.71E07161@NOSPAMhome.com>`

IF statements are always in line.  The END-IF make things easier, but
are not necessary.  You can't call yourself an experienced COBOL
programmer without understanding nested IFs.

Also, if people can work with 2 dimensional tables, they can work with 3
dimensional tables.  The thought process is the same.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<P2h33.44$K2.1579@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>,
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote:
>On Thu, 27 May 1999 17:08:24, "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com> 
>wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sentence...

Point of information... as I recall it, a nested IF has different levels
of logical dependency; the simples cases:

IF condition-1
    IF condition-2

... are the equivalent of IF condition-1 AND condition-2.

In an IF-ELSE structure all conditionals are at the same level of logical
dependence.

(note: this point annoys me when it comes to matters of indentation; I was
taught that indentation should be correlated to this.  Given this
teaching:

IF condition-1
ELSE
    IF condition-2

... or ...

IF condition-1
    ELSE
    IF condition-2

are both misleading and *wrong*; the only Good, Decent and True way to
code is:

IF condition-1
ELSE
IF condition-2

... maintaining, in the indenting, the appropriate level of dependence and
evaluation.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374DAF94.C387BCD9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <P2h33.44$K2.1579@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
> (note: this point annoys me when it comes to matters of indentation; I was
> taught that indentation should be correlated to this.  Given this
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Religious War !!!!!!

Put in END-IFs if you like, I am illustrating that nested IFs work
either way.  But the following is how the nested if would look if all
possibilities do something.  Replace any perform with CONTINUE.  The
only way I would code things your way is if I am really not doing nested
IFs, but have a compiler which can't support EVALUATE.  Nested IFs would
be hard to maintain in that case and with enough indentions, would be
impossible with a limited width screen!

IF condition-1
    IF condition-2
         PERFORM a
    ELSE
         PERFORM b
ELSE
    IF condition-3
         PERFORM c
    ELSE
         PERFORM d
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_Ci33.57$K2.1952@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <P2h33.44$K2.1579@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374DAF94.C387BCD9@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <374DAF94.C387BCD9@NOSPAMhome.com>,
Howard Brazee  <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>> (note: this point annoys me when it comes to matters of indentation; I was
>> taught that indentation should be correlated to this.  Given this
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>Religious War !!!!!!

Pfah... only an Unbeliever would say that.

>
>Put in END-IFs if you like, I am illustrating that nested IFs work
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>    IF condition-2
^^^^^^^ logically subordinate to previous IF, 'properly' indented
>         PERFORM a
>    ELSE
^^^^^^^^^ at the same logical level as the previous IF, also 'proper'
>         PERFORM b
>ELSE
>    IF condition-3
^^^^^^^ bleargh! logically equivalent to the very first IF, 'improper'
>         PERFORM c
>    ELSE
^^^^^^^^^ logically equivalent to the previous ELSE, *also* 'improper'
>         PERFORM d
>.

This *perfectly* demonstrates why the style with which I disagree is used
only by the addlepated, the deluded and the inbred; pray tell, what
justification can you offer for having the same indentations for IFs and
ELSEs which differ in logical hierarchy?

Same hierarchy,
Same indentation.

Dependent hierarchy,
    Different indentation.

If you want to put it in religious terms then think of the stereotypical
depiction of Moses descending Sinai (never mind how it is that a Semite
gets those Caucasian features!); he carries two tablets containing Ten
Commandments, each of equal importance and all having the same
indentation.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374E97C4.EEFA1469@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <P2h33.44$K2.1579@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374DAF94.C387BCD9@NOSPAMhome.com> <_Ci33.57$K2.1952@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
> >Religious War !!!!!!
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> ^^^^^^^ bleargh! logically equivalent to the very first IF, 'improper'

Logically equivalent to the 2nd IF.   All of the subsequent IFs are
equally dependent upon what the first IF finds.

> >         PERFORM c
> >    ELSE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> ELSEs which differ in logical hierarchy?

They have the same hierarchy.
Solve the first IF.
    Depending on what the First IF finds, solve the subsequent IFs.
 
> Same hierarchy,
> Same indentation.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

I always thought it would be fun to have someone looking like Sadat
playing Moses.  Egyptions are Semites, and Moses was from Egypt.  But
while it is not part of this discussion to dispute your contention that
all of the commandments are of equal importance (that isn't what I was
taught), I will dispute your statement that this is what we're talking
about:

In my code, the indented IF-ELSEs are subsidary to the main IF-ELSE. 
They are dependent upon what the first one finds.  It doesn't make any
difference whether the first IF found a match or not.   Flow chart it
out and you won't see any difference between the IF and the ELSE.

                     IF
                   /    \
                 yes    no
                 |       |
                IF       IF
               /  \     /  \
             yes  no   yes  no
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374EDCB3.25F479EC@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <374DAF94.C387BCD9@NOSPAMhome.com> <_Ci33.57$K2.1952@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374E97C4.EEFA1469@NOSPAMhome.com> <ICA33.122$K2.4187@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Religious wars continued
> >Logically equivalent to the 2nd IF.   All of the subsequent IFs are
> >equally dependent upon what the first IF finds.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

If you indent properly, you understand more easily.

The ELSE is the other half of the IF statement.
IF condition
    whatever1
ELSE
    whatever2
END-IF

whatever1 and whatever2 are at the same level.

Replacing whatever1 with
    IF condition-1
         whatever 1.2
    ELSE
         whatever 1.2
    END-IF

Is logically equivalent to replacing whatever2 with
    IF condition-2
         whatever 2.2
    ELSE
         whatever 2.2
    END-IF
```

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

Then please do so in the example which caused me such confusion; as I read

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374EEFB8.ED73D2DD@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <374E97C4.EEFA1469@NOSPAMhome.com> <ICA33.122$K2.4187@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374EDCB3.25F479EC@NOSPAMhome.com> <WuB33.130$K2.4272@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
> >If you indent properly, you understand more easily.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

I don't have that example, so I will try to recreate it, first without
END-IFs then with END-IFs, and finally annotated.

IF condition-1
    IF  condition-2
        response-1.2.1
    ELSE
        response-1.2.1
ELSE          
    IF condition 3
        response 1.3.1
    ELSE
        response 1.3.2
.

Which is the same thing as
IF condition-1
**** if condition 1 and condition 2
    IF  condition-2
        response-1.2.1
    ELSE
**** if condition 1 and not condition 2
        response-1.2.1
    END-IF
ELSE
****   if not condition 1 and if condition 3
    IF condition 3
        response 1.3.1
    ELSE
****   if not condition 1 and if not condition 3
        response 1.3.2
    END-IF
END-IF
.

Or annotated:
IF condition-1
    IF  condition-2
        response-1.2.1
    ELSE
        response-1.2.1
    END-IF
ELSE
    IF condition 3
        response 1.3.1
    ELSE
        response 1.3.2
    END-IF
END-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZvC33.136$K2.4512@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <374EDCB3.25F479EC@NOSPAMhome.com> <WuB33.130$K2.4272@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374EEFB8.ED73D2DD@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <374EEFB8.ED73D2DD@NOSPAMhome.com>,
Howard Brazee  <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>> >If you indent properly, you understand more easily.
>> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>        response 1.3.1

Bingo... here is the rub.  Response 1.3.1 is triggered *not* for
condition-3 but, because of the ELSE, IF ((not condition-1) AND
(condition-3)).

The ELSE puts the IF for condition-3 on the same logical level as the IF
for condition-1, hence my conclusion that the IF for condition-3 should
have the same indentation (or lack thereof) as the IF for condition-1.

DD

>    ELSE
>        response 1.3.2
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374EF5FA.8DB0C9F8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <374EDCB3.25F479EC@NOSPAMhome.com> <WuB33.130$K2.4272@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374EEFB8.ED73D2DD@NOSPAMhome.com> <ZvC33.136$K2.4512@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Religious wars continued:

> >IF condition-1
> >    IF  condition-2
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> (condition-3)).

Its trigger uses the exact same criteria (different values) as 1.2.1
does in the binary tree.
If 1.2.1 should be indented, so should 1.3.1.  They're at the exact same
level.

Flow chart it and see.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9D33.138$K2.4435@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <374EEFB8.ED73D2DD@NOSPAMhome.com> <ZvC33.136$K2.4512@iad-read.news.verio.net> <374EF5FA.8DB0C9F8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <374EF5FA.8DB0C9F8@NOSPAMhome.com>,
Howard Brazee  <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>Religious wars continued:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Flow chart it and see.

I was taught that flowcharting came before the code, not after... but
rewriting it in oldBOL shows me what you are asserting.

IF condition-1
    IF condition-2
        imperative.    <=== period/full stop.

IF NOT condition-1
    IF condition-3
        imperative.    <=== period/full stop.

... which puts condition-3 on the same logical level as condition-2.

My apologies for my misunderstandings, if there were any.

DD
```

I have written COBOL as early as 1969, but the USAF and some more
schooling got in the way so my career really started about then.  My
first job had me moving unsigned numbers to signed numbers and back
again - IBM mainframe DOS and IBM mainframe OS kept the signs
differently.  I was glad to find fewer and fewer programs with ALTERs in
them as time went on.  And I have pretty much forgotten how to segment
my prodedure division memory.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.c
om>, Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> writes
>
>I was the only person at my last shop to employ a 3 dimensional table.
> 2 was common.
>

Three dimensional tables are fine. I have a *real* problem dealing
with anything above this, because I can't 'see' it in my head. In
common (I believe) with most programmers, I have a picture of my
program and its data in what passes for my brain while I'm
working. This breaks down horribly with more that 3 dimensions
for a table, so I don't use them!

Anyone ever had a good reason to use > 3 dimensions? If so,
why?

Just curious!

Best regards

Nigel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7iptnt$mjh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
They are fairly easy to envision if you think of them as a tree structure.
Each dimension of the table is simply another level on the tree.

A standard directory on a PC, for example, is a real good example.  The root
is dimension 1, then next level up dimension 2, etc.
It is sparsly populated, and each level infinite (so to speak) in size.

Nigel Eaton wrote in message ...
>In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.c
>om>, Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> writes
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7iqf0b$rv5$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <7iptnt$mjh$1@news.igs.net>`

```
The first thing that came to my mind was TIME.  Many "snapshots" of data can
be kept to create a time-line
depiction.  Of course, not much of this is done in BUSINESS DP, but the
original limit of three was expanded
because "we didn't know all the applications" being implemented.

Warren Simmons

news:7iptnt$mjh$1@news.igs.net...

root

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Funnily enough, I find it easier to think in four dimensional physical
geometry than to think in terms of three spacial dimensions plus time. Time
is sufficiently different from space in my mind that the analogy does not
hold, whereas there are contructions like hypercubes and Klein bottles that
can be "seen" in the minds eye.

Warren Simmons wrote in message <7iqf0b$rv5$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>...

can

>depiction.  Of course, not much of this is done in BUSINESS DP, but the

structure.

```
    I can not help with "why" someone would want to use, say, a 6
dimensional array, but I do not have any trouble picturing the aray in my
head.

    Assume an array with items pic x.

# dimensions     -   mental picture.
1 - line on a piece of paper.
2 - an entire piece of paper (each side is considered a unique object)
3 - A book
4 - a shelf full of books
5 - Which shelf on this bookcase
6 - Which bookcase in this room
7 - Which room in this library
8 - What state
9 - What country.

    There - 9 dimensions uniquely descibes a single character out of every
library in the world.

Why - ????????????????
```

his response was 'For a LINKAGE SECTION I'm designing'... at which point,
of course, I asked 'Why are you designing something which requires the use
of something as... uncommon as a 66?'  'Because I *can* do it', he
replied... at which point I said that if he 'can' design with a 66 then he
most certainly *can* RTFM and find out what kind of headache he'd be
making for the 2-yr wonder who'd be maintaining such slop.

oddly enough, when left to his own devices he Suddenly and Magically found
a way to use a simple REDEFINES)

DD

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6bg43.4420$LA2.74276@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <eQhRAdnq#GA.401@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com> <_he43.4267$LA2.68660@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <are43.209$K2.7987@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
I was merely positing a plausible explanation.

Given the application (a "node" for every book on every shelf in every
library, etc..), I certainly would not use a table, as adding a country or
even a shelf would require a re-compilation. (Not even considered: the
"empty" spaces).

However, I would not be surprised if some of the current "supercomputer"
applications such as weather forecasting or nuclear weapons research (you've
heard of the trendy new dish, "The Los Alamos" at some Chinese restaraunts?
All of column A, all of column B, all of column C; no charge.) would not
find 9-dimemensional tables a useful design.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Level 66 - was Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375275ec.1072413082@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <eQhRAdnq#GA.401@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com> <_he43.4267$LA2.68660@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <are43.209$K2.7987@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
On Sun, 30 May 1999 17:20:06 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:


>(a few years - maybe three or so - I was asked by a programmer relatively
>new to COBOL about the proper syntax for a 66.  When I picked my jaw up
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>making for the 2-yr wonder who'd be maintaining such slop.

Oddly enough, I found something recently that just BEGGED for a 66. 

A Vsam file with overlapping key fields.

01  The-Record.
      03  Prime-Key.
            05  Key-Name Pic X(30).
            05  Key-Number Pic 9(9).
      03  Key-Zip Pic X(10).
      03  Data-Values Pic X(200).

This is of course ficitcious, but the alternate key is made up of the
Key-Number and Key-Zip, so using the 66 at the end:

66  Alt-Key Renames Key-Number Thru Key-Zip

Using this rather than redefining the entire record (which will be an
IMPLICIT redefines as it is part of an FD) really does make sense.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
Nigel Eaton wrote in message ...
>
>In common (I believe) with most programmers, I have a picture of my
>program and its data in what passes for my brain while I'm working.

That's an interesting point.  I also work that way, and as I write
logic, I visualize it being executed, sort of like an animation in a
debugger.  I can't imagine how one could program any other way, and
used to think that no one did or could.  But I have an associate who
is a very accomplished programmer with over 20 years experience.
When I casually commented to him about how programming was visually
oriented, he replied that it was not at all visually oriented to him.
I thought we had mis-communicated, but he kept insisting that it was
a very non-visual process to him, and was a bit surprised at the idea.
Whatever is going on in his head obviously works, but I can't imagine
what it's like. :-)

I'm very curious about this, and would like to take an informal survey.
How many of you program by creating a mental 'image' of your program
and the processes, and how many do something else?  And would you
describe as best you can how you do it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7isboo$738$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`

```
mmm. Interesting question.  Unlike the others that have responded, I do not
really think that visually ... I am more "verbally" oriented, though that is
not really correct eithor.  Let say that I have a private conceptual
language that is close to psuedocode, and I tend to think in that. That is
design phase.

Visual patterns, though, do play a part in debuging and recognizing sections
of code.  I was thinking about that the other day with regard to how you
would test for Cobol knowledge without being platform specific.  For
example, I'll bet 95% of experienced Cobolers could answer the following,
but few beginers could:

What section would the following code be found in, and what does it do?

    xx xxxxxxx
        xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
        xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx.
    xx xxxxxxx.
        xx                                    xxxxxxxxxxxxx.

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Nigel Eaton wrote in message ...
>>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Verbally, I believe, you will think 0-9, A-Z, and X (any).  I don't really
know enough to say why
we picked them, but most everyone accepted the concept right off.

There are times when I believe we could have used better words, i.e. Go and
Return instead of Perform,
etc., but we only had one English Major in the committee. <VBG>\

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37513987@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote
> Nigel Eaton wrote in message ...
> >In common (I believe) with most programmers, I have a picture of my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> describe as best you can how you do it?

I don't quite work that way, but close:
I have to SEE the structure of a program or routine either on paper or
on screen to be able to understand it. To me, a program is a set of patterns
that I visually recognize. I can often spot bugs simply be inspection of
those
patterns. I don't have to READ the program to see that something is wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375139B9.A84EF202@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> How many of you program by creating a mental 'image' of your program
> and the processes, and how many do something else?  And would you
> describe as best you can how you do it?

I work 100% in my mind (so to speak).  If you cannot think it
through to the extreme logical conclusion without cutting code
then you cannot design a complete system no matter how hard you
try, IMHO.

I am reasonably photgraphic too (my recall ability not my mug). 
In a testing situation I can sometime recall the actual page from
my memory and read it.  Whether it is a true representation of the
page or not is another matter.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37527e73.1074596202@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`

```
On Sun, 30 May 1999 07:34:32 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>I'm very curious about this, and would like to take an informal survey.
>How many of you program by creating a mental 'image' of your program
>and the processes, and how many do something else?  And would you
>describe as best you can how you do it?

Well, I can't say it's truly visual - like a debugger, but it's on a
larger scale. I never imagine the individual lines of code until I
write them, then I forget them.  The bigger picture is a kind of
visual flow in my head, but I sure can't explain it.

How - mostly in the shower!!

I don't have to actively work on a problem to solve it.  All I have to
do is UNDERSTAND what the problem is, then I let it "percolate" on the
back of my brain, until the answer comes to me.  Sometimes I have to
send it back again because it's insufficient or it won't work, or the
specs have changed in the mean time.

I've been "working" on a particularly sticky problem recently - how to
overcome a situation where a key should have been unique, but isn't.
Yesterday, in the shower again, I figured out that for my purposes, it
really doesn't matter - I found another way.  (It's a whole lot more
complicated that I have explained).

I also find it VERY useful to have someone else punch holes in my
design.  This is done visually, on a white board - in broad strokes,
sometimes at the level of record layout.

Does this help?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DAw43.367$3D3.110@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`

```
From some of the answers, it appears my question was a bit simplistic,
in that the ways system design and programming are approached may be
very different.

When I do system design, I visualize the system in a sort of symbolic
block form.  I can't quite describe it, but I see how everything will
relate together and function.  I don't visualize code here, except in
areas where I want to think through how I will handle some specific
problem.  And then it is something like the 'private pseudo code' that
Donald described.  This is done as I conduct the user interviews, and
is usually complete by the time interviews are complete.  The rest of
system design is mostly documenting the process in detail, which I do
at the keyboard, using notes written during the interviews.

When programming, I address the whole program similarly to system
design, in symbolic block form.  Logic is visualized on-the-fly in my
private pseudo code, down to the detail level as coding continues.
For very complex or difficult code, I may draw logic diagrams, truth
tables, etc., write prototypes, or otherwise use paper or something
other than mental/visual, but not often.

Like all experienced programmers, I suspect, most new programming is
similar to things I have written dozens of times before, and for most
common situations have developed methods that can be used with very
little creative effort.  That process is pretty much just pattern
recognition and plugging in or coding pre-thought logic.

One thing that is very interesting to me here is how we may all use
somewhat different methods to come to a very similar result. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ivaas$rvi$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia> <DAw43.367$3D3.110@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>From some of the answers, it appears my question was a bit simplistic,
>in that the ways system design and programming are approached may be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem.  And then it is something like the 'private pseudo code' that
<snippage>

I have thought about this a bit over the last day or two, and I think the
thing that varies a lot is the stage of coding.  During the design stage, I
am really thinking in terms of data structures and functions, not in terms
of how to code it.  I assume that if I can figure out what I want to have as
data, and what I want to accomplish with it, then at a later stage I can
figuring out the way to code it.

The trouble at that stage is that there are always a dozen ways to envision
any particular data scheme.  The example that started all this is a perfect
one ... an array, a tree structure, and a simple list with catagory numbers
are all really just different ways of looking at the same data, and maybe
even just different ways of coding a structure that is the same at the
theoretical level.

If one thinks in terms of a specific structure too early in the game, then
it can adversly affect the design.  By keeping that decision on the back
burner for a while, it allows you to concentrate on *what* you want to do,
rather than how to do it.

I think that one of the main reasons that I tend not to envision specific
structure diring the early phases is that my specialty has always been
realtime process control.  Typically, I have at least several threads and
several machines all running at once.  In effect, I have been doing event
driven stuff all my life.

Often, the structure that is best for one task is the worst for another.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ivbgp$niu$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia> <DAw43.367$3D3.110@news3.mia> <7ivaas$rvi$1@news.igs.net>`

```
More insightful than most....

we do what we know...

In my history, the programmers where juniors who had for their designers,
and seniors people with lots of
tab experience.  You punched a card and it had retrictions, and alternatives
were few.
You sorted the cards, and much was done to insure this was right because so
much of the following
processing depended upon the sequence being correct.  Then you had matches,
merges, punching and listings.
Eventually, the calculator got a little more capable, but everything we saw
in 1954 looked like a deck of cards
being converted to tape, sorted, and edited. Printing was an off-line
funtion, and punching cards was an out of town function.  Lots of time was
spent trying to convert tab procedures to what we had learned which was not
much at
the time.
Seniors had experience converting manual to tab, and they arbitarily
designed runs, and connected them into chains of runs to create an
application.  Tape time was six minutes to read, or one hour to sort.  Two
inputs, and two outputs strained the amount of CORE available to us.  It was
not until about 1970 ish before we had RAM available and few if any knew one
iota about how to do Indexed Sequential, and everyone learned how to do DISK
I/O better than IBM in a few years.

You can see that my visualization of the programming might have been very
old fashioned compared to one on even a PC today.  Now, if you can dream of
it, you probably can do it, and in a few years it will be old hat.

Warren Simmons
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:7ivaas$rvi$1@news.igs.net...
>

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

I

as

numbers

> even just different ways of coding a structure that is the same at the
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374f31eb.1354780@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
'Twas Thu, 27 May 1999 11:08:24 -0600, when "Frank Swarbrick"
<infocat@sprynet.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> I have a newly hired co-worker who claims to have 20 years COBOL experience
> and yet not understand "nested IF's".  He says that no-one at his previous
> shop understood them either.

I had a Cobol teacher (twenty some years ago) who not only said not to use
nested IF, he said don't use ELSE.  He said it was better to use GO TO,
because it was clearer.

Those who can, do....

I also knew a programmer who was a real wiz with nested IFs.  What he
didn't believe in was compound conditions.  Where I would write:

    IF  (FIELD-A = 1) OR ((FIELD-B = 2) AND (FIELD-C = 3))
        MOVE 4 TO FIELD-D
        MOVE 5 TO FIELD-E.

He wrote

    IF  (FIELD-A = 1)
        PERFORM 0100-MOVE-TO-D-AND-E-ROUTINE
           THRU 0100-MOVE-TO-D-AND-E-ROUTINE-EXIT
    ELSE
        IF  (FIELD-B = 2)
            IF  (FIELD-C = 3)
                 PERFORM 0100-MOVE-TO-D-AND-E-ROUTINE
                    THRU 0100-MOVE-TO-D-AND-E-ROUTINE-EXIT
            ELSE
                NEXT SENTENCE 
        ELSE
            NEXT SENTENCE.
    ...
    0100-MOVE-TO-D-AND-E-ROUTINE.
        MOVE 4 TO FIELD-D.
        MOVE 5 TO FIELD-E.
    0100-MOVE-TO-D-AND-E-ROUTINE-EXIT.
        EXIT.

I'm not exaggerating.  Every IF had an ELSE; every PERFORM had a THRU; and
he never used AND or OR.  He called this "structured programming".  This
was in the days before END-IF.  I'm sure he would have used it if he could
have.

Does anyone still believe in paying programmers for "productivity"?
```

<silliness>
There is a small cafe on Les Champs-Elys�es a couple blocks from the
Arc De Triomphe where you can get an excellent goo dfreeare.  Watch
out though, it'll give you a chocolate headache!

The colpiler you can find from Miner's Supply Company in Charleston
West Virginia.  They're not cheap though.  A good colpiler that will
pile a ton of coal an hour runs about a quarter of a mil!

;-)

</silliness>

With many apologies...I just can't help myself.  All in good fun
though!

Seriously...

The shareware compiler you can download from
www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html

However, Damon, I must warn you that the shareware compiler may not do
everything you want.  It is a DOS only compiler for one thing and
requires a linker which seems to be quite elusive.  A MUCH, MUCH
better alternative would be to buy Thane Hubbell's book.  Read on, my
friend!

The best place to get a free compiler is to buy Thane Hubbell's book,
"Teach Yourself COBOL In 24 Hours!"

It not only includes a great book, but also incudes a 16 bit version
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at http://www.barnesandnoble.com

...and stay away from the goo dfreeare!  They're just loaded with fat
and calories!!

;-)





Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com

<a id="t-78a4d684da"></a>
## Thread: Printing Compiler Listings — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Printing Compiler Listings

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37485CB0.FA4A80D3@worf.netins.net>`

```
This is actually an operating system question, but it arises
out of my use of Fujitsu COBOL.  I can get the compiler to
generate listings at the time that the source code is
compiled.  The compiled code includes page breaks which show
up in note pad as little square blocks on the page.  This is
how they print when I send the content to the printer.  The
little square block is actually an ASCII '0C'h.  

I have written a small VB [:(] program to read and translate
the '0C'h into a page break, but there ought to be a simpler
way to print properly formatted compiler listings.  Any help
or suggestions would be appreciated.  Thank you.  

Floyd H. Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing Compiler Listings

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zck23.2087$6y2.30993@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<37485CB0.FA4A80D3@worf.netins.net>`

```
>I have written a small VB [:(] program to read and translate
>the '0C'h into a page break, but there ought to be a simpler
>way to print properly formatted compiler listings.  Any help
>or suggestions would be appreciated.  Thank you.


Another thing to try would be to print it as a text file from DOS. What you
actually have is a printer image of your listing. This means the file is
formatted to be printed from DOS. Assuming my assumptions are correct.

Just in case you have forgotten how to print from DOS:

c:\> TYPE "LONG FILE NAME.LISTING" >> LPT1

to set up lpt1:

C:\> MODE LPT1: cols=80 lines=6

where obviously, <cols> is the column width of the page (80 or 132) and
<lines> is the lines per inch, 6 or 8.

SEE ATTACHED BAT FILE
- place BAT file in dir where listing is, or create shortcut on desktop
- drag file from explorer onto BAT file or shortcut and file will be printed
minus the rectangles.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Compiler Listings

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lsk23.2090$6y2.31165@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<37485CB0.FA4A80D3@worf.netins.net> <Zck23.2087$6y2.30993@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
>>I have written a small VB [:(] program to read and translate
>>the '0C'h into a page break, but there ought to be a simpler
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>- drag file from explorer onto BAT file or shortcut and file will be
printed
>minus the rectangles.


Furthermore you could create a file association to ".LST" files and create
an action of PRINT. Then you will be able to right click on the file and
choose print and the file will be sent to your LPT1 port as if you printed
it from DOS. This only works when printing from the machine the printer is
directly attached to, not over a network. That is beyond my knowledge.


begin 666 letmeprint.bat
M;6]D92!L<'0Q(&-O;',],3,R(&QI;F5S/38-"G1Y<&4@(B4Q(B ^/B!L<'0Q
"#0H`
`
end
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Compiler Listings

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374A9AE2.28B797E2@worf.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<37485CB0.FA4A80D3@worf.netins.net> <Zck23.2087$6y2.30993@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:

> Another thing to try would be to print it as a text file from DOS. 
> What you actually have is a printer image of your listing. This 
> means the file is formatted to be printed from DOS. 

Not a DOS function but an old printer code.  The ASCII '0C'h
is the old ASCII code for page break.  Most old dot matrix
printers recognized this code and generated a page break. 
Unforunately (at least in this instance) the only printers
that I currently have available use either ink jet or laser
technologies.  Apparently, these "improvements" no longer
recognize the ASCII '0C'h as a simple page break command.  

> Just in case you have forgotten how to print from DOS:
>      c:\> TYPE "LONG FILE NAME.LISTING" >> LPT1
> to set up lpt1:
>      C:\> MODE LPT1: cols=80 lines=6

I'll give it a try - but I think I may have tried this a
while back when I was dealing with other issues and didn't
want to worry about the printer.  Now is the time to worry
about the printer [:)].  Thanks for the tip.

QUESTION:  Since this seems to be a new issue without a
standard answer, can I conclude that in industry that
compiler generated listings are only rarely used in current
practice?  I know its true when I use VB for my production
work, but hadn't even asked the question given my use of
hardcopy as part of the grading process for COBOL or C++ or
whatever.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Compiler Listings

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KyE23.2730$6y2.36959@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<37485CB0.FA4A80D3@worf.netins.net> <Zck23.2087$6y2.30993@storm.twcol.com> <374A9AE2.28B797E2@worf.netins.net>`

```
>Unforunately (at least in this instance) the only printers
>that I currently have available use either ink jet or laser
>technologies.  Apparently, these "improvements" no longer
>recognize the ASCII '0C'h as a simple page break command.


These are not improvements they are variations from the standard. If your
printer does not accept standard ASCII character codes it has varied from
the standard which has been established. I have tested my example on an
Epson Stylus 400 color inkjet printer. It worked just fine and feed the
pages at the designated page breaks.

>QUESTION:  Since this seems to be a new issue without a
>standard answer, can I conclude that in industry that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>whatever.


Compiler listings are used in production, however in my experience the most
prevalent use is for the auditor geeks who insist on a paper trail. It is
very important for determining the compiler options used when the load
module was compiled and for tracing down some abends. This has been used
less and less due to Abend -Aid and other utilities. For tracking purposes
they can't be beat, although hard copies aren't really needed any more with
the advent of CD recorders and improvements in tape storage.

They are rarely used. Hopefully some day we will no longer need them, but
for now they are a necessary evil.
```

<a id="t-e374cdd53e"></a>
## Thread: Question : Binary Format? — 10 msgs

### Question : Binary Format?

- **From:** "David Mowat" <david@dandm.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au>`

```
Hi Newsgroup,
Can anybody help me with this one? An IBM mainframe running MVS and COBOL 3
can output a COBOL file in "Ascii" or "Binary" Format as a text file
readable on an Ms DOS machine. This is done by an "emulator". Because some
of the COBOL numerical datafields are "COMP" Ascii isnt suitable, so
"Binary" was chosen. But what is the character set for "Binary"? I need to
be able to read the "Binary Format" text file and convert back to Ascii on
my Ms DOS PC system. I will read the text file in COBOL as a single Ascii
character record sequential file, convert each character, then move the data
into an indexed file with the same structure as it had on the mainframe.

Any ideas on what "binary format" would be? I asked them to look up the
manual, but no response.
Thankyou for your help
David
david@dandm.co.nz
```

#### ↳ Re: Question : Binary Format?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ia6h3$k80@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au>`

```
If you define all the numeric fields (on the mainframe) as USAGE DISPLAY -
SIGN IS LEADING SEPARATE - and then take the EBCDIC (not ASCII) file and
download it to a PC using EBCDIC/ASCII conversion, you will have a "totally
usable" PC file.
```

- **References:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au> <7ia6h3$k80@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```

I can suggest this to the company with the mainframe who is distributing the

David.

Since you mentioned converting to an indexed file, I suspect you have
some COBOL on the PC, would it by chance be MicroFocus?

Given the FD from the mainframe you can run it through "WFL" under
MicorFocus COBOL and create your indexed file directly from the
mainframe.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question : Binary Format?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37493f6a.468543431@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au> <7ia6h3$k80@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <9k123.6253$vM1.10935@ozemail.com.au> <3748b0b1.432000920@news1.ibm.net> <o5323.6282$vM1.11059@ozemail.com.au>`

```
On Mon, 24 May 1999 14:47:50 +1200, "David Mowat" <david@dandm.co.nz>
wrote:

>Yes I have MicroFocus (Microsoft vs5.0) COBOL on the PC and the FD from the
>mainframe. Is "WFL" a microfocus utility? How can I use it?
>

I do not know if WFL was included with the Microsoft versoin of
MicroFocus or not.

In MF it was ALT F9 from the workbench menu.

First you need to put the FD into a program (any program) and compile
it.  Then using the .IDY created, you can "create" a format in WFL to
convert.  It's fairly self explanitory once you start the WFL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question : Binary Format?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ic5gg$a92@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au> <7ia6h3$k80@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <9k123.6253$vM1.10935@ozemail.com.au> <3748b0b1.432000920@news1.ibm.net> <o5323.6282$vM1.11059@ozemail.com.au> <37493f6a.468543431@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I think that WFL was *NOT* included in the (long passed) Microsoft product.
I think it was based on the Micro Focus "Toolset" product - while WFL was
just included in the "Workbench" product. The same is true (as I recall -
but again this is from long ago) for CHARSET(EBCDIC) support (which might
provide another solution to this problem).  I think that the compiler will
accept this, but that the run-time won't handle it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question : Binary Format?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vJj23.2078$6y2.30554@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au>`

```

David Mowat wrote in message ...
>Hi Newsgroup,
>Can anybody help me with this one? An IBM mainframe running MVS and COBOL 3
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>"Binary" was chosen. But what is the character set for "Binary"? I need to


There is no character set for BINARY. It is either ASCII or EBCDIC. These
formats can be corrupted by the transfer mode, which is ASCII or BINARy (AKA
IMAGE) under FTP. The IND$GET transfer protocol also allows for ASCII
translation, in most cases you will want this off to protect the binary data
within. In the absence of a nice utility to do it for you, a custom
translator will most likely need to be created. If it is a single record
format file this should be relatively easy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question : Binary Format?

- **From:** "David Mowat" <david@dandm.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Srs23.9095$vM1.14272@ozemail.com.au>`
- **References:** `<ti023.6236$vM1.10850@ozemail.com.au> <vJj23.2078$6y2.30554@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Thanks Jeff,
It is just EBCDIC!

Jeff wrote in message ...
>
>David Mowat wrote in message ...
>>Hi Newsgroup,
>>Can anybody help me with this one? An IBM mainframe running MVS and COBOL
3
>>can output a COBOL file in "Ascii" or "Binary" Format as a text file
>>readable on an Ms DOS machine. This is done by an "emulator". Because some
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>formats can be corrupted by the transfer mode, which is ASCII or BINARy
(AKA
>IMAGE) under FTP. The IND$GET transfer protocol also allows for ASCII
>translation, in most cases you will want this off to protect the binary
data
>within. In the absence of a nice utility to do it for you, a custom
>translator will most likely need to be created. If it is a single record
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question : Binary Format?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990525210350.08345.00004975@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<vJj23.2078$6y2.30554@storm.twcol.com>`

```
In article <vJj23.2078$6y2.30554@storm.twcol.com>, "Jeff" <a@a.com> writes:

>
>There is no character set for BINARY. It is either ASCII or EBCDIC. These
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not only do yu have to be concerned with the download bringin the information
down correctly, but you also have to take into account the big-endian / little
endian loading of binary fields (left to right or right to left )

Mainframe format (AFAIK) is always least significant byte is on the rightmost
position of the field.
```

screens.

employee who quit and took all of her development tools with her.  I
need to recreate an indexed file.  Which I am assuming is the same as
a VSAM.   How can I do this?

When I try using the file utilities, I get some weird error about
record length of something.  It does not want to take a fixed length
sequential file and turn it into an indexed file.

Can anyone help who may have done this operation before?

Thanks,

Please respond via e-mail.

Arthur
amerar@unsu.com
```

---

<a id="t-30a1f1ca72"></a>
## Thread: VSAM-ESDS on VSE OS — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### VSAM-ESDS on VSE OS

- **From:** Ogan.Karaman@t-online.de (Oganb G���nther Karaman)
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3749913d.3985956@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hello,

I need badly help on COBOL with VSAM-ESDS-Files.
Our Mainframe works with the VSE OS. The Compiler is COBOL 85 Standard
I am working as an Programmer for Business-Aplications since October
1998. At school we worked just on PC's.
Now, i have to fill a ESDS-file with my program. Because the
mainlanguage in the company is ASSEMBLER there is nobody I can asked
about.
Now the problem:
There is a VSAM-ESDS-File with a Record-Length of 410 Bytes. I have to
append records to this file.
How to declare it in th SELECT-Clause?
How to Open the File? ( When i try OPEN EXTEND the compiler says It
may occur a abend on VSE)

A another problem is the Record-Length :
I already tryed OPEN I-O, but I could not open it because the
File-Status sayed 'wrong record length'.

Maybe someone has some suggestions for me.

thanks Ogan



Mfg Ogan Gï¿½nther Karaman

Ogan.Karaman@T-Online.de
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM-ESDS on VSE OS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ic6ou$apj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3749913d.3985956@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Have you already looked at:


http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYVA001/2%2e9%2e4%2e3

which is the section
   "2.9.4.3 Coding Input/Output Statements for VSAM Files"
in
  Title: COBOL/VSE Programming Guide
  Document Number: SC26-8072-01

I think that this section (and the other sections showing what JCL to use)
should help you with your questions.  I come primarily from an MVS (OS/390)
background, so I don't feel very comfortable answering your specific
questions - but hopefully one of our VSE experts will give you more
direction.
```

Mfg Ogan Gï¿½nther Karaman

Ogan.Karaman@T-Online.de

#### ↳ Re: VSAM-ESDS on VSE OS

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ijv18$ckf$2@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3749913d.3985956@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Oganb G�nther Karaman <Ogan.Karaman@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:3749913d.3985956@news.btx.dtag.de...
> Hello,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe someone has some suggestions for me.

I'm curious as to what you've tried already.  This is just a guess, but I
think this may be your problem.  In your select statement, instead of doing
something such as "SELECT FILENAME  ASSIGN TO FNAME" (where "FILENAME" is
your COBOL file name and "FNAME" is your DLBL) you should instead do "SELECT
FILENAME ASSIGN TO AS-FNAME".  If you simply use FNAME it defaults to a
regular SAM (or VSAM managed SAM) file.  Using the "AS-" prefix lets COBOL
II know that it's a VSAM ESDS.

I believe this is all correct, but check your COBOL manual for more details.
Of course, this may not be your problem at all, but it's all I can think of
without seeing your source.
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM-ESDS on VSE OS

- **From:** Ogan.Karaman@t-online.de (Oganb G���nther Karaman)
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374f028d.5738066@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<3749913d.3985956@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Thank's to all who tried to help me!

The solution of my problem was actually the "AS-" prefix in the
SELECT-Statement.

Thank you very much 

Regards  Ogan Karaman
Mfg Ogan Gï¿½nther Karaman

Ogan.Karaman@T-Online.de
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM-ESDS on VSE OS

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7int8d$4cf$2@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3749913d.3985956@news.btx.dtag.de> <374f028d.5738066@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Oganb G�nther Karaman <Ogan.Karaman@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:374f028d.5738066@news.btx.dtag.de...
> Thank's to all who tried to help me!
>
> The solution of my problem was actually the "AS-" prefix in the
> SELECT-Statement.

My god, I was right.  I may faint...

:-)
```

## Thread: Cobol for Windows Development — 11 msgs

### Cobol for Windows Development

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simon@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea5f2$b3610060$4a0870c3@email>`

```
All beta fixes have now been incorporated into Casegen Cobol for Windows
Professional including improved support for Microsoft Access databases.

The "Introductory Price" of $125 (or $50 to upgrade from a paid earlier
release) is still valid and if you are looking to develop or convert to
Windows GUI systems in Cobol this could well be your best development tool
as performance and simplicity are the main design objectives. Full access
is provided to ODBC databases though our point-and-click SQL builder.

Please contact me for more details.

Simon Cordingley
Technical Manager
Casegen Systems Ltd
www.casegen.co.uk
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for Windows Development

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simon@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea690$2ba83d20$6b0870c3@email>`
- **References:** `<01bea5f2$b3610060$4a0870c3@email> <7ich4j$khu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi John

The Casegen COBOL for Windows Professional product is a Windows development
tool that allows Cobol programs to simply and easily build effective
Windows GUI programs and thir associated logic in Cobol.

Its like "Visual Basic", but simpler and the language used is standard
Cobol, together with our EXEC GUI statements to do things with Windows such
as disable a control on the Windows form or hide a bitmap .

Casegen is very easy to use, we believe the easiest there is. You
just drop controls on the Windows form with the mouse, then add "event"
code in Cobol such as the processing that takes place when a button is
clicked. There are lots of different controls with full support for
dynamically-loaded bitmaps and bitmaps on buttons (and therefore the
appearance of a toolbar).

When you are finished, or think you are, just press one button and Casegen
generates all the Cobol code necessary and loads and runs the program in as
little as one second! Under the covers its compiling and linking the Cobol
using the Fujitsu compilier, version 3 or 4.

The "Professional" product now includes ODBC support. This means that you
can press a single button and import table information from any defined
32-bit ODBC Data Source. You can then build the SQL just by specifying the
Table, Call Type(Select, Insert, Update, Delete or Cursor) and the name of
the Cobol paragraph to generate the SQL call into. The full SQL is then
displayed which you can customise using the mouse. There is full support
for most types of SQL including joins. Even if Casegen cannot build exactly
the SQL you want, it just drops the current SQL into an editor for you to
edit.

Casegen then generates the full SQL for you, you just PERFORM the paragraph
from within event code, checking the results as normal. (The Fujitsu
compiler version 4 converts this "Embedded SQL" into ODBC calls
automatically).

We (and others) have tried Casegen with Microsoft Access, DB2, Sybase SQL
Anywhere and  Microfocus XDB. Of course any ODBC-compliant database should
work OK.

All the EXEC GUI calls are fully documented and mostly have only two or
three parms. All can be copy/pasted out of the help facility into the
Casegen editor which has full Cobol syntax highlighting.

Windows 95/98/NT supported.

Good Points: 
1) Generates the fastest possible Windows application.
2) Easy to use
3) Uses standard Cobol
4) All the power of Fujitsu facilities are available
5) Easy to specify and use SQL/ODBC
6) No restrictions - if we don't support it, you can issue your own Windows
calls until we do!
7) Tried and tested in medium-size applications
8) Very inexpensive
9) Double-click on a generator or compiler error takes you straight to the
line in error
10) High specification Cobol editor.
11) Full Cut/Copy/Paste between the same or other forms
12) Full Cobol subroutine support
13) Supports multiple instances of Casegen
14) No need to understand compile/link command lines
15) Support for DLLs

Bad Points
1) No support for ActiveX controls
2) Not as many controls as VB
3) SQL/ODBC facility requires Fujitsu Version 4 (see www.adtools.com for
ordering details)        

The CD comes with Fujitsu Version 4 (courtesy of Fujitsu) and a 60-day
evaluation copy of SQL Anywhere (courtesy of Sybase), losts of examples and
electronic documentation for $125 including delivery with a money-back
guarantee (less postage costs).

Printed "Getting Started" - add $10.

We have many testamonials available on request. Please send email for
ordering details.


Best regards

Simon Cordingley
simon@casegen.co.uk
Visit our web page at
www.casegen.co.uk


John <jchafin@cgwinc.com> wrote in article <7ich4j$khu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
> Simon,
> 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for Windows Development

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simon@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea75c$3cf6f540$810870c3@email>`
- **References:** `<01bea5f2$b3610060$4a0870c3@email> <7ich4j$khu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <01bea690$2ba83d20$6b0870c3@email> <374c0d39.53844456@cnews.newsguy.com>`

```
Not specifically, but the Cobol we generate should work with most compilers
its just a matter of telling us what changes YOU require for your compiler.

We would have to also make adjustments to the display of the compiler error
messages, we need to know the format because you can double-click on them
to take you to the original line in error irrespective of the block of
event code that was typed.

Of course, why not use Fujitsu? Version 3 is free and Version 4 very
inexpensive!

Regards

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd
www.casegen.co.uk

#### ↳ Re: Cobol for Windows Development

- **From:** SiD <sdawson@netspace.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374EAB4D.9FCF1823@netspace.net.au>`
- **References:** `<01bea5f2$b3610060$4a0870c3@email>`

```
Simon,

    Can we still use our existing Oracle databases? i.e. is it compatible with
Oracle?

    And, is the code converted completely or is a certain amount of "tweaking"
of the new code required?

Simon

Simon Cordingley wrote:

> All beta fixes have now been incorporated into Casegen Cobol for Windows
> Professional including improved support for Microsoft Access databases.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> www.casegen.co.uk
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for Windows Development

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simon@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea9af$762d3e00$b40870c3@casegen.casegen.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01bea5f2$b3610060$4a0870c3@email> <374EAB4D.9FCF1823@netspace.net.au>`

```
Hi Simon

One of our customers uses Oracle in our batch product,  but we have not
tried it with Casegen Cobol for Windows Professional. Fujitsu Version 4
documentation says that it is supported and as we generate and compile
using Fujitsu - it should work.

From my little knowledge of Oracle, I believe there is two choices -
(please let me know if I am wrong anybody):

1) Use the Oracle precompiler to convert EXEC SQL statements to cobol
statements and calls to an Oracle stub, then compile with Fujitsu then link
with the stub. We already do this with CICS, and we have experience of
doing this with DB2.
2) Use ODBC with Oracle just another 32-bit data source. However, who
provides the 32-bit ODBC driver? In this situation, we do not even know
that the database is Oracle - its just another ODBC database.

Option 2 will just work with Casegen provided the ODBC driver follows the
rules. Option 1 would require the precompiler presumably supplied by
Oracle. We would have to make some small changes in our build process
(automatic generate/compile/link) but we have allowed for this in the
design with configuration files.   

If you are converting an old application to GUI, it would be normal to cut
and paste blocks of code directly into the Casegen event code. Subroutines
are just reused without change. The important thing to understand is to
make sure you get the design right!

Fujitsu seems very flexible, so it should handle most variants of the
standard, depending upon your existing compiler.

Hope this helps.

Best regards

Simon Cordingley

SiD <sdawson@netspace.net.au> wrote in article
<374EAB4D.9FCF1823@netspace.net.au>...
> Simon,
> 
>     Can we still use our existing Oracle databases? i.e. is it compatible
with
> Oracle?
> 
>     And, is the code converted completely or is a certain amount of
"tweaking"
> of the new code required?
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > All beta fixes have now been incorporated into Casegen Cobol for
Windows
> > Professional including improved support for Microsoft Access databases.
> >
> > The "Introductory Price" of $125 (or $50 to upgrade from a paid earlier
> > release) is still valid and if you are looking to develop or convert to
> > Windows GUI systems in Cobol this could well be your best development
tool
> > as performance and simplicity are the main design objectives. Full
access
> > is provided to ODBC databases though our point-and-click SQL builder.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
