```
thinking
AcuCobol gags on the possibility of creating that latter file as an index
and might want to forget about the ".new" part of the index name.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prob w/ separate index file using AcuCobol-85

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OM#uvtFq#GA.293@nih2naac.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<jBX23.736$LI5.80493@axe.netdoor.com> <374c4736.1573430@news.indigo.ie> <CZZ23.742$LI5.81689@axe.netdoor.com>`

```
I can not speak for Accucobol, but with MF, the root name (8 of 8.3) must be
unique when dealing with indexed files.  Ie, you can NOT have FILENAME.DAT
and FILENAME.OLD as the data portion of 2 part indexed files.

    Of course, the rules are a little different with unix, but If you hope
to maintain compatability ...
```

#### ↳ Re: Prob w/ separate index file using AcuCobol-85

- **From:** "Erik Yhlen" <ey@datamann.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ij6ke$52j$1@miri.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<jBX23.736$LI5.80493@axe.netdoor.com>`

```

Warren Porter <warrenp123@netdoor123.com> wrote in message
news:jBX23.736$LI5.80493@axe.netdoor.com...
> In developing a set of y2k conversion programs using AcuCobol-85, creating
a
> file name by appending ".new" gave us no problems when the index was
> contained in the file itself.  On another system we get a file status of
93
> (File locked by another user), but the index is a separate file; e.g., if
we
> create an indexed file named FILENAME, a file named FILENAME.vix is
created
> at no extra charge to hold the index.  I don't see any sign of a file
named
> FILENAME.vix.new or FILENAME.new.vix  when we tried to run one of these
> programs on our new system.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Your problem is, that your run on vision 4 (index-files). Change or insert
"V-Version   3" in your cblconfi and rebuild the file, this should solve
your problem

Best regards
     Erik Yhlen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Prob w/ separate index file using AcuCobol-85

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nOd33.790$LI5.86020@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<jBX23.736$LI5.80493@axe.netdoor.com> <7ij6ke$52j$1@miri.tele.dk>`

```

Erik Yhlen wrote in message <7ij6ke$52j$1@miri.tele.dk>...
>
>Warren Porter  wrote in message
>>> In developing a set of y2k conversion programs using AcuCobol-85,
creating
>a
>> file name by appending ".new" gave us no problems when the index was
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> programs on our new system.

>Your problem is, that your run on vision 4 (index-files). Change or insert
>"V-Version   3" in your cblconfi and rebuild the file, this should solve
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Erik Yhlen


Many thanks to you and Domenyk Newman.  We already had scripts to handle the
renaming of FILENAME and FILENAME.new to FILENAME.old and FILENAME
and back so keeping that setup would have been convenient.  I changed ".new"
to "anew" (same for ".old") so no period would remain in the name of the
file and had to make new versions of the renaming scripts.  IOW, evasion
turned out to be the answer.

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: Prob w/ separate index file using AcuCobol-85

- **From:** "D.Newman" <Domenyk.Newman@MCL-Group.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<927811234.12703.0.nnrp-12.9e98ccda@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<jBX23.736$LI5.80493@axe.netdoor.com>`

```
Run vutil -i to see what Vision version the files are.
You are creating version 4 files. You can set "v-version 3" in your
configuration file to keep the version to 3.
You can also rebuild the version 4 files back to version 3 if you require.
"vutil -r -3 filename.dat"
I recently encountered the same scenario.

Regards
Domenyk Newman
```

<a id="t-f352d0084f"></a>
## Thread: Netexpress 3.0 and Active-x — 4 msgs

### Netexpress 3.0 and Active-x

- **From:** "tommy" <tommynilsen@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ihmn8$30g$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Hi.

I'm trying to use the Crystal Reports 7.0 ocx in Netexpress 3.0.
I call a function named "GetPrinterName" which returns the printername in an
object-reference datatype .

Could anyone please tell me how to move the printername from
object-reference to a pic x datatype.
I've looked in the help-file , but can't figured it out.

Has anyone else tryed using the Crystal Reports 7.0 ocx in Netexpress ??
It sometimes crashes my DialogSystem session , does this happen to others
too ??

Thanks

Tommy Nilsen
```

#### ↳ Re: Netexpress 3.0 and Active-x

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<L3_23.27452$Q_5.4161435@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ihmn8$30g$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Assuming you mean GetPrinterName() returns a pointer, then the trick is
to declare a linkage item and set it's address to the value returned in
the pointer.

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  MY-VAR        PIC X(256).

    CALL APIENTRY "GetPrinterName" USING ....

   IF my-pointer <> NULL
       SET ADDRESS OF MY-VAR TO my-pointer
   END-IF


tommy <tommynilsen@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:7ihmn8$30g$1@nntp.newmedia.no...
> Hi.
>
> I'm trying to use the Crystal Reports 7.0 ocx in Netexpress 3.0.
> I call a function named "GetPrinterName" which returns the printername
in an
> object-reference datatype .
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anyone else tryed using the Crystal Reports 7.0 ocx in Netexpress
??
> It sometimes crashes my DialogSystem session , does this happen to
others
> too ??
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Netexpress 3.0 and Active-x

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ijab6$s6i$1@zutalors.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7ihmn8$30g$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
"tommy" <tommynilsen@yahoo.com> wrote:
>I'm trying to use the Crystal Reports 7.0 ocx in Netexpress 3.0.
>I call a function named "GetPrinterName" which returns the printername in an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I've looked in the help-file , but can't figured it out.

Are you CALLing a function or INVOKEing a method called "GetPrinterName"?

If you are CALLing a function, you should not be getting back an object 
reference datatype as a return value. In which case Karl Wagner's reply 
will probably help.

If you are INVOKEing a method, then it depends what type of object the 
method returns. The debugger in the IDE can give you a clue here when you 
look at the data item.

If it is an automation object, then the object will have another method 
defined in the Crystal Reports docs that gets the actual name. These 
methods should be listed under the type of object that the property 
"PrinterName" returns.

Otherwise, the object might be an instance of the class library "chararry" 
class - in which case, use the "getValue" (or other) method(s). (In this 
case, it would be almost as easy to use a pic x(n) return type.)

In each case, finalize the object returned when you've finished with it.

>Has anyone else tryed using the Crystal Reports 7.0 ocx in Netexpress ??
>It sometimes crashes my DialogSystem session , does this happen to others
>too ??

Please make sure that you have installed the latest WebSync updates. 
If the problem persists, and you think the problem lies in the Net 
Express product, please report it to Technical Support. They'll probably 
ask for some example code and (as they probably don't have Crystal 
Reports 7.0) the relevant parts of the documentation.

Steve.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Netexpress 3.0 and Active-x

- **From:** tomnilse@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ijc61$h18$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ihmn8$30g$1@nntp.newmedia.no> <7ijab6$s6i$1@zutalors.mfltd.co.uk>`

```


I'm using the INVOKE method..

Thanks for the tip.

Tommy
```

## Thread: Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -exponential and logs — 6 msgs

### Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -exponential and logs

- **From:** Ryan Coffey <rcoffey@jps.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net>`

```

I was wondering if you could help me w/ the syntax of a COBOL program
that would do exp. like below.  My goal would be to have it read a value

for variable x and b and give me U.  I took Fortran 77 a couple years
ago and today my dad asked me if I knew cobol.  I said I would try.  I
managed to screw around on the school's Unix accont, but I can't get it
to work.  I was wondering if you could help me out.

Ryan



  b^u=x

or Log termination U=log to the base b (x)

I want to know if I know x and b, how to I find U.
```

#### ↳ Re: Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -exponential and logs

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374be2f2@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net>`

```

give you logarithms in Cobol.

news:374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net...
>
> I was wondering if you could help me w/ the syntax of a COBOL program
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -exponential and logs

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374be752.642625936@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net>`

```
On Wed, 26 May 1999 00:49:06 -0700, Ryan Coffey <rcoffey@jps.net>
wrote:


>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I want to know if I know x and b, how to I find U.

For Log, try "Function Log" or "Function Log10" to find the natural
log of a number.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -expo nential and logs

- **From:** Tom O' Toole <Tom.Otoole@policymaster.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BA5679A31104D311A55D0000E218F7420C5974@MAROON>`
- **References:** `<374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net> <374be752.642625936@news1.ibm.net>`

```
>>
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>I want to know if I know x and b, how to I find U.

>For Log, try "Function Log" or "Function Log10" to find the natural
>log of a number.

And then use log (x) / log (b) to find U.  Or log10 (x) / log10 (b).
Just be consistent.
```

#### ↳ Re: Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -exponential and logs

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72T23.1824$v%.372@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net>`

```
Ryan Coffey wrote:
>
>  b^u=x
>
>I want to know if I know x and b, how to I find U.

  b^u = x
  ln(b^u) = ln(x)
  u*ln(b) = ln(x)
  u = ln(b)/ln(x)

           COMPUTE U = FUNCTION LOG(B) / FUNCTION LOG(X).

Make sure both B and X > 0 and X <> 1!
```

#### ↳ Re: Stumper Question for me: simple question for this group -exponential and logs

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lL433.4321$v%.746@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<374BA772.603E72EB@jps.net>`

```
Try :  answer-wanted = base**exponent.
```

## Thread: New website found for free online C++ certifications — 1 msgs

### New website found for free online C++ certifications

- **From:** aludlow@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7iguj0$p7o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dear COBOL Programmer,

TekMetrics has extended their offer of free on-line certifications
through the end of June.  Simply go to
http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/default.html to order your free
certification.  You can either elect to take the test right away, or
come back later, using the PIN you are provided when you order.

To qualify as a Certified COBOL Programmer, you must pass the
examination with a score of 2.75 or higher.  To be certified as a Master
COBOL Programmer, you must obtain a score of 4.00 or higher.  At the end
of the test, your score will be provided (with a scoring range of 1-5).
Once certified, you can add the logo associated  with the test (standard
or master logo, depending on how well you do) to your resume or website.

Furthermore, those that pass will be mailed a certificate, once again,
at no cost.  There's no catch.  We're simply trying to prove a concept
during a beta trial period.  Thus far, the feedback has been extremely
positive!  Over 80,000 technical workers worldwide have registered for
these certifications since we started the program in January.

Why bother getting certified by TekMetrics?

1)  More and more corporations recognize TekMetrics Certifications as an
indication not only of the technical workers knowledge level, but also
as an indicator that they were willing to demonstrate their skills to
them.

2)  It's free and it's fun.  They won't be free forever, but they are
now. So take advantage of them.  Furthermore, those who have completed
the tests generally indicate that they enjoyed the experience and
considered both the delivery of the test and the items themselves to be
of high quality.

3)  Over 5000 technical workers from around the world are ordering our
certifications each week, and that number is steadily rising.

4)  Strut your stuff.  See if you are good enough to make the "top
scores" list.

5)  Find out both your strengths and weaknesses in COBOL.  At the end of
the test, an account will have been set up for you from which you can
view your strengths and weaknesses.  And yes, that too is free.

All we ask for in return is your feedback at the end of the exam.  We
will be continually focusing on the improvement of the delivery of the
test and quality of the questions.  Any feedback you can provide,
positive or negative, will help us greatly.

A FAQ is, "How do you know the person who supposedly took the test was
actually the same person?  What if it was his hotshot COBOL Programmer
brother, sister or friend?  In other words, what is to prevent him from
cheating?"  Our solution:  we allow employers to spotcheck the technical
workers with a short test at no cost at their corporate locations, if
they so choose. Corporations have unanimously applauded this solution.

Lastly, data you provide in the certification process is not used
without your permission other than to store your certification record
and verify to valid employers at their request.  We do, however, publish
names on our site in a Top Scores list for each category, but no
addresses, phone numbers, email, etc.. are shown.  For more information,
see our privacy statement at
http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/t/privacy.html

Well, that's pretty much it!  We're trying to establish a new
cost-effective and convenient alternative to today's certification
environment.  During this beta trial period, you will be the benefactor
of this fun and challenging experience.

If you have any questions/concerns regarding the COBOL Programmer
certification, please do not hesitate to contact our customer support
department at <mailto:support@tekmetrics.com>.

In addition to COBOL, we're also offering the following certifications
at no cost.  Feel free to try as many as you like:

C Programmer            Object-Oriented Developer
C++ Programmer          Oracle 7 DBA
CICS Programmer         Oracle Developer 2000 Programmer
COBOL II Programmer     Oracle PL/SQL Programmer
DB2 DBA                         Perl Programmer
Delphi Programmer       PowerBuilder
HTML Programmer                 RDBMS Developer
Informix DBA            SAS Programmer
Java Programmer                 SEI CMM Programmer/Analyst
Javascript Programmer   Sybase DBA
Lotus Notes Programmer  Unix Programmer
MS Access Programmer    Unix System Administrator
MS SQL Server DBA       Visual Basic Programmer
MS SQL Server Programmer     Web Developer
Novell Administrator    Windows NT Administrator
Mumps                   Windows 95 Administrator
Windows 98 Administrator

Good luck!

Mike

Michael Littman
VP Operations
TekMetrics, Inc.
www.tekmetrics.com <http://www.tekmetrics.com>
```

```
Automatic Cobol conversion tools make it possible to convert/certify millions
of lines of Cobol code in a fraction of the time required for conventional data
conversions.

For more information, please visit:

www.japiinfo.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Year 2000 Crisis

- **From:** "Robert" <rls@nospam.thanks.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7inj6f$v7r$1@dumber.ktis.net>`
- **References:** `<19990527110219.24164.00007321@ng33.aol.com>`

```

Japiinfo <japiinfo@aol.com> wrote in message > Automatic Cobol conversion
tools make it possible to convert/certify millions
> of lines of Cobol code in a fraction of the time required for conventional
data
> conversions.

If you are just now converting code and thinking you are going to get it
converted, tested, re-tested and re-tested with redundant data for any large
system you might as well forget it.

I've yet to see one of these "automatic" programs do anything more than miss
75% of the date fields and mangle up any valid data that were there to begin
with.

Rob
```

## Thread: NEWBIE Question about ACCEPT — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### NEWBIE Question about ACCEPT

- **From:** "Koen Van Impe" <101460.651@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ik58b$opi$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
Hello,

I'm only just started to work in Cobol and I'm experiencing a problem.

I want to use the accept commando but with a preinstalled value. For example
when I set the variable   name = "UNKNOWN"
and when I would then use Accept name line 10 position 5 there should be
already UNKNOWN written on the screen, in such a way that the user can
modify the text UNKNOWN.

Is there anyone who can help me?

Best Regards,

Koen Van Impe
Belgium
KoenVan_Impe@csi.com
```

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

example
>when I set the variable   name = "UNKNOWN"
>and when I would then use Accept name line 10 position 5 there should be
>already UNKNOWN written on the screen, in such a way that the user can
>modify the text UNKNOWN.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: Converting PC signed numbers — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting PC signed numbers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374D9ACD.F8BFA516@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
A co-worker is getting lots of data with PC generated numbers.  Some of
it is PIC ---,--9.99 etc.   It's not too hard to do INSPECTS to turn it
into COBOL data, but I was going to direct him to a Web site which
already had such code - but I lost the bookmark.

Anybody have such code or the web location?
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting PC signed numbers

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-sJmOTz5Tw1Bv@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<374D9ACD.F8BFA516@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 May 1999 19:19:41, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> 
wrote:

> A co-worker is getting lots of data with PC generated numbers.  Some of
> it is PIC ---,--9.99 etc.   It's not too hard to do INSPECTS to turn it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anybody have such code or the web location?

Howard:

Compute New-Number = Function Numval (edited-number)

and if it has currency symbols (DB, CR, $$)

Compute New-Number = Function Numval-c (Edited-Number)



-------------------------

this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

<a id="t-ffa18be6d5"></a>
## Thread: online software engineering study (win prizes) — 1 msgs

### online software engineering study (win prizes)

- **From:** Alex Kotov <kotov@cse.ogi.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<374DE72B.805AD170@cse.ogi.edu>`

```
Dear newsgroup readers,

I would like to ask for your help.

I'm a student at the Computer Science department of the Oregon
Graduate Institute. I have been following the developments in the Open
Source community for about 4 years and I've been fascinated with the
way Open Source movement works. I've been doing research in empirical
software engineering and I was wondering if the same principle that
drives the Open Software movement can work in other areas.

The main difficulty in empirical software engineering studies is the
great variability in people's performance (sometimes as much as 25:1),
which causes a lot of "noise" in analysis. Therefore studies with 30
or 40 participants are often just not powerful enough to get credible
results.

This gave me the idea of an "Open software engineering study". Using
the same principle utilized by the Open Source software, such studies
could leverage contributions of multiple participants spread all over
the world to overcome the "noise" problem and help us better
understand the effectiveness of different software engineering methods
and techniques. All the participants would have to do is connect to a
central web server and spend a little time performing some simple
software engineering tasks online. The large number of participants
would allow us to filter out the "noise" and make a justified
conclusion about which techniques work better under which
circumstances.

This kind of study has never been attempted before and some of the
professors on my committee are not convinced that such studies would
be able to attract enough volunteers to make them worthwhile.

This is where I need your help. I have put together a survey to find
out if there are enough people in the world who would like to make a
contribution to the field by participating in an internet-based
software engineering study. If you are interested in participating (or 
even if you are not), please take 10 minutes out of your busy schedule 
to take the survey. The results of this survey should either convince
my committee that online studies might work, or convince me that I'm
wasting my time working on them.

All people who take the survey will be entered in a drawing for
prizes. 

To take the survey, point your web browsers to

http://software-engineering.cse.ogi.edu/surv/driver.cgi?R=OG

If you have any questions or comments please email me at
kotov@cse.ogi.edu or call (503)748-7385. If you know someone who might
be interested in participating, please pass this information to them!

Thank you for your time,

Alex Kotov

Pacific Software Research Center
Oregon Graduate Institute of Science and Technology
```

---

<a id="t-18bf381ded"></a>
## Thread: Network API and cobol — 4 msgs

### Network API and cobol

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <tawright@i1.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6kn33.27$WA.238585@news1.i1.net>`

```
Has anyone ever successfully used implimented a tcp/ip connection over a
network using cobol?  If so can you help in pointing me in the right
direction.

Thanks
Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Network API and cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374e11ea.784622696@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6kn33.27$WA.238585@news1.i1.net>`

```
On Thu, 27 May 1999 21:38:46 -0500, "Tom Wright" <tawright@i1.net>
wrote:

>Has anyone ever successfully used implimented a tcp/ip connection over a
>network using cobol?  If so can you help in pointing me in the right
>direction.

http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/download.htm

I have an example there using Fujitsu COBOL and MicroFocus Cobol
(written by someone else, who deserves credit but who's name I cannot
remember this late) of Winsock2 API calls.  I have used this method to
interface PC to PC and Pc to Mainframe via TCP/IP.
```

#### ↳ Re: Network API and cobol

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xqo33.2551$u03.1669@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<6kn33.27$WA.238585@news1.i1.net>`

```
Tom Wright <tawright@i1.net> wrote in message
news:6kn33.27$WA.238585@news1.i1.net...
> Has anyone ever successfully used implimented a tcp/ip connection over a
> network using cobol?  If so can you help in pointing me in the right
> direction.

Tom, MERANT's Micro Focus compiler products have a proprietary run-time API
called CCI (Common Communication Interface, i think) which enable you to
establish such a connection (from/to NT to/from UNIX too). indeed, the
Fileshare (networked file i/o) service uses CCI for all of it's comms.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Network API and cobol

- **From:** Mr. Scott <scott_davis@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7im860$j6v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<6kn33.27$WA.238585@news1.i1.net> <Xqo33.2551$u03.1669@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Also, if you're running under Unix, you can make direct system calls to
the sockets API.  I did, and it works great.  Columbia Sportsware's
entire warehouse is automated under a COBOL sockets program I wrote.
If you'd like to see some code I'd be more than happy to supply it.
Just reply back.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
We have a number of FREE CDs to give away of Casegen Cobol for Windows. The
CDs include Fujitsu Cobol compiler Version 3 and contain the complete
product but limited to small/medium sized programs and without DLL support.
You can of course always upgrade later to the latest "Casegen Cobol for
Windows Professional".

This is a great way to learn Cobol or to get familiar with Windows
programming. All common Windows controls are included as well as our
powerful COBOL editor which syntax checks your Cobol and makes sure you
start in the right columns. The editor can also be used standalone when you
click on a Cobol (.CBL) or copy file (.CPY). Works with Windows 95/98 and
NT 4.0. 

We must charge $10 US for CD, postage and packing, so please send cash or
check to:

Casegen Systems Ltd
Swan House
White Hart Street
High Wycombe
Bucks
UK HP11 2HL

You can send cash in other major European, South African, Australian or New
Zealand currency or a check in Euros or UK pounds. 

Be quick! If we run out of CDs, we guarantee to return your money.

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd
www.casegen.co.uk
simon@casegen.co.uk
```

<a id="t-376382caa3"></a>
## Thread: The Fate of COBOL....... — 26 this month / 107 total

### The Fate of COBOL.......

- **From:** SiD <sdawson@netspace.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au>`

```
What are your thoughts about the fate of COBOL as a programming
language....?

COBOL seems to be keeping programmers like myself, employed and well
paid ,in the short term, with the Y2K, the euro dollar and (in
Australia, anyway) the introduction of GST. And the maintenance of such
fixes, in the mid term, will continue this. But what about the long term
prospects?

I have recently returned to programming in VAX COBOL after 4 years and
find it more of a challenge compared to VB or Progress (GUI 4GL like
VB). Line coded languages have more character (pardon the pun) and I
want to keep developing robust ACMS/DecForms/DCL/COBOL/SQL systems for
the next 20-odd years before I start living a real life. Will COBOL
survive?

Simon
```

#### ↳ Re: The Fate of COBOL.......

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simon@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea920$ea3d8320$4c0870c3@email>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au>`

```
I thought your email ID was rather unfortunate for your point. I believe
SID stands for "Sudden Infant Death" or cot death. 

Its a good job we couldn't possible think of Cobol as being an "infant"!

Simon Cordingley

SiD <sdawson@netspace.net.au> wrote in article
<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au>...
> What are your thoughts about the fate of COBOL as a programming
> language....?
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: The Fate of COBOL.......

- **From:** "Robert" <rls@nospam.thanks.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au>`

```

SiD <sdawson@netspace.net.au> wrote in message ...
> What are your thoughts about the fate of COBOL as a programming
> language....?

This is the classic debate.  For as long as their has been COBOL, there has
been a group of people claiming COBOL was dead.

While I was in college a few years ago I was told that COBOL was dead, I
would never find a job.  Every other week there must be some article that
crosses my desk about new WhizBang for the PC that will kill the mainframe
and put us COBOL folks out of business.

It isn't going to happen.  Take a look at who feeds this line to you.  It is
usually some hotshot PC person who thinks their 400mhz Intel chip can handle
terrabytes of data per day and 3,000 users simulatenously logged in.

COBOL will be around for a long, long time.  I'm 25 now and when I finally
retire I am sure we'll still be coding in COBOL.

Rob
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Fate of COBOL.......

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374F6A72.48D6D24A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net>`

```
Robert wrote:
> 
> While I was in college a few years ago I was told that COBOL was dead, 

'EBCDIC is a redundant coding no-one uses it anymore' quoted from
a lecture on computer fundamentals last year in University.

Cobol is fantastic to run on IBM MVS, if you run C code you will
find that it performs very badly.  There is a basic difference
with memory allocation on MVS that just makes Cobol perform much
better.

I will keep on doing it for years yet,
Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net>`

```
Intel has now released the details of the Merced chip (64 bit)
and all the packed decimal support is gone from the instruction
set. This makes writing a fast Cobol implementation that much
harder. The design is totally focussed on running C and Java
programs fast. Clearly Intel sees COBOL having a limited future.

They have got it wrong in the past. Remember the 432, designed
for ADA? Or the 286 - accurately described by Bill Gates as brain
damaged.

Tim Josling


Robert wrote:
> 
...
> 
> COBOL will be around for a long, long time.  I'm 25 now and when I finally
> retire I am sure we'll still be coding in COBOL.
> 
> Rob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

- **From:** "Doug McKibbin" <dmckib@goodnet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qzpxvotbbqargpbz.fch7dv0.pminews@enews.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Intel is not a major player in mainframe shops running COBOL apps.  IBM
will never abandon it.  Based on intel's past problems with every recent
chip, it will be years before Merced is usable.  Security is job #1 in the
mainframe world, and that is the weakness of any Intel chip.

Doug

On Sat, 29 May 1999 16:44:49 +1000, Tim Josling wrote:

>Intel has now released the details of the Merced chip (64 bit)
>and all the packed decimal support is gone from the instruction
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

- **From:** "Robert" <rls@nospam.thanks.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au>`

```

----- Original Message -----
From: Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>

> Intel has now released the details of the Merced chip (64 bit)
> and all the packed decimal support is gone from the instruction
> set. This makes writing a fast Cobol implementation that much
> harder. The design is totally focussed on running C and Java
> programs fast. Clearly Intel sees COBOL having a limited future.

Intel is a "never ran" in the world of COBOL and big business.  MVS COBOL is
the tool of choice for the thousands of COBOL shops out there that run it.
I would never try and run a serious COBOL application on any Intel platform.
It just doesn't make good business sense.

IBM is the key player for COBOL.  And I can assure you that they have no
plans on dropping COBOL support.

Looking back on Intel's other decisions in the past decade, we'll see how
fast they backtrack this one as well.

Rob

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
------------
Robert L. Stinnett                          Email:  rls at ktis dot net
COBOL Programmer by Choice!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7iptnv$mjh$3@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net>`

```
Robert wrote in message <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net>...
>Intel is a "never ran" in the world of COBOL and big business.  MVS COBOL
is
>the tool of choice for the thousands of COBOL shops out there that run it.
>I would never try and run a serious COBOL application on any Intel
platform.
>It just doesn't make good business sense.
>


The PC is here, like it or not.  Languages are chosen based on what you want
to program, not on what platform you are going to use (at least providing
that the language exists).  There are about half a dozen decent Cobols that
run on PC's, and at least three that have major established user bases.

Bussiness sense has to do with cost vs benefit, not  byte vs word, or
mainframe vs micro.  It does not make bussiness sense to specify a mainframe
for a small bussiness, anymore than it makes sense to specify a PC as the
main server for a large multinational.

As I said in the previous post, declaring that lack of BCD opcodes on a
machine means Cobol obsolesence makes about as much sense as delcaring that
the lack of byte oriented architecture means the obsolesence of alphabets.
Or that computers are binary means base ten will no longer be used in
bussiness, for that matter.

That is why we program them.  To get them to do things that are not single
opcodes.
```

```
I'm afraid thats as myopic as the reverse.  You've been sold the idea
the COBOL is for the mainframe only, and it's just not so.  The FIRST
program I wrote on an intel chip was a COBOL program.  As far as
efficiency goes, the source language really doesn't matter. What
matters is the object code, and how much processing is offloaded from
the main CPU ot helpers such as graphics cards, IO subsystems etc.

BTW: I am primarily an MVS (OS/390) COBOLer at this point.

>IBM is the key player for COBOL.  And I can assure you that they have no

Second we assume that at one point it WAS.  Troll bait taken - hook,
line and sinker.
```

```
packed numbers to/from the floating point stack (as floating point
numbers).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3750d6ac.19973531@netnews>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net>`

```
'Twas Sat, 29 May 1999 10:20:30 -0400, when "donald tees"
<donald@willmack.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> I have been coding in Cobol for 30 years, and I have yet to use packed
> decimal.

It is certainly quite possible now to write Cobol without using packed
decimal.  But if you were writing Cobol 30 years ago without using packed
decimal, you were either wasting expensive storage space, wasting
expensive processing power, or both.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00

```
David makes a good point, but he did not address the cost of converting the
comp-3 back to display for display
purposes, NOR the differents between comp-3 on one system vs. another.  Add
those factors (at least) then
we have the basis for a discussion.

Warren Simmons
David K. Bryant <dbryant@netcom.com> wrote in message
news:dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com...
> "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
There are about a dozen points that he missed.  For example, thirty years
ago, the machine that I was coding on did not *have* packed decimal.  The
most efficient storage on that particular machine was called "usage
display-6" and was sixbit packed 6 bytes per 36 bit word. That was a PDP-10,
running Tops-10, circa 1968.

The fact also remains that there is absolutely no reason to convert, with
the exception of a few platforms that convert to do arithmetic.  That is
certainly not true on a number of machines.  On several, stuff is converted
to integer, then converted back.  Packed takes *extra* time on those
platforms, not less.  On others, arithmetic is done in Ascii, digit by
digit.  The assumption that Cobol always converts to packed decimal to do
math is simply erroneous.

Thirdly, lets look at that 10:1 figure.  It assumes that the only thing done
in a program is mathmatics.  In fact, a typical Cobol program is about 3-4%
clculation.  I-O accounts for about 90%, typically.  Moves typically take up
another 8-9%.  Most Cobol is looking up data, and formating it for I-O.  If
one takes that into consideration, then packing all the data may very well
cost, rather than save time.  If I look up a figure and put it on a page or
screen, rather that look up a figure and do a multiply, then put it back
without printing or examining it any way, then anything gained by lack of
conversion for the one function is lost by the time taken to convert for the
other.  What do you do most in Cobol?  Calculations or reporting and
updating?  The 10:1 figure might apply if you are doing large sacle matrix
arithmetic, but *that* I would not use Cobol for.  Even if I did, I would
make the data floating point, not packed. I doubt you could measure the
execution time difference in 95% of all Cobol programs.

Then there is storage efficiency.  The gain there is roughly 50%, if all
your numbers are an even number of digits, and none of your data is anything
but numbers.  However, the ratio of text to numbers in most data processing
is about 10 to 1.  When you look at actual figures the savings are not that
great.  At todays prices, probably about dollar per system, in my case.  My
systems sell for $20,000.  A buck of disk I do not worry about.  If it costs
me a day every five years, then it is not justified.

Next we get to machine compatability.  Every system that I have worked on
for the last 25 years has involved at least two platforms. Usage clauses, of
various sorts, are begging for trouble in that kind of situation.  A simple
standard of usage display, sign separate, gets rid of about 50% of all
programming problems, not only for mixed platforms but also for mixed
languages.  How often, in the last year, have we had to deal with people
attempting to read Cobol files from another language and getting screwed by
nibble oriented system designs?  Ascii is standard.  Bytes and overpunches
are not.  I stick to standards because they work.

There are several other points I could make, but that should be enough of a
rant to get the talk going.

;<)

Warren Simmons wrote in message <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>...
>David makes a good point, but he did not address the cost of converting the
>comp-3 back to display for display
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ivaat$rvi$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net> <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au>...
>About 13 years ago I saved 45 minutes from a six hour run time.  I
>did it by adding COMP-3 to a lot of working storage elements.
>
>This was a heavy calculation module though (percentages and
>projected earnings).

Yes, I can see that.  There are, obviously, times when a particular program
is calculation bound.  In those cases, I see no reason not to use a machine
specific usage for efficiency.  I have also written the odd assembler
routine for precisely the same reason ... a specific requirement for high
efficiency in a specific module. That efficiency may be required and/or
justified.

There is a major jump from that reasoning, though, to asserting that all
numbers should be exressed in BCD, or that not using BCD is a mark of
incompetance.  There is also a need to realize that there can be negative
aspects of those methods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net> <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au> <7ivaat$rvi$2@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> There is a major jump from that reasoning, though, to asserting that all
> numbers should be exressed in BCD, or that not using BCD is a mark of
> incompetance.  There is also a need to realize that there can be negative
> aspects of those methods.

"BCD"? That's the internal coding structure for many of IBM's 2nd
generation machines, e.g., 1400-series stuff; and has nothing to do with
packed decimal data (which arrived with S/360 and the 8-bit byte). BCD
is a 6-bit code and maps well, if not perfectly, to punched cards (for
those who weren't there or who don't remember, any punched card column
could contain the digits 0-9, plus two zone punches, "11" & "12").

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375359b5@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net> <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au> <7ivaat$rvi$2@news.igs.net> <37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net>`

```
nevertheless,
Intel refers to the FBLD co-processor instruction as:
'Load Packed BCD Memory Operand into ST'
so BCD is also used as a name for packed data.

William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote in message
news:37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net...
> donald tees wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > incompetance.  There is also a need to realize that there can be
negative
> > aspects of those methods.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3750E7EF.8964584C@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au>`

```
A few people accused me of predicting COBOL's demise. I don't
think so. Cobol is good for business apps due to the decimal
instructions including 18 digit support ($1 billion doesnt fit in
a C 'int' - oops) and because more people seem to be able to cope
with cobol than with C,

But the effort to write a decent free cobol compiler for Intel is
that much harder due to the dropping of the decimal instructions
(FBSTP, FBLD, AAA, AAD, AAM, AAS).

I can understand why Intel have done it. IBM's mainframe CPUs use
a lot of silicon on the decimal instructions which the
competition use to make C programs run faster, so there is a real
cost (see IEEE Micprocessor Magazine earlier this year),

Tim Josling


Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> Intel has now released the details of the Merced chip (64 bit)
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Rob
```

```

wrote:

>Intel has now released the details of the Merced chip (64 bit)
>and all the packed decimal support is gone from the instruction

Troll....

Here's something interesting:

........excerpt from http://www.adtools.com

Fujitsu COBOL and IA-64 

Fujitsu recently announced the development of a 64-bit COBOL compiler
for Intel's IA-64 architecture. The first implementation of the COBOL
compiler will be on Merced, the code name for Intel's first
microprocessor based on the IA-64 architecture.
```

<a id="t-36c9eee673"></a>
## Thread: How different is Wang cobol? — 3 msgs

### How different is Wang cobol?

- **From:** johnrben@home.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7im82i$j46$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Can a Cobol programmer work with Wang cobol? Is it much different?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: How different is Wang cobol?

- **From:** "George Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airfoce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5760720EA79936A8.D4E7CBF479B03DC2.92432864477B7AD9@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7im82i$j46$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
COBOL is COBOL.  It really doesn't matter what the platform is.  (And before
the purists jump on my case -  You just have to learn and adjust to the
nuances of the platform/operating system involved which isn't all too
difficult.)

I went the other way, I cut my teeth in programming on Wang VS's and then
made the jump to Client-Server COBOL programming.  Wang has (had?) some very
nice features that I miss but I have learned to adjust.
```

#### ↳ Re: How different is Wang cobol?

- **From:** JimMorcombe <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3751DB5E.88C88F6B@byronics.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7im82i$j46$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Wang Cobol is a nice and simple Cobol.  I worked with VS Cobol years ago
and still have fond memories of the product.

The screen handling is quite different from normal cobol, but easy to
come to grips with.  I found that new Cobol programmers without VS Cobol
experience were immediately productive.

Jim Morcombe


johnrben@home.com wrote:

> Can a Cobol programmer work with Wang cobol? Is it much different?
>
> Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

## Thread: Information needed - we will pay! — 1 msgs

### Information needed - we will pay!

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simon@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea922$be3b6ba0$4c0870c3@email>`

```
$10 reward!

We need to find the names and addresses of Development or Programming
Managers working in IBM Mainframe sites that use the TELON Cobol
application generator currently owned by Computer Associates - now called
CA-TELON.

We want to tell them about our service to convert Telon programs to Ansi
'85 100% Cobol programs without any assembler runtimes, thus removing the
need to licence Telon. We charge $40 US per program for this service which
can be completed on or off-site.

If you know any Telon sites and the name of a Development or Programming
Manager please send us the company name, manager name and company address
together with your name and address and we will send you a crisp ten dollar
note.

Rules: 

1) You must be first with this information
2) It MUST be accurate   
3) Must be a Telon Cobol mainframe site
4) The UK is not covered - we already know the sites

There is no limit to the number on ten dollars notes we will send you and
if you are familiar with a book in your country that provides this
information, please let us know and we will send several ten dollar notes!

Many thanks for your time.

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd
simon@casegen.co.uk
www.casegen.co.uk
```

## Thread: info — 5 this month / 18 total

### info

- **From:** "pc computer" <pccomput@tin.it>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bea8f9$f6e0c000$8202bac2@UGate>`

```
I'm italian, and please :
How i can find on net some Cobol tutorial's in italian ? 

Thank
```

#### ↳ Re: info

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TmA33.2996$u03.1790@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<01bea8f9$f6e0c000$8202bac2@UGate>`

```
pc computer <pccomput@tin.it> wrote:
> I'm italian, and please :
> How i can find on net some Cobol tutorial's in italian ?

find a tutorial in english and then use AltaVista's translation service. you
can find it at:

http://babelfish.altavista.com/cgi-bin/translate

there's even a babelfish tool that you can download and plug-in to your
browsers toolbar, so you can translate at the push of a button.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: info

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374eeb62.26796671@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<01bea8f9$f6e0c000$8202bac2@UGate> <TmA33.2996$u03.1790@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Gazaloo:

I think that you are probably correct that this is the best approach
for our Italian friend if self-study is desired, but he should be
forewarned that the Babelfish translation tool (as all translation
tools I have ever used) does a poor job of translating to a proper
grammatical format.

Language translation is very complex and Babelfish tends to translate
the text in a fairly "literal" fashion.  It usually translates well
written English into poor Italian or excellent Italian into poor
English.

That doesn't mean that it is useless.  As long as it is recognized
that the text may be a bit awkward, the translated text can generally
be understood.  

"Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com> wrote:

>pc computer <pccomput@tin.it> wrote:
>> I'm italian, and please :
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Serious COBOL Development for Windows and Thin Client — 1 msgs

### Serious COBOL Development for Windows and Thin Client

- **From:** "ESI" <askesi@esiusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7imqav$9c4$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Wouldn't it be nice to use a COMPLETE development environment that builds
fully executable programs.
Programs that include your own COBOL application logic...
Programs developed through a full VISUAL interface...

You can!
Generate applications for the Windows Desktop, the department Server or Thin
Client.
Use an enterprise development environment - MPACT

MPACT generates COBOL Windows applications (16-bit and 32-bit).  It will
generate 2-tier client/server accessing indexed files and relational
databases via native ODBC calls (32-bit only).

You can take a closer look at MPACT on our Web site at:

 http://www.esiusa.com/MARKET/Product/AppDev/mpactcsd.htm

or contact us for info at:

 email:  askesi@esiusa.com
 phone:  (850)575-0179

Benefits:

Repository/Metadatabase

 Use is optional, but it's a tremendous asset in a complex database
environment

Flexibility

Local testing of applications with a simulated database

Portability

Automatic I/O generation for ODBC/ISAM databases
 Just regenerate code with a different switch to access a different database

Currently we have database support for both Oracle and NT Sql Server.

Compilers supported are:

  Micro Focus 3.2 and 3.4 (16-bit)
  Micro Focus 4.0
  NetExpress
  Fujitsu (4.0 and later)
  Unisys COBOL85 for the 3-tier server program
```

<a id="t-4fcf668531"></a>
## Thread: Need title for new OS/390 applications programming class — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Need title for new OS/390 applications programming class

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990529112802.01097.00001610@ng-cq1.aol.com>`

```
Hi,

I've been developing a new course for a few months now, and I'm getting close
enough to start describing it to training coordinators. The two people I've
talked to about it apparently heard something different than I was describing.

My new course is an in-depth exploration of subroutines and mainlines: how to
call and be called in four different languages (Assembler, COBOL, PL/I and C),
in an LE environment. It includes (for all four languages): 

* a review of describing elementary data items, structures, and arrays
* how to handle null-terminated strings
* how to handle the PARM from the EXEC statement in batch jobs 
   (including how to use CEE3PRM, and when that doesn't return 
   what you would expect)
* syntax of call / function references
* calling to / from the same language and different languages
  (trying to avoid special cases like pragmas or calling language 
     intialization entry points)
* format of object modules (OBJ, XOBJ, GOFF)
* how the linkage editor and program binder work
* load modules and program objects
* how parameters are passed in an argument list
* passing and receiving data by reference and by value
* language-specific options for calling
* language-specific options for passing / receiving data
* omitting arguments
* handling variable argument lists
* static vs. dynamic calls: whys and hows
* module attributes and their impact: reuable, reentrant
* mainenance issues and techniques
* DLLs

When I've told people about this, they seem to hear "Oh, how to pass data
between the mainframe and a work station"! Now that sounds like a useful class,
and I will turn my attention to it next, perhaps, it is _not_ the class I am
currently developing.

My thought is that a good, snappy, descrptive title might help. So I am turning
to the usenet groups for two areas of help: 

1) can you come up with a name that would help describe and sell this course
succinctly? 

and 2) is this a course anyone would be interested in or am I wasting my time
(I've spent a lot of time, learned a lot of things that I thought I knew but
was wrong about, but it may be that this work only is helpful to me)?

Here are some titles I've been tossing about...

Secrets of InterProgram Communication
Subroutines
Advanced Subroutines
Mainlines and Subroutines


Thanks for any suggestions / feedback.


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Need title for new OS/390 applications programming class

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37500C13.62C@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<19990529112802.01097.00001610@ng-cq1.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock wrote:
> My new course is an in-depth exploration of subroutines and mainlines: how to
> call and be called in four different languages (Assembler, COBOL, PL/I and C),
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> succinctly?

Except for the first few items in your list, I would describe it as:
"Linking/Binding and Subroutine Linkage in the LE Environment"

> and 2) is this a course anyone would be interested in or am I wasting my time
> (I've spent a lot of time, learned a lot of things that I thought I knew but
> was wrong about, but it may be that this work only is helpful to me)?

I think there is a great need for this type of course, as most
programmers, especially younger ones, I encounter don't really know much
about load modules, and static vs. dynamic calls, etc.  Most have "grown
up" maintaining existing applications, as opposed to the big development
efforts of the 70's & early 80's...

Of course, interest/need is completely different from marketability...

How do DLL's fit in with your course ?  I thought they were PC only...

Bob 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need title for new OS/390 applications programming class

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7irsvn$4jb$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7ipigs$k0e$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <19990530103106.02731.00002492@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```

At an ACM conference celebrating 25 years or some such thing, Charles
Bachman won the award for his IDS efforts.
I believe he invented it while working for GE in NY. Charles was a member of
the Data Base Task Force of the
COBOL committee during it's efforts to bring this invention into the public
domain.

At the meeting where he received his award, his speach used the idea of
transaction processing being like navigating the seas. First following one
path, then another.  Now, that was a long time ago, and my memory is not
that good, but
ACM must have that talk in their files somewhere.  I have not heard from
Charlie for some time so don't know if he is
still active with the company he started.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need title for new OS/390 applications programming class

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7irsfp$ssr$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7iptpd$mjl$1@news.igs.net> <19990530103247.02731.00002493@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Not so much "not a seller" as "common in other courses".  You should note
that I am not a mainframe person ... but with that in mind I think that any
good course on any language is going to cover subroutine calls.  From the
sound of your course, the main difference is that you are looking at that
topic specifically, and spend time on interlanguage calls,  That particular
topic is rare, if only because it assumes students with a base knowledge of
several languages.

Of course, that could be simply mentioned in the course synopsis. I think,
though, that it is important enough to mention in the title.

S Comstock wrote in message
<19990530103247.02731.00002493@ng-bg1.aol.com>...
>Don,
>
>Thanks for the suggestion. But it doesn't also indicate that the course is
good
>for single language applications. Or don't you think that's a "seller"?
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>USA
```

```
using Netexpress I call a window from a DLL using the dll-name as entry

DialogSystem). I am able to influence the behaviour of the window, show
it clipped or as an owned window ...
Now it should be possible to make this call to the same dll from some
other language (I use Borland's Delphi).
I managed that but I am not able to make the 'dll-window' behave like an
owned window. (hiding automatically when I minimize the main-app ...)

May anyone tell me how to achieve that?
I think I have to give my app's handle as parameter with the call?!?

Thank you in advance for any help,
Florian.
```

```
Thank You Very Much

Please mail me
Roel.Beckers@village.uunet.be

Roel
Belgium
```

#### ↳ Re: Run Time Error

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<374fd6f1@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7iojqp$lob$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
If you are using MicroFocus (always tell us which compiler you are using
!!),
then 043 means that "file information missing for indexed file". This
normally
means that the .IDX index file is missing or not accessible.

RoBe <roel.beckers@village.uunet.be> wrote in message
news:7iojqp$lob$1@nickel.uunet.be...
> Does anybody know what this Run Time Error means
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-c37a7c25d6"></a>
## Thread: the "CALL" function in cobol — 3 msgs

### the "CALL" function in cobol

- **From:** "RoBe" <roel.beckers@village.uunet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7irn28$6a0$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
Hello...

I have some problems

I made a menu in cobol. In this menu you can choose between some programs
that launch automatically when you press enter.

Only when I launch to many programs (with the function CALL) after each
other, i get this run time error

Run Time Error 198

It means that I used to many CALLS

Is there a way to avoid that problem?

Please mail me
Roel.Beckers@village.uunet.be
Thank You


Roel
Belgium
```

#### ↳ Re: the "CALL" function in cobol

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8yh43.5893$NJ.1154@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7irn28$6a0$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
See  if the 'CANCEL' verb is available.  It frees the memory used.

RoBe wrote in message <7irn28$6a0$1@nickel.uunet.be>...
>Hello...
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: the "CALL" function in cobol

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7irsfr$ssr$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7irn28$6a0$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
You can follow each call with a cancel, and the program space will be freed
up.  Of course, that also slows down the application as each subroutine must
be re-load each time it is called.

A better approach is to cancel the modules that are rarely used, and leave
the ones that are frequently used in memory.  That takes a bit of tinkering
to get it right, as you could run out of memory, but will result in better
execution times.

A third solution is to convert to an operating system that allows
unrestricted memory useage, and add lots of memory, though that tends to be
*lots* of work.


RoBe wrote in message <7irn28$6a0$1@nickel.uunet.be>...
>Hello...
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Do you program visually or non visually? — 1 msgs

### Do you program visually or non visually?

- **From:** charlie.smith@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928106323.1@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com>`

```
 -=> Quoting judmc123@bellsouth.net to All <=-

 ju> That's an interesting point.  I also work that way, and as I
 write
 ju> logic, I visualize it being executed, sort of like an animation
 in a
 ju> debugger.  I can't imagine how one could program any other way,
 and
 ju> used to think that no one did or could.  But I have an associate
 who
 ju> is a very accomplished programmer with over 20 years experience.
 ju> When I casually commented to him about how programming was
 visually
 ju> oriented, he replied that it was not at all visually oriented to
 him.
 ju> I thought we had mis-communicated, but he kept insisting that it
 was
 ju> a very non-visual process to him, and was a bit surprised at the
 idea.
 ju> Whatever is going on in his head obviously works, but I can't
 imagine
 ju> what it's like. :-)

Hi Judson,

I work the same way you do - visualizing what the program does as the
data passes thru.  I'm always finding that I stop right in the middle
of coding and mentally talk the logic out - what happens if this
particular piece of data comes in, etc.

Has worked very well for more than 15 years now and I don't see it
changing either.  Shoot, most of the time I sit down with just a
 rough
idea of what needs to be done and can code it up in vry little time.
My manager always wants an estimate (gotta bill that client!) and it
usually takes me longer to work up that than it would to just program
it.

Charlie Smith



-----------------------------------------------------------------
Charlie Smith                      Smith Data Processing Services
csmit002@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com      The CyberSpace BBS - 1:231/992
Decatur Township Info Network      BBS/PPP/FAX --- 1 317 856 9020
-----------------------------------------------------------------

... Backup not found: (A)bort (R)etry (S)lap nearest innocent
 bystander.
```

<a id="t-3f2d7ccf16"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and a future — 6 this month / 13 total

### COBOL and a future

- **From:** "fs" <fred_snyder@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rYb43.44$cb5.12265@ord-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In the COBOL debate thread I didn't see mention of UNIX platforms. I think
you will find that most UNIX users downsized from various mainframe
platforms and are running COBOL apps very successfully. In addition the
number of NT sever apps written in COBOL is on the rise (now that NT is
"starting" to mature as a serious operating system). Several companies, my
employer included, are planning web apps within an NT environment with COBOL
and Oracle on the server and a thin-client browser front-end. I have also
been lucky enough to get involved with a voice response system that uses
Micro Focus COBOL on the server with very good response times.

In other words COBOL will out live us all, and our grandson's will be having
this same debate after we're long gone.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<rYb43.44$cb5.12265@ord-read.news.verio.net>`

```
COBOL programmers will one day be frantically attempting to deal with the
Y3K problem.
And they will be asked to do so constrained by a totally inadequate budget.
```


##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and a future

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uWKCTY6q#GA.361@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<rYb43.44$cb5.12265@ord-read.news.verio.net> <37528279.655A17E@zip.com.au>`

```
    I would assume that the Y3K problem could start at any time, but 2080
would be my best guess.  Some people have been "solving" the Y2K problem
with pivot dates.  Once you pass the pivot date in the new cetury, the fix
fails.

    Of course, why would someone want to look at detailed sales history from
100 years ago.  How many applications truly create data that will be
meaningfull 100 years from now?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and a future

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375314FB.ED25F29C@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<rYb43.44$cb5.12265@ord-read.news.verio.net> <37528279.655A17E@zip.com.au> <uWKCTY6q#GA.361@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
>     I would assume that the Y3K problem could start at any time, but 2080
> would be my best guess.  Some people have been "solving" the Y2K problem
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> meaningfull 100 years from now?

  That wouldn't be a "Y3K" problem, as it has no relation to year 3000,
only to reaching the end of a 100-year window.  Those who have chosen a
fixed windowing scheme to reduce the effort of Y2K fixes could start
having problems way before 2080.  Current personnel files can contain
birthdates back into the 1930's and 1940's, and if a "common" window for
all date fields was chosen, things could begin to get dicey as early as
2030 or 2040. Earlier problems could occur for files that also contain
future dates.  Any company that has taken the easy approach on Y2K had
better start giving serious thought by 2001 as to how they are going to
get windowed dates out of database files and out of program internals
while there is still plenty of time.  One of the lessons of Y2K that
should have been learned by anyone who has been awake the last several
years is that programs, and especially the databases they create, have a
lifetime and an influence beyond their lifetime that is much, much
longer than their designers anticipate.
  If those who took the easy path on Y2K properly remedy the use of
fixed windowing (and the use of any other clever compression techniques
with a limited span)  while there is still plenty of time, there should
not be any major date-related problems on any century transition until
there is either a calendar change or a 9999 to 10,000 year transition. 
If they don't learn from the lessons of the past, then they will be
repeating the recent date maintenance exercise every 50-80 years.
  Another lesson that should have been learned is that an installation
should standardize on one routine for any date calculation, especially
that influenced by leap year considerations.  This is both to avoid
errors (it's amazing how many people including some programmers have
problems with the leap-year rules), and to be kind to future generations
who will be working with the cloned descendants of your code when the
next major calendar adjustment is required (at present rates, the
leap-year rules may require the introduction of a 3200-year exception
rule on or about 3200).  We can't write code to implement rules that
don't yet exist, but we can at least centralize things so that only one
routine needs to be changed to address a change in all applications.
  Historians could have an interest in all kinds of mundane data from
previous generations.  They are going to have serious problems with
records which are only preserved in machine readable form on obsolete
media for obsolete programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and a future

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3753251e@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<rYb43.44$cb5.12265@ord-read.news.verio.net> <37528279.655A17E@zip.com.au> <uWKCTY6q#GA.361@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com> <375314FB.ED25F29C@acm.org>`

```
unix will have a problem around 2031 or thereabouts when the number of
seconds
since 1970 won't fit in 32-bits.
```

<a id="t-78926dc753"></a>
## Thread: How different is Wang cobol? — 2 msgs

### Re: How different is Wang cobol?

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFCK0A6.Lo8@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<abc@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
"George Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airfoce.net> writes:

>COBOL is COBOL.  It really doesn't matter what the platform is.

Yeah, right.
 

Wang VS Cobol (74 & 85) has many nice features that make
moving to another dialect difficult.  For instance, I've been 
writing Wang Cobol for 15 years and have never needed to write
a DECLARATIVE.  But the biggest difference is screen definitions.

Wang probably has the most powerful screen abilities of any
Cobol I've found (I think I'm up to six different dialects now).
Things like controlling what data can be accepted into a screen
field (upper only, uplow, numeric), codeless validation (attach 
a validation table or a RANGE statement to a screen field), and
<drum roll> repeating fields (define it once; repeat horizontally,
repeat vertically, or both).

It's all described in Chapter 4 of the Cobol manual (with samples).


If there's anything about Wang you want to know, just ask me.
```

#### ↳ Re: How different is Wang cobol?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eyMeTY6q#GA.119@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<abc@library-proxy.airnews.net> <dbryantFCK0A6.Lo8@netcom.com>`

```
    Wang Cobol is nice if you are converting TO Wang Cobol.  If going the
other direction, God help you.  Mostly screen stuff.  Of course I have not
seen Wang VS cobol in years.
```

<a id="t-5409af4ab1"></a>
## Thread: Common Procedures in PowerCOBOL — 2 msgs

### Common Procedures in PowerCOBOL

- **From:** "Mark Hallett" <mark@bizwerkz.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7isu3e$2tkg9$1@titan.xtra.co.nz>`

```
Hi,

I have a small query regarding the calling of common procedures
from event procedures in Fujitsu PowerCOBOL (v4).

I wish to be able to call a common procedure (in this case to update the
fields on a form) from more than one event procedure (such as the
clicking on several different navigation buttons).  It seems that each
event procedure is a separate entity unto itself, and seems unable to
share any common code -- apart from cutting and pasting the required
procedure code into each and every event procedure... a very wasteful
and inefficient method.

Is there any way to have a set of common procedures, which are specific
to a certain form, that can be utilised from any of the form's other
procedures - including event procedures?  They don't need to be
available outside the form.

Thanks.

Mark Hallett
mark@bizwerkz.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Common Procedures in PowerCOBOL

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7itv6g$5i0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7isu3e$2tkg9$1@titan.xtra.co.nz>`

```
In your form, you can declare a procedure.  That procedure can be called
from any event.

>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** Bigg-Daddy <d.clem@popmail.csuohio.edu>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928193960.16457@www.remarq.com>`

```
if i do   divide a to b giving c.
        How can I Multiply 100 by c ?

Without getting a numeric edited error?

Please help me, i am a beginner.



**** Posted from RemarQ - http://www.remarq.com - Discussions Start Here (tm) ****
```

## Thread: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work? — 1 this month / 9 total

_Thread started [1999-06](1999-06.md#t-dd595ee917)._

#### ↳ Re: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ivp2q$qns@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7ivk7f$fpl$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>`

```
Is there a reason that you aren't sticking with Micro Focus' (MERANT's)
"windows" products - and using NetExpress? This may not be the cheapest
approach (for compilers or tools) but is almost certainly the easiest for
"conversion" purposes.

If you want a new (more portable) user-interface, you could stick with the
NetExpress compiler and go with Sp2 (see many threads in this newsgroup) for
developing the interface.
```
